Other Requirements / Notes: When indicated by conditional-admission status or academic standing, certain students will be required to complete this course successfully in a given semester.
Full Course Description: This course is designed to enhance students’ success in college studies through the development of effective academic and self-management skills. Topics include note taking, time management, reading and test-taking skills, concentration, memory techniques, handling stress, motivation, and exploring and clarifying academic and life goals. Course activities may include readings, self-assessments, quizzes and exercises to practice skills and techniques, consultations with an instructor to review work plans and progress, and a final exam. When indicated by conditional-admission status or academic standing, certain students will be required to complete this course successfully in a given semester. Fall. Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: Develop an understanding of accounting concepts and procedures as they apply to the business cycles of revenue, expenditure, and conversion.
Full Course Description: The course explores the nature and use of accounting information, business transactions and the content of financial statements in the context of reporting to external corporate stakeholders. Students will prepare financial statements in accordance with Generally Accepted Accounting Principles. In doing so you should be able to accomplish the following: 1. Develop an understanding of accounting concepts and procedures as they apply to the business cycles of revenue, expenditure, and conversion. 2. Use accounting information for short-term planning and decision- making. 3. Apply internal control principles and procedures as they apply to cash.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Prerequisites: ACCT 2301 with a C or higher.
Other Requirements / Notes: Areas of emphasis include the usefulness and understanding of accounting information for managerial decision-making.
Full Course Description: This course introduces the preparation, compilation, interpretation, and utilization of basic managerial accounting principles and concepts. Areas of emphasis include the usefulness and understanding of accounting information for managerial decision-making. Some of the major topics include the fundamentals of job order costing, process costing, activity- based costing, cost-volume-profit analysis, make-or-buy and special- order decisions, capital budgeting, and ratio analysis. Prerequisite: ACCT 2301 with a C or higher.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: Successful completion of this course satisfies the Ethics academic course requirement for candidates sitting for the CPA exam in the State of Texas.
Full Course Description: This course encompasses the study of ethical philosophies and ethical reasoning with an emphasis on ethical dilemmas faced by members of the accounting profession. Topics include: ethical reasoning, integrity, objectivity, independence and other core values of the accounting profession, as well as professional codes of conduct (from TSBPA, AICPA, SEC, and others). These areas will be researched, analyzed, and applied through the use of various publications, electronic media and case studies. Successful completion of this course satisfies the Ethics academic course requirement for candidates sitting for the CPA exam in the State of Texas. Prerequisite: PHIL 3313 or equivalent. Corequisite: ACCT 4650. Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Prerequisites: Junior standing, ACCT 2301, and ACCT 2303, both with a C or higher.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: Junior standing, ACCT 2301, and ACCT 2303, both with a C or higher.
Full Course Description: This course covers recording business transactions in accordance with U.S. Generally Accepted Accounting Principles. The course also provides exposure to International Financial Reporting Standards and the use of FASB’s Accounting Standards Codification website. Topics include: accounting for cash, receivables, inventories, investments, intangible assets, current liabilities and contingencies, revenue recognition, and plant, property and equipment. Prerequisites: Junior standing, ACCT 2301, and ACCT 2303, both with a C or higher.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: Junior standing and ACCT 3331.
Full Course Description: This course is a continuation of ACCT 3331. It continues the coverage of recording business transactions in accordance with U.S. Generally Accepted Accounting Principles. The course also provides exposure to International Financial Reporting Standards and the use of FASB’s Accounting Standards Codification website. Topics include: accounting for taxes, pensions, bonds and corporate capital. Prerequisites: Junior standing and ACCT 3331.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Prerequisites: ACCT 2301 and ACCT 2303 both with a C or higher.
Full Course Description: Accumulating, analyzing and reporting information relevant for managerial decision-making. Topics include standard and direct costing using job order and process cost methodologies; variance analysis; accounting for material, labor and overhead; budgeting and capital budgeting; relevant costs; lean accounting; and computer spreadsheet applications. Prerequisites: ACCT 2301 and ACCT 2303 both with a C or higher.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: Introduction to the federal income tax system, determination of income and deductions, and taxation of property transactions, with emphasis on individual taxpayers. Basic research techniques and tax return preparation. Prerequisites: ACCT 2301, ACCT 2303, and ACCT 3331.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Prerequisites: COSC 2425 with a C or better.
Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listing: COSC 3337.
Full Course Description: Topics include introduction to database concepts, data models, data normalization, data description languages, query facilities, file organization, index organization, file security, and data integrity and reliability. Cross-listing: COSC 3337. Prerequisite: COSC 2425 with a C or better.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: Topical issues in accounting. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisites: Vary by topic.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: Fund accounting and reporting for government and nonprofit entities, including state and local governments, colleges, hospitals and other public sector organizations. Prerequisite: ACCT 3332.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: An introduction to the theory, concepts and principles of auditing, emphasizing audit evidence, audit risk, ethical conduct and legal restrictions, professional standards, audit planning, and audit reports. Prerequisite: ACCT 3332.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: Analysis of fundamental accounting information systems. Emphasis on semantic modeling and systems design; relationship of system and organization; objectives, policies, procedures and plans. Prerequisite: ACCT 3331.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Prerequisites: 12 hours of upper-division accounting coursework, major in accounting, and permission of instructor.
Semester Offered: offered online and requires the completion of written assignments during the entire semester.
Other Requirements / Notes: Supervised experiential learning in a public, private, not-for-profit, or governmental accounting organization is required for this internship course. Students are required to complete a minimum of 120 hours at their internship and must secure an internship offer prior to enrolling. Prerequisites: 12 hours of upper-division accounting coursework, major in accounting, and permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Supervised experiential learning in a public, private, not-for-profit, or governmental accounting organization is required for this internship course. Students are required to complete a minimum of 120 hours at their internship and must secure an internship offer prior to enrolling. The course is offered online and requires the completion of written assignments during the entire semester. Candidates sitting for the CPA exam cannot count more than one three-hour internship (at either the undergraduate or graduate level) toward the TSBPA’s 30-accounting- hour requirement. Only one accounting internship course may be applied toward the undergraduate degree requirements. Prerequisites: 12 hours of upper-division accounting coursework, major in accounting, and permission of instructor.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Prerequisites: Junior or senior standing, major in accounting, completion of 12 hours of upper-division accounting and permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Students are required to complete a minimum of 240 hours at their internship. Prerequisites: Junior or senior standing, major in accounting, completion of 12 hours of upper-division accounting and permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Supervised experiential learning in a public accounting firm during the Spring semester. The course is designed to provide accounting students with an opportunity to apply classroom knowledge acquired in the field of accounting. Students are required to complete a minimum of 240 hours at their internship. Prerequisites: Junior or senior standing, major in accounting, completion of 12 hours of upper-division accounting and permission of instructor. Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: Students will conduct research with a faculty member on a problem of mutual interest. Students will spend the semester investigating, designing evolving and articulating a solution to their problem. Students will use authoritative literature and pronouncements, (i.e. codification database and/or professional tax research tools like RIA) to identify, organize and integrate diverse sources of information to reach a conclusion. Students will work with the faculty member while developing their research idea to discuss their plan, challenges, and progress toward the design of their solution.
Various leadership techniques, including drill and ceremonies, customs and courtesies, and uniform standards. Two laboratory hours a week. Graded as pass/no pass. Fall. Spring.

Introductory course exploring the overall roles and missions of the United States Air Force and career fields available in the Air Force. Emphasis on military customs and courtesies, appearance standards, Air Force core values, and written communication. One lecture hour a week. Corequisite: ARFS 1100. Fall.
Continuation of ARFS 1102, with an introduction to American military history and emphasis on personal communication. One lecture hour a week. Corequisite: ARFS 1100. Spring.

Course Title: Evolution of USAF Air and Space Power I
Full Course Description: Key historical events and milestones in the development of air power as a primary instrument of United States national security. One lecture hour a week. Corequisite: ARFS 1100. Fall.

Course Title: Evolution of USAF Air and Space Power II
Full Course Description: Continuation of ARFS 2111. Corequisite: ARFS 1100. Spring.

Leadership laboratory course for upper-division students. Further development of leadership skills through leadership positions within the cadet corps. Includes training of freshman and sophomore students as well as a practicum in Air Force unit operation. Two laboratory hours a week. Graded as pass/no pass. Fall. Spring.

Prerequisites: ARFS 1102, ARFS 1103, ARFS 2111, ARFS 2112, and a four- or five-week field training course or equivalent ROTC or military training.
Other Requirements / Notes: A study of leadership, management fundamentals, professional knowledge, Air Force personnel and evaluation systems, leadership ethics, and communication skills required of an Air Force junior officer.
Full Course Description: A study of leadership, management fundamentals, professional knowledge, Air Force personnel and evaluation systems, leadership ethics, and communication skills required of an Air Force junior officer. Case studies are used to examine Air Force leadership and management situations as a means of demonstrating and exercising practical application of the concepts being studied. Three lecture hours a week. Prerequisites: ARFS 1102, ARFS 1103, ARFS 2111, ARFS 2112, and a four- or five-week field training course or equivalent ROTC or military training. Corequisite: ARFS 3120. Fall.
A continuation of ARFS 3321. Three lecture hours a week. Prerequisite: ARFS 3321. Corequisite: ARFS 3120. Spring.

Examines national security policy making, decision, and execution processes, regional studies, advanced leadership ethics, and Air Force doctrine. Special subjects of interest focus on the military as a profession, officership, military justice, civilian control of the military, preparation for active duty, and current issues affecting military professionalism. Within this structure, continued emphasis is given to refining communication skills. Three lecture hours a week. Prerequisite: ARFS 3322. Corequisite: ARFS 3120. Fall.
Continuation of ARFS 3333. Prerequisite: ARFS 3333. Corequisite: ARFS 3120. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This course is intended to provide students majoring in Animation with an opportunity to focus on an animation related topic not covered in the major. Specific topics vary depending upon the faculty area of expertise. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. 2399 Topics in Animation This course is intended to provide students majoring in Animation 3 hours with an opportunity to focus on an animation related topic not covered in the major. Specific topics vary depending upon the faculty area of expertise. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

This survey course will cover the history, tools, and techniques of making animated films. Students may explore such animation processes as flipbooks, stop-motion, hand-drawn, rotoscoping, and digital animation.

While the practice of creating expressive motion from still images is almost as old as motion pictures themselves, the field of motion graphics is relatively young and rapidly evolving. This course will study traditional and digital techniques designed to make images and text more lively across a range of implementations appropriate for animated films, video games, presentations, and web design.

Prerequisites: Junior standing or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: Junior standing or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course is intended to provide students majoring in Animation with an opportunity to focus on an animation related topic not covered in the major. Specific topics vary depending upon the faculty area of expertise. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: Junior standing or permission of instructor.

This advanced digital animation course will build upon fundamentals from prior courses to explore a range of techniques which combine multiple visual elements within a single shot, including rotoscoping, digital motion capture, transformation, lighting, and particle effects. Prerequisite: ANIM 3328 or VGAM 3327.

Prerequisites: ANIM 3328 or VGAM 2323 or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Course may be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: ANIM 3328 or VGAM 2323 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This studio course will allow students to create a story and express it through the medium of animation. The course will cover all the necessary steps to produce a short digitally animated narrative film, from concept to final mix. Course may be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: ANIM 3328 or VGAM 2323 or permission of instructor.

Prerequisites: Senior standing or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: Senior standing or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course is intended to provide students majoring in Animation with an opportunity to focus on an animation related topic not covered in the major. Specific topics vary depending upon the faculty area of expertise. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: Senior standing or permission of instructor.
A beginning course stressing comprehension, speaking, reading and writing skills. Extensive listening outside of class is an integral part of the course. Fall.

A continuation of the development of fundamental skills stressed in Arabic I, further development of vocabulary and grammatical structures needed for basic competence. Extensive listening outside of class is an integral part of the course. Prerequisite: ARAB 1311. Spring.

The semester course begins with a short review of basic grammatical patterns taught at the introductory level. There is continued development of vocabulary, listening comprehension, reading and writing skills. Oral language activities stress conversation skills needed for ordinary interactions with Arabic speakers. Prerequisite: ARAB 1312. Fall.
Continuation of the development of intermediate-level skills in listening comprehension, speaking, reading and writing stressed in Arabic 2311. Prerequisite: ARAB 2311. Spring.

In this course the relationship between art and design is explored through common texts in conjunction with experimentation in two-, three- and four-dimensional media with an emphasis on process and problem solving. Fall. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: Exercises are designed to promote an understanding of form and expression while developing drawing skills.
Full Course Description: The emphasis of this course is on the basic concepts of drawing. Exercises are designed to promote an understanding of form and expression while developing drawing skills. Conceptual, analytical and observational techniques will be employed in this course. Subjects range from still life to architecture to conceptual and observational volumes. The focus is on line and tonal exercises in black-and-white media, with an emphasis on linear perspective. Fall. Spring.

This course is an introduction to materials, terminology, and handbuilding techniques involved in the ceramics process. Through demonstrations and discussions, students will learn fundamental handbuilding methods, various surface treatments, and the proper use of tools and equipment. By achieving technical expertise, students can begin to develop a personal artistic language employing clay as an art medium. Students will complete a variety of projects employing coil, pinch, slab, and additive/subtractive modeling techniques, with an emphasis on craft, concept, and design. Lectures will expose students to the many possibilities of contemporary ceramics. Fall. Spring.

A general survey course covering world visual art and architecture from prehistory to the late medieval period. This course is designed to acquaint the student with the relationships between history, culture and art. In addition to tests on readings and lecture materials, students will write essays meant to explore diverse historical methodologies and issues. Fall.

A general survey course covering the Renaissance through the modern period. This course is designed to acquaint the student with the relationships between history, culture and art. Although focused on the Western tradition, this course includes non-Western material and perspectives. In addition to tests on readings and lecture materials, students will write essays meant to explore diverse historical methodologies and local art collections. Recommended prerequisite: ARTS 2303. Spring.

This course provides students with a cave-to-contemporary vocabulary of artistic styles and processes in the visual arts. Critical attention is given to the use of past styles as a tool in making meaning both through historic case studies and through creative application. Consideration is further given to such meta issues as the implications of appropriation, cultural, historic, and otherwise. Fall.

This course will introduce students to both oil and water-based painting mediums and techniques. Emphasis is placed on the plastic medium of paint as it relates to pictorial representation, color, form, texture, and space. Prerequisite: ARTS 1316. Spring.

From the revolutionary creativity of the early 1900s (a cubist collage, a Kollwitz print, or a graffitied Mona Lisa poster) to the late-century embrace of conceptualism, performance, installation, and video, the 20th century proved both transformational in its artistic expression and in the modes through which that creativity found its voice. Students will become familiar with the major works, art movements, artistic processes, and practitioners of the period as well as the contexts (artistic, historic, and other) that help to explain them. In addition to tests on lecture materials and readings, students will undertake focused research and explore objects in local collections. Fall.

Other Requirements / Notes: Open to non-majors with permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: An introduction to the history and techniques of printmaking processes. These processes will include drypoint, relief, monotype, and etching. This class will explore what it means to work in a medium that creates multiples and how those multiples can be utilized in an artistic practice. Open to non-majors with permission of instructor. Prerequisites: ARTS 1311 and ARTS 1316 recommended. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: By exploring a variety of materials, learning terminology, mastering techniques, and understanding the proper use of tools and equipment, students can begin to develop a personal artistic language employing sculpture as an art medium.
Full Course Description: This course is an introduction to principal elements, concepts, and processes in sculpture. Through demonstrations, exercises, material experiments, and discussions students will learn fundamental methods of sculpture. By exploring a variety of materials, learning terminology, mastering techniques, and understanding the proper use of tools and equipment, students can begin to develop a personal artistic language employing sculpture as an art medium. This course emphasizes the development of critical thinking and problem solving in relation to three-dimensional form with an emphasis on craft, concept, material examination, and design. Clay, plaster, wood, paper, and readymade materials will be used over the course of the semester. Lectures and presentations will expose students to contemporary sculptors. Fall.

This course is a continuation of ARTS 1316, as students expand upon preexisting knowledge of line, volume, light, shading and perspective to initiate conceptual development in their work. This course is designed to expand the student’s use of materials and processes within the context of drawing and to specifically pose questions about concept. Emphasis will be placed on the exploration of color media and compositional choices. Subjects range from still life to non-objective drawing. Prerequisite: ARTS 1316. Fall.

This course is an introduction to wheel throwing techniques, terminology, and equipment. Through demonstrations, discussions, and independent studio hours, students will learn fundamental wheel throwing and related handbuilding methods, various surface treatments, and the proper use of tools and equipment. Students will systematically throw a variety of functional forms with an emphasis on craft, function, and design. Lectures and readings will expose students to the many possibilities of wheel thrown ceramics. Fall. Spring.

An introductory course in the use and application of watercolor and water-based mediums in both traditional and experimental styles. Mediums employed in this course range from transparent watercolor to gouache, acrylic, and India ink. Fall. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

This course encourages creative exploration of the human figure through 2D, 3D and 4D approaches. Students will investigate the figure through proportional representations, narrative approaches, and as a means to understand cultural, sociopolitical, racial, sexual and/or gender identities. Emphasis will be on experimentation with both material and image. Prerequisite: ARTS 1316. Fall.

This course focuses on the art, artists and art world (from museums to the market) of the later 20th and early 21st centuries. Material will be framed in the vocabulary, issues and broader theoretical context of the era, including introductions to formalist, feminist, psychological, sociological, Marxist, structuralist and deconstructivist criticism. Course content will focus on readings, lectures, gallery/museum visits and discussion. Writing assignments may include journal entries, short essays, critical reviews and a multistage individual research paper. Prerequisite: ARTS 2304. Recommended prerequisites: ARTS 2303 and ARTS 2322. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This course examines a specific topic in art history in its cultural context. Possible topics might include a specific period (Italian Renaissance, Ancient/Medieval Mediterranean, the 19th century, or Germany in the 20s), the history of a particular art form (prints, portraits, or architecture) or an otherwise narrowed field (Maya kingship, an individual artist, history of collecting, or issues in conservation). In addition to lectures and discussion, a significant aspect of this course will be written exercises designed to explore diverse art historical issues and methodologies and to allow students to develop their own interests in greater depth. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Fall. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Junior standing.
Full Course Description: In this course students will engage in independent creative research with an emphasis on studio practice. Through an arts-based visual research approach, students will devise inquiry-driven problems, engaging in subject matter, concepts, and media appropriate for their individual interests. Knowledge of research processes and methodologies will be gained through problem generation, data/content generation, analysis, interpretation and representation. Prerequisite: Junior standing. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit.
Full Course Description: Continued emphasis on composition, visual concepts in drawing and experimentation with materials. Specific problems are designed in collaboration with the student. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: ARTS 2332 or equivalent. Fall. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: At this level students are expected to work more independently, exploring personal content and vision, designing their own projects and working with the instructor to develop a critical understanding of their work. May be repeated for credit.
Full Course Description: This course is intended for students who have a serious interest in the area of painting. At this level students are expected to work more independently, exploring personal content and vision, designing their own projects and working with the instructor to develop a critical understanding of their work. Special problems will be assigned according to individual interests, including research and writing assignments. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: ARTS 2321. Spring.

Prerequisites: ARTS 1318 or ARTS 2347 or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: ARTS 1318 or ARTS 2347 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course is intended for students who have a particular interest in the area of ceramics. At this level students must be capable of working independently and be willing to assume more personal responsibility for their creative direction. “Special problems” are assigned to advance the student’s technical knowledge of clay, glazes and firing. Prerequisite: ARTS 1318 or ARTS 2347 or permission of instructor. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit.
Full Course Description: For Art majors who wish to concentrate in printmaking. The student may pursue a particular area of interest in the medium. Individuals are expected to develop disciplined work habits. May be repeated for credit. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: Students with junior or senior standing whose interests lie in studio art, museum studies or arts administration may consider an internship with a professional artist, local art organization, gallery or museum. May be repeated once for credit. Prerequisite: permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Students with junior or senior standing whose interests lie in studio art, museum studies or arts administration may consider an internship with a professional artist, local art organization, gallery or museum. Students gain professional experience in a real-world setting, preparing them for future opportunities in the arts. May be repeated once for credit. Prerequisite: permission of instructor. Fall. Spring.

This course will require students to continue to produce an independent creative research project that they began in the fall semester. The outcome of which will result in completed artwork in which parameters, subject, content and materials etc. are determined by arts-based visual research. Knowledge of research processes and methodologies will be gained through problem generation, data/content generation, analysis, interpretation and representation. Prerequisite: ARTS 3351. Spring.

This is a course that is intended to provide students with the opportunity to focus on a specialized topic in an inter- or cross- disciplinary field. This course meets the Exploring Expressive Works General Education requirement, where students analyze significant theatrical, performative, visual and literary works, learn the characteristics of these major genres relevant to the subject matter and become familiar with some of the cultural and historical factors that shape artistic works. They will apply concepts of critical analysis and draw connections between artistic works and relevant cultural issues. 1312 Topics in Arts and Humanities: Creativity

This is a course that is intended to provide students with the opportunity to focus on a specialized topic in an inter- or cross- disciplinary field. This course meets the Creativity and Making General Education requirement, where students develop a tool kit and craft vocabulary, experiment with invention of forms, processes, actions or ideas and make a body of original creative output. 1314 Topics in Arts and Humanities: Diverse

This is a course that is intended to provide students with the opportunity to focus on a specialized topic in an inter- or cross- disciplinary field. This course meets the Diverse American Perspectives General Education requirement, where students analyze significant historical changes and developments that have occurred in the United States, examine social identities such as race, ethnicity, nationality, gender, sexuality, language, citizenship, religion, social class, age and physical ability in American society and culture, and investigate the advantages and challenges inherent in a pluralistic society, with discussions about human rights, inequity, differences in power, and struggles for social justice. 2316 Topics in Arts and Humanities: Global

Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies the Culminating Experience in the Arts and Humanities.
Full Course Description: This is a course that is intended to provide students with the opportunity to focus on a specialized topic in an inter- or cross- disciplinary field. This course meets the Global Perspectives General Education requirement, where students demonstrate knowledge about an area of the world, country or region within a global context, compare, analyze and evaluate diverse perspectives and identify issues of global concern and apply critical, moral and ethical analyses drawing on multiple perspectives such as inequality, economic status, identity, gender, class, ideology, ethnicity, and power relations. 4350 Culminating Experience in the Arts and Humanities 3 hours Students will investigate an open-ended question to be answered or a problem to be solved using processes appropriate to their disciplinary or interdisciplinary fields and synthesize and apply skills and knowledge gained form the curriculum and co-curriculum to produce a product appropriate to their field of study. Satisfies the Culminating Experience in the Arts and Humanities.

Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies the General Education Natural Sciences requirement.
Full Course Description: Introduction to the study of stars, galaxies, and cosmology. Includes the nature of space and time, light and matter, telescopes, spectroscopy, stellar formation and evolution, galaxies, and unsolved mysteries about the universe. Will include lab activities integrated into the course. Some elementary algebra will be used. Satisfies the General Education Natural Sciences requirement.

An introduction to animal behavior using a broad biological approach (linking physiology, ethology, behavioral ecology, comparative psychology). Focus of the class will be on the organization and evolution of the behavior of individuals. Topics include a review of evolutionary theory, development of behavior, communication, the proximate causes of behavior, the control of behavior (neural and organizing mechanisms), mating systems, learning and memory, and social organization (including evolution of human behavior). Prerequisites: BIOL 1307 and BIOL 1308.

Prerequisites: PSYC 2301, PSYC 2321 and a grade of C or higher in PSYC 2317.
Full Course Description: This course provides an introduction to the neurosciences. Students will examine the function and anatomy of the central nervous system and how it mediates perceptions, emotions, thoughts, memories and other behaviors. Course requirements include a one-hour anatomical and behavioral laboratory experience. Prerequisites: PSYC 2301, PSYC 2321 and a grade of C or higher in PSYC 2317.

Prerequisites: PSYC 2301, PSYC 2321, and a grade of C or higher in PSYC 2317.
Full Course Description: This course explores several of the contemporary approaches that allow scientists to study the mechanisms of learning and memory. Students will study processes that span from invertebrates to humans, drugs to disease, and theories to clinical treatment. The course provides a brief background of learning principles and basic neural anatomy and physiology in order for students to have a framework for integrating relevant research. Prerequisites: PSYC 2301, PSYC 2321, and a grade of C or higher in PSYC 2317.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This course addresses advanced topics related to the field of behavioral neuroscience. The concepts covered will vary each semester to allow detailed and complex exploration of the interplay between physiology and psychology. Topics include the neurobiology of addiction, the mechanisms of pain, the contributions of the neuroimmune system and glial cells to behavior, the influence of hormones on neuronal function and consequent behavior, and related topics based on faculty expertise. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisites: NEUR 4442 or BIOL 2135 and BIOL 2335.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: An introduction to the sound and word systems of English, followed by a more thorough study of the syntactic structure of the language. The course considers questions of usage, speech versus writing, semantics and historical change. Fall.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Prerequisites: Junior standing, EDUC 1330, EDUC 2331, and admission into the Teacher Education program. Fall, odd years. 3339 Language Acquisition and Second-Language Methodology 3 hours This course examines the nature of language and linguistic competence, the processes of first-language acquisition and alternative theories of second-language acquisition. In addition, it examines major trends in second-language-teaching methodology with practical application to the classroom, including diagnosis and prescription, lesson planning and demonstrations using appropriate techniques.; EDUC 1330 and EDUC 2331.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: Junior standing, EDUC 1330, EDUC 2331, and admission into the Teacher Education program.
Full Course Description: Consideration of philosophies, research and theories of bilingual education and their application to an instructional program. Examination of instructional strategies appropriate for teaching the content areas to a bilingual child. Utilization of the native language as the medium for instruction. Prerequisites: Junior standing, EDUC 1330, EDUC 2331, and admission into the Teacher Education program. Fall, odd years. 3339 Language Acquisition and Second-Language Methodology 3 hours This course examines the nature of language and linguistic competence, the processes of first-language acquisition and alternative theories of second-language acquisition. In addition, it examines major trends in second-language-teaching methodology with practical application to the classroom, including diagnosis and prescription, lesson planning and demonstrations using appropriate techniques. Prerequisites: EDUC 1330 and EDUC 2331. Fall.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: Situated Bilingual Literacy Practices introduces students to the theory and practice of critical pedagogy. Participants spend time within formal and informal community spaces, observing and assisting in bilingual classrooms and investigating culturally and linguistically diverse community spaces (neighborhoods, churches, businesses, social services, and so on). Students will interview members of the community in which they are placed. Includes a field-based component. Prerequisites: EDUC 1330 and EDUC 2331.

Other Requirements / Notes: An introductory course whose purpose is to provide a basic understanding of the science of genomics (i.e., the study of genome data). A major emphasis will be placed on understanding the logic behind the different genomic approaches and their capabilities and limitations when used to investigate biological processes.
Full Course Description: An introductory course whose purpose is to provide a basic understanding of the science of genomics (i.e., the study of genome data). A major emphasis will be placed on understanding the logic behind the different genomic approaches and their capabilities and limitations when used to investigate biological processes. Three hours of lecture and three hours of lab per week. Prerequisite: BIOL 2334. Fall, odd years.

Other Requirements / Notes: No previous coding or computer science experience is required.
Full Course Description: An introduction to statistical methods and their application to biology. Topics include basics of probability, measures of central tendency, graphing, and parametric tests including but not limited to t-test, Chi- square, ANOVA, two-way ANOVA, correlation, and regression. This course utilizes R programming code and will spend time developing basic coding skills. No previous coding or computer science experience is required. Biological examples will be emphasized throughout. Prerequisites: MATH 2413, BIOL 1307 and BIOL 1107. Spring.

Taught in the context of biological research, this course introduces students to the principles and tools necessary to manipulate and analyze biological data. With an emphasis on data acquisition and analysis, topics may include computational techniques used in the study of genomes and proteomes, ecological data analysis and mathematical modeling of biological systems. Pre- or corequisites: BIOL 1307 and BIOL 1308. Offered on sufficient demand.

Prerequisites: MATH 2413 and either COSC 1305 or COSC 1423.
Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with COSC 3338, MATH 3338 and PHYS 3338.
Full Course Description: A survey of standard techniques used to solve numerical and other science-based problems. Topics include roots of equations, systems of linear equations, curve fitting, numerical integration and differentiation, error analysis, number representation, and data visualization. Problems will be solved using programs written in appropriate languages and operating systems. Cross-listed with COSC 3338, MATH 3338 and PHYS 3338. Prerequisites: MATH 2413 and either COSC 1305 or COSC 1423. Offered on sufficient demand.

Full Course Description: The course topics offered will vary. Topics are selected to satisfy the special interests and needs of upper-division Bioinformatics students. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisites: Vary by topic. Offered on sufficient demand.

Prerequisites: 20 hours of Bioinformatics, junior or senior standing, and permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prior approval of both faculty supervisor and cooperating lab must be obtained. Sixty work hours required per semester hour of credit. Prerequisites: 20 hours of Bioinformatics, junior or senior standing, and permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: An internship in a cooperating industrial, medical or research laboratory. Prior approval of both faculty supervisor and cooperating lab must be obtained. Sixty work hours required per semester hour of credit. Prerequisites: 20 hours of Bioinformatics, junior or senior standing, and permission of instructor. Fall. Spring. Summer.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prior approval of both faculty supervisor and cooperating lab must be obtained. Sixty work hours required per semester hour of credit. Prerequisites: 20 hours of Bioinformatics, junior or senior standing, and permission of instructor.

Sixty work hours required per semester hour of credit. Prerequisites: 20 hours of Bioinformatics, junior or senior standing, and permission of instructor. Fall. Spring. Summer.

Prerequisites: 20 hours of Bioinformatics, junior or senior standing, and permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prior approval of both faculty supervisor and cooperating lab must be obtained. Sixty work hours required per semester hour of credit. Prerequisites: 20 hours of Bioinformatics, junior or senior standing, and permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: An internship in a cooperating industrial, medical or research laboratory. Prior approval of both faculty supervisor and cooperating lab must be obtained. Sixty work hours required per semester hour of credit. Prerequisites: 20 hours of Bioinformatics, junior or senior standing, and permission of instructor. Fall. Spring. Summer.

Prerequisites: Junior or senior standing and permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: Junior or senior standing and permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Faculty-directed, independent student research leading to the preparation of a senior seminar. A multi-semester course in which students, under the guidance of a faculty member, will: learn research methodology and how to search bioinformatics literature and databases; select a research question, prepare a written proposal or project summary and conduct an independent investigation of their research question; and present a seminar in the senior year based on results obtained from their research question. All students will be encouraged to present their findings at a scientific meeting. Prerequisites: Junior or senior standing and permission of instructor. Fall. Spring. Summer.

Other Requirements / Notes: Lab and field techniques, including Excel and basic statistics, are learned and practiced before students are required to utilize these skills to design and implement an experiment, analyze the data, and finally present their findings in both written and oral forms. Pre- or corequisite: BIOL 1307 (concurrent registration is recommended).
Full Course Description: This lab is a hands-on experience with the scientific method and experimental design as they relate to the field of biology. Lab and field techniques, including Excel and basic statistics, are learned and practiced before students are required to utilize these skills to design and implement an experiment, analyze the data, and finally present their findings in both written and oral forms. Research questions will center around water quality and land use. Pre- or corequisite: BIOL 1307 (concurrent registration is recommended). Fall. Spring on sufficient demand.

This is a course in the fundamental principles of biology with an emphasis on cell theory, structure and function, genetics, and metabolism. Intended for any student requiring a Biology course. Recommended corequisite: BIOL 1107. Fall. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: Lab and field techniques, including Excel and basic statistics, are learned and practiced before students are required to utilize these skills to design and implement an experiment, analyze the data, and finally present their findings in both written and oral forms. Pre- or corequisite: BIOL 1308 (concurrent registration is recommended).
Full Course Description: This lab is a hands-on experience with the scientific method and experimental design as they relate to the field of biology. Lab and field techniques, including Excel and basic statistics, are learned and practiced before students are required to utilize these skills to design and implement an experiment, analyze the data, and finally present their findings in both written and oral forms. Research questions will center around bioindicators of water quality. Pre- or corequisite: BIOL 1308 (concurrent registration is recommended). Spring.

This course covers the fundamental principles of biology in the context of the vast diversity that exists including viruses, prokaryotes, protists, fungi, plants, and animals. Levels of organization from individuals through populations are explored, stressing ecology, phylogenetic relationships, and the mechanisms of evolution. Intended for any student requiring a Biology course. Recommended corequisite: BIOL 1108. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies the General Education Natural Sciences requirement.
Full Course Description: An introduction to the fundamental principles of biology through an in-depth study of a current topic of contemporary interest. In this lecture/discussion course, students will study the specific topic from organizational levels ranging from genetic and cellular to population. Different sections emphasize different topics. Sample topics include animal behavior, the biology of AIDS, the biological basis of human behavior, entomology, and cancer biology. In each section, emphasis will be placed on the cell theory as the primary organizing principle throughout biology. The course will include at least 10 hours of experiential learning. Satisfies the General Education Natural Sciences requirement. Fall. Spring. Summer.

This course teaches students to distinguish between science and pseudoscience in nutrition through analysis of methodology, understand the basics of dietary analysis, macronutrients (proteins, fats, and carbohydrates), micronutrients (vitamins and minerals), phytochemicals, energy metabolism, and the relationship between diet and disease. Fall. Spring. Summer.

Other Requirements / Notes: Not open to Biological Science majors pursuing a Bachelor of Science.
Full Course Description: This is the first semester of a two-semester sequence dealing with the structure and function of the human body and mechanisms for maintaining homeostasis. This includes the study of cells, tissues and the integumentary, skeletal and muscular systems. Not open to Biological Science majors pursuing a Bachelor of Science. Three hours of lecture and three hours of lab per week. May not be taken if student has earned credit for BIOL 2303, 2103, 2335 or 2135. Prerequisite: BIOL 1307 or BIOL 1310 or BIOL 1322. Corequisite: BIOL 2001. Fall.

Other Requirements / Notes: Not open to Biological Science majors pursuing a Bachelor of Science.
Full Course Description: This is the second semester of a two-semester sequence focusing on the structure and function of the human body and mechanisms for maintaining homeostasis. This includes the cardiovascular, endocrine, urinary, reproductive and nervous systems. Not open to Biological Science majors pursuing a Bachelor of Science. Three hours of lecture and three hours of lab per week. Prerequisite: BIOL 2401. Corequisite: BIOL 2002. Spring.

In-depth study of human anatomy, including all organ systems, as well as the histology of various tissue types. May not be taken if student has earned credit for BIOL 2401. Prerequisites: BIOL 1307, BIOL 1308, BIOL 1107 and BIOL 1108. Corequisite: BIOL 2303. Spring.

This course is designed to prepare the pre-health professions major with a comprehensive introduction to human anatomy and histology. Emphasis will be placed on the anatomy of the organ systems, especially the musculoskeletal and nervous systems. The structure and function of human organs and tissues will be further described by examination of their histologic appearance. A more in-depth examination of anatomic structures and histology will be performed in the accompanying laboratory. May not be taken if student has earned credit for BIOL 2401. Prerequisite: BIOL 1307, BIOL 1308, BIOL 1107 and BIOL 1108. Corequisite: BIOL 2103. Spring.

This course is designed to promote scientific literacy. Students will gain skills in scientific logic, reasoning, analysis, and communication. Prerequisites: BIOL 1307, BIOL 1308, BIOL 1107, and BIOL 1108. Fall. Spring.
An introduction to microbiology laboratory techniques including bacterial culturing, identification and growth control. Prerequisites: BIOL 1307 and BIOL 1107. Corequisite: BIOL 2318. Spring.

Clinical Microbiology is the study of microorganisms with an emphasis on infectious microorganisms and disease. It will include an overview of structure-function, physiology, and genetics of microorganisms while focusing on human health and pathogenesis. Prerequisites: BIOL 1307 and BIOL 1107. Corequisite: BIOL 2118. Spring.

Prerequisites: BIOL 1308 and BIOL 1108. Fall, even years.
Other Requirements / Notes: Survey of all the major insect orders emphasizing evolution, systematics, morphology, physiology, life history and behavior.
Full Course Description: An introduction to the structure and function of insects and their close relatives. Survey of all the major insect orders emphasizing evolution, systematics, morphology, physiology, life history and behavior. Insect collection a requirement of lab. Optional collecting trips (day, overnight) will be scheduled on weekends/school holidays. Trips greatly facilitate completion of the collection requirement. Prerequisites: BIOL 1308 and BIOL 1108. Fall, even years.

This course examines fundamental ecological concepts within the context of global change. Following a thorough analysis of biodiversity and the benefits provided by diverse systems, students will evaluate the combined effects of climate change and other disruptive factors on the performance of individuals, community interactions, and ecosystem functioning. Class discussions will evaluate novel approaches used to address global change questions over multiple spatial and temporal scales, and also conservation approaches used to maintain the functioning of ecosystems in the future. Finally, throughout this class students will apply their knowledge of ecology and global change biology to investigate ongoing changes in the structure and functioning of terrestrial ecosystems in Austin and the surrounding Hill Country. Prerequisites: BIOL 1108 and BIOL 1308. Fall.

This lab complements the material studied in BIOL 2324. In this lab, students study the diversity and physiological processes exhibited in plants in open investigations. Topics for investigation include photosynthetic regulation, genetic structure and expression. A collection through which students master plant morphology and systematics is a major component of the lab. Corequisite: BIOL 2324. Spring, odd years.

Prerequisites: BIOL 1307 and BIOL 1308. Spring, odd years.
Full Course Description: A study of the plant kingdom with emphasis on flowering plants. The course includes survey of the major orders of flowering plants, emphasizing physiology, anatomy, life history and classification. The laboratory will require a plant collection and successful use of dichotomous keys for identification. Prerequisites: BIOL 1307 and BIOL 1308. Spring, odd years.

Prerequisites: BIOL 1108 and BIOL 1308 (or corequisite with permission of instructor).
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: BIOL 1108 and BIOL 1308 (or corequisite with permission of instructor).
Full Course Description: This course will examine the ecology of terrestrial plants, including topics such as plant physiological ecology, plant-soil interactions, plant community dynamics, plant responses to disturbance, and terrestrial ecosystem processes. Students will evaluate the effects of climate change on existing ecological relationships and will also assess current research in plant ecology. Finally, students will develop expertise in common laboratory and field techniques used to study plants and apply this knowledge in written research proposals. Prerequisites: BIOL 1108 and BIOL 1308 (or corequisite with permission of instructor). Spring.

Prerequisites: BIOL 1308 and BIOL 1108. Fall, odd years.
Full Course Description: The evolution and natural history of vertebrates, the course uses a phylogenetic approach for studying the major vertebrate classes. The course focuses on organismal function that integrates information about morphology, physiology, behavior and ecology to present a view of the way vertebrates interact with their environments. Laboratory exercises serve to familiarize students with vertebrate morphology and systematics. Field trips provide direct experience with vertebrate diversity, natural history and ecology and are scheduled during some lab periods and occasionally on weekends (the latter optional). Three hours of lecture and one lab each week. Prerequisites: BIOL 1308 and BIOL 1108. Fall, odd years.

Prerequisites: BIOL 1307, BIOL 1107, BIOL 1308, and BIOL 1108 or BIOL 1307, BIOL 1107 and any statistics course.
Full Course Description: Epidemiology is the survey of the incidence, pathogenesis and investigation of human infectious diseases. It includes descriptions of medically important microbes, factors that influence infectious disease transmission in humans, and the epidemiological techniques employed to study their incidence and prevalence in human populations. Prerequisites: BIOL 1307, BIOL 1107, BIOL 1308, and BIOL 1108 or BIOL 1307, BIOL 1107 and any statistics course. Fall.

Offers a study of the structure and function of DNA as the genetic material and focuses on details surrounding the “central dogma of molecular biology,” including DNA replication, transcription, RNA processing and translation, as well as mechanisms of control and regulation of expression of genetic information. Prerequisites: BIOL 1107 and BIOL 1307. Fall. Spring.

Empirical examination of selected systems studied in Physiology lecture (respiratory, cardiac, muscle and neuronal physiology). May not be taken if student has earned credit for BIOL 2401. Prerequisites: BIOL 1107 and BIOL 1307. Corequisite: BIOL 2335. Fall. Spring.

A cellular and systems approach to the study of the living functions of the human body with emphasis on homeostasis. Various clinical disorders are discussed. May not be taken if student has earned credit for BIOL 2401. Prerequisites: BIOL 1107 and BIOL 1307. Corequisite: BIOL 2135. Fall. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: Emphasis will be placed on Mendelian and multifactorial inheritance, the importance of human genetic diversity, allele distributions in populations, the tools used to locate and analyze genes involved in human disease and therapies derived from that understanding.
Full Course Description: Human Genetics is the in-depth study of genetics as it is applied to the human species. Emphasis will be placed on Mendelian and multifactorial inheritance, the importance of human genetic diversity, allele distributions in populations, the tools used to locate and analyze genes involved in human disease and therapies derived from that understanding. Prerequisite: BIOL 2334. Fall, even years.

Recommended Prerequisites: BIOL 2135 and BIOL 2335. Fall, odd years.
Full Course Description: Neurobiology of Mental Illness is at the interface between basic and clinical neuroscience. The course offers an in-depth study of disease mechanisms ranging from clinical manifestations and diagnostic criteria to neuronal, molecular and genetic underpinnings of the illness. Emphasis is placed on establishing connections between molecular and neuronal deficits, affected circuits and functional losses. Current and optimal treatments are also discussed. Prerequisite: BIOL 2334. Recommended prerequisites: BIOL 2135 and BIOL 2335. Fall, odd years.

Prerequisites: BIOL 1307, BIOL 1107, BIOL 1308, BIOL 1108 and BIOL 2334. Fall, odd years.
Full Course Description: Parasites are eukaryotic organisms that live in or on a host organism, obtaining from it part or all of its nutrition or needs of existence to the net detriment of the host. Parasite diversity far outnumbers free-living organisms and parasites are the cause of some of the deadliest and most debilitating human illnesses worldwide. This course will cover a wide range of topics concerning the biology of parasitism, including the evolution, ecology, behavior, pathogenesis, molecular and cell biology of parasites and their hosts. The course will discuss parasites of human medical and veterinary importance as well as the parasitism of invertebrates. Prerequisites: BIOL 1307, BIOL 1107, BIOL 1308, BIOL 1108 and BIOL 2334. Fall, odd years.

Virology is the scientific discipline that seeks to understand the nature of viruses. Viruses are extremely important organisms that affect many areas of our lives. They are important pathogens of humans, animals and plants. Indeed, viruses cause some of the most important human diseases and are the focus of intense research. Throughout this course the student will discover the nature of viruses, including their structure, molecular biology, mechanisms of replication and the manner in which they cause human disease. Prerequisite: BIOL 2334. Spring, odd years.

Immunology is the scientific discipline that seeks to understand the nature of the human immune system. The immune system is of paramount importance to the success of humans, as the human body is inundated daily by a multitude of potentially pathogenic microorganisms that left unchallenged would quickly destroy all human bodies. Throughout this course the student will discover the molecular nature of the human immune system including the cellular and noncellular components involved and how these factors interact to combat disease. Prerequisite: BIOL 2334. Fall, even years.

Evolutionary development of typical vertebrate forms — cephalochordates to mammals. In laboratory, special emphasis is placed upon the anatomy of the shark and the cat. Three hours of lecture and one laboratory a week. Prerequisites: BIOL 1308 and BIOL 1108. Offered on sufficient demand.

Prerequisites: BIOL 1307, BIOL 1107, BIOL 1308, BIOL 1108, and BIOL 2334.
Full Course Description: Microbiology Lab focuses on experimental design and the culture and identification of bacteria. Prerequisites: BIOL 1307, BIOL 1107, BIOL 1308, BIOL 1108, and BIOL 2334. Corequisite: BIOL 3339. Fall. Spring.

Prerequisites: BIOL 1307, BIOL 1107, BIOL 1308, BIOL 1108, and BIOL 2334.
Full Course Description: Microbiology is the study of microorganisms with an emphasis on their structure, function, and role in sickness, health and research. Prerequisites: BIOL 1307, BIOL 1107, BIOL 1308, BIOL 1108, and BIOL 2334. Corequisite: BIOL 3139. Fall. Spring.

An introduction to animal behavior using a broad biological approach (linking physiology, ethology, behavioral ecology, comparative psychology). Focus of the class will be on the organization and evolution of the behavior of individuals. Topics include a review of evolutionary theory, development of behavior, communication, the proximate causes of behavior, the control of behavior (neural and organizing mechanisms), mating systems, learning and memory, and social organization (including evolution of human behavior). Prerequisites: BIOL 1307 and BIOL 1308. Spring.

Recommended Prerequisites: BIOL 2135 and BIOL 2335. Fall, even years.
Full Course Description: Neuroscience offers an investigation of the mechanisms mediating learning and memory in the nervous system. Particular emphasis is placed on neural circuits, plasticity and the cellular processes that support learning and recollection. Classroom activities include discussion of a variety of learning deficits and discussion of relevant current literature. Prerequisite: BIOL 2334. Recommended prerequisites: BIOL 2135 and BIOL 2335. Fall, even years.

Full Course Description: The topics offered will vary from year to year to satisfy special needs and interests of students in biology and related fields. May be repeated when topics vary. Offered on sufficient demand.

This course is intended to lead students to understand how to analyze spatial patterns in biological systems through the use of computerized tools. Among other things, students will learn to identify and evaluate appropriate spatial data, manage them and use them to analyze and model natural systems. In the course, students learn to communicate the results of spatial analyses through effective, accurate and authentic maps. They will also learn how and why to apply remotely collected data to solve problems in ecological contexts. Fall.

A study of the cellular, molecular and genetic mechanisms controlling animal development. Using a variety of model organisms as examples, we will study basic developmental processes and their regulation. Students will learn about the modern experimental approaches used in developmental biology research and will interpret and critique current research in developmental biology. The lab section will focus in more detail on selected concepts and will introduce students to several model organisms used in developmental biology research. Prerequisite: BIOL 2334. Spring, even years.

The main focus of the course is to use a holistic approach to analyze cancer. Thus, this course will invite you to explore the societal causes and impacts of the disease while learning the biological principles underlying cancer. Prerequisite: BIOL 2334. Spring, odd years.

This course provides the building blocks of basic medical terminology by focusing on the component parts of medical terms: prefixes, suffixes and word roots. Rules for combining word parts into complete medical terms will be stressed. Operative, diagnostic, therapeutic and symptomatic terminology of all body systems, as well as systemic and surgical terminology, will be discussed. Spring.

Prerequisites: BIOL 1307, BIOL 1308 and BIOL 2334. Spring, even years.
Other Requirements / Notes: This area of research is relatively new and is quickly revolutionizing our understanding of human health and disease, as well as, the role of inter- and intra-species interactions (microbe-microbe and microbe-human) in human development, growth and physiology.
Full Course Description: The Human Microbiome is composed of all the microorganisms (bacteria, protists, fungi, viruses) that live within and on the human body. This area of research is relatively new and is quickly revolutionizing our understanding of human health and disease, as well as, the role of inter- and intra-species interactions (microbe-microbe and microbe-human) in human development, growth and physiology. This course will explore the microbiota found in four environmental niches in/on the human body: (1) the gastrointestinal tract, (2) the oral cavity and airways, (3) the urogenital tract, and (4) the skin through scientific tools and techniques utilized by researchers to study the human microbiome. Prerequisites: BIOL 1307, BIOL 1308 and BIOL 2334. Spring, even years.

Recommended Prerequisites: BIOL 2335 and BIOL 2135. Spring, even years.
Full Course Description: This course emphasizes the physiological aspects of ecological relationships of animals to their environment. In particular, the focus will be on interesting physiological solutions (i.e., responses and adaptations) associated with common, changing, and/or extreme environmental stresses. Because physiology is a feature of living organisms, it must be investigated in both the ecological and evolutionary contexts relevant to the living organism. Structural and functional aspects of physiological mechanisms will be emphasized at both the proximate and ultimate levels in order to stress the relationship between physiological function and environmental conditions. Prerequisites: BIOL 1307 and BIOL 1308. Recommended prerequisites: BIOL 2335 and BIOL 2135. Spring, even years.

Prerequisites: Junior standing, BIOL 2334, CHEM 2323, and CHEM 2123.
Other Requirements / Notes: Any understanding of biologic principles is predicated upon an understanding of the biology of the cell. Prerequisites: Junior standing, BIOL 2334, CHEM 2323, and CHEM 2123.
Full Course Description: All living things are composed of cells. Any understanding of biologic principles is predicated upon an understanding of the biology of the cell. Throughout this course the student will investigate the structure of the eukaryotic cell, the trafficking of macromolecules within cells, the mechanisms by which cell division is regulated, the ways in which cells communicate and the biogenesis of cancer. Laboratory emphasizes current techniques utilized to study cell biology. Prerequisites: Junior standing, BIOL 2334, CHEM 2323, and CHEM 2123. Recommended prerequisites: BIOL 3139 and BIOL 3339. Corequisite: BIOL 4037. Fall. Spring.

Students collect and analyze data in experiments designed to test the theories presented in lecture. These include studies of distribution patterns, foraging behavior, and population and community structure. Corequisite: BIOL 4342. Spring.

Prerequisites: BIOL 1308, BIOL 1108, and seven hours of BIOL at or above the 2000 level.
Full Course Description: Study of the abundance and distribution of populations and the factors of the environment that affect them. Emphasis will be on mathematical approaches with confirmation by field data. Prerequisites: BIOL 1308, BIOL 1108, and seven hours of BIOL at or above the 2000 level. Corequisite: BIOL 4142. Spring.

Prerequisites: BIOL 2334 or BIOL 2423 and 18 hours of BIOL credit. Recommended
Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies part of the Biological Sciences Culminating Experience course sequence in conjunction with BIOL 4167.
Full Course Description: In-depth analysis of evolution via natural selection using examples from all major classes of organisms. Emphasis is placed on the mechanism and resulting products of evolutionary change. Evolutionary change is examined at the molecular, organismal and population levels of organization. Topics such as speciation, extinction, sociality, sexual selection, extinction, macroevolution, adaptation, and biodiversity are investigated in detail. Must be taken in residence. Satisfies part of the Biological Sciences Culminating Experience course sequence in conjunction with BIOL 4167. Prerequisites: BIOL 2334 or BIOL 2423 and 18 hours of BIOL credit. Recommended corequisite: BIOL 4167 Fall. Spring.

Prerequisites: Twenty hours of Biology with a GPA of 3.00 or higher, junior or senior standing, and permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: A minimum of 60 hours of work are required for a semester hour of credit. May be repeated for credit, although a maximum of three hours of Biology elective credits may be earned from enrollments in BIOL 4146, BIOL 4246, BIOL 4346, BIOL 4150, BIOL 4250, and BIOL 4350 combined. Prerequisites: Twenty hours of Biology with a GPA of 3.00 or higher, junior or senior standing, and permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Supervised independent investigation of advanced biology topics utilizing the pertinent literature. Project culminates in a written (and perhaps oral) presentation of results. A minimum of 60 hours of work are required for a semester hour of credit. May be repeated for credit, although a maximum of three hours of Biology elective credits may be earned from enrollments in BIOL 4146, BIOL 4246, BIOL 4346, BIOL 4150, BIOL 4250, and BIOL 4350 combined. Prerequisites: Twenty hours of Biology with a GPA of 3.00 or higher, junior or senior standing, and permission of instructor. Offered on sufficient demand.

Prerequisites: Twenty hours of Biology with a GPA of 3.00 or higher, junior or senior standing, and permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: A minimum of 60 hours of work are required for a semester hour of credit.
