Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit, although a maximum of three hours of Biology credits may be earned for this course. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: With faculty guidance, students select a research question and conduct an independent investigation. Students will learn about research methodology and how to search relevant literature and databases within their field of study. Research projects may be completed in one semester or students may take the course repeatedly in order to complete a longer investigation. Students are encouraged and facilitated in presentation of their findings at a campus, regional or national meeting, and may present their results at the annual Senior Seminar Symposium, usually in their senior year. May be repeated for credit, although a maximum of three hours of Biology credits may be earned for this course. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor. Fall. Spring.

Prerequisites: BIOL 2427 and permission of instructor.; 18 hours of BIOL credit.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: BIOL 2427 and permission of instructor. Satisfies part of the Biological Sciences Culminating Experience course sequence in conjunction with BIOL 4344.
Full Course Description: The content of this course will change, depending on the destination site. Obvious options would include the deserts of the Southwestern United States, the Rocky Mountains of Colorado and New Mexico, the Appalachian Mountains of the Southeastern US, and the Neotropics of Panama. Regardless of the site, the structure of the course would be consistent: a review of background theory on the ecological relationships manifested in that site (e.g., desert ecology, montane ecology, tropical ecology) and an active assessment of the structure of the major communities present in the ecosystems visited. Prerequisites: BIOL 2427 and permission of instructor. Spring. 4167 Culminating Experience in Biological Sciences 1 hour This course evaluates the results of the inquiry process in relation to topics covered in BIOL 4344. This evaluation includes communication of project outcomes and reflection of the culmination of the undergraduate experience at St. Edward’s University in the context of the university’s mission. Must be taken in residence. Satisfies part of the Biological Sciences Culminating Experience course sequence in conjunction with BIOL 4344. Prerequisites: 18 hours of BIOL credit. Pre- or corequisite: BIOL 4344. Fall. Spring.

Prerequisites: Permission of Medical Laboratory Science program director and acceptance into an affiliated program at Austin State Hospital or Baylor Scott & White.
Other Requirements / Notes: 41290 12 hours Students enroll in this course during the semesters in which they are completing affiliated program required courses for the Medical Laboratory Science program. Prerequisites: Permission of Medical Laboratory Science program director and acceptance into an affiliated program at Austin State Hospital or Baylor Scott & White.
Full Course Description: 41290 12 hours Students enroll in this course during the semesters in which they are completing affiliated program required courses for the Medical Laboratory Science program. Prerequisites: Permission of Medical Laboratory Science program director and acceptance into an affiliated program at Austin State Hospital or Baylor Scott & White.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: This is an introductory course designed as an overview of the structure and functions of business. The course will cover the economic framework for business in a global environment and the importance of business ethics and social responsibility. It will introduce the basic structures and functions of business: • Forms of business ownership; • Accounting and finance; • Marketing and pricing; • Management and leadership; • Ethics and global business; • Teamwork and communications; • Starting your business (entrepreneurship); • Production and operations; • The financial system The course will also highlight for students resources related to their professional and career development, such as the Munday Library and the Career and Professional Development office.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: Students sharpen their understanding of concepts through hands-on in-class and assigned exercises as well as practice certification questions. Prerequisite for non-business majors and minors: Permission of department chair.
Full Course Description: Spreadsheets, Excel in particular, have become an indispensable tool for analyzing data, resulting in better decision-making and greater productivity across a wide range of business functions. This course covers the skills needed to get the most out of Excel and prepare an individual for Excel certification. Students gain the proficiency needed to apply Excel in a number of core and advanced business courses and to meet the expectations of employers upon hire. The course progresses from basic concepts like creating worksheets/workbooks and navigating the user interface to advanced skills including creating data dashboards, crafting custom functions, and automating with macros. Students sharpen their understanding of concepts through hands-on in-class and assigned exercises as well as practice certification questions. Prerequisite for non-business majors and minors: Permission of department chair. Fall. Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: The student will learn that understanding the legal rules affecting business is incomplete unless the student understands law’s general nature, its functions and how judges interpret it.
Full Course Description: Provides an introduction to the legal environment. The course material will include ethical and global issues; address the influence of political, social, legal and regulatory, environmental, and technological issues on business; and address the impact of ethical and public policy concerns. The course content is designed for the student entering the workforce to understand that the government regulates most aspects of a firm’s operations — for example, agency, torts, contracts, advertising, product safety, company agreements, employee relations and behavior toward competitors. The student will learn that understanding the legal rules affecting business is incomplete unless the student understands law’s general nature, its functions and how judges interpret it. A strong emphasis is placed on ethical issues through relevant case studies. Fall. Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: Applies descriptive and inferential techniques to business problems. Summarizing and describing data, discrete and continuous probability distributions, sampling methods and sampling distributions, hypothesis testing, interval estimation, correlation and regression analysis. Prerequisite: MATH 1324 or MATH 2312. Fall. Spring. 2318 Legal Environment of Business for Accountants 3 hours This course focuses on the substantive and procedural laws governing business transactions, including provisions in the Uniform Commercial Code. This course exposes students to business law principles and concepts to assist in preparation for the CPA examination. Corequisite: ACCT 4650. Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies the General Education requirement for Oral Communication.
Full Course Description: Principles of oral business communication. Practice in drafting and delivery of various types of oral professional communication exchanges for business (e.g. sales presentations, interviews, meetings, negotiations, group discussions). Emphasis is placed on persuasion, building credibility, and increasing confidence in oral communication. Satisfies the General Education requirement for Oral Communication. Fall. Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: This course will study the numerous forms of social enterprise that are appearing on the global business landscape today. A social enterprise is an organization that applies commercial strategies to maximize improvements in human and environmental well-being, rather than maximizing profits for external shareholders. This course uses journal articles and organization reports to study the forms social enterprise is taking in the U.S., such as the Benefit Corporation and social purpose corporation, and in other countries such as China, and the U.K. It will analyze how these organizations apply business principles to achieve their goals, access socially conscious investments, employ social media to communicate their messages, and use global certifications and third- party assessors, such as GRI and KPMG to evaluate their performance. It will include analysis of local organizations that are social enterprises.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Course Title: The Art and Science of Negotiation
Full Course Description: The purpose of this course is to teach students about the theory and practice of negotiation and build their skills as effective negotiators. Negotiation is a crucial skill in leadership and life. Business leaders need both analytic skills and interpersonal skills to be effective negotiators. This course is intended to complement the technical and diagnostic skills learned in other courses in The Bill Munday School of Business Bachelor of Business Administration program. Students build competence and confidence as negotiators through a combination of pedagogical activities including reading, discussion, projects, and experiential exercises. Each class will focus on a different facet of the negotiation process.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: The course provides an understanding of the key success factors for managing a project in any industry.
Full Course Description: The course objective is to help prospective project managers to understand how projects contribute to the strategic goals of an organization and to master project management tools/techniques and interpersonal skills necessary to orchestrate projects to completion. Students learn the fundamentals of managing all aspects of a project, including scope definition, work planning, project control, resource management, change management, and risk management. The course provides an understanding of the key success factors for managing a project in any industry. The course is interactive and includes hands-on exercises in which the students apply – with the aid of PM software – the methods and concepts studied.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: It develops an understanding of the principles of Corporate Social Responsibility through analyzing real-world cases to provide a foundation for developing responsible business decision-making processes aimed at solving social problems while still making profit. Prerequisite: Junior standing.
Full Course Description: This course explores the new social and ethical responsibilities of business to create a just world for all stakeholders. It develops an understanding of the principles of Corporate Social Responsibility through analyzing real-world cases to provide a foundation for developing responsible business decision-making processes aimed at solving social problems while still making profit. Organizational CSR initiatives are researched and evaluated. Through reflecting on global social justice problems, examining what companies are doing to address these, and participating in a S.E.R.V.E one day project, students will create a product or service that has a social purpose for a developing country. This course does not fulfill the General Education Ethics requirement. Prerequisite: Junior standing. Fall. Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Prerequisites: WRIT 1301 and WRIT 2302 and either BUSI 2321 or COMM 1317.
Full Course Description: This course provides students a conceptual framework and practical tools for communicating in professional environments to achieve strategic goals for their careers and businesses. The emphasis of this core course is on written and oral communication, persuasive strategy, and collaborative skills necessary for workplace success. Prerequisites: WRIT 1301 and WRIT 2302 and either BUSI 2321 or COMM 1317. Fall. Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: This course introduces students to various concepts, software tools and methods used to gather and analyze data for the purpose of making better decisions within an organization. Students will have hands-on exercises to extend their ability to effectively use integrated software specifications to identify and provide access to various information sources. The course focuses on applying information and internet technologies that span normal business functions for the development and implementation of solutions to managerial problems. Students will also examine the system development life cycle and system development methodology to better understand which systems can work in various organizational environments to help organizations successfully compete in the marketplace. Prerequisite: BUSI 2305.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business

May be repeated for credit with permission of instructor. Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Supervised experiential learning in a public or private, paid or unpaid, business or organization. This is an asynchronous online class requiring students to complete a minimum of 120 hours at their internship site along with several reflective assignments that emphasize the development of communication skills, problem solving and ethical decision making. The final reflective assignment demonstrates the accomplishment of the internship goals and objectives. Students are responsible for locating their own internship and staying in contact with the instructor regarding assignments and paperwork. If a student has not provided the instructor with proof of an internship by the deadline for an unrecorded drop, a grade of WA will be assigned. May be repeated for credit with permission of instructor. Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing or permission of instructor. Fall. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: This course is considered the culminating course integrating strategic management concepts with projects that demonstrate an understanding and ability to utilize skills, approaches, and methods from across the spectrum of core business functions. Prerequisites: Senior standing and completion of all core course requirements in The Bill Munday School of Business.
Full Course Description: Students take the perspective of top management of the total organization and do business research and planning in situations representative of the real business world. This course allows students to hone and showcase key skills they have been developing throughout their St. Edward’s University career by a series of simulation and case study projects. This course is considered the culminating course integrating strategic management concepts with projects that demonstrate an understanding and ability to utilize skills, approaches, and methods from across the spectrum of core business functions. Prerequisites: Senior standing and completion of all core course requirements in The Bill Munday School of Business. Fall. Spring. Summer.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Prerequisites: Senior standing and completion of all core course requirements in The Bill Munday School of Business.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: Senior standing and completion of all core course requirements in The Bill Munday School of Business. Corequisite: BUSI 4349 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course examines business practice from the executive team’s perspective. The student will learn to gather, analyze and utilize data in making executive decisions. Prerequisites: Senior standing and completion of all core course requirements in The Bill Munday School of Business. Corequisite: BUSI 4349 or permission of instructor. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Course Title: Topics in Career Planning and Management
Other Requirements / Notes: Edward’s University seniors who are currently in the process of applying to law school as well as any student interested in developing a deeper understanding of the law school application process. Ideally, this course is for students preparing to take the LSAT exam within the next year but is open to all students.
Full Course Description: Each section of CPAM 1110 covers a different career topic. Career Management Strategies This course is for students who want to assess and confirm their career goals, develop or strengthen their career action plan, and learn effective job search strategies for a highly competitive job market. Students will be exposed to career resources at St. Edward’s, take a career assessment, learn about the current job market and recruiting/hiring trends, learn how to communicate experience gained from internships and extracurricular experiences, build a LinkedIn profile and learn networking strategies. Fall. Spring. Summer. Navigating the Law School Application Process This course is designed for St. Edward’s University seniors who are currently in the process of applying to law school as well as any student interested in developing a deeper understanding of the law school application process. Students who are currently in the process of applying to law school will receive assistance with producing a strong applicant profile and creating personalized action plans regarding their law school applications. LSAT Preparation This course covers the content and structure of the LSAT admission test, as well as how to study and prepare for the LSAT. Students also learn how the LSAT fits into the overall graduate school application process. Ideally, this course is for students preparing to take the LSAT exam within the next year but is open to all students. Course fee: $799. Fall. Spring. Navigating the Pre-Health Application Process This course focuses on educating students to present competitive applications for their respective health professions program. Students learn about the application process, including preparation of a resume/ CV, writing a personal statement, building networking skills, and interview strategies. This course is also designed to help students think about the meaning of vocation and mission, as it ties into higher education and the traditions of the St. Edward’s University community, as they transition into their desired education/career in the health professions. MCAT Preparation This course is designed to help students prepare for the Medical College Admission Test. Students will learn and develop strategies to optimize their performance on the MCAT. Students will be expected to manage their time wisely and create a customized study plan with input from the instructor. Students should plan to dedicate approximately 350 hours of prep time (on average). Course fee: $1,399.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Successful completion of the course is contingent upon satisfying the minimum number of required work hours at the internship site as well as submission of additional written assignments.
Full Course Description: This course is designed to provide students flexibility while participating in a credit-bearing internship experience. In-person and online oversight of the experience will be provided by Career and Professional Development staff throughout the duration of the internship experience, providing students flexibility while exploring career opportunities, obtaining meaningful exposure to a college-level career field, and developing new skills under the direction of industry professionals. To participate in the course, an approved internship must be provided by an off-campus company or organization, spanning at least six weeks during an academic term. Rather than providing a letter grade at the conclusion of the CPAM Internship class, CR will be entered on the student’s transcript indicating successful completion of the internship course. Failure to successfully complete the course will result in a grade of N (no pass). Successful completion of the course is contingent upon satisfying the minimum number of required work hours at the internship site as well as submission of additional written assignments. The CPAM Internship class may be taken for one, two, or three credit hours, corresponding to the number of hours worked at the internship site. • CPAM 4150 Internship – 1 credit: 50-99 hours • CPAM 4250 Internship – 2 credits: 100-149 hours • CPAM 4350 Internship – 3 credits: 150+ hours Students concurrently enrolled in this course and any other internship course at St. Edward’s University must ensure that they are using a distinct internship site/experience and a distinct site supervisor for each internship course. While enrolled at St. Edward’s University, students may accrue up to six credit hours through CPAM Internship courses. Fall. Spring. Summer.

This course will provide a survey and study of the major beliefs, traditions, and practices of the Catholic Church. Making use of historical documents and contemporary theology, this course will explore Catholic theological reflections on topics such as community, revelation, Christ, morality, and spirituality. This course considers Catholicism in dialogue with other traditions. Crosslisted with RELS 1316.

This course takes a multifaceted approach to the study of the gospels, the epistles and the Book of Revelation. The course will emphasize the methodology of historical criticism for the purpose of identifying the authors of each New Testament work, as well as their respective theologies and witness to Jesus Christ. Crosslisted with RELS 2322.

This course examines the issues of justice and peace within the context of theology and ethics. Grounded in the teachings of the Israelite prophets, the New Testament teachings of Jesus, and recent works in contextual theology, this course will examine current social issues in light of Catholic Social Teaching. Crosslisted with RELS 2323.

Course Title: Crisis and Development in the Church
Other Requirements / Notes: Most recently, the Second Vatican Council has sought to renew understandings of liturgy, ecclesiology and religious liberty.
Full Course Description: The Catholic Church has a long history responding to crises and seeking opportunities for renewal. Most recently, the Second Vatican Council has sought to renew understandings of liturgy, ecclesiology and religious liberty. With particular attention to the history and theology of the 19th and 20th century, this class will focus on the ways the Church has responded to crises through development and renewal.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This course will examine particular topics within the area of Catholic studies. These topics will be general in nature and will not assume prior background. Possible topics may include Catholic Writers, Latino/a Catholicism and Catholic Art Through the Ages. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

This class examines the ways Catholics and Christians, more broadly, have thought through faith from both philosophical and theological perspectives. Investigating fundamental questions concerning the interplay between faith, reason, and the Church’s intellectual heritage, students will engage a variety of historical, philosophical, and theological sources in the Western tradition. 3338 Ritual and Imagination: Sacramental Theology 3 hours This course examines the relationship between ritual and imagination with particular attention to Catholic sacramental life. Using a variety of methods and tools from the study of religion including history, ritual studies, sacramental and liturgical theology, and anthropology of religion, students will consider the role of the sacramental life in the Church in historical and contemporary contexts. Crosslisted with RELS 3338.

Prerequisites: CATH 1316. Specific topics may have additional specific prerequisites.
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: These topics will be focused in nature and will assume prior background. Possible topics include Catholicism and Film, The Church in the Modern World, and From Catacombs to Cathedrals: The History of Catholic Art and Architecture. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisites: CATH 1316. Specific topics may have additional specific prerequisites.

This course is designed to teach an upper-division student how to research and write an in-depth paper. Focus can be on any aspect of the Catholic Tradition. In consultation with the instructor, students will select a research topic and form a thesis; students will develop their thesis and be responsible for reading the assigned material and developing a paper. Prerequisites: CATH 1316.

Prerequisites: CATH 1316. Specific topics may have additional specific prerequisites.
Full Course Description: This course will examine advanced topics within Catholic studies. Prerequisites: CATH 1316. Specific topics may have additional specific prerequisites.

Other Requirements / Notes: Course topics emphasize a conceptual understanding of chemical phenomena, chemical terminology and nomenclature, the classification of chemical reactions, and the mathematical solution of basic chemical problems.
Full Course Description: A survey of the concepts of chemistry. Course topics emphasize a conceptual understanding of chemical phenomena, chemical terminology and nomenclature, the classification of chemical reactions, and the mathematical solution of basic chemical problems. The course is designed for students needing a one-semester introductory chemistry course. Credit for CHEM 1305 may not be counted as part of the minimum hours for graduation by persons who have previously received credit for General Chemistry or higher-division chemistry courses. Fall. Spring.
Topics include laboratory applications of the principles presented in General Chemistry lecture. Corequisite: CHEM 1340. Fall. Spring.

An overview of selected chemical principles for natural science majors. Course topics include the subatomic composition and electronic structure, aqueous solutions and chemical reactivity, molecular bonding and structure, reaction driving forces. Corequisite: CHEM 1140. Fall. Spring.

Students will learn about research methodology, how to search relevant literature and databases within their field of study, how to critically examine papers in the chemical research literature, and how to develop a research proposal. Pre- or Corequisite: CHEM 2323. Spring.
Topics include laboratory applications of the principles presented in CHEM 2320. Corequisite: CHEM 2320. Fall. Spring.

An introduction to chemical equilibrium, chemical kinetics, acid-base theories and reactions, and electrochemistry. This is a foundational course for upper division chemistry courses. Prerequisite: CHEM 1340. Corequisite: CHEM 2120. Fall. Spring.
Synthesis, identification and purification of compounds studied in CHEM 2323. Corequisite: CHEM 2323. Fall. Spring.

Organic nomenclature, relationship between structure and reactivity, stereochemistry and isomerism, organic reaction mechanisms, aromaticity, and introduction to multistep synthesis, and the preparation and reactions of alkanes, alkenes, alkyl halides, alkynes, alcohols and aromatic compounds. Prerequisite: CHEM 2320 or CHEM 2334. Corequisite: CHEM 2123. Fall. Spring.
Analysis, method development, spectroscopy, data analysis, and results reporting. Corequisite: CHEM 2327. Spring.

Prerequisites: CHEM 2334 and CHEM 2134 or CHEM 2320 and CHEM 2120.
Full Course Description: An introduction to general experiment construction and operation, quantitative analysis, method development, spectroscopy, data analysis, and results reporting. Prerequisites: CHEM 2334 and CHEM 2134 or CHEM 2320 and CHEM 2120. Corequisite: CHEM 2127. Spring.
Experimental and computational experience related to bonding, symmetry, chemical equilibrium, acid-base, and redox reactions. Corequisite: CHEM 2334. Spring.

An introduction to foundational principles of inorganic chemistry with a focused discussion of bonding theories, symmetry principles, chemical equilibrium, acid-base, and redox reactions. Prerequisites: CHEM 1340 and CHEM 1140. Corequisite: CHEM 2134. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit by investigation of different topics.
Full Course Description: A course that examines current topics from all areas of chemistry. The course may include laboratory work if appropriate to the topic. May be repeated for credit by investigation of different topics. Specific topic entered on transcript. Prerequisites: Vary by topic. Offered on sufficient demand.

Principles of designing multistep synthesis schemes. Chemistry of aldehydes, ketones, carboxylic acids and their derivatives, aromatic compounds, amines, amides, and proteins. Prerequisite: CHEM 2323. Spring.
An introduction to methods and techniques used for qualitative and quantitative analysis of chemicals in environmental systems. Prerequisites: CHEM 2123 and CHEM 2127.

An introduction to the behavior and interactions of chemicals in natural and anthropogenic systems; their distribution, transport, and fate. Prerequisites: CHEM 2323, CHEM 2123, CHEM 2327, and CHEM 2127.

Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with PHYS 3136.
Full Course Description: An introduction to experimental and computational techniques used in the study of thermodynamics and quantum mechanics. Students will explore the behavior of real and ideal gases, heat release during combustion, rotational and vibrational signatures of small polyatomic molecules and more. Corequisite: CHEM 3336. Cross-listed with PHYS 3136. Spring.

Prerequisites: CHEM 2320 or CHEM 2334, MATH 2414 and PHYS 2325.
Corequisites: CHEM 3136 and PHYS 2326. Cross-listed with PHYS 3336.
Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with PHYS 3336.
Full Course Description: An introduction to the fundamental concepts in thermodynamics and quantum mechanics. The first half of the course covers gaseous behavior and the laws of thermodynamics. The second half of the course covers the origin of quantum mechanics and steps through foundational systems including a particle in a box, the harmonic oscillator, and the Hydrogen atom. Prerequisites: CHEM 2320 or CHEM 2334, MATH 2414 and PHYS 2325. Corequisites: CHEM 3136 and PHYS 2326. Cross-listed with PHYS 3336. Spring.

Prerequisites: CHEM 3336, MATH 2321 and PHYS 2326. Cross-listed with PHYS 3337.
Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with PHYS 3337.
Full Course Description: A survey of physical chemistry topics with an emphasis on real-world challenges and interdisciplinary problem solving. Core topics rotate but may include kinetics and dynamics, chemical equilibrium, solution and solid-phase chemistry, and advanced spectroscopies. Prerequisites: CHEM 3336, MATH 2321 and PHYS 2326. Cross-listed with PHYS 3337. Fall.

Survey of the fundamentals of biochemistry. Topics include structure of proteins, nucleic acids, lipids and carbohydrates, catalysis, kinetics, bioenergetics and metabolism, and information pathways. Emphasis will be on providing the student with an overview of the field, through in- depth coverage of protein structure and enzyme catalysis. Prerequisite: CHEM 2323. Fall. Spring.

Introduction to the fundamentals of theoretical and computational Molecular Orbital (MO) Theory. Exemplify this new rigor through application of MO theory to pericyclic reactions, correlation diagrams, and ground state and transition state aromaticity. Introduction of isotope effects and mixed kinetics to elucidate the principles governing modern organic mechanistic determination. Selection of a current open question in organic structure and/or mechanism for a collaborative, class wide computational research project. Prerequisite: CHEM 3325. Offered on sufficient demand.

A research-based course focused on the synthesis of novel inorganic, and organometallic compounds. This work will be driven by the writing of research proposals. Air-sensitive techniques will be developed and instrumental spectroscopic techniques will be applied to the characterization and determination of structure for these new compounds. Prerequisites: CHEM 3325 and CHEM 3125. Corequisite: CHEM 4325. Fall.

An in-depth examination of coordination chemistry including underlying theories as well as principles of organometallic and bioinorganic chemistry. These topics will be explored through the lens of primary literature analysis. Prerequisites: CHEM 3325 and CHEM 3125. Fall.

A case study involving a fictional crime scene analysis is introduced. Successful completion will involve four fundamental forensic analyses: (i) serological and DNA analysis, (ii) trace analysis of fragments, (iii) solid-phase extraction followed by a full toxicological screen, and (iv) forensic drug analysis. Corequisite: CHEM 4331. Spring, even years.

Prerequisites: CHEM 2323 or CHEM 2327. Spring, even years.
Full Course Description: Major facets of forensic analytical chemistry will be covered, including drug analysis, forensic toxicology, trace analysis, and arson and DNA typing. Analytical methods will be reviewed, including HPLC, TLC and GC-MS. The use of Bayesian probability in the evaluation of guilt and innocence will also be covered. Statistical techniques will be introduced as a means of establishing the link between data quality and evidence admissibility. Real-world case studies will be used to illustrate the application of these principles. Prerequisite: CHEM 2323 or CHEM 2327. Spring, even years.

Other Requirements / Notes: Experimental investigations involving instrumental methods to enhance the understanding of sampling, sample preparation, use of analytical instrumentation, data analysis, and results reporting.
Full Course Description: Experimental investigations involving instrumental methods to enhance the understanding of sampling, sample preparation, use of analytical instrumentation, data analysis, and results reporting. Corequisite: CHEM 4342. Fall.

A study of some of the modern instrumental methods including atomic and molecular spectroscopy, analytical separations, mass spectrometry, magnetic resonance spectrometry, and other salient techniques. Prerequisite: CHEM 2327. Corequisite: CHEM 4142. Fall.

The purpose of this laboratory course is to provide the student with a modern experience in basic and advanced techniques of experimental biochemistry. Areas include spectroscopic methods, electrophoretic techniques, chromatographic separations, isolation and characterization of nucleic acids and proteins, enzymatic catalysis, peptide isolation and characterization. Instruction will also be given in the use of computer modeling for visualization of macromolecular structures and for the study of biochemical processes. Corequisite: CHEM 4344. Spring.

Bioenergetics and the metabolism of carbohydrates, lipids, amino acids, and nucleotides. Emphasis on integration and regulation by metabolic, hormonal and genetic factors. Prerequisite: CHEM 3343. Spring.

Supervised investigation of advanced chemistry topics utilizing the pertinent literature. Project is culminated with a written and oral presentation of results. The course may include laboratory work if appropriate to topic. Specific topic entered on transcript. Prerequisites: Vary by topic. Offered on sufficient demand.

Supervised investigation of advanced chemistry topics utilizing the pertinent literature. Project is culminated with a written and oral presentation of results. The course may include laboratory work if appropriate to topic. Specific topic entered on transcript. Prerequisites: Vary by topic. Offered on sufficient demand.

Supervised investigation of advanced chemistry topics utilizing the pertinent literature. Project is culminated with a written and oral presentation of results. The course may include laboratory work if appropriate to topic. Specific topic entered on transcript. Prerequisites: Vary by topic. Offered on sufficient demand.

Supervised investigation of advanced chemistry topics utilizing the pertinent literature. Project is culminated with a written and oral presentation of results. The course may include laboratory work if appropriate to topic. Specific topic entered on transcript. Prerequisites: Vary by topic. Offered on sufficient demand.

Prerequisites: Junior or senior standing, 24 hours of Chemistry, and permission of faculty supervisor and cooperating laboratory.
Other Requirements / Notes: Students will be required to work regular shifts, observing and participating in the actual daily routine. Requires a minimum of 60 hours of working time per semester hour of credit. Prerequisites: Junior or senior standing, 24 hours of Chemistry, and permission of faculty supervisor and cooperating laboratory.
Full Course Description: An independently planned internship in a cooperating industrial, medical or research laboratory. Students will be required to work regular shifts, observing and participating in the actual daily routine. A log is to be kept of each day’s activity. Details are to be worked out with the faculty supervisor and the cooperating laboratory. Requires a minimum of 60 hours of working time per semester hour of credit. Prerequisites: Junior or senior standing, 24 hours of Chemistry, and permission of faculty supervisor and cooperating laboratory. Fall. Spring. Summer.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit.
Full Course Description: Students will, with the guidance of a faculty member, select a research question crafting a proposal or summary of the question and will present this proposal in written or oral form. Students will conduct an independent investigation of their research question under the tutelage of a faculty member and will be encouraged to present their findings at a scientific meeting. All students will prepare a written research report in accordance with ACS guidelines. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: CHEM 2100. Fall. Spring. Summer.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Course Title: Introduction to Chicanx and Latinx Studies
Full Course Description: This course provides an introduction to the fields of both Chicanx and Latinx Studies. This interdisciplinary course covers the foundational concepts of Chicanx studies, and will also integrate material from the broader field of Latinx studies.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This is an interdisciplinary course that explores the history, landscape and contemporary cultures of the U.S. Mexico borderlands, examining social and political issues that have emerged in this region, and expressions of popular culture.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This is an interdisciplinary course that examines the historical and contemporary movements for civil and human rights within Latinx communities. It would focus on crucial issues confronting Latinx communities and explore strategies for meaningful social change. Prerequisite: CHLX 1302.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: Different topics will be presented under this heading, including topics related to colonialism, imperialism, race, ethnicity, class, gender, sexuality, feminisms, migration, transnationalism, and how these issues affect the Chicanx/Latinx community. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
A beginning course stressing comprehension, speaking, reading and writing skills. Extensive listening outside of class is an integral part of the course. Fall.

A continuation of the development of fundamental skills stressed in Chinese I, further development of vocabulary and grammatical structures needed for basic competence. Extensive listening outside of class is an integral part of the course. Prerequisite: CHIN 1311. Spring.

The semester course begins with a short review of basic grammatical patterns taught at the introductory level. There is continued development of vocabulary, listening comprehension, reading and writing skills. Oral language activities stress conversation skills needed for ordinary interactions with Chinese speakers. Prerequisite: CHIN 1312. Fall.
Continuation of the development of intermediate-level skills in listening comprehension, speaking, reading and writing stressed in CHIN 2311. Prerequisite: CHIN 2311. Spring.

Prerequisites: Freshman or sophomore standing only, or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Freshman or sophomore standing only, or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course serves as the prelude to the Communication major. Students are introduced to definitions, concepts and contexts crucial to the discipline of communication as well as to the origins and development of the pedagogical study of communication. Course includes visits from faculty and/or guests to discuss their specific areas of study (e.g., organizational, cultural, media, persuasive) and serves to guide new majors into the core curriculum and various tracks. Prerequisite: Freshman or sophomore standing only, or permission of instructor. Fall. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies the General Education requirement for Oral Communication.
Full Course Description: The course is an introduction to public speaking in both large and small group situations. It provides practice in a variety of speech styles and purposes, with emphasis on the more commonly used speech forms. Satisfies the General Education requirement for Oral Communication. Fall. Spring. Summer.

This course introduces students to the various methodological approaches that currently govern research in the field of communication. Through an examination of quantitative, qualitative, and critical paradigms, this course will explore how communication researchers from these different traditions understand knowledge (epistemology) and theory building, utilize specific research designs (e.g., sampling) and engage in primary research: ethnography, survey research, focus groups, content analysis and other research methods. Additional topics discussed in class include research ethics, how to evaluate research, rhetorical criticism and the mixed methods approach to research. Prerequisite: COMM 2302. Fall. Spring.

This course is a survey of the major theoretical approaches, concepts and terms used in communication studies. Theories covered in the course provide a framework for more advanced classes in Communication. Course objectives are accomplished through text readings, lectures, discussions, group presentations, research and written analyses. Prerequisite: COMM 1306. Fall. Spring.

As our society continues to become an increasingly visual one, it is imperative that we begin to investigate how visual imagery affects us. This course explores major theoretical and practical approaches to the creation and analysis of visual images. Students will develop their visual literacy skills by learning to evaluate and construct visual arguments, with an emphasis on the ethical and social concerns of image construction. Students will learn to design professional-looking images created with the latest image-editing and graphic-layout software. This course is taught in a Macintosh environment.

Other Requirements / Notes: This course aims to provide an understanding of the field of Critical Media Communication and its impact on contemporary society.
Full Course Description: This course aims to provide an understanding of the field of Critical Media Communication and its impact on contemporary society. The course covers a diverse range of issues concerning contemporary media communication, especially in news media, entertainment media, advertising, and the impact of social media. Students will critically assess and evaluate the impact of media messages on society by presenting visual arguments on contemporary media issues. Students are encouraged to be critical authors, as well as critical consumers, of media. Prerequisite: COMM 1306.

This course is an introduction to the theory and practice of interpersonal communication. Students will study notions of self, other and relationships by focusing on how interpersonal communication intersects the development of these constructs. Students will use theory to assess communication in family, personal, romantic and social relationships in order to improve the communication in such relationships. Fall. Spring.

Students design and deliver presentations as they study audio/visual communication theory, audience identification, appropriate media selection, presentation settings and different speaking styles. Students learn effective use of multimedia and computer-aided presentations.

Other Requirements / Notes: Through an understanding of how gender roles are formed and maintained in our society, students will also consider how they can act to influence gender expectations.
Full Course Description: Students in this course will explore the interactive relationship between gender and communication in three major areas: (1) the creation and perpetuation of gender roles in families, schools, media and society in general; (2) the way these socially created gender differences affect our feelings of personal success, satisfaction and self-esteem; and (3) specific social issues highlighting the transition of gender roles in our culture. Through an understanding of how gender roles are formed and maintained in our society, students will also consider how they can act to influence gender expectations.
Students in this course will study nonverbal behaviors and how they repeat, underscore, substitute for and regulate verbal communication in the communication environment and between communicators.

In this course, students will study and analyze the forms and strategies of persuasive discourse from both rhetorical and psychological perspectives. The course will focus on political persuasion and persuasion in the media.

This course will analyze the role of communication in the context of modern global, complex organizations (government, business, industry, education, nonprofits, etc.). In addition to a review of theory, methodology and application of communication research in organizational settings, the course will focus on communication networks, styles and culture in organizations. The role of communication professionals in organizational structures will be emphasized.

This course provides a comprehensive approach to listening competence. Students will learn to improve their listening skills through exposure to a wide range of listening materials, such as instructional videos, taped conversations, class calls, coaching calls, and practice application activities and through continuous self-monitoring. Students should expect to demonstrate a higher level of listening competency inside the classroom, as well as in the workplace and in social situations.

This is an introductory course for all types of students, providing a strong foundation for visual storytelling, using video production to tell those stories, and giving the basics of Final Cut Pro editing. 2361 Argumentation and Advocacy for Social Justice 3 hours This course will be an introduction to basic concepts of argumentation theory, such as problems of evidence or inference, induction, deduction, logical fallacies in arguments, persuasive campaigns, arguments from authority, rhetorical terms, and audience. Students will develop the ability to analyze as well as construct arguments. Beyond serving as an introductory course to argumentation theory, this course will be framed through the lens of social justice. Students will learn the important role argument plays as a form of advocacy to address a variety of social justice issues: income equality, prison reform, immigration, religious persecution, ethnic cleansing and racial reconciliation. Prerequisite: COMM 1317.

Prerequisites: COMM 1306 and either sophomore standing or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: COMM 1306 and either sophomore standing or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course will introduce students to cultural studies, a group of international, interdisciplinary, critical approaches that prepare students to work inclusively and peacefully with people from diverse communities to confront complex issues, address inequities and make positive social change. Students will study how constructs of race, ethnicity, nationality, class, gender, sexuality, age, bodies, and ability inform practices of power that influence popular culture, communication, technology and everyday life. Prerequisite: COMM 1306 and either sophomore standing or permission of instructor. Fall. 2365 Introduction to Public Relations and Advertising 3 hours An introductory course surveying the evolution and integration of the public relations and advertising professions. This comprehensive overview clarifies similarities and differences in the fields and explores industry structure, principles and tactics, current trends in marketing communication, and career paths. Fall. Spring.

Semester Offered: offered on occasion to introduce new topics or to allow students to explore special topics or special projects within the field of communication.
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This course will be offered on occasion to introduce new topics or to allow students to explore special topics or special projects within the field of communication. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

Other Requirements / Notes: This course explores the past, present, and future possibilities of religion in the major institutions of human experience. Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing.
Full Course Description: All the big ideas in human life didn’t really get going until we had the words to describe and explore them. Chief among the things in this category is religion. Spiritual notions are probably as old as language itself. But religion is particularly interesting from a rhetorical standpoint because, more than faith or belief, there is a public aspect to it. From the beginning, religion has been as much about human power and control as about the search for the divine. 10,000 years into recorded human history, religion influences how we talk about everything, from government to family to sexuality. This course explores the past, present, and future possibilities of religion in the major institutions of human experience. Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing.

The focus of this course is on the study of communication as it relates to health professionals and health education, including the study of provider-client interaction and the role of communication in provider diagnosis, treatment, social support, prevention and public health campaigns.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing.
Full Course Description: Students in this course will explore established and emerging social media technologies and study their application in contemporary PR practice. We will study how to strategically use such technologies to monitor conversations on the internet, engage online communities, identify influencers, establish thought leadership, optimize content for search engines, and measure performance across social media platforms. Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing.

This course will take students through current and engaging case studies in media as they investigate topics and issues in broadcasting, advertising, public relations and journalism that are relevant in today’s society. Students will gain insight into real-world situations as well as understand how professionals handle ethical dilemmas in contemporary media, both successfully and unsuccessfully. Does not satisfy the General Education requirement for Ethics.

Prerequisites: COMM 1306 and junior or senior standing, or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: COMM 1306 and junior or senior standing, or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course is an introduction to the basic concepts of rhetorical communication proposed by rhetorical theorists from classical through contemporary times. We will focus on contemporary theorists, including Foucault, Habermas and Burke, and discuss feminist, Afrocentric and Asian challenges to the Western tradition of rhetoric. Because theories of rhetoric reflect our culture, issues concerning contemporary American culture and society will be central to our discussion. Prerequisites: COMM 1306 and junior or senior standing, or permission of instructor.

Prerequisites: COMM 1306, COMM 2302, and junior or senior standing.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: COMM 1306, COMM 2302, and junior or senior standing.
Full Course Description: This course is designed to acquaint students with rhetorical criticism as a method for answering research questions in communication. Students will be provided with opportunities to apply critical methods in the writing of essays analyzing various kinds of data. Prerequisites: COMM 1306, COMM 2302, and junior or senior standing. Fall. Spring.

Prerequisites: Junior or senior standing or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course will explore the role of teams as collaborative and community building entities. Students will actively participate in task-oriented, problem-solving groups. Topics may include: teams vs. small groups, general system theory, organizing and managing effective meetings, synergy and cohesion, interpersonal needs, conflict, member roles, leadership types and theories, team decision making and problem solving, virtual groups. Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing or permission of instructor.

Prerequisites: Sophomore standing or permission of instructor. 3345 Native American and Chican@x Communication 3 hours This course will engage students in a focused inquiry of communication practices and contributions made by America’s Indigenous peoples. Students will survey the role of language, discourse and media in past and in contemporary, political and social issues including: citizenship, human, civil, religious, labor and land rights. Students will be introduced to Indigenous perspectives through mediums of communication such as: narratives, oral histories, pow-wow songs, corridos, performance art, film and documentaries. Students will have opportunities to participate in Austin’s Native American and Chican@x community events and engage with elders, activists and artists.; COMM 2302 and either junior standing or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Sophomore standing or permission of instructor. Prerequisite: COMM 2302 and either junior standing or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: The dynamic relationship between communication and culture will be the area of study. Students will survey intercultural, international, cross-cultural, multicultural and cultural studies approaches in order to advance awareness of the ways in which culture shapes how individuals and groups communicate and how that communication also sustains, expresses and affects culture. Distinct patterns and styles of communication as well as shared values and goals within, between, among and across national, ethnic, racial and social groups will be examined. Prerequisite: Sophomore standing or permission of instructor. 3345 Native American and Chican@x Communication 3 hours This course will engage students in a focused inquiry of communication practices and contributions made by America’s Indigenous peoples. Students will survey the role of language, discourse and media in past and in contemporary, political and social issues including: citizenship, human, civil, religious, labor and land rights. Students will be introduced to Indigenous perspectives through mediums of communication such as: narratives, oral histories, pow-wow songs, corridos, performance art, film and documentaries. Students will have opportunities to participate in Austin’s Native American and Chican@x community events and engage with elders, activists and artists. Prerequisite: COMM 2302 and either junior standing or permission of instructor.

Prerequisites: Junior or senior standing or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: In this course, students will examine communication structures, systems, dynamics and patterns particular to families. Students will be encouraged to examine family structures in order to see how internalized family patterns impact their relational lives. Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing or permission of instructor.

Other Requirements / Notes: Students in this course will gain an understanding of working on an account team within a public relations or advertising agency and how to foster productive communication between clients, internal staff, partner agencies and suppliers. Prerequisite: COMM 2365 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Students in this course will gain an understanding of working on an account team within a public relations or advertising agency and how to foster productive communication between clients, internal staff, partner agencies and suppliers. Students will explore all aspects of a simulated account, including research, media, creative and production and will work together to develop a fully integrated communication campaign. Prerequisite: COMM 2365 or permission of instructor.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: COMM 2365 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Students will use their creativity to design strategic advertising and public relations messaging. A working knowledge of Adobe Photoshop and InDesign will be developed as students plan and execute messages in a variety of media, with focus on strategy, copywriting, layout and design. The class will work with a local nonprofit organization to provide advertising and PR materials. Basic training in Adobe Creative Cloud programs is included. Prerequisite: COMM 2365 or permission of instructor.

Prerequisites: Junior or senior standing or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: In this course, students will study the complexities of the mother- daughter relationship from a variety of theoretical perspectives that intersect interpersonal communication, family communication and women’s studies. Relying on (but not limited to) dialectical theory and narrative theory, students will explore what has been called the mother of all relationships through scholarly research, films, novels and self- reflection. In this course, we confront such fundamental questions as who we are and who we want to be, and we investigate how the ways in which we relate to others are inextricably intertwined with our identities as daughters/mothers. 3370 Social Movement Communication and Public Advocacy 3 hours Social movements play a fundamental role as democratic vehicles for social change and resistance to change within social, cultural, and political contexts of large-scale conflicts. This course explores the intersection of social movements and rhetoric with a specific focus on how social movements deploy rhetorical strategies to achieve persuasive goals. Students will learn about different movement typologies, a movement’s life cycle, agitation and control tactics and strategies, legitimization and leadership, the role of social media in contemporary movements and more. The course will also examine different types of social movements (e.g., civil rights, environmental, right to die, animal rights) from the Revolutionary War to the present. Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing or permission of instructor.

Prerequisites: Sophomore standing. 3374 The Dark Side of Interpersonal Communication 3 hours This course examines communication that is considered negative, challenging, complicated, stressful, or unpleasant. Topics include (but are not limited to) teasing/bullying, jealousy, (passive) aggression, and communication incompetence. The phenomena covered range from the everyday to the extreme, and impact our well-being as well as our relationships.; COMM 1306 and COMM 2312. 3376 Public Relations and Advertising in a Digital World 3 hours Focusing on the influence and integration of social media technologies throughout public relations and advertising campaigns, students build the fundamentals of stakeholder relationship management. Strategic communication principles are applied to established and emerging digital platforms, addressing planning, creative, research, engagement, safeguards, and measurement.; COMM 2365 or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Provides an exploration of a wide range of topics related to the understanding of the importance of sports, the cultural identities of those who engage in sports communication and the organizations involved in sports. Prerequisite: Sophomore standing. Prerequisite: COMM 2365 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Provides an exploration of a wide range of topics related to the understanding of the importance of sports, the cultural identities of those who engage in sports communication and the organizations involved in sports. Specific topics include community in sport, sports media, mythology, fan culture, gender and race in sport, employment in sport, and crises communication to name a few. Prerequisite: Sophomore standing. 3374 The Dark Side of Interpersonal Communication 3 hours This course examines communication that is considered negative, challenging, complicated, stressful, or unpleasant. Topics include (but are not limited to) teasing/bullying, jealousy, (passive) aggression, and communication incompetence. The phenomena covered range from the everyday to the extreme, and impact our well-being as well as our relationships. Prerequisites: COMM 1306 and COMM 2312. 3376 Public Relations and Advertising in a Digital World 3 hours Focusing on the influence and integration of social media technologies throughout public relations and advertising campaigns, students build the fundamentals of stakeholder relationship management. Strategic communication principles are applied to established and emerging digital platforms, addressing planning, creative, research, engagement, safeguards, and measurement. Prerequisite: COMM 2365 or permission of instructor.

Prerequisites: COMM 2302 and either junior standing or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: COMM 2302 and either junior standing or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course examines popular culture and the complex debates, histories, controversies and possibilities surrounding it. Students will learn critical, cultural and rhetorical approaches to analyzing popular culture artifacts, texts, and performances and will learn to write popular culture reviews for general and scholarly audiences. Students will conduct an extensive analysis of a popular culture event in the Austin community and will engage in a service-learning project with SXSW during spring break. Prerequisite: COMM 2302 and either junior standing or permission of instructor.

This course will survey a diverse body of films, from Hollywood blockbusters to art-house flicks, independent cinema to experimental and foreign films. Students will learn to explore films through the major film theories of the past century, including auteur theory, genre, spectatorship, ideological and gender criticism, visual and audio editing, and narrative style.

This course focuses on the examination of leadership theories, models, principles, styles and functions. Students will examine topics such as the leader-mentor relationship, motivation, team-work, power, trust, persuasion, facilitation, negotiation, change and ethics.

Prerequisites: COMM 2302 and either junior standing or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: COMM 2302 and either junior standing or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course will focus on advanced cultural studies initiatives proposed by world-renowned cultural studies scholars. Students will examine social and political intersections among every day discursive practices, popular culture, institutions, media and technology and how notions of race, ethnicity, nationality, class, gender, sexuality, age, the body and ability contribute to the ways in which culture is produced, reproduced, negotiated and changed. Students will have opportunities to apply and extend cultural studies approaches to their own research, artistic work, or creative expression. Prerequisite: COMM 2302 and either junior standing or permission of instructor.

Prerequisites: COMM 2365 and either junior standing or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: COMM 2365 and either junior standing or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Students will examine current work by feminist theorists on issues related to gender, feminism, rhetoric and communication. Students gain interdisciplinary study in feminist perspectives by engaging in issues such as audience, co-cultures, power, race and feminist popular culture. 4326 Public Relations for Nonprofit Organizations3 hours The process and the practice of public relations will be explored through the review and analysis of actual case studies. Students will also learn the public relations process by planning and preparing their own projects for an Austin client or organization. Prerequisites: COMM 2365 and either junior standing or permission of instructor.

Prerequisites: COMM 3358 or junior or senior standing or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: COMM 3358 or junior or senior standing or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Incorporating public relations and advertising tactics into an integrated strategic communication approach, students will build brand relationships and brand equity. Theory, strategy, and best practices converge throughout the course as students analyze, plan, budget and launch a fully integrated campaign of consistent, persuasive, and engaging content and outreach. Prerequisite: COMM 3358 or junior or senior standing or permission of instructor.

Prerequisites: COMM 3344 and either junior standing or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: COMM 3344 and either junior standing or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course will investigate the historic portrayal and (mis)representation of Native American and Chican@x communities in film along with the communication strategies used by Native American and Chican@x filmmakers for self-empowerment. Students will gain an appreciation for alternative approaches to film narrative, genres, visual communication, representation, production, etc. Students will have the opportunity to produce a short film, screenplay, storyboard or critique. Prerequisite: COMM 3344 and either junior standing or permission of instructor.

Prerequisites: Communication Core, sophomore, junior or senior standing, and permission of instructor.; COMM 2365 or permission of instructor.
Semester Offered: Fall.; Spring.; offered on occasion to introduce new and/or timely topics or to allow students to explore familiar topics in public relations and advertising in greater depth.
Other Requirements / Notes: The internship will be a real-world experience for the Communication major in which the student will work in a communication setting that involves the practical use of the theories and skills that have been introduced in the previous courses. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisites: Communication Core, sophomore, junior or senior standing, and permission of instructor. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: COMM 2365 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: The internship will be a real-world experience for the Communication major in which the student will work in a communication setting that involves the practical use of the theories and skills that have been introduced in the previous courses. This work may be on or off campus and will involve the opportunity for the student to practice the skills and knowledge involved in a communication career. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisites: Communication Core, sophomore, junior or senior standing, and permission of instructor. Fall. Spring. 4352 Special Topics in Public Relations and Advertising 3 hours This upper-division course will be offered on occasion to introduce new and/or timely topics or to allow students to explore familiar topics in public relations and advertising in greater depth. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: COMM 2365 or permission of instructor.

Regardless of deceptive intent, every act of communication involves basic decisions about what information to include or leave out. From this perspective, deception can be thought of as a specialized subset of communication, with a particular focus on intent, awareness, and context. This course explores lying and other forms of deception as a communicative phenomenon—from interpersonal relationships to the intrapersonal (self-deception) to the level of whole societies (public deception in politics, writing, and the visual arts). Special attention is given to the nature of truth, ethical frameworks for evaluating deception, how children develop deceptive skills (and the ways parents react). This course also explores liar behavior (from the everyday to the pathological) and lie detection (particularly the problematic use of machines like polygraphs.

Other Requirements / Notes: In addition to a general understanding of conflict resolution, the course will examine a variety of techniques for dealing with conflict and describe how to select appropriate techniques in a variety of settings. Prerequisite: Junior standing.
Full Course Description: This course will present theories of conflict and conflict resolution with a specific emphasis on interpersonal, group and organizational conflict. In addition to a general understanding of conflict resolution, the course will examine a variety of techniques for dealing with conflict and describe how to select appropriate techniques in a variety of settings. Prerequisite: Junior standing.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing.
Full Course Description: The most significant developments in human history did not occur until language gave us the ability to describe and explore them. Much of what we take for granted as the fundamental structures of human life -- all the things that we know collectively as “culture”—cannot be separated from language itself. In fact, language may be the universal basis of all of the various elements of culture. This course traces the inextricably intertwined nature of language and human culture throughout history and explores the possibilities (and cautionary tales) that await us. Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing.
Full Course Description: This course examines the important role rhetoric plays in one of the largest industries in the world—tourism. Since tourism involves the exchange of people and culture, this course will also explore the impact of tourism on intercultural communication contact between tourists and hosts. Topics include the evolution of tourism, language of tourism, modernism and postmodernism, public memory, authenticity and place staging, tourist identities, roles and motivations, cultural commodification, toxic tourism and social justice, and the socio-cultural consequences of tourism. This course will examine different types of tourism including mass tourism, cultural tourism, religious and spiritual tourism, ecotourism, sex/romance tourism, poverty tourism and dark tourism. Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing.

Prerequisites: Junior or senior standing or permission by instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: In addition to understanding the rhetorical nature of public memory, students will learn some of the major issues of debate among public memory scholars—the dialectical tension between remembering and forgetting, conflicts between official and vernacular cultures, the role of memory in supporting and contesting dominant ideologies, the intersection of memory, identity and public policy. Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing or permission by instructor.
Full Course Description: This course examines the foundational role rhetoric play in how publics remember the past and its relationship to the present. In addition to understanding the rhetorical nature of public memory, students will learn some of the major issues of debate among public memory scholars—the dialectical tension between remembering and forgetting, conflicts between official and vernacular cultures, the role of memory in supporting and contesting dominant ideologies, the intersection of memory, identity and public policy. This course will explore a wide variety of artifacts related to memory studies—museums, memorials, monuments, images/photographs, popular music, eulogies—within distinct thematic contexts (e.g., slavery, the civil rights movement, war, terrorism, forgiveness and reconciliation). Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing or permission by instructor.

Prerequisites: COMM 2327. 4382 Social and Environmental Corporate Communication 3 hours Explores the multidimensional nature and content of corporate social responsibility (CSR) communication through a competitive global business sphere. Addresses organization messaging, storytelling, collaboration, and reporting of social, environmental, corporate, and ethical responsibilities.; Junior or senior standing.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing.
Full Course Description: Provides a senior level overview and examination of the cultural construction of work. Specific topics include tracing the evolution of the meaning of work from Aristotle to the The Office, self- fulfillment, identity, community, and workplace freedom among others. Prerequisite: COMM 2327. 4382 Social and Environmental Corporate Communication 3 hours Explores the multidimensional nature and content of corporate social responsibility (CSR) communication through a competitive global business sphere. Addresses organization messaging, storytelling, collaboration, and reporting of social, environmental, corporate, and ethical responsibilities. Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing. Fall.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing.
Full Course Description: This upper division course focuses on the application of health communication principles in the development and implementation of health campaigns used by health communication professionals. Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing. Spring.

May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: COMM 3308 or permission of instructor.

Semester Offered: offered on occasion to introduce new topics or to allow students to explore, in greater depth, topics or special projects within the field of communication that are relevant to students in all specializations.
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This upper-division course will be offered on occasion to introduce new topics or to allow students to explore, in greater depth, topics or special projects within the field of communication that are relevant to students in all specializations. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: Junior or senior standing or permission of instructor.

In this course, students will consider the use of language in society and how it relates to cultural identity. They will explore what language is and how it provides a framework for how we think and form our cultural perspectives. Students will explore questions like what is the relation between language and power and why linguistic prejudice contributes to social inequality.

This course explores how diverse ethnic groups have experienced both marginalization and empowerment through minority language usage. First, we explore how multilingualism has been viewed throughout United States history and how languages have evolved after contact with English. We then examine historical, social, and linguistic characteristics of minority language groups in the United States. Finally, we consider how language ideologies are formed through collective experiences and how these ideologies inform politics, education, and social movements. Prerequisite: CLLC 1310.

This course will introduce students to the wide variety of language- learning methods (from natural, linguistic, and communicative methods to self-study methods) while also providing basic sample training in each one. We will analyze the teaching methods employed in a variety of language textbooks and the course will ultimately serve as a toolkit for how to organize second language courses. Prerequisite: CLLC 1310.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary. This course is open to all students majoring in Spanish, or minoring in French or German, or with permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course is intended to provide students majoring in French and Spanish with an opportunity to focus on a comparative topic in modern languages, literatures and cultures not covered in the traditional curriculum. Specific topics vary depending upon the faculty area of expertise. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. This course is open to all students majoring in Spanish, or minoring in French or German, or with permission of instructor. 4330 Topics in Comparative Languages, Literatures and Cultures 3 hours This course will explore global topics and texts that link language areas and cultural traditions. Topics may include identity, space, memory or class and gender among others, as shaped by cross-cultural intersections. It provides diverse cultural frames of reference and alternative perspectives on current global issues.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary. This course is open to all students majoring in Spanish, or minoring in French or German, or with permission of instructor.
