It provides diverse cultural frames of reference and alternative perspectives on current global issues.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prior programming experience is not required.
Full Course Description: This course is an introduction to computational problem solving for students. Topics covered include basic programming structures, procedural abstraction, data types and representation, control structures, iteration, file I/O, lists, and functions using the Python programming language. Students will become confident in using an Integrated Development Environment to develop, execute, and debug programs. Students will develop skills in computational thinking and build algorithmic solutions that can be applied to a variety of problems and disciplines including numerical computation and text processing. This course is designed for students not majoring in Computer Science. Prior programming experience is not required. Students who have credit for COSC 1423 or higher may not take this class for credit. Fall. Spring.

This course introduces students to fundamental aspects of the field of computing, focusing on problem-solving and software design concepts and their realizations as computer programs using Python. Topics include procedural and data abstraction, control structures, and lists. Introduction to Python for the purpose of gaining mastery of these principles will be done in a 75 minute laboratory each week as part of this course. Pre- or corequisite: MATH 1314 or higher. Fall. Spring.

Prerequisites: COSC 1423 with a grade of C or better.
Full Course Description: This course emphasizes object-oriented problem solving using the Java programming language. Topics covered include principles of object-oriented design and problem solving, abstract data types and encapsulation, an introduction to algorithm analysis, transformations (searching and sorting), and introductory data structures including arrays, stacks, queues, and linked lists. Applications of topics will be achieved in weekly 75 minute laboratory setting as part of this course. Prerequisite: COSC 1423 with a grade of C or better. Fall. Spring.

Prerequisites: COSC 1423 with a grade of C or better.
Full Course Description: An introduction to programming concepts used on the world wide web. Concepts include form processing scripts, structuring data, basic database connectivity, and developing appropriate structure and style for the world wide web. HTTP requests and responses, authentication, authorization and session management will be explored. Dynamic html, DOM and cascading style sheets will be incorporated into the course. Programming will occur in one or more appropriate scripting languages. Prerequisite: COSC 1423 with a grade of C or better. Fall. Spring.

Prerequisites: COSC 2425 with a grade of C or better.
Full Course Description: Students will learn how to use and produce software components, specification, design, implementation, and verification. Topics include design by contract, unit testing, interface design, and best practices. Prerequisite: COSC 2425 with a grade of C or better. Fall. Spring.

Prerequisites: COSC 2425 with a grade of C or better.
Other Requirements / Notes: This course provides a comprehensive understanding of how computer systems work, from the hardware level to the operating system.
Full Course Description: This course provides a comprehensive understanding of how computer systems work, from the hardware level to the operating system. Topics include design and functionality of processors, memory hierarchies, input/output mechanisms, instruction set architectures, memory management, process scheduling, and system-level programming. Prerequisites: COSC 2425 with a grade of C or better.

Prerequisites: COSC 2329 with a grade of C or better.
Semester Offered: offered using various programming languages.; Offered on sufficient demand.
Full Course Description: Selected topics are offered using various programming languages. Advanced programming concepts are applied to science, business, engineering or mathematics problems. Challenge credit may not be earned for this class. Special topic will appear on transcript. Prerequisite: COSC 2329 with a grade of C or better. Offered on sufficient demand.

Prerequisites: COSC 2329 with a grade of C or better.
Full Course Description: This course introduces students to problems in the mobile computing arena including design and development of applications deployed on mobile devices. Topics include memory and data management, economy of computation, user interface design, network techniques, and implementation and testing of applications on mobile platforms. Prerequisite: COSC 2329 with a grade of C or better. Fall.

Prerequisites: COSC 2329 with a grade of C or better.
Full Course Description: An examination of data structures and algorithm design and analysis. Topics include searching, advanced sorting, proof of correctness, performance analysis of algorithms, trees, graphs and hashing techniques. Prerequisite: COSC 2329 with a grade of C or better. Fall. Spring.

Prerequisites: COSC 2425 with a grade of C or better.
Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with ACCT 3337.
Full Course Description: Topics include introduction to database concepts, data models, data normalization, data description languages, query facilities, file organization, index organization, file security, and data integrity and reliability. Cross-listed with ACCT 3337. Prerequisite: COSC 2425 with a grade of C or better. Fall. Spring.

Prerequisites: MATH 2413 and COSC 1305 or COSC 1423, with a grade of C or better in the COSC course.
Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with BINF 3338, MATH 3338 and PHYS 3338.
Full Course Description: A survey of standard techniques used to solve numerical and other science-based problems. Topics include roots of equations, systems of linear equations, curve fitting, numerical integration and differentiation, error analysis, number representation, and data visualization. Problems will be solved using programs written in appropriate languages and operating systems. Cross-listed with BINF 3338, MATH 3338 and PHYS 3338. Prerequisites: MATH 2413 and COSC 1305 or COSC 1423, with a grade of C or better in the COSC course. Offered on sufficient demand.

Prerequisites: COSC 3327 and COSC 3337, both with a grade of C or better.
Full Course Description: An introduction to the theory, methods and tools of software engineering and system analysis. Each student will take part in the specification, design, implementation, testing, evolution, maintenance and management of a large-scale team project. Students will be exposed to best practices and use industry-standard tools. Topics include software development methodologies, object-oriented design, software testing, client management and technical communication. Prerequisite: COSC 3327 and COSC 3337, both with a grade of C or better. Corequisite: COSC 4157. Spring.

Prerequisites: MATH 2315 and COSC 2329 with a grade of C or better.
Full Course Description: A primer to the field of artificial intelligence (AI), including its basic concepts, and evolving uses and applications. Students will learn about different types of AI systems, such as rule-based systems, machine learning, and natural language processing, and their real-world uses. The course will also cover ethical and social implications surrounding AI, such as bias and privacy, and discuss the potential consequences of AI on society and the workforce. Prerequisite: MATH 2315 and COSC 2329 with a grade of C or better.

Prerequisites: COSC 2329 with a grade of C or better.
Full Course Description: This course is a foundational course that covers the basics of cybersecurity including cybersecurity principles, common cyber threats and vulnerabilities, and attack and defense mechanisms. This introductory course provides students with a solid foundation for more advanced cybersecurity coursework. Prerequisite: COSC 2329 with a grade of C or better.

Course Title: Introduction to Data Mining and Analysis
Prerequisites: COSC 3327 with a grade of C or better and MATH 2315.
Full Course Description: An introduction to data mining and data analysis in the context of artificial intelligence. Building upon a solid programming foundation and using multiple programming languages, students will learn how to identify patterns, extract useful information, and make predictions from large datasets. Topics include various data mining methods, including clustering, classification, and association analysis. Prerequisites: COSC 3327 with a grade of C or better and MATH 2315.

Prerequisites: COSC 3343 with a grade of C or better.
Full Course Description: This course places its emphasis on ensuring the security of computer networks. Students learn about network security threats and vulnerabilities, as well as how to design and implement secure network architectures. Course topics include a wide range of subjects including network protocols and vulnerabilities, firewalls, intrusion detection systems, and encryption techniques. Prerequisite: COSC 3343 with a grade of C or better.

May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Special topic will appear on transcript. Prerequisite: COSC 3327 with a grade of C or better. Offered on sufficient demand.

Semester Offered: Offered with prior approval of the cooperating work site.; Fall.; Spring.; Summer.
Other Requirements / Notes: Students will be required to work regular shifts, observing and participating in the daily routine. Offered with prior approval of the cooperating work site. Requires a minimum of 60 hours per semester of working time. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: An internship at a cooperating work site. Students will be required to work regular shifts, observing and participating in the daily routine. A log is to be kept of each day’s activity. Offered with prior approval of the cooperating work site. Requires a minimum of 60 hours per semester of working time. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor. Fall. Spring. Summer.

Prerequisites: COSC 3340 with a grade of C or better and MATH 3305.
Other Requirements / Notes: This course will examine the algorithms used in machine learning and provide a comprehensive understanding of their underlying principles.
Full Course Description: This course will examine the algorithms used in machine learning and provide a comprehensive understanding of their underlying principles. Students will gain practical experience in applying various machine learning algorithms such as regression, decision trees, neural networks, and clustering to real-world datasets. Topics include model selection, parameter tuning, and overfitting. The course requires a strong foundation in algorithms and programming languages. Prerequisites: COSC 3340 with a grade of C or better and MATH 3305.

Prerequisites: COSC 3340 with a grade of C or better.
Full Course Description: An introduction to natural language processing (NLP). Students will learn about various NLP techniques such as sentiment analysis, named entity recognition, and machine translation, and their practical applications in teaching machines to understand and generate human language. Topics include an introduction to recurrent neural networks and transformers. Prerequisites: COSC 3340 with a grade of C or better.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Students will conduct research with a faculty member on a problem of mutual interest. Topics include feasibility assessment, risk management, as well as standard tools and techniques. Students will spend the semester investigating, designing, evolving and articulating a solution to their problem. Students will produce a specification and plan for their solution. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor. Fall. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Students will conduct research with a faculty member on a problem of mutual interest. Topics include feasibility assessment, risk management, as well as standard tools and techniques. Students will spend the semester investigating, designing, evolving and articulating a solution to their problem. Students will produce a specification and plan for their solution. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor. Fall. Spring.

Prerequisites: COSC 3340 with a grade of C or better.
Full Course Description: A primer to the field of computer vision. Students will learn about various computer vision techniques such as image segmentation, object recognition, and facial recognition, and how they are used in applications such as autonomous vehicles and imaging. Topics include an introduction to deep learning techniques for computer vision. Prerequisites: COSC 3340 with a grade of C or better.

Prerequisites: COSC 3339 and COSC 4157, both with a grade of C or better.
Other Requirements / Notes: Formal presentations of progress are required throughout the semester. Students are also required to provide documentation of their work including a written description of their chosen problem, written use cases and scenarios for the problem, a system diagram, main interfaces, substantially completed components, and a project plan that includes at least two milestones.
Full Course Description: Students will conduct research on a non-trivial problem that is approved by the course instructor. Students are expected to thoroughly research a computing problem, investigate a possible solution to the problem, design, evolve, and articulate a solution to their approved problem. Students meet individually (or as a team) with the instructor on a weekly basis to discuss their plans, challenges, and progress toward the implementation of their solution. Formal presentations of progress are required throughout the semester. Students are also required to provide documentation of their work including a written description of their chosen problem, written use cases and scenarios for the problem, a system diagram, main interfaces, substantially completed components, and a project plan that includes at least two milestones. Prerequisites: COSC 3339 and COSC 4157, both with a grade of C or better. Fall.

Prerequisites: COSC 4360 with a grade of C or better.
Other Requirements / Notes: Students are required to participate in a formal seminar and to deliver a professional presentation of their project and demonstrate their solution.
Full Course Description: Students in this second semester will complete and present their developed solution and artifacts that were approved and started in the prior fall semester. Students meet individually (or as a team) with the instructor on a weekly basis to discuss their plans, challenges, and progress of their project solution. Students are required to participate in a formal seminar and to deliver a professional presentation of their project and demonstrate their solution. Prerequisite: COSC 4360 with a grade of C or better. Spring.

Prerequisites: COSC 3343 with a grade of C or better.
Other Requirements / Notes: This course provides students with a comprehensive understanding of the principles on securing computer systems, including operating systems, applications, and data.
Full Course Description: This course provides students with a comprehensive understanding of the principles on securing computer systems, including operating systems, applications, and data. Students will acquire knowledge on how to protect computer systems from a variety of threats, and will learn about threat identification, vulnerability assessment, and the implementation of effective security measures. Prerequisite: COSC 3343 with a grade of C or better.

Prerequisites: COSC 3343 with a grade of C or better.
Other Requirements / Notes: This course provides students with a deep understanding of the specialization including its emerging threats and vulnerabilities, as well as cutting-edge security solutions and technologies.
Full Course Description: Designed for students who have a solid foundation in cybersecurity principles, this special topic course places its emphasis on one specialization within the dynamic field of cybersecurity. This course provides students with a deep understanding of the specialization including its emerging threats and vulnerabilities, as well as cutting-edge security solutions and technologies. Prerequisite: COSC 3343 with a grade of C or better.

Prerequisites: COSC 3327 with a grade of C or better, MATH 2315, and MATH 2413.
Full Course Description: An examination of advanced algorithmic paradigms, complexity, and NP-completeness. Topics include divide-and-conquer, backtracking, dynamic programming, and how to cope with NP-complete problems. Prerequisites: COSC 3327 with a grade of C or better, MATH 2315, and MATH 2413.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: The history and analysis of the American criminal justice process. Includes an overview of crime theory, measurement, factors of crime, law enforcement, court system, prosecution and defense, trial process, and corrections.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: Discourse and study on the general principles of criminal liability and imputability, and an examination of the modern statutory formulations of the various offenses against the person, offenses against habitation and occupancy, and offenses against property.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: Discourse and study on the history, structure, law and mechanics of criminal prosecution and adjudication. Prerequisite: CRIJ 1302. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Prerequisites: CRIJ 1302. 2322 Juvenile Delinquency and the Juvenile Justice System 3 hours Analysis and interpretations of theories of causation and prevention of juvenile delinquency; examination of the historical development and contemporary practices of the juvenile justice system, including the roles of police, the juvenile court, juvenile probation and private community- service treatment programs.; CRIJ 1302 or PSYC 2301.
Full Course Description: Analysis and evaluation of contemporary correctional systems and their clientele. Contemporary American corrections are placed in perspective along historical, philosophical, legal, political and cross-cultural dimensions. Prerequisite: CRIJ 1302. 2322 Juvenile Delinquency and the Juvenile Justice System 3 hours Analysis and interpretations of theories of causation and prevention of juvenile delinquency; examination of the historical development and contemporary practices of the juvenile justice system, including the roles of police, the juvenile court, juvenile probation and private community- service treatment programs. Prerequisite: CRIJ 1302 or PSYC 2301.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: Analysis of the history and practices employed in policing American society, with emphasis given to the role and function of the police in the United States and the critical issues currently facing law enforcement. Prerequisite: CRIJ 1302.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course is an examination of the causes of crime, and the competing explanations for crime. Included will be a discussion of the basics of rational choice theory, trait theory, social structure theory, and social processes theory. Also covered in the course are important sources of crime data, typologies of offenders and crime, and crime prevention.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: Discourse and study on the two basic questions of law pertaining to criminal evidence and proof: (1) What evidentiary materials of either accusatory or exculpatory nature should be admissible for consideration by the trier of fact, and (2) what use is (or should be) made of the admitted materials by the trier of fact. In particular, the course examines the substance and application of the Federal Rules of Evidence, including the rules addressing relevance, impeachment, hearsay, opinions, privileges, witnesses and scientific evidence. Prerequisites: CRIJ 2306.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Prerequisites: CRIJ 2306 or CRIJ 2328 or FRSC 3321/ FRSC 3121.
Full Course Description: This course explores effective advocacy skills, appropriate professional conduct and trial preparation. This is a performance and presentation- based course that provides students the opportunity to present direct and cross examinations of witnesses, opening statements and closing arguments, and the introduction of evidentiary exhibits with appropriate foundation. Prerequisites: CRIJ 2306 or CRIJ 2328 or FRSC 3321/ FRSC 3121.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Students will obtain a thorough understanding of how research is conducted and how practitioners can benefit from this knowledge.
Full Course Description: This course will provide an introduction to research methods in criminal justice. Foundations of research, research design, analysis of findings, and ethical issues in researching criminal justice issues will be addressed. Students will obtain a thorough understanding of how research is conducted and how practitioners can benefit from this knowledge. This course covers the basic techniques and procedures used in the process of criminal justice research. Topics include: defining research problems, ethics in criminal justice research, selecting and measuring variables, stating hypotheses, sampling, and developing experimental research designs. This course will culminate with a proposal that students may use in future research. Prerequisite: CRIJ 1302.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course examines crime through the lens of the victim. It encompasses a survey of the literature, research and current trends; particular attention is given to theories of victimization, victim rights and services, and impact of crimes on victims. Prerequisite: CRIJ 2336.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course provides students with an applied approach to statistics and the role that research and statistics play in the study of criminology and criminal justice. Students will explore various types of data used within criminal justice and the fundamentals of elementary statistical analysis including the appropriate use of data, limits of various data methods, how data is collected and how to interpret findings. Prerequisite: CRIJ 3330. Corequisite: MATH 0104 or satisfactory placement.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course explores and analyzes the relationship between drugs and crime. It also examines the current relationship between drug types/ usage, criminal behavior, and illicit and legitimate markets. A review of policies developed to control drug trafficking, drug abuse, and drug- related crime will also be covered.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course examines and critically analyzes the reflexive relationship and observed disparities between social constructions of race, ethnicity, gender, crime and justice.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: CRIJ 1302 and junior standing.
Full Course Description: An in-depth comparative study of major criminal justice models throughout the world. Includes an examination of the basic structures of government, philosophies of law, structure and responsibilities of law enforcement, courts, and corrections. Prerequisites: CRIJ 1302 and junior standing.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: CRIJ 1310 and junior standing.
Full Course Description: Discourse and analysis of the constitutional, common law, and statutory underpinnings of the procedures governing the prosecution and adjudication of offenses in American courts. The course examines the system from arrest through appeal, including how individual rights are impacted by the processes. It surveys case law developed in the U.S. Supreme Court interpreting the spirit of the Constitution. Includes an examination of procedural law related to confessions and identification, witnesses, pretrial process, prosecutors, grand juries, defense counsel, pleas and plea bargaining, trial, conviction, sentencing, and appeals. Focus on the 5th, 6th, 8th, and 14th Amendments. Where applicable, Texas law will be included. Prerequisites: CRIJ 1310 and junior standing.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies the Culminating Experience for Criminal Justice majors. Prerequisite: Senior standing.
Full Course Description: An upper-division course in which students hone and showcase key skills they have been developing throughout their St. Edward’s University career, including online and field research, written and oral communication, and critical thinking and moral reasoning. The course is grounded in the university mission and seeks to prepare students to enter into the field of criminal justice post-graduation. Students complete projects, assignments, and essays reflecting the goals of the criminal justice discipline. This course is writing intensive. Satisfies the Culminating Experience for Criminal Justice majors. Prerequisite: Senior standing.

Students will also be introduced to tools and techniques to develop an Internet site incorporating publishing software, server technologies and transaction systems. The goal of this course is to provide the student with an implementation perspective of how technology supports digital media development and distribution. Prerequisite: BDMM 2301. Fall.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: We will focus on understanding how entire economies behave.
Full Course Description: This course is an introduction to macroeconomics. We will focus on understanding how entire economies behave. This course is a study of basic economic concepts in a macroeconomic context. These include aggregate supply, aggregate demand, laws of supply and demand; the nature of markets and market equilibria; economic growth; the fundamentals of international trade; the causes and effects of trade and budget deficits; and how the government can affect the economy by spending, taxing, and adjusting the money supply.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Prerequisites: ECON 2301 or permission of department chair.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: ECON 2301 or permission of department chair.
Full Course Description: An analysis of how pricing, output and employment are determined; the role of markets in the allocation of resources; the types and effects of market structures in a mixed economy. Prerequisite: ECON 2301 or permission of department chair. Fall.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Prerequisites: ECON 2302 or permission of department chair.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: ECON 2302 or permission of department chair.
Full Course Description: A systematic analysis of the concepts and measurement of income and output; the classical, Keynesian and new macroeconomic models; and problems related to economic growth. Prerequisite: ECON 2302 or permission of department chair. Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Prerequisites: ECON 2301 or ECON 2302 with a C or better.
Full Course Description: This semester-long course will study the economics of public policy toward natural resources and the environment. We will start with the concepts of externalities, public goods, and property rights, market failure, and social cost benefit analysis. Within this framework, we will consider a few additional problems such as information, uncertainty, and risk analysis. The first set of applications of these tools will involve natural resources. Other applications include air pollution, water pollution, solid waste management, and hazardous substances. In addressing each of these problems, we will compare public policy responses such as administrative regulation, marketable permits, tax incentives, and direct subsidies. We will study several methods to value environmental benefits. Prerequisite: ECON 2301 or ECON 2302 with a C or better.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Prerequisites: ECON 3301 or permission of department chair. Spring, even years.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: ECON 3301 or permission of department chair.
Full Course Description: Analysis of imperfectly competitive markets and market-like situations. Topics include monopoly, oligopoly, price discrimination, product differentiation, entry deterrence, vertical restraints, asymmetric information and regulation. Game-theoretic reasoning is emphasized throughout. Prerequisite: ECON 3301 or permission of department chair. Spring, even years.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: This course is designed to develop an economic understanding of issues related to healthcare.
Full Course Description: This course is designed to develop an economic understanding of issues related to healthcare. We will use economic theory to understand the supply and demand for healthcare, healthcare costs, cost-benefit analysis of healthcare, market for healthcare and market inefficiencies, management of the healthcare industry, supply and demand for healthcare providers, public spending on healthcare, and contemporary issues in the United States healthcare sector.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: The nature of money, financial markets and institutions, commercial banking, asset markets, credit structure, monetary policy, the role of the Federal Reserve System, international finance and monetary theory. Prerequisite: ECON 2302. Fall.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: This course offers an overview of our legal and economic systems and analyzes how regulatory constraints and judicial decisions affect our market economy. Areas of study will include property rights, contract law, criminal law and antitrust. Conventional economic analysis and legal case studies will be used. Prerequisite: ECON 2301. Spring, odd years.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with IBUS 3336.
Full Course Description: Introduction to the theories of trade; the international monetary system; and the financing of international trade and economic development. The U.S. balance of payments is analyzed and its effect on foreign investments and operations is studied. Problems and opportunities unique to multinational business are considered. Cross-listed with IBUS 3336. Prerequisite: ECON 2302. Fall.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: This course presents an overview of the Chinese economy. It focuses on China’s ongoing transition from a planned economy to a more market-oriented economy. Topics covered include reform in the rural agriculture and urban state-owned enterprises, banking, financial markets development, international trade and foreign direct investment, labor and wage issues, and macroeconomic policies. Prerequisite: ECON 2302.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: Topical issues in economics. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisites: Vary by topic.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Prerequisites: BUSI 2305, ECON 2301 and ECON 2302. Spring, odd years.
Full Course Description: This course combines economic theory with statistics to analyze and test economic relationships. Actual economic data will be used to test familiar economic principles and to offer explanations as well as make predictions. Various applications of econometric techniques to specific industries and to macroeconomic modeling will be offered. The course will better prepare students for the job market and for graduate study in economics. Prerequisites: BUSI 2305, ECON 2301 and ECON 2302. Spring, odd years.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: Fundamental issues underlying contemporary labor problems, standards of living and distribution of income, wage theories, social implications of unemployment and full employment, social security and unemployment compensation, collective bargaining, and labor legislation. Prerequisite: ECON 2301. Recommended prerequisite: ECON 3301. Fall, even years.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Students will have opportunities to develop an understanding of the historical, philosophical and sociological foundations of U.S.
Full Course Description: This course introduces students to the education profession. Students will have opportunities to develop an understanding of the historical, philosophical and sociological foundations of U.S. public and private education, particularly their influences on current issues and the future of education. Upon completion, students should be able to contribute an intelligent voice to and make informed choices in the democratic process on issues affecting the quality of education and the teaching profession. This survey course is designed to assist students in affirming or modifying academic and career goals with respect to teacher education. Course requirements include a field experience. Prerequisite: WRIT 1301 or FSTY 1311. Fall. Spring.

Full Course Description: A course designed for elementary teachers providing them with the basic concepts, processes and skills involved in the creation, appreciation and evaluation of the visual arts and music. Though designed for future educators, course content will be applicable for persons directing fine arts programs for children and adolescents outside of school. This course will address the current Texas educator standards (TExES) for the EC– Grade 6 Generalist, the EC–Grade 6 Bilingual Generalist and the EC– Grade 12 Art certifications. Prerequisites: Sophomore standing, EDUC 1330, and EDUC 2331. Fall. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course addresses the foundational learning theories governing informational processing strategies and skills related to learning and lesson planning, as well as the effects of the teacher and the environment on the learning experience. Attention is focused on the educational implication and application of research in the areas of cognitive and language development, psychosocial development, educational needs of exceptional students, cultural differences, constructivism, self-regulation, and the prominent theories of learning and motivation. Measurement theory and the development and use of appropriate evaluation instruments will also be addressed. Prerequisite: EDUC 1330. Fall. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies the Computational Skills requirement when a C or better is earned. Emphasis is placed on understanding the developing child and the impact that development has on mathematics instruction.
Full Course Description: This course examines computing technology, components and applications and the impact of computing in the classroom. Laboratory experiences consist of application software, which may include word processing, spreadsheets, databases, desktop publishing, telecommunications including Internet usage, videotaping and editing, web page design, Web Quest creation, and e-portfolio creation. Additional topics will include evaluation and assessment, mentoring, curriculum development, human growth and development, legalities of technology in education, technology management techniques, identifying resources to enhance education, and/or helpful technology tools for teachers. Satisfies the Computational Skills requirement when a C or better is earned. Prerequisite: EDUC 1330. Fall. Spring. 2337 Teaching Mathematics in the Elementary School 3 hours This course prepares prospective elementary school teachers to create a positive learning experience for elementary students in learning mathematical concepts, skills and applications. Emphasis is placed on understanding the developing child and the impact that development has on mathematics instruction. The Curriculum and Teaching Standards adopted by the National Council of Teachers of Mathematics are stressed. This course builds upon the arithmetic and geometric concepts addressed in MATH 1335 and MATH 1336 and includes a field experience. Prerequisites: EDUC 1330 and EDUC 2331. Corequisite: MATH 1335 or MATH 1336. Spring.

Course Title: Teaching Science in the Elementary School

Full Course Description: This course is intended to provide a comprehensive overview of the objectives, skills, concepts, experiments (hands-on activities), materials and methods necessary to teach science to elementary school children. A series of independent and group activities that will motivate the future educator to teach science in a confident and competent manner will be achieved through class presentations. Satisfies the General Education Science in Depth requirement for EC–6 students only when taken for a letter grade. Prerequisites: EDUC 1330 and EDUC 2331. Pre- or corequisite: Natural Science General Education requirement. Fall.

Also used are courses of study, textbooks, instructional technology and other resources that enhance teaching and learning. A 30-clock-hour internship in an approved secondary school setting is a course requirement. Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: Junior standing, successful completion of the THEA requirements, admission into the Teacher Education program, EDUC 1330 and EDUC 2331.
Full Course Description: This course is a comprehensive study of current developmental theory and early childhood educational theory and practice. State and national standards for the education of young children will be introduced. These include the promotion of child development and learning; building family and community relationships; observing, documenting and assessing to support young children and families; teaching and learning in the early years; and becoming an early childhood professional. Inquiry-oriented activities and creative projects will be used to facilitate active learning and critical thinking. This course is designed to address the current Texas educator standards for the Early Childhood–Grade 6 Generalist and the Early Childhood–Grade 6 Bilingual Generalist. Prerequisites: Junior standing, successful completion of the THEA requirements, admission into the Teacher Education program, EDUC 1330 and EDUC 2331. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences

Full Course Description: Literature appropriate to children: its sources, prominent authors and illustrators, critical evaluation and presentation. Attention is given to the fundamental principles underlying the choice of children’s stories and the techniques of selecting and telling stories. Students preparing to become bilingual teachers will become acquainted with Spanish children’s literature. Does not meet requirements for the English Literature major. Cross-listed with READ 3334. Spring. 3337 Elementary Instructional Methods and Classroom Management 3 hours This course focuses on practical strategies for teaching academic content and for managing student behavior in the elementary classroom. Cooperative learning is emphasized as a teaching approach. Skills for managing behavior include a variety of models. Students are expected not only to master theory at the cognitive level, but to demonstrate competency through participation in internships at local schools. Prerequisites: Senior standing, admission into the Teacher Education program, EDUC 1330, SPED 2324 and EDUC 2331. Corequisite: READ 3348.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Students will create units of instruction based on the “Understanding by Design” model. In addition, students will demonstrate competence in under-standing both criterion- and norm-referenced tests. Prerequisites: Senior standing, admission into the Teacher Education program, EDUC 1330, SPED 2324, EDUC 2331, and EDUC 2332.
Full Course Description: May be repeated for credit when topics vary. 4334 Curriculum and Evaluation in the Secondary School 3 hours This course focuses on the development of curricular materials appropriate for the secondary school, linking content objectives, instructional materials, teaching strategies, appropriate computer applications, the Texas Essential Knowledge and Skills (TEKS) and lesson design. Students will create units of instruction based on the “Understanding by Design” model. In addition, students will learn to apply contemporary research-based ideas about curricular design to the classroom setting and will become adept at designing and analyzing a variety of assessment tools. A 30- clock-hour internship in an approved setting is a requirement for this course. Prerequisites: Senior standing, admission into the Teacher Education program, EDUC 1330, SPED 2324, EDUC 2331 and EDUC 2332. Corequisite: READ 4343. Fall. Spring. 4338 Curriculum and Evaluation in the Elementary School 3 hours This course focuses on elementary school curriculum by linking content objectives, lesson design, instructional materials, various teaching strategies, appropriate computer applications and the Texas Essential Knowledge and Skills (TEKS). Students will create thematic units of instruction that integrate all content areas. In addition, students will demonstrate competence in under-standing both criterion- and norm-referenced tests. The use of technology is included throughout, as students utilize the Internet to conduct research for units, participate in online discussions, and ultimately add their own research and units to a web page dedicated to the course. A 30- clock-hour internship in an approved setting is a requirement for this course. Prerequisites: Senior standing, admission into the Teacher Education program, EDUC 1330, SPED 2324, EDUC 2331, and EDUC 2332.

Other Requirements / Notes: Attendance at a summer mini-session, weekly evening seminars, and several daylong sessions throughout the semester will be required. Full Course Description: This seminar is designed to educate and support students enrolled in a student teaching internship in grades 4-8, grades 7-12, or grades EC-12. Attendance at a summer mini-session, weekly evening seminars, and several daylong sessions throughout the semester will be required. Application for admission is required with approval of the Teacher Education Advisory Committee. Corequisite: EDUC 4361. Fall. Spring.

Full Course Description: This field-based course provides the student with the opportunity to develop and demonstrate diagnostic, organizational, group-processing, and program-planning skills in an educational, community, or university setting. Prospective students must procure the internship handbook and contract from course instructor. Permission to enroll in the course will be granted upon subsequent approval of an internship contract by the instructor. Prerequisites: Junior standing, EDUC 1330, EDUC 2331, EDUC 2332, and SPED 2324. Pre- or corequisites: EDUC 4338 or EDUC 4334 and READ 4343. Fall. Spring. Summer.

Other Requirements / Notes: Required for all ENGL majors and serves as prerequisite for courses numbered 3000 and 4000. Satisfies the Exploring Artistic Works requirement and a Writing Rich Mission Marker.
Full Course Description: This course introduces students to basic critical and analytical methods, in particular the skills of careful, close reading. Students will strengthen precritical skills such as the ability to identify and analyze rhetorical and linguistic features of texts (metaphor, imagery, metonymy, etc.), perceive and discuss complexities of theme, and understand how generic elements (epic, lyric, narrative, etc.) function to create meaning. Students will be introduced to interpretive frames of reference (philosophy, psychology, history, etc., that are fundamental to advanced literary critical analysis. In writing essays, students will develop critical arguments that interpret literature. Texts for study will be drawn from world literature, including works in translation. Required for all ENGL majors and serves as prerequisite for courses numbered 3000 and 4000. Must be taken in residence. Satisfies the Exploring Artistic Works requirement and a Writing Rich Mission Marker.

Other Requirements / Notes: A survey of American literature and literary history from the colonial period to the Civil War, this course fosters an understanding of the canon of American literature as it is typically conceived and considers the complex circumstances that have shaped its construction. Satisfies the Exploring Artistic Works requirement.
Full Course Description: A survey of American literature and literary history from the colonial period to the Civil War, this course fosters an understanding of the canon of American literature as it is typically conceived and considers the complex circumstances that have shaped its construction. Topics addressed include the nature of American individualism; the conflict between liberty and equality in American social thought; the emergence of American exceptionalist ideology and a corresponding national mythology inscribed with notions about divinity, nature, self, and society; the dialectic of freedom and slavery in American rhetoric; the American focus on race; the ideology of domesticity; and the aesthetics of American romance. Satisfies the Exploring Artistic Works requirement.

Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies the Exploring Artistic Works requirement.
Full Course Description: A survey of American literature from the period following the Civil War to the present. Attention will be devoted to literary movements, regional writing, native and immigrant cultures, and the increasingly multiple perspectives characterizing modern American experience. The course will explore modernism, then consider subsequent literary and cultural developments such as the politics and aesthetics of representation, and the conflicted discourses of race, religion, environmentalism, gender and class. Satisfies the Exploring Artistic Works requirement.

Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies Exploring Artistic Works and Social Identities requirements.
Full Course Description: Students will read, analyze, discuss, and write about African American literature. Emphasis is placed on the 20th century. Genres include the novel, short story, poetry, and drama. Texts will be explored not only from the perspectives of race, but also in the larger context of American literary, artistic, and cultural themes and movements. Satisfies Exploring Artistic Works and Social Identities requirements.

Other Requirements / Notes: This course enriches understanding of how Chicanx writers’ narratives and counternarratives have shaped American literature and culture.
Full Course Description: This course enriches understanding of how Chicanx writers’ narratives and counternarratives have shaped American literature and culture. Students will be challenged to critically consider issues of identity while exploring poetry, fiction, and other works by Mexican American authors, spanning cultural traditions, contemporary questions, and the process of reimagining the future. Fulfills the Exploring Artistic Works and Social Identities mission markers.

Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies Exploring Artistic Works requirement.
Full Course Description: This course contextualizes the recent proliferation of LGBT representation in popular culture by tracing shifts over the past few decades. We will study works from a number of different genres including novels, plays, children’s literature, and graphic narratives as we explore how art both reflects and shapes marginalized communities. Satisfies Exploring Artistic Works requirement.

With an emphasis on works by Latin American authors published after the nineteenth century, spanning multiple genres and forms, this course explores the relationship between creative expression and sociopolitical experiences while considering Latin America’s post-colonial legacies, cultural contexts, and artistic communities. The texts of this course are taught in translation to English with the option for Spanish speakers to read the texts in Spanish. Fulfills the Exploring Artistic Works and Social Identities mission markers.

Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies Exploring Artistic Works requirement.
Full Course Description: This course explores the relationship between film and literature, including the adaptation of literary works to the screen. In addition, the course brings literary studies’ emphasis on close textual reading to the study of film. The course teaches vocabulary for analyzing film and introduces relevant critical approaches. Satisfies Exploring Artistic Works requirement.

Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies the Exploring Artistic Works requirement.
Full Course Description: This course offers a survey of literary cultures and texts in Britain. Material will extend from Beowulf through the eighteenth century, sampling the huge varieties of authorship, genre, and poetic technique within that span. Discussion of texts will also note changes in language (from Anglo-Saxon to Modern English), shifting religious and national identities, the integration of printing technologies, the role of women in culture, the function of monarchy, and the rise of the middle class. Satisfies the Exploring Artistic Works requirement.

Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies the Exploring Artistic Works requirement.
Full Course Description: This course continues the first survey of British authors, beginning with the major writers of the Romantic period and ending with modern poetry and short fiction. Special attention will be given to the Romantic poets, the development of the novel, Victorian poetry and prose, and the Modernist movement. Satisfies the Exploring Artistic Works requirement.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be taken for upper-level credit (ENGL 3339) with instructor permission. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Satisfies the Exploring Artistic Works requirement and a Writing Rich Mission Marker.
Full Course Description: This is an introductory-level course organized around a theme or topic, intended to expose students to a variety of literary texts and provide entry-level skills in close reading and textual analysis. Emphasis will be placed on surveying a diversity of genres and perspectives, as well introducing students to analytical writing and interpretive claims. May be taken for upper-level credit (ENGL 3339) with instructor permission. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Satisfies the Exploring Artistic Works requirement and a Writing Rich Mission Marker.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This is an introductory-level course organized around a theme or topic, intended to expose students to a variety of literary texts and provide entry-level skills in textual close reading and analysis. Emphasis will be placed on surveying a diversity of genres and perspectives, as well introducing students to literary interpretation. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

This course will examine major literary texts and their cultural contexts between 800 and 1500 C.E. Special attention will be paid to the origin of literary traditions and genres as they impacted British and/ or European cultures. The focus may be thematic (such as Arthurian Romance, or Medieval Spirituality) or devoted to a major author (such as Chaucer or Dante). Prerequisite: ENGL 2300 or approved transfer course.

The English Renaissance, sometimes called the Early Modern Period, extends roughly from the reign of Henry VIII at the beginning of the 16th century through the writing of Paradise Lost in the middle of the 17th century. The course will examine how the literature of this era became foundational for so much of the English literary tradition that followed. Because of the incredible variety of innovation during these years, this course may focus on one particular genre (such as Renaissance drama), notable writer (such as John Milton), or theme (such as political upheaval). Prerequisite: ENGL 2300 or approved transfer course.

Prerequisites: ENGL 2300 or approved transfer course. Satisfies Exploring Artistic Works requirement.
Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies Exploring Artistic Works requirement.
Full Course Description: British Romanticism covers the literature from 1780-1832. During this period British writers responded to the French Revolution and the Industrial Revolution with a revolution in poetic language and focus. Canonical male writers like William Wordsworth, William Blake, S.T. Coleridge, John Keats, Lord Byron and Percy Bysshe Shelley published in the same literary marketplace as women and working class writers, such as Charlotte Smith, Anna Laetitia Barbauld, Mary Robinson, John Clare, Robert Burns, and others. Studying the texts of these writers together helps us see how varied life in Britain during this period was. We will study gender and genre, debates over the slave trade and the rights of women, literature’s turn toward autobiography and its engagement with radical politics and the Napoleonic wars. Prerequisite: ENGL 2300 or approved transfer course. Satisfies Exploring Artistic Works requirement.

Novels appear in America as far back as the colonial period. Important early novelists include Susanna Rowson and Charles Brockden Brown; the later nineteenth century sees works by Nathaniel Hawthorne, Herman Melville, Harriet Beecher Stowe, and Henry James become famous around the world for their philosophical depth, and their implicit international dialogue concerning the identity and destiny of the United States. Along with the dialogue between romance and realism, and with reactions to both—such as naturalism—the course will address how the American novel treats 19th century cultural developments in science, economics, education, and female emancipation. Prerequisite: ENGL 2300 or approved transfer course.

This course explores the development of the novel and short story during an age of turbulent social, political, and intellectual change. Along with the realist novel, the major genre of the 19th century, texts may include the industrial novel, the sensation novel, horror and science fiction, and the detective story along with fiction featuring “the New Woman,” aestheticism, and decadence. Prerequisite: ENGL 2300 or approved transfer course.

This course covers the period from the Restoration of the English monarchy in 1660 up to the beginning of the French Revolution in 1789. England, and, after 1707, Great Britain, became a modern nation during this period. Its system of government became more firmly established as a constitutional monarchy, its strength in Europe and around the world advanced dramatically, and its literature moved from being a delicacy for the elite to mass entertainment. London’s growth as a commercial city, the rise of a literate middle class, and the expansion of printing all made this possible. This period saw the development of the professional writer and the decline of noble and royal patronage as the main support for the arts. Prerequisite: ENGL 2300 or approved transfer course.

Covers selected 19th-century poetry and prose by writers living in this age of profound cultural and social change. Poets may include Alfred, Lord Tennyson, Elizabeth Barrett Browning, Robert Browning, and Christina Rossetti. Prose writers may include John Stuart Mill, Charles Darwin, Matthew Arnold, and Henry Mayhew. The class will emphasize poetic technique and genre—the Victorians excelled in lyric poetry, long narrative poems, and their chief accomplishment, the dramatic monologue—and different perspectives on social and cultural debates, including debates about the nature and purpose of art. Prerequisite: ENGL 2300 or approved transfer course.

Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies the Exploring Artistic Works and Social Identities requirements.
Full Course Description: This course focuses on the rich diversity of women’s voices in literature, emphasizing works by authors from various racial, socioeconomic, sexual, and cultural identities, with an emphasis on intersectional identity. Students will read, discuss, and write about literature across genres, including novels, short stories, essays, and poetry. In addition to exploring the contributions of women writers to feminist thought and the American literary canon, the course also situates their work within relevant historical and social contexts. Satisfies the Exploring Artistic Works and Social Identities requirements.

The course examines a selection of Shakespeare’s plays, weighted toward his later output. Emphasis will be placed on the conventions and practices of Renaissance theater, the Elizabethan and Jacobean political landscape, and the interpretive dilemmas faced by modern readers and audiences. Prerequisite: ENGL 2300 or approved transfer course.

The course examines a selection of Shakespeare’s plays, weighted toward his earlier output. Emphasis will be placed on the conventions and practices of Renaissance theater, the Elizabethan and Jacobean political landscape, and the interpretive dilemmas faced by modern readers and audiences. Prerequisite: ENGL 2300 or approved transfer course.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: An examination of major dramatists of America and Europe and of the theoretical, political and social forces shaping their work. Special attention will be paid to modern and postmodern theory and the aesthetic movements — minimalism, theater of the absurd, “new theater,” etc. Emphasis may be historical, theoretical, thematic or critical. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: ENGL 2300 or approved transfer course.

Prerequisites: ENGL 2300 or approved transfer course. Satisfies Exploring Artistic Works requirement.
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Satisfies Exploring Artistic Works requirement.
Full Course Description: From time to time courses not covered by the above descriptions will be offered. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: ENGL 2300 or approved transfer course. Satisfies Exploring Artistic Works requirement.

Prerequisites: ENGL 2300 or approved transfer course. 4311 Regional Writers Ever since Hector Guillaume St. John de Crèvecœur and other writers 3 hours of the Early Republic imagined that American identity might be shaped by geographical region, readers have studied American literary works and the creative process in connection with local influences such as community, the natural environment, and region-specific historical, political, or social conditions. This course might focus on works by writers from the American South, the American West, the Southwest, the Midwest, big cities such as New York or Chicago, or other defined spaces. It will often challenge traditional views of these regions and the writers that come from them. For example, the traditional view of literature of the Midwest is undermined by consideration of midwestern Native American and African-American writers’ perspectives on these regions. Authors, genres, regions, historical periods, themes, and critical approaches will vary in different years. Satisfies the Social Identities Mission Marker.; ENGL 2300 or approved transfer course.
Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies the Social Identities Mission Marker.
Full Course Description: This course will survey the great diversity of forms and voices constituting American poetry from the turn of the previous century up to about 1980, beginning with a brief glance back to the daring innovations of Walt Whitman and Emily Dickinson. Particular attention will be paid to movements that gave voice to previously voiceless sectors of American society, and to poets who have challenged complacent social and political mores. Prerequisite: ENGL 2300 or approved transfer course. 4311 Regional Writers Ever since Hector Guillaume St. John de Crèvecœur and other writers 3 hours of the Early Republic imagined that American identity might be shaped by geographical region, readers have studied American literary works and the creative process in connection with local influences such as community, the natural environment, and region-specific historical, political, or social conditions. This course might focus on works by writers from the American South, the American West, the Southwest, the Midwest, big cities such as New York or Chicago, or other defined spaces. It will often challenge traditional views of these regions and the writers that come from them. For example, the traditional view of literature of the Midwest is undermined by consideration of midwestern Native American and African-American writers’ perspectives on these regions. Authors, genres, regions, historical periods, themes, and critical approaches will vary in different years. Satisfies the Social Identities Mission Marker. Prerequisite: ENGL 2300 or approved transfer course.

Prerequisites: ENGL 2300 or approved transfer course. 4322 The Novel in Transition: The Turn of the Century to Early Modernism 3 hours Students will read, analyze, discuss, write about and conduct a modest amount of library research in connection with very short novels representing the turn of the century and the early modern period. Authors could include Virginia Woolf, Oscar Wilde, Bram Stoker, Kate Chopin, and Nella Larsen. Students should expect to learn much about this fascinating literary historical period, about the novel as a genre within this historical context, and about these authors in particular.; ENGL 2300 or approved transfer course.
Full Course Description: This course involves study of representative literary texts from both the modern and postmodern period, with much discussion devoted to defining the periods and differentiating modern (1910–1965) from postmodern (1965 and after) thought and works. Twentieth-century literature is characterized by revolution and radical experimentation, by both liberal and reactionary politics and, in general, by agonized conflicts characterizing a “post-Enlightenment” response to Eurocentric “master narratives.” Thus, there will be an emphasis on historical and cultural contexts of the literature studied. Reading assignments will include relevant secondary materials on modernism and postmodernism, as well as on selected authors’ works. Modernist authors considered might include James Joyce, Virginia Woolf, Edith Wharton, William Faulkner, Willa Cather, Richard Wright, Ernest Hemingway, Eugene O’Neill, Flannery O’Connor and Carson McCullers. Postmodern authors may include John Barth, Patrick White, Vladimir Nabokov, Thomas King, Margaret Drabble, Doris Lessing, John Fowles, Edward Albee and Toni Morrison. Prerequisite: ENGL 2300 or approved transfer course. 4322 The Novel in Transition: The Turn of the Century to Early Modernism 3 hours Students will read, analyze, discuss, write about and conduct a modest amount of library research in connection with very short novels representing the turn of the century and the early modern period. Authors could include Virginia Woolf, Oscar Wilde, Bram Stoker, Kate Chopin, and Nella Larsen. Students should expect to learn much about this fascinating literary historical period, about the novel as a genre within this historical context, and about these authors in particular. Prerequisite: ENGL 2300 or approved transfer course.

Examines the growth of the novel as a literary form from John Bunyan’s Pilgrim’s Progress and Aphra Behn’s Oroonoko through Daniel Defoe’s novels and those of Samuel Richardson, Henry Fielding, Laurence Sterne, Tobias Smollett and Frances Burney. The course covers canonical works and lesser-known ones, such as Charlotte Lennox’s Female Quixote and Eliza Haywood’s The Adventures of Miss Betsy Thoughtless, in an effort to show how the novel emerged into the dominant literary form by the end of the 18th century. Prerequisite: ENGL 2300 or approved transfer course.

An introduction to historical and contemporary critical theories and practices, with some time devoted to how these ideas are applied to literary texts. Prerequisite: ENGL 2300 or approved transfer course.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for elective credit. Satisfies Culminating Experience for English Literature majors. Open to junior and senior non-majors. Prerequisites: ENGL 4341 and senior standing.
Full Course Description: In this course, senior Literature majors will engage in independent, in- depth analysis and research of a literary topic and present their findings through a formal oral presentation and either a single long term paper or a series of shorter, interrelated writing projects. Course content and readings are to be determined by individual instructors. May be repeated for elective credit. Satisfies Culminating Experience for English Literature majors. Open to junior and senior non-majors. Prerequisites: ENGL 4341 and senior standing.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: Entrepreneurship joins the theory of business creating with practical experience in the development of a new venture. Students will prepare and present a professional business plan to deter-mine the feasibility of starting their own ventures. The course focuses on developing skills conducive to venture success, including developing a competitive advantage, creating an identity, marketing research, promotion and financing requirements. Selected experts will provide advice and insight on business plan development. Fall.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: This course will study the numerous forms of social enterprise that are appearing on the global business landscape today. A social enterprise is an organization that applies commercial strategies to maximize improvements in human and environmental well-being, rather than maximizing profits for external shareholders. This course uses journal articles and organization reports to study the forms social enterprise is taking in the U.S., such as the Benefit Corporation and social purpose corporation, and in other countries such as China, and the U.K. It will analyze how these organizations apply business principles to achieve their goals, access socially conscious investments, employ social media to communicate their messages, and use global certifications and third- party assessors, such as GRI and KPMG to evaluate their performance. It will include analysis of local organizations that are social enterprises.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: Entrepreneurial Finance examines the process of financing a business venture and its growth. In this course students will be exposed to aspects of finance and accounting methods and practices that are relevant for the entrepreneur. Students will learn through the practical exercises focused on entrepreneurial ventures. The major focus of the course will be on the startup phase and the initial phases of development. The student will study how to develop and control a financing plan that matches the growth of the entrepreneurial venture. Topics covered will include sources of financing, including angel financing; venture capital; legal implications of financing; valuation; and taxes. Cases, presentations and guest speakers will be used throughout the course to emphasize entrepreneurial business practices. Prerequisites: ENTR 2301 and ACCT 2301.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: The Entrepreneurial and Design Thinking course exposes students to methods and practices used by entrepreneurs to identify needs, develop proposed solutions, and utilize design methodology to be able to develop successful business models. This course examines various ways of determining the needs of the customer and transforming that information into design criteria, product specifications and product prototypes. The course introduces students to innovation processes that will be based on approaches ranging from those used to create new social ventures in developing economies to entrepreneurial practices in technology markets. Prerequisite: ENTR 2301. Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: This is a course that is focused on the management of an entrepreneurial business, covering all the aspects of the organization and the operation of a business, with an emphasis on processes and structures for the efficient and effective use of resources that generate value, growth and innovation for start-up business and existing small businesses. Entrepreneurial management encompasses all the unique global, ethical, technological and ecological factors impacting new venture creation that sustain and challenge a range of businesses, including for-profit enterprises and social entrepreneurship entities. This course can be selected as an elective by Entrepreneurship majors and other business majors. Prerequisite: ENTR 2301. Fall.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with BDMM 3332.
Full Course Description: In this course students learn powerful frameworks and methodologies such as Lean Startup and traditional business plan development that are useful for planning and launching entrepreneurial ventures. Students will use real-world tools and approaches in conjunction with a semester- long project that entails each student teams researching and developing a business plan and investor presentation for a different startup business concept. Cross-listed with BDMM 3332. Prerequisite: ENTR 2301. Fall. Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Prerequisites: ENTR 2301. 4382 New Product/Service Design and Development 3 hours This course examines various ways of determining the needs of the customer and transforming that information into design criteria, product specifications and product prototypes. This is a project-oriented course focused on understanding and implementing the interaction among design, engineering, manufacturing and marketing. Through this multifunctional approach, the student will research a new product concept, implement the product design and initiate a development process through the prototype stage. The course includes a final product concept presentation.; ENTR 2301 and MGMT 3340.
Semester Offered: offered to students to better prepare them for their individual entrepreneurial ventures.; Spring.
