Proposed topics include international entrepreneurship, topics pertaining to the Arts, small and family business management, social enterprises, and new venture funding. Prerequisite: ENTR 2301. 4382 New Product/Service Design and Development 3 hours This course examines various ways of determining the needs of the customer and transforming that information into design criteria, product specifications and product prototypes. This is a project-oriented course focused on understanding and implementing the interaction among design, engineering, manufacturing and marketing. Through this multifunctional approach, the student will research a new product concept, implement the product design and initiate a development process through the prototype stage. The course includes a final product concept presentation. Prerequisites: ENTR 2301 and MGMT 3340. Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: Technology Entrepreneurship provides a core body of entrepreneurial knowledge and training focusing on technology-based ventures. The course is designed to guide students in the development of their technology-based ventures as they move through the various stages of development. Prerequisite: ENTR 2301. Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: The Entrepreneurial Practicum provides students with the experiential opportunity to create and participate in a start-up venture while at St. Edward’s University. Students will participate in venture creation, business plan development, venture formation and management. Upon completion of the venture experience, they will report on results and lessons learned. Prerequisite: ENTR 2301. Fall.

Other Requirements / Notes: Students will apply their knowledge of these concepts through experiential learning activities that will enable better understanding of the theory of sustainability as well as its practical implications.
Full Course Description: This course provides an interdisciplinary introduction to environmental science and policy by examining the environmental, economic and social dimensions of sustainability. Students will learn the sociopolitical concepts necessary to understand the interrelationships between human and natural systems and then use these tools to analyze alternative responses to pressing environ-mental issues such as biodiversity conservation, sustainable development and human population growth. Students will apply their knowledge of these concepts through experiential learning activities that will enable better understanding of the theory of sustainability as well as its practical implications. This course will encourage students to identify the multidisciplinary nature of environmental issues as well as prepare them for the upper-division specialized courses in the major. Fall. Spring.

The content of this class not only covers the usual topics, including plate tectonics, rocks and minerals, soils, and landforms, but also includes related environmental issues and their human impact. Examples are drawn from around the world, with an emphasis on the interconnectedness of earth systems and their effects on how different cultures interact with their environments. Spring. 2310 Climate Change Science, Impacts, and Responses 3 hours In this course, we examine the scientific evidence for anthropogenic climate change, the impacts on human society and natural ecosystems, and the management and policy responses. During the first segment, students learn how the climate system works, what factors cause climate to change, how climate has changed in the past, and how scientists use models, observations, and theory to study past climates and make predictions about the future. Next, we explore the impacts of climate change on terrestrial and marine ecosystems, agriculture, and human society. During the final unit, we study responses to climate change, including major policies and management approaches. The course includes several hands-on lab exercises and field activities where students collect, analyze, and evaluate climate change data.

Other Requirements / Notes: In the first section of the course, we will examine Costa Rica’s economic, political and cultural history and institutions to develop an understanding of how the institutional context influences their policy choices.
Full Course Description: Costa Rica has long been unique among its Central American neighbors for its stable democracy and strong economic growth and has become globally renowned for its national policy of sustainable development. In this course we will examine how Costa Rican governments have taken this largely theoretical concept and attempted to implement it in practice, thereby serving as a model for other nations. In the first section of the course, we will examine Costa Rica’s economic, political and cultural history and institutions to develop an understanding of how the institutional context influences their policy choices. In the second section, we will examine specific policies the nation has implemented to achieve sustainable development in practice, such as payments for ecosystem services, environmental education and ecotourism, and assess how successful they have been at meeting the multiple sustainable development goals of economic development, environmental conservation and social equity. In so doing, we will examine how Costa Rica’s experience can serve as a model for other nations.

In this course, we will explore the ecoregions of Texas and the organisms, processes, and conditions that characterize them. Students will learn about how climate, soils, geologic formations, biotic interactions, and disturbances have shaped the ecosystems of Texas, using key concepts and theories from biogeography and ecology. We will spend time in the field practicing using dichotomous keys and learning to identify common plant and animal species. Lastly, we will discuss and explore the life history strategies and other adaptations that Texas organisms have evolved that allow them to survive and thrive in the various ecoregions where they are found. This class involves substantial time outdoors exploring ecosystems around the St. Edward’s campus, along with several optional field trips to sites further afield. Students can expect to come away from this class with practical skills and knowledge that can be applied to jobs in environmental management and conservation, both in Texas and beyond 2318 Communities and Wildlife Conservation in Africa 3 hours Africa’s wildlife resources are globally renowned yet increasingly under threat. In this class, we will examine how Africa’s pressing environmental and social challenges, including climate change, deforestation, economic development, population growth, poverty and political instability frequently conflict with efforts to conserve wildlife. We will examine different strategies that attempt to address the opportunities and constraints involved in conserving African wildlife resources while improving local living conditions, including national parks and protected areas, ecotourism, economic incentives, consumptive wildlife utilization and community-based conservation. 2120 Career Paths in Environmental Science and Policy 1 hour This course examines characteristics of environmental science and policy as a discipline and profession. Course topics include post- baccalaureate degree options (masters and doctoral) and current trends in environmental science and policy as they relate to professional and career opportunities. Students will explore the diversity of potential career options by interacting with experts who are currently employed in a variety of occupations in the field. This course will also provide information and skills that will assist students with selecting and pursuing an appropriate career that reflects their interests and talents.

This course includes in-depth study of the physical, biological and human environment. Emphasis is on the multitude of interrelated factors that affect the life forms on our planet and the evolutionary processes that are responsible for their abundance and distribution. Contemporary issues related to changes in our ecosystems are analyzed from a scientific perspective throughout the course. May satisfy the Science in Depth requirement for non-ENSP majors. Fall. Spring.

At any given point on earth, atmospheric conditions of temperature, air pressure, winds and precipitation vary by time and season, but they are related to the global energy transfer system driven by the sun. This course will help you understand what is going on outside your window right now while putting it into the context of the global system. The course also will help you under-stand the difference between weather and climate. Topics covered include tornados, hurricanes, fronts, weather maps, the jet stream, El Niño, the monsoon, water budgets, clouds, the limits of forecasting, climate classification systems, climate change and the influence of climate on people and the distribution of plants and animals.

Other Requirements / Notes: Life- cycle analysis, including the concepts of energy, environmental and resource cost of chemicals and fate in environment, will be studied as a method of understanding the risks and benefits of natural and synthetic chemicals in the environment.
Full Course Description: An overview of environmental chemistry. Course topics include chemistry in air, water and soil environments, environmental pollutants in each medium with study of effects and methods of treating. Life- cycle analysis, including the concepts of energy, environmental and resource cost of chemicals and fate in environment, will be studied as a method of understanding the risks and benefits of natural and synthetic chemicals in the environment. Course will cover the basics of key analytical techniques used in environmental area, GC, GC/MS and AA/ ICP. Prerequisites: CHEM 1340, CHEM 1140 and BIOL 1307. Spring.

Course Title: Topics in Environmental Science and Policy
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary. This class involves several required field trips.
Full Course Description: Specialized courses that explore different environmental topics related to current events, faculty research and student interests. Specific topics vary year to year. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. 3326 Natural Resources Conservation and Management 3 hours This course serves as an introduction to natural resource management and conservation with an emphasis on ecosystem management, which combines basic principles of conservation biology with practical aspects of human dimensions for a more holistic approach to environmental planning. Students will learn the technical scientific information necessary for good conservation planning, and also gain familiarity with the socioeconomic and institutional contexts within which natural resource management decisions are made. We will draw from local and regional examples of ecosystem management and conservation, visit public and private restoration and conservation projects, and meet with land managers around Texas from a variety of organizations and agencies. This class involves several required field trips.

Students will learn the technical scientific information necessary for good conservation planning, and also gain familiarity with the socioeconomic and institutional contexts within which natural resource management decisions are made. We will draw from local and regional examples of ecosystem management and conservation, visit public and private restoration and conservation projects, and meet with land managers around Texas from a variety of organizations and agencies. This class involves several required field trips.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary. This class involves several required field trips.

May be repeated for credit when topics vary. 3326 Natural Resources Conservation and Management 3 hours This course serves as an introduction to natural resource management and conservation with an emphasis on ecosystem management, which combines basic principles of conservation biology with practical aspects of human dimensions for a more holistic approach to environmental planning. Students will learn the technical scientific information necessary for good conservation planning, and also gain familiarity with the socioeconomic and institutional contexts within which natural resource management decisions are made. We will draw from local and regional examples of ecosystem management and conservation, visit public and private restoration and conservation projects, and meet with land managers around Texas from a variety of organizations and agencies. This class involves several required field trips.

In this course, we will explore a wide variety of environmental and ecological field methods used by researchers, academics, and professional land managers via academic article discussions and on-the-ground practice. Students will also gain experience with proposal writing, analyzing data, and delivering professional presentations. Topics of emphasis include research design, techniques of data collection, research ethics, interpretation, and critical reading of professional literature. Students are encouraged to take this class prior to ENSP 4352. 3336 Environmental Outreach for Diverse Audiences 3 hours This course will equip students majoring in Environmental Science and Policy with the essential skills to effectively teach complex environmental issues to diverse audiences. In today’s world, the ability to explain and teach others about environmental concerns is paramount. Climate change, air and water pollution, continued threats to endangered species and other current environmental issues have never been higher. Learning how to teach others through outreach activities is an effective skill all ENSP students will need. This course explores the many facets of environmental outreach, emphasizing the integration of scientific knowledge with informal education strategies. Prerequisite: ENSP 2324.

This course examines the major environmental issues in the greater Austin region, such as urban sprawl, endangered species protection, and air and water pollution. Austin provides a wonderful “natural laboratory” for environmental issues because it has a plethora of highly active community organizations that work on various issues and because it is home to both city and state agencies with environmental responsibilities. In order to include these resources in the course, leaders of local environmental organizations will be featured as guest speakers when we discuss the issue on which their organization works. The organizations themselves will be available to students in the course as resources for coursework. Staff from City of Austin and state of Texas agencies dealing with environmental issues will also be invited to speak and serve as resources for students.

Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with BIOL 3353.
Full Course Description: Geographic Information Systems (GIS) is a digital tool for integrating and analyzing spatial data with broad applications in environmental science and policy including urban and regional planning, wildlife habitat studies and environmental quality assessment. In this course, students will learn the basic concepts needed to use GIS for environmental applications. Concepts presented in lecture will be put into practice in lab exercises and real-world projects using ArcGIS software. We will cover data acquisition and preparation (including data from field-based experiences and publicly-available sources), raster analysis, and tools for manipulating, querying, analyzing, and visualizing spatial-based data. Students will be exposed to a variety of GIS tools, data formats, sources of data, and environmental issues they are likely to encounter to study or work in an environmental career. Cross-listed with BIOL 3353. 3399 Environmental Law This course is designed to introduce you to America’s attempts to 3 hours regulate the environment. The primary goal of the course is to provide participants with the tools to find the law, interpret the law and use it. After an initial introduction to the American legal system itself, the majority of the coursework will involve the reading and discussion of environmental legislation, regulations and cases at the U.S. federal level; focus will be made at the end of the course on state environmental law. Particular emphasis will be placed on the legislative model as a tool to address environmental problems. Fall.

Other Requirements / Notes: Junior standing, Fall.
Full Course Description: In the early 21st century, new demands worldwide for dealing with the risks of climate change, population growth, loss of biological diversity and terrorism will force governments everywhere to rethink policy strategies and find new ways to reconcile environmental and economic goals. In this course, we will explore these questions, paying particular attention to the unique characteristics of environmental issues, the proper role of government, appropriate environmental goals and the means to achieve them, as well as the prioritization between economic and environmental objectives. Recognizing the global impacts of environmental problems, we will contrast the similarities and differences between environmental concerns in the United States and other parts of the world. Junior standing, Fall.

Fall. 4157 Research Experience in Environmental Science and Policy 1 hour With faculty guidance, students select a research question and conduct an independent investigation. Students will learn about research methodology and how to search relevant literature and databases within their field of study. Research projects may be completed in one semester or students may take the course repeatedly in order to complete a longer investigation. Students are encouraged and facilitated in presentation of their findings at a campus, regional or national meeting. The course may be repeated with the same or different faculty mentor/s, although a maximum of three hours of ENSP credits may be earned. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor. Fall. Spring.

This course examines film as a narrative art. Students will explore introductory approaches to film analysis and visual storytelling while developing skills in critical viewing. Topics also include the history of cinema and the way movies engage with and contribute to cultural narratives.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This is an introductory-level course organized around a theme or topic, intended to immerse students in a variety of films and provide entry-level skills in film analysis. Emphasis will be placed on surveying a diversity of perspectives, as well introducing students to relevant theoretical claims and historical context. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

Course Title: Advanced Topics in Film and Narrative
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This is an upper-level course organized around a theme or topic, intended to expose students to a variety of films and provide advanced- level skills in film analysis. Emphasis will be placed on surveying a diversity of perspectives, as well introducing students to relevant theoretical claims and historical context. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: FILM 1301.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Prerequisites: Mathematics placement that would qualify a student to take MATH 1314 or higher.
Full Course Description: This course introduces students to the personal financial management skills needed to make ethical individual and business decisions. Topics covered include time value of money, budgeting, tax planning, consumer credit, spending decisions, insurance, investment selection, and retirement planning. Consideration is given to how individual’s finances are impacted by business and government practices. Sustainable investing practices are discussed, and emphasis is placed on individual due diligence for corporate social responsibility and human rights. Students develop quantitative business research skills and solve problems using financial applications and Excel. This course is intended to fulfill the Quantitative Reasoning requirement of the General Education curriculum. Prerequisite: Mathematics placement that would qualify a student to take MATH 1314 or higher.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Prerequisites: ACCT 2301 and ECON 2301 with a C or higher.
Pre- or Corequisites: ACCT 2303 with a C or higher and ECON 2302.
Full Course Description: This course is an introduction to the theory and practice of business finance, with emphasis on value-adding financial management decisions. Topics covered include time value of money, valuation of stocks and bonds, risk and return, ratio analysis, capital budgeting. Prerequisites: ACCT 2301 and ECON 2301 with a C or higher. Pre- or corequisite: ACCT 2303 with a C or higher and ECON 2302.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: A comprehensive examination of corporate finance theories and their application to corporate decisions in the areas of capital structure, working capital, dividends, corporate structuring, leasing, bankruptcy and reorganization, mergers and acquisitions. Prerequisite: FINC 3330. Corequisite: ACCT 3331. Fall. Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: Topical issues in finance. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: FINC 3330.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: Introduction to investment vehicles and operation of the security markets, investment analysis and portfolio management. Considers investment decisions in the context of risk-return analysis. Investment valuation is examined beginning with the economic outlook through analysis of specific securities. Prerequisite: FINC 3330. Fall. Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: Real Estate Finance will provide the student a foundation in the areas of property law, mortgage underwriting practices, mortgage insurance programs, financial analysis, valuation principles, federal income tax laws, investment analysis, real estate development and capital markets, focusing on commercial real estate. The course topics include an introduction to commercial real estate valuation methods used in commercial real estate investment and lending communities, and real estate negotiations. Prerequisite: FINC 3330. Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with IBUS 4346.
Full Course Description: This course serves as an introduction to the fundamentals of international financial management and the challenges and opportunities that corporations and investors face in the global economy. The nature of exchange rates and their impact on corporate decisions provide a foundation for discussing corporate strategy and the decision to invest abroad, foreign exchange market equilibrium conditions and forecasting exchange rates, managing exchange risk, multinational capital budgeting and cost of capital, and sources of financing. The nature and value of international trade and international financial flows are evaluated, and students are introduced to the less popular concept of balance of payments. Conceptual content include ethical issues encountered in the international arena, principles of business strategy, economics, political science, financial management, and derivative instruments. Cross-listed with IBUS 4346. Prerequisite: FINC 3330.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: Students will develop an understanding of money and capital markets; the flow of funds in the economy; the role of credit; the term structure of interest rates; risk management; regulation and deregulation; and financial crises.
Full Course Description: This course is an introduction to the financial system and will provide students with an overview of how financial markets and institutions operate. Students will develop an understanding of money and capital markets; the flow of funds in the economy; the role of credit; the term structure of interest rates; risk management; regulation and deregulation; and financial crises. The role of the Federal Reserve System and its impact on monetary policy will be discussed. Students will also be introduced to the management and regulation of institutions such as commercial/investment banks and investment funds. Prerequisite: FINC 3330. Fall. Summer.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: Application of financial decision making using case problems that include financial statement analysis, financial planning, capital budgeting, valuation, cost of capital, capital structure, working capital management, lease versus buying, and entrepreneurship. Incorporates computer spreadsheet models for efficient application of sensitivity and scenario analysis. Prerequisite: FINC 3330. Fall.

Students enroll in the topic specific to learning needs. CAMP This course provides College Assistance Migrant Program scholars with information to assist in the transition to college. Emphasis is placed on access to campus resources, supplementing their writing development skills and emphasizing the resources of the Career and Professional Development office. First Generation Students This seminar for first generation college students provides important opportunities to acclimate to college life, foster community, build traits for success, tackle barriers, and sharpen relevant academic skills. International Students The First Year Seminar for International students consists of learning modules designed to support the international student’s educational adjustment and multicultural experience, while emphasizing immigration regulations and expectations. Athletes Topper Pride is designed to enhance the quality of the student-athletes’ college experience and to support the student-athlete’s preparation for success in life. Pre-Health This course provides students with an opportunity to learn more about the variety of careers and tracks in the Health Professions, including but not limited to Medical, Dental, Veterinary, Physical Therapy, Pharmacy, Nursing, and more, by placing them in contact with the health professionals, programming, and academic support specific to the health professions.

This introductory course will survey the field of forensic science and examine its role in the search for truth and justice. The objective of the course is for students to examine and understand the many disciplines that make up forensic science and understand its role in the criminal justice system. Fall. Spring.

Full Course Description: Students will examine, learn, understand and employ the techniques of crime scene investigation. The objective is to teach the fundamentals of crime scene investigation. Students should be able to assess any crime scene and determine proper procedures to identify, document, collect and handle evidence. Prerequisites: FRSC 1319 and BIOL 1107/1307 or CHEM 1140/1340 (the science courses may be taken concurrently). Corequisite: FRSC 3120. Minors will need permission to register from the instructor. Fall.

Prerequisites: FRSC 3320 and FRSC 3120.; FRSC 3320 and FRSC 3120.
Full Course Description: Prerequisites: FRSC 3320 and FRSC 3120. Corequisite: FRSC 3321. Spring. 3321 Crime Scene Investigation II This course continues the examination of techniques of crime scene 3 hours investigation begun in FRSC 3320. The evidence collected at the scene of a crime can be used to determine the nature of the crime and to help identify the perpetrators. Detection and analysis of DNA evidence, fiber, hair, body fluids, fingerprints, drugs and toxic substances are fundamental to building a criminal case. Proper collection and handling and careful observation and interpretation of crime scene evidence are vital to criminal investigation and prosecution. Crime Scene Investigation I and II will school students in the fundamentals of crime scene investigations. Prerequisites: FRSC 3320 and FRSC 3120. Corequisite: FRSC 3121. Spring.

Crime scene photography is a key step in documenting crime scenes. Students will utilize digital cameras, and review other sources of technology used to record crime scenes. The course is supported throughout by realistic practical exercises to practice a range of skills. Prerequisite: CRIJ 1310 or FRSC 1319. Fall.

Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies the Culminating Experience for Forensic Science majors.
Full Course Description: An upper-division course in which students hone and showcase key skills they have been developing throughout their St. Edward’s University career, including online and field research, written and oral communication, and critical thinking and moral reasoning. The course is grounded in the university mission and seeks to prepare students to enter into the field of forensic science. Students complete projects, assignments, and essays reflecting the goals of the forensic science discipline as well as preparing a path for moving forward post- graduation. This course is writing intensive. Satisfies the Culminating Experience for Forensic Science majors. Prerequisites: FRSC 3321, FRSC 3121, and 80+ hours. Spring. Summer on sufficient demand.
Laboratory skills are an important component within the forensic science discipline. This course prepares students to use a number of laboratory instruments. Prerequisites: FRSC 3321 and FRSC 3121.

Crimes committed in nature pose a unique set of challenges for the investigators. This advanced course critically analyzes environmental factors affecting outdoor crime scenes. It also evaluates samples within forensic botany, soil recovery and analysis, and anthropology. Prerequisites: BIOL 2334, CHEM 2323, and CHEM 2123.

Course Title: Crime Scene in a Digital World
Full Course Description: As the world we live in becomes more technically advanced, the electronic items encountered at a crime scene become an increasingly valuable component to any criminal investigation. This course aims to align the crime scene processing procedures of ‘recognition’, ‘preservation’ and ‘collection’ with the data recovery analysis of the items found within the scenes. It will also expose students to the legal aspects as they relate to electronic/digital evidence. On completion of this course, students will be able to identify digital devices located in a crime scene, document electronic evidence, understand laws governing electronic evidence, and be able to perform basic analysis on various types of digital evidence.

Course Title: Principles and Practices of Science and Law
Full Course Description: Students will receive instruction on the principles, procedures and practices used in solving societal and legal issues. Students will examine the scientific method and apply scientific knowledge through expert testimony. Students will improve their critical thinking and reasoning skills in examining and debating different positions of current issues at the junction of science and law. Prerequisites: FRSC 3321 and FRSC 3121. Fall.
Forensic genetics with an emphasis on human molecular genetics, population genetics, and genetic application in the forensic sciences. Prerequisite: BIOL 2334. Fall.

This course is an introduction to forensic aspects of death investigations. The course will review the role of responding officers and investigators/ detectives. Death investigations include but are not limited to drowning, homicides and infant deaths such as SIDS. The various stages of death and its effects on the human body will also be covered. Prerequisites: FRSC 3320 and FRSC 3120 or CRIJ 1302. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: This course must be passed for the Bachelor of Science degree with a major in Forensic Science to be awarded.
Full Course Description: This course contains the final survey for students who have completed all Forensic Science coursework and are in their final semester. Enrolled students will complete a major field test, and internal surveys regarding the Forensic Science program. This course is graded as pass/fail with no bearing on the GPA. This course must be passed for the Bachelor of Science degree with a major in Forensic Science to be awarded.

Course Title: Introduction to the Culture of France
Full Course Description: Taught in English. Introduction to the contemporary culture of France and the historical development of cultural norms, customs and popular and fine arts of the French people. Topics addressed include geography of France and Europe, contemporary values, and religious, social and political structures. Examples of French literature in translation, music, film, video and other media will supplement traditional textbooks as source material for assignments and class discussion.

A beginning course stressing communication skills, acquisition of basic vocabulary and grammar. Extensive online work and study of the material prior to class is an integral part of the course. Students must take a placement test unless they have never had French before. French I and II fulfill a graduation requirement but can also be taken on their own merits for non-language majors and those with non-professional interests, for purposes of tourism, business, cultural exchange and pleasure. During the first year students will learn basic conversational patterns, idiomatic vocabulary, basic grammar and syntax, and general cultural patterns.

A continuation of the development of fundamental skills in French with increased emphasis on functional language skills, cultural literacy, and an awareness of French language and culture outside of France. Vocabulary for travel and survival in the Francophone world will be emphasized and will benefit people with a continued interest in the language (minor) as well as other students. Prerequisite: FREN 1311 or placement test.

A short review of all basic grammatical concepts and all conversational functional skills that were taught at the introductory level. More focus on details in all four skills (reading, writing, speaking and listening). Course will be based on adapted and authentic texts, excerpts from online videos, and films. Traditional textbooks will be intertwined with online sources and lessons from TV5 Monde. Prerequisite: FREN 1312 or placement test.

Focus on vocabulary acquisition and fine-tuning of basic French grammar. Oral expression and essay-writing assignments will be based on reading and viewing of a variety of target language texts from different genres: video clips, films and listening to song lyrics. As in French I through III the focus will be on functional and contemporary use of the language. Prerequisite: FREN 2311 or placement test.

Prerequisites: FREN 2311 with a grade of C or better, appropriate score on the placement test or permission of instructor. Pre- or corequisite for French minors: FREN 2312.; FREN 2326 with a grade of C or better or permission of instructor.

Prerequisite: FREN 2326 with a grade of C or better or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Designed to help students improve fluency and pronunciation and develop vocabulary needed for general purposes. Classes will require preparation for informal conversation with other students and the instructor. Textbooks, video and audio materials, and interviews with native speakers will provide source materials. Prerequisite: FREN 2311 with a grade of C or better, appropriate score on the placement test or permission of instructor. Pre- or corequisite for French minors: FREN 2312. Fall. 3331 Topics in French and Francophone Literature and Film 3 hours This course will examine literature or film of a specific period, region, genre, or theme and may be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: FREN 2326 with a grade of C or better or permission of instructor.

Prerequisites: FREN 2326 with a grade of C or better or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: FREN 2326 with a grade of C or better or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course will provide a survey of French history from the development of the medieval monarchy through the rise of the Fifth Republic in 1959. Students will also learn about demographic forces, artistic developments, cultural changes and religious movements, all of which contributed to the development of France from medieval to modern times. Prerequisite: FREN 2326 with a grade of C or better or permission of instructor.

Full Course Description: Students will study the formal language of business activities in France, with an introduction to the structure and function of various aspects of French economic life. This course is useful for students in preparing for the proficiency examinations of the Chambre de Commerce de Paris. Additional reading: AU CONTRAIRE: Figuring out the French (reading in English to support the understanding of French business culture). Prerequisite: FREN 2326 with a grade of C or better or permission of instructor. 3337 Topics in French and Francophone Cultural Studies 3 hours This course will examine contemporary cultural issues in the Francophone world and may be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: FREN 2326 with a grade of C or better or permission of instructor.

Prerequisites: FREN 2326 or permission of instructor. 4335 Advanced French Composition and Conversation 3 hours This class will fine-tune students’ knowledge of the structures of French. Students will write essays on a variety of topics and discuss issues in contemporary French culture, literature or any other subject that is conducive to a lively classroom discussion. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Other Requirements / Notes: This course will examine French as a conceptual, symbolic system and is intended for students who wish to deepen their understanding of French and languages in general. Full Course Description: Students can study or work abroad in a Francophone country and use the time to do independent research that will be supervised and graded by a St. Edward’s University faculty member with the relevant qualifications. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

In this seminar students form a community of learners and actively engage in academic and co-curricular exploration. As they do so, they will develop critical thinking skills necessary to become successful students and lifelong learners by meaningfully confronting questions of social justice through the course materials and co-curricular experiences.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: Examination of the geographical development of regional variations in culture. Topics include the distribution of races, languages and religions. Fall. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: Historical background and geographic analysis of the various regions of the world. Emphasis on the encounter between the modernizing West and the traditional societies of the non-Western world. Fall. Spring.

Course Title: Introduction to the Culture of Germany
Full Course Description: Taught in English or German. Alternating topics: German history, philosophy and culture until WWII, and contemporary German society. Topics include cultural facts and values and religious, social and political structures. Materials will consist of a mix of traditional textbooks and literary sources, as well as contemporary multimedia sources, film and music.

Other Requirements / Notes: Extensive reading and preparation of materials prior to class is required.
Full Course Description: A beginning course stressing comprehension, speaking, reading and writing and acquisition of basic vocabulary. Extensive reading and preparation of materials prior to class is required. The only students who can register for this class are those that were placed at this level. Students must take the placement test unless they have never had German before. Fall.

Other Requirements / Notes: Extensive reading and preparation of materials prior to class is required.
Full Course Description: A continuation of the development of fundamental skills stressed in German I and further development of vocabulary and grammatical structures needed for basic competence. Extensive reading and preparation of materials prior to class is required. Prerequisite: GERM 1311 or placement test. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: Focus on the acquisition of cultural literacy and the understanding of contemporary issues in Germany through a variety of media sources.
Full Course Description: The semester course begins with a short review of basic grammatical patterns taught at the introductory level. Continued development of vocabulary, listening comprehension, reading and writing skills. Reading and writing assignments reinforce students’ command of more- complex grammatical structures and provide topics for oral discussion. Focus on the acquisition of cultural literacy and the understanding of contemporary issues in Germany through a variety of media sources. Prerequisite: GERM 1312 or placement test. Fall.

Reading and writing assignments stress development of vocabulary and increased flexibility in expression. Oral communication skills receive extensive practice in discussion of reading assignments on a variety of topics in German culture and contemporary life. Extensive listening outside of class is an integral part of this course. Prerequisite: GERM 2311 or placement test. Spring.

Prerequisites: GERM 2311 or permission of instructor. 3331 Culture and Civilization of Modern Germany3 hours The traditions, values, institutions and arts of modern Germany are examined with a view toward understanding contemporary cultural patterns and their historical antecedents. This course focuses especially on the radical changes in German society through the 20th century. Class conducted in German. For upper-division credit in German, students read parallel assignments in German textbooks and write at least one paper in German.; Minimum of one year of German or permission of instructor. For German credit: GERM 2311, placement test or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: GERM 2311 or permission of instructor. 3331 Culture and Civilization of Modern Germany3 hours The traditions, values, institutions and arts of modern Germany are examined with a view toward understanding contemporary cultural patterns and their historical antecedents. Prerequisite: Minimum of one year of German or permission of instructor. For German credit: GERM 2311, placement test or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course is an introduction to the discipline of German Studies through the exploration of culture, literature and film. It builds on the linguistic foundations established in the first three semesters and allows students to continue to work on their spoken and written German by studying primary documents, historical events, and political change in the German-speaking world. Courses are conducted in German. Vocabulary development, standard pronunciation and grammar are stressed. Sample topics include an examination of the works of major novelists, short story, turn-of-the-century literature in translation and film as literature. Class conducted in English or German. Full Course Description: A high-intermediate level course that develops an understanding of German business culture as well as the lexicon utilized in different areas of business, such as accounting, banking, credit, management and marketing. No previous knowledge of business concepts is required; non-Business majors are welcome. Course will be multimedia focused and will rely heavily on German business news from Deutsche Welle TV and the program Made in Germany. Requires a minimum of intermediate proficiency in speaking, listening, reading and writing. Some background in business courses is helpful. Full Course Description: This course is for advanced students, ideally those who have already completed a minor in German or who have at least passed the Zertifikat Deutsch examination offered by the Goethe Institute (administered in Houston or at St. Edward’s). The internship gives students an opportunity to put language skills into use in a specific professional setting, in Germany or in the United States. Students will continue to develop specialized communication skills and will be supervised by a member of the German faculty. They may tutor or teach basic German language skills in schools or private businesses that need individuals who can communicate in written or oral German. Specific objectives will be developed for each intern, taking into account the needs and goals of that student. Internships in Germany, especially in the city of Koblenz, may be arranged for summer and academic-year sessions. Students must preregister for internship courses. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

Other Requirements / Notes: Through analysis and practice, students will gain an understanding of how various modes of information delivery and methods of interaction affect user experiences.
Full Course Description: A laboratory course that builds awareness of designed experiences across the natural, built, and digital worlds. Through analysis and practice, students will gain an understanding of how various modes of information delivery and methods of interaction affect user experiences. Coursework consists of a fast succession of hands-on experiments and application of fundamental user experience principles. Fall.

Introduction to letter forms as they relate to visual/verbal communication. Fundamental typographic principles, theory and history are studied, discussed and demonstrated in relation to course projects. Emphasis on the vocabulary of typographic form and its relationship to message/purpose. Coursework employs handwork and computer technology. Fall. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: An introductory studio course for graphic design with an emphasis on the understanding of “marks” and mark-making.
Full Course Description: An introductory studio course for graphic design with an emphasis on the understanding of “marks” and mark-making. Students will describe, generate and evaluate designed marks, icons, symbols, and signs from conceptual, personal, cultural, socio-political, visual, and technological points of view. Coursework consists of a series of projects requiring integration of design skills. Fall.

A laboratory course that builds fluency in visual language and style. Students will explore a variety of tools and methods for analyzing and producing original images. Coursework consists of a fast succession of hands-on experiments aimed at the invention of visual form and its subsequent application in graphic design. Prerequisites: GDES 1311 and GDES 1314. Fall. Spring.
Integrated studio. Students integrate skills and concepts from prior courses to solve a complex multi-phase design problem. Prerequisites: GDES 1314, GDES 2313 and GDES 2321. Spring.
A lecture and discussion course that critically investigates and explores graphic design history and theory. Prerequisite: ARTS 2304. Fall.
Advanced study of typography incorporating more nuanced and complex issues in both print and interactive environments. Prerequisites: GDES 1314 and GDES 2313. Fall.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: An advanced-level studio course focusing on the study of professional- level contemporary topics. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: GDES 2331. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course is designed for junior or senior students wishing to pursue an advanced topic within graphic design. The work may be applied or theoretical. Students will work individually with an instructor on a project contract basis. Students must file an approved learning contract for this course with their instructor prior to enrollment. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated once for credit.
Full Course Description: An opportunity for students to gain on-the-job experience with current technologies, methods and concepts related to the field while working under the supervision of a creative professional (designer, art director, creative director, etc.). May be repeated once for credit. Prerequisite: VISU 3335.

Full Course Description: This course offers an exploration of emerging and interdisciplinary topics in the field of health sciences and human health. Topics may include but are not limited to healthcare career education/training, public health issues, healthcare policy, digital health innovations, mental health, health disparities, and the social determinants of health. Through lectures, discussions, case studies, and practical applications, students will gain insights into the evolving landscape of health sciences. The course may include guest lectures from experts, opportunities for hands- on experiences, and collaborative projects. These topics will be general in nature and will not assume prior background. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

Full Course Description: This advanced-level course delves into complex topics in health sciences, designed for students with a solid foundation in the field. The course focuses on the integration of advanced theoretical knowledge and practical applications to address challenges in healthcare, public health, and health-related research. Topics may include but are not limited to patient care, health systems management, health policy analysis, clinical research methodologies, and the intersection of technology and healthcare. The course encourages students to critically evaluate current issues, design innovative solutions, and apply interdisciplinary approaches to real-world health problems. Students may also explore the ethical, legal, and societal implications of advances in health sciences, preparing them for leadership roles in healthcare settings, policy- making, and research. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisites: Completion of foundational courses in health sciences in areas such as biology, public health, epidemiology, or healthcare systems. Approval from the instructor may be required.

Prerequisites: Admission to the Health Systems and Administration program.; Completion of at least 30 credits in the Health Systems and Administration program, including all other core courses.
Pre- or Corequisites: WRIT 2302. 4350 Health Systems and Administration Internship/Practicum 3 hours Students will integrate and apply their learning from the Health Systems and Administration program in an approved professional setting, with at least 150 hours of experiential learning required to receive credit. Students will assume responsibility for one or more significant projects that require application of program learning outcomes and result in high-quality, tangible work products. Students will also complete a reflective paper that reports on their internship/practicum experience and describes their growth and learning throughout the program.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Admission to the Health Systems and Administration program. 4350 Health Systems and Administration Internship/Practicum 3 hours Students will integrate and apply their learning from the Health Systems and Administration program in an approved professional setting, with at least 150 hours of experiential learning required to receive credit.
Full Course Description: This course explores contemporary issues in health care from a lens of inclusion, equity, and social justice. Topics will vary depending on the expertise of the instructor and may include: health care systems in the United States and abroad; health disparities and socioeconomic determinants of health; health care law and policy; and political, social, religious, and ethical issues in health care. This course serves as an introduction to the Health Systems and Administration major, providing students an opportunity to reflect on their own values, careers, and potential contributions to the field of health care. Does not satisfy the General Education Ethics or Studies in Theology and Religion requirement. Prerequisite: Admission to the Health Systems and Administration program. Students will also complete a reflective paper that reports on their internship/practicum experience and describes their growth and learning throughout the program. Prerequisites: Completion of at least 30 credits in the Health Systems and Administration program, including all other core courses.

Begins with the confrontation of European explorers with Indians and continues to include the development of colonial institutions, the break with Great Britain, the making of the Constitution, new American institutions and changing national character, westward expansion, the division of the nation in the Civil War, and Reconstruction.

Begins with the industrialization and urbanization of the nation after the Civil War and continues to the present with attention to involvement in international wars and the Cold War with attendant responsibilities, movements for reform, depression and affluence, advances in technology, and momentous changes in values and lifestyles.

This course is designed to explore the history of Texas, especially the experiences of historically marginalized communities in the state. We will discuss Texas through all of the major historical periods to understand the main currents of the state’s history. We will pay particular attention to analysis of primary sources, and how Texas history is represented in various forms of public history and popular culture, including museums, monuments, and film.

This class is designed to introduce students to how historians think, argue, research and write. The course will engage in a shared research project which will vary by semester. Through that project the class will focus on specific issues of importance to the work of historians. Working with your classmates, you will learn how to design a research project, frame a key question of inquiry, locate primary and secondary sources, read them critically, reach an evidence-based interpretation of your own, and present solid work in the forms of proposals, annotated bibliographies, book and literature reviews, brief research papers, and oral presentation. 2304 Conquest to Resistance: History of Latin America 3 hours This course looks at the pre-Columbian world and pursues a selected study of Latin American societies through the eras of conquest and times of resistance. The course will demonstrate the variety of Latin American society and how it has changed through the centuries. Must be taken in residence. (World)

This course will examine the patterns of thought, systems of values, and social relations within Western Civilization, which have resulted in the formation of European culture. The course will search for characteristic modes of political and economic organization, for an intellectual tradition, and for an artistic heritage. Must be taken in residence. (Europe).

This course explores issues of power, race, ethnicity, gender, religion, sexuality, and class in U.S. society. It examines the context of conflicts over these issues, the efforts to achieve equality, and the lasting changes those efforts produced. Must be taken in residence.

This class explores episodes in imperialism and anti-imperialism from the 1880s through the 1960s. Students will read arguments for and against the “new imperialism” of the 1880s-1910s; study the various methods used to control territory and impose the colonial state; discuss the impact of imperialism and colonial rule on those subject to it; and investigate the roles of colonized peoples in the functioning and undermining of the colonial system. The course makes extensive use of primary documents to provide insight into views on and experiences of imperialism. Must be taken in residence. (World).

Western Civilization is a survey of the history and culture of Western Civilization from the Classical World through the Renaissance in Northern Europe (1500). The course will emphasize the problems of social organization, especially mutual obligations and responsibilities of individuals and states and evolving concepts of justice. Other concepts will include religious ideas and institutions and basic philosophical concepts. Western Civilization will include a writing component that emphasizes analytical and critical skills based upon a variety of historical sources. The class will prepare students for HIST 3320.

Major topics in world prehistory are treated as background for a survey of the early civilizations of India, China, Southeast Asia, Japan, Africa, the Mediterranean basin and the Americas. This course will examine the religious, political and cultural development of world civilizations and early globalization. Fall.

This course will survey the process of further globalization after 1500 and the evolution of modernism. Stress will be placed on the development of Western science and technology, political liberalism, industrialization, the rise of nationalism and imperialism, recent technological and communication revolutions, and lastly the diffusion of modernism globally with its attendant consequences.

The course will analyze how different cultures and societies have understood the natural world and how these understandings have changed over time.
Full Course Description: This course offers an introduction to the History of Science. It covers the development of understanding of and thinking about the natural world in the Western tradition as well as other cultures, starting in the Ancient world and continuing through the present age. The course will analyze how different cultures and societies have understood the natural world and how these understandings have changed over time. We will explore topics such as: What is the History of Science, science in the ancient and medieval world (including Greece, China, and the Arab world), the Scientific Revolution and development of the Scientific Method, the Enlightenment, Evolution, and Medicine among others. We will grapple with overarching themes such as changes in what are recognized as legitimate sources of knowledge about the natural world, the development of scientific institutions, and the ways in which factors such as race, class, gender, and religion impact the development of scientific ideas.

This course examines the social, political, and economic impacts of war and wartime throughout the 20th century. Through historical analysis, students will examine U.S. involvement in World War I, World War II, the Cold War and Vietnam, and the immediate post-Cold War era. In doing so, students will question how war has shaped governance, civil liberties, economic policies, and public opinion. This course will also examine the historical impact of war on sub-national debates over the meanings of freedom, justice, and national identity, as well as the lasting consequences of militarization and the growth of executive authority that occurred throughout the twentieth-century.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Junior standing.
Full Course Description: This course will give each student firsthand experience in community historical research. Using historical analysis, students will investigate a topic, give an oral report and complete a specific project. Teamwork, community outreach and insights into the personal nature of the historical process will all be benefits derived from this course. Prerequisite: Junior standing.

This course will examine the Crusades in their broader European and Mediterranean contexts. The class will explore the religious, social, political, economic and cultural history of the Crusades in Europe and the Eastern Mediterranean regions. We will seek to understand how the various communities involved – Latin Christians, Byzantines, Muslim and Jews – contributed to and were impacted by the crusading movement. Through a mixture of primary and secondary materials we explore the different perspectives of the groups involved and how the Crusades have been interpreted and continue to be remembered today. (Europe)

This course will examine the development of events and ideas of the French Revolutionary Era, from its origins in the Ancien Régime through the end of the Revolutionary era with Napoleon. In particular, the course will focus on the development of the idea of human rights, how it changed over the course of these years, and how it was applied. 3315 The American Revolution and the Early Republic 3 hours This course is designed to examine in depth the society, economy, culture and politics of the 13 colonies that became the United States from 1754 through 1812. There is particular emphasis on the Seven- Years War and how it shaped imperial policies, the creation of a colonial revolutionary ideology, the divergence of opinions among Americans concerning the wisdom of republican rule, and the culture of the early republic.

This course will examine the changing roles of women in European history. The class will consider the impact of religion, class, ethnicity, and race on women’s identities, experiences and opportunities. Through a mixture of primary and secondary sources we will explore how women have defined and shaped their own lives, those of their families and those of their communities. We will explore women’s participation in important political, economic, religious and cultural movements and events and how the position of women in European society evolved as a result. (Europe)

This course is designed as an in-depth examination of the society, economy, culture and politics of the United States from 1815 through 1856. There is particular emphasis on the evolution of American foreign policy from the War of 1812 to the Mexican-American War, the development of cotton plantation slavery and the growth of a distinct American racialism, and the economic and cultural transformation of the country due to the market revolution.

The industrial revolution was a complex series of changes that brought fundamental change not just in how things were made, but in the lives of people involved. This course will examine the multiple ways in which the changes in production that emerged in Europe in the 18th century impacted not only economies, but also politics, culture, and everyday life. (Europe) 3320 European History: Reformation Through

This course examines the history and culture of the West from the Reformation beginning in the 16th century through the 19th century. The course will cover such issues as the construction of political authority and its relationship to the developing ideas about political liberty, the emergence of scientific thinking, religious toleration, the nature and varieties of revolution, the development of market economics, and the beginnings of globalization and colonization. Students will analyze historical sources, incorporating them into a research paper.

This course examines the contact and connections between peoples living around the Atlantic Ocean between the 15th century and the early 19th century. It will examine the destruction and reconfiguration of indigenous societies, the creation of new labor markets, the voluntary and involuntary migration of peoples, the social, political and religious effects of exploration, con-quest and settlement, the economic and intellectual impact that the New World had upon the Old, the transmission of ideas and technologies between continents, the advent of colonial independence, and the efforts to end slavery and the international slave trade.

This course will explore the rich scholarship of the daily lives of people. We will examine various elements of the everyday life, including beliefs systems, family, work, and community. These elements of everyday life were influenced by powerful forces of tradition and change. Through reading the work of historians and analyzing the words and images of created by people at the time, we will seek to understand the changing contexts in which people lived their lives and how different circumstances could influence personal choices and outcomes. (Europe)

This course explores the causes, course, and consequences of the American Civil War, from the 1850s to 1877, including the crisis of union and disunion; slavery, race, and emancipation as a national problem, personal experience, and social process; the experience of the war for individuals and society; and the political and social challenges of Reconstruction.

This course examines the history of sexuality in the United States from the nineteenth century to today. Topics the course will study include changing sexual norms and practices; the control of sexuality; the politics of prostitution, abortion, and pornography; the social construction of sexual identities; the development of sexual communities and movements; and the intersections of sexuality with gender, ethnicity, race, class, and religion. Readings and sources will include both secondary scholarship and primary sources (such as diaries, letters, newspaper articles, government documents, political statements, and more). (US)

Students will demonstrate knowledge of global processes, including current global economics, global politics, and cultural issues, as they influence and are changing China in relation to the rest of the world. This course consists of looking at China from a global perspective. The problems with which China deals are linked to and reflect the global issues that the world faces. Students will analyze these issues in cultural context. (World)

Topics in social history are interwoven into traditional political and diplomatic chronology. Subjects include sports, movies, music, radio and television; the impact of scarcity and abundance; isolationism, war, Cold War and nuclear threat; expansion and contraction of American power; national fears and exploitation of them; struggles for equality and role change; transitions in manners and morals.

American attempts at adjustment to industrialization and urbanization from 1890 to 1920; reform efforts by Theodore Roosevelt, Taft, Wilson, Lafollette, Jane Addams, DuBois, the Muckrakers and others; social changes reflected in manners, morals, fashions, automobiles, motion pictures, the suffragettes, reforms for blacks, labor unrest; conflicts over imperialism; causes of involvement in World War I and difficulties in building for world peace; the continuation of the reform impulse.

This course will explore the causes and consequences of changes in women’s roles in American society from the late-19th century to the present. The class will examine the roles race, class, ethnicity, sexual orientation, religion, region, health and age play in shaping women’s identities and experiences. And it will study the ways women have defined and shaped their own lives, those of their families, and those of their local and national communities.

This course will deal with both political and social developments in Mexico at the time of Independence, the Post-Independence Wars, the Reform Period, the Porfiriato, and the Revolution of 1910 and will include the events and issues of a changing nation at the end of the 20th century.

This course is a comprehensive survey of the basic structure of Latin American history. There is a section on pre-Colombian culture and then a selected study of key Latin American nations that have made a lasting impact on the entire area. The course is designed to show the variety of Latin American society and the changing nature of life from pre- Colombian days to the present.

Course Title: The Prophet Muhammad and the Caliphate
Full Course Description: This course focuses on the history and politics of Islam from the foundation of the first Islamic community in Medina in 622 CE to the abolishment of the Ottoman-Sunni caliphate in 1924. The course will dedicate considerable attention to the formative era of Islam and cover the role of Muhammad as a prophet and a statesman, who united the disparate tribes of Arabia into a loosely held together Muslim umma (community), which briefly fragmented after Muhammad’s death in 632 CE. The course will then shift to the role of various caliphates, from the Rashidun caliphs to the Ottoman sultan-caliphs, under whose authority political Islam took shape and the identity of a “united” Muslim community was defined and redefined as Muslim rule and territory expanded and contracted over the centuries.

Course Title: History of Oil in the Modern Middle East
Full Course Description: This course examines how oil has shaped the social, political, and economic development of the modern Middle East. Beginning with the discovery of oil in the region in the early 20th century, this course traces the role of oil in colonial and postcolonial state formation, the rise of petro/rentier states, and the geopolitical struggles over energy resources. The course will explore the impact of oil wealth on domestic governance, economic structures, and social transformations, including labor migration, urbanization, and environmental impacts. 3362 History of Religious Identities, Politics, and Violence in the Modern Middle East 3 hours This course examines the historical evolution of sectarian politics and violence in the modern Middle East, tracing its roots from the late Ottoman Empire to the turn of the 21st century. We will analyze how colonial policies, state formation, and global power dynamics have shaped sectarian identities, focusing on key historical moments such as the dissolution of the Ottoman Empire, the mandates period, the rise of nationalist movements, and the political and ideological transformations of the 20th century. 3366 Nukes, WMD and Rogue States in the Modern Middle East 3 hours This course will introduce students to the history and politics of weapons of mass destruction (WMD) in the modern Middle East. With a focus on nuclear energy and nuclear weapons, this course will explore the existing scholarly literature on these issues with an emphasis on analyzing how historical context has influenced WMD developments in various countries of the region. It will also examine how nuclear strategies and theories have affected the historiography of nuclear weapons development in the region.

This course examines the history of the Global War on Terror. It traces the ideological underpinnings that shaped US policy in the wake of the 9/11 attacks as well as those of the militant Islamist groups the United States and its allies sought to oppose. Subjects include the rise and spread of militant Islamist movements in the wake of the Iranian revolution and the Soviet Invasion of Afghanistan, the enduring legacies of the United States’ interventions in Afghanistan and Iraq, and the subsequent spread of sectarian conflict throughout the Middle East.

This is a senior-level course focusing on the Reformation in Europe during the 16th century. We will examine the Late Medieval and Renaissance background to the Reformation. We will study the major leaders and their ideas of the Protestant as well as Roman Catholic reforms. We will also look at the political, social, cultural, and economic changes that took place during the 16th and early 17th centuries in order to place these reform movements in their proper contexts as well as understand their broader impacts. In the process of covering this material students will be introduced to a few of the major historiographical issues concerning the study of religious reform in Early Modern Europe.

Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies the Culminating Experience for History majors in conjunction with HIST 4164.
Full Course Description: In this course students integrate skills in locating and analyzing sources which they have learned in the History major. One significant paper is produced which draws on primary and secondary sources and includes an evaluation of the historiography of the subject. Participants in the course will learn how historians draw on other disciplines. Sound and fair-minded research will be demonstrated by competent written and oral communication. Satisfies the Culminating Experience for History majors in conjunction with HIST 4164. Prerequisite: 75 credit hours earned.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Junior standing.
Full Course Description: The people of Europe in the 20th century experienced catastrophic wars, fundamental redefinition of themselves and their place in the world, and ongoing political and economic reorganization. These developments are analyzed to give insight into the struggles and triumphs of the people of Europe in the 20th century. Prerequisite: Junior standing.

The wars in 20th century Europe have been catastrophic and transformative, affecting almost every aspect of society and culture in Europe. This course will examine conflict in 20th century Europe and the ways in which it affected Europeans through examination of a variety of kinds of source materials. The course will analyze the causes and consequences of conflict, focusing on the experiences of the peoples of Europe. We will explore the ways in which people understood the meaning and nature of the conflict from the different national perspectives and seek to understand the complex ways in which wars impacted populations. (Europe)

Other Requirements / Notes: Students will organize the work that they have collected throughout their History coursework and complete reflective assignments that address their understanding of the causes and consequences of power imbalances as they have learned about history from a range of perspectives. Satisfies the Culminating Experience for History majors in conjunction with HIST 4342.
Full Course Description: This course will be the final stage of the development of the History student’s electronic portfolio. Students will organize the work that they have collected throughout their History coursework and complete reflective assignments that address their understanding of the causes and consequences of power imbalances as they have learned about history from a range of perspectives. The resulting electronic portfolio will represent a summative body of work that demonstrates the skills of the historian that the student can use in pursuit of future graduate and professional opportunities. This will be work that represents careful research and analysis to address significant questions, demonstrate critical thinking and clear communication to seek to understand the past. Satisfies the Culminating Experience for History majors in conjunction with HIST 4342. Prerequisite: 75 credit hours earned.

Course Title: The Printed Page and the Silver Screen
Full Course Description: This course has a dual focus: it will teach students how to analyze literature and film as art and entertainment, and it will take a hands- on approach to creating short videos. The first part of the course will review the elements of literature and definitions of literary terms, teach students a vocabulary for analyzing film, and introduce several critical approaches to literature and film. Students will analyze literary texts and films through class discussion and short critical papers. Their analysis will include exploration of the relation between an artistic work and its historical and cultural context as well as exploration of multiple points of view on the works studied. The second part of the course will involve all students in writing a script for and producing short videos. This will be followed by work on a longer video, for C&M students, or a writing project, for EAW students. Work on these projects will be iterative with students completing creative exercises, producing work in stages, engaging in critique of their own and classmates’ work, revising or recreating, and reflecting on what they are learning about the creative process. For Honors students only. This course may be substituted for the General Education requirement for either Exploring Artistic Works or Creativity and Making (not for both). 2312 Topics in Oral Communication

Full Course Description: This course focuses on significant contemporary world issues as they impact a particular (non-U.S.) area of the world. Students will demonstrate knowledge of global processes, including current global economics, global politics and cultural issues, as they influence at least one non-U.S. area in relation to the rest of the world. Students will have on- and/or off-campus curricular and co-curricular experiences that contribute to the understanding of contemporary world issues. These experiences will be documented on the students’ co-curricular transcripts. This course requires a project in which students will be required to demonstrate global understanding of the impact of global processes including global economics, politics and cultural issues in this particular region of the world. Recommended prerequisite: Junior standing.

Course Title: The Shaping of the Modern World
Other Requirements / Notes: Recommended prerequisite: Junior standing.
Full Course Description: This course surveys world civilization from the Medieval period to the present and traces the evolution of political, economic and cultural institutions in Europe, Asia, the Americas and the Middle East, and highlights remarkable achievements in art, architecture, science and literature. The course will draw connections to earlier eras, while making distinctions between modern and premodern sensibilities and practices. This global course provides a long view on contemporary world issues and is good preparation for study abroad. Recommended prerequisite: Junior standing.

Full Course Description: The focus of this course is conceptual knowledge, vocabulary and strategies for comprehension of college-level materials. Students also develop flexibility of reading rate for varied materials and purposes. Critical thinking, values analysis skills and efficient study techniques are also stressed. Students may be required to take Reading for College Success on the basis of their placement scores. Students may also elect to take this course to improve their critical reading and study strategies. This course meets three hours per week. Corequisite: HDED/READ 0210 must be taken the same semester as WRIT 0385, WRIT 0387 or WRIT 0389 (depending on the writing assessment), unless placement scores indicate otherwise. Fall. Spring.

Prerequisites: Permission of the faculty athletic representative.
