Full Course Description: *Personal Responsibility in Developing Excellence. This course is designed to enhance the quality of the student-athlete’s college experience and to support the student-athlete’s preparation for success in life. Goals include (1) supporting efforts of student-athletes toward intellectual development and graduation; (2) preparing for success in life; (3) promoting respect for diversity; (4) building self-esteem; (5) making meaningful contributions to the community; (6) developing ownership for academic, athletic, personal and social responsibilities; and (7) developing leader-ship skills. Required of all freshman and transfer student-athletes. Prerequisite: Permission of the faculty athletic representative. Fall.

Attention is also given to the development of reading rate flexibility, while also improving concentration, comprehension and retention. Study skills and test-taking strategies are also key elements of the course. When college entrance exams indicate, traditional freshmen may be required to take HDED/READ 1323 while taking the designated content area course. Course may be repeated by international students for elective credit. Fall. Spring.

The course will provide a practical overview of, and orientation to, the position. Through specific explorations of leadership, communication, critical thinking and reflective analysis, the course will offer useful perspectives for paraprofessionals and student leaders in their daily lives. The course emphasizes experiential learning and includes out-of-class observations, group activities, class discussions and readings. Classes will meet once a week for 12 weeks. All first- semester resident assistants are required to complete the course; however, any student interested in learning about the RA position is encouraged to enroll. Fall.

The course will focus on interpersonal leadership skills utilizing various assessment tools, reflections, and other assigned projects. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor. Fall.

Prerequisites: Acceptance into the BSS Interdisciplinary Scholars minor and senior standing.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: Acceptance into the BSS Interdisciplinary Scholars minor and senior standing.
Full Course Description: Designed as the capstone academic experience of the Interdisciplinary Scholars minor, this course introduces students to the theory and practice of interdisciplinary research and writing. The course explores disciplinary perspectives in the social sciences, focusing on the defining elements of disciplines: what they study, their assumptions, the data they draw on, the methods they use, and their theories. The course demonstrates how students can draw on knowledge of disciplines to inform the interdisciplinary research process. The course introduces students to integrative models of interdisciplinary writing and culminates in a significant research project. Over the course of the semester, students will identify a complex problem suitable for interdisciplinary research, engage in interdisciplinary research, and complete a written project which integrates disciplinary insights. Each student will participate in the BSS Honors Symposium and present his or her research paper. Prerequisites: Acceptance into the BSS Interdisciplinary Scholars minor and senior standing.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: In this course students will learn to differentiate between the primary disciplines of history, political science, geography and economics, and how each contributes to the understanding of international affairs.
Full Course Description: Global Issues provides a basic introduction to the central concepts and approaches necessary to explore the field of international affairs. In this course students will learn to differentiate between the primary disciplines of history, political science, geography and economics, and how each contributes to the understanding of international affairs. Drawing on each of these disciplines, this course will also offer an overview of the major regions of the world as well as exposure to a variety of current challenges and crises confronting the world today. Fall. Spring.

Full Course Description: Migration has been a constant feature in the course of human history. In this course, we will examine the politics of migration in countries of the Global North and the Global South, with a focus on North and South America. Over the semester we will focus on the current political, social and economic debates and resulting policies surrounding migration as associated with the countries of the Global North and the Global South.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course introduces students to the field of global public health. Students will study how biological, spatial, historical, political, structural and socio-economic factors influence the health of populations and the means in which different international and domestic agencies try to protect public health. Issues discussed in class will vary from year-to- year, but may include such topics as the debate over individual and collective rights regarding public health, an analysis of how national, intergovernmental and non-governmental organizations respond to and serve public health needs, and the relationship between the environment and spread of disease.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Prerequisites: POLS 2332. 4341 Issues and Challenges of Global Development 3 hours There is a highly synergistic relation among food systems (production- consumption- disposal), water, the environment, and public health. This course will evaluate global development policies and practices related to contemporary global issues and assess the impact of fluctuating climate and natural disasters, biodiversity, and natural resources on agricultural production, food security, public health and poverty-reduction strategies. A “food-water-environmental justice” framework will be utilized to promote sustainable development that meets these many challenges.
Full Course Description: This course provides an introduction to the theory and practice of both intergovernmental organizations (IGOs) and nongovernmental organizations (NGOs) and the increasingly important role they play in world politics. It will examine the general theories behind the historical development of international organizations, focusing in particular on such groups as the United Nations, regional organizations (e.g., NATO, the EU, the OAS), and NGOs and their roles in peacekeeping and security, social services, humanitarian assistance, economic development, international law and environmental protection. Prerequisite: POLS 2332. 4341 Issues and Challenges of Global Development 3 hours There is a highly synergistic relation among food systems (production- consumption- disposal), water, the environment, and public health. This course will evaluate global development policies and practices related to contemporary global issues and assess the impact of fluctuating climate and natural disasters, biodiversity, and natural resources on agricultural production, food security, public health and poverty-reduction strategies. A “food-water-environmental justice” framework will be utilized to promote sustainable development that meets these many challenges.

They will also complete reflection papers on the experience relating it to academic content gained from previous courses, due when they return. Satisfies the Culminating Experience for International Affairs majors. Prerequisite: Senior standing. Fall. Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with ECON 3336.
Full Course Description: Introduction to the theories of trade, the international monetary system, and the financing of international trade and economic development. The U.S. balance of payments is analyzed and its effect on foreign investments and operations is studied. Problems and opportunities unique to multinational business are considered. Cross-listed with ECON 3336. Prerequisite: ECON 2301 or ECON 2302.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: This course examines the issues of managing global business with an understanding of the differences and similarities in global cultures, and how cultures inform business operations and employee management. Cross-listed with MGMT 3338. Prerequisite: Junior standing.
Full Course Description: This course examines the issues of managing global business with an understanding of the differences and similarities in global cultures, and how cultures inform business operations and employee management. It provides an examination of communication strategies for effective communication, team management, and resolution of ethical conflicts in an integrated and interdependent world economy. Cross-listed with MGMT 3338. Prerequisite: Junior standing.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: Topical issues in international business. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisites: Vary by topic.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: An inquiry into the marketing structure, organization, policies and procedures in international marketing. The impact of foreign competition in domestic markets is considered, along with challenges in penetrating foreign markets. Effects of U.S. and foreign antitrust, taxation and regional trade agreements are explored. Readings, discussion, cases, and individual and group assignments are used. Cross- listed with MKTG 4341. Prerequisite: MKTG 2301. Fall. Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with FINC 4346.
Full Course Description: This course serves as an introduction to the fundamentals of international financial management and the challenges and opportunities that corporations and investors face in the global economy. The nature of exchange rates and their impact on corporate decisions provide a foundation for discussing corporate strategy and the decision to invest abroad, foreign exchange market equilibrium conditions and forecasting exchange rates, managing exchange risk, multinational capital budgeting and cost of capital, and sources of financing. The nature and value of international trade and international financial flows are evaluated, and students are introduced to the less popular concept of balance of payments. Conceptual content includes ethical issues encountered in the international arena, principles of business strategy, economics, political science, financial management and derivative instruments. Cross-listed with FINC 4346. Prerequisite: FINC 3330.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Junior standing.
Full Course Description: This course examines the global forces that shape the economic, political, and social environment in which multi-national, global and transnational companies operate. Students will analyze the influence of a country’s culture, religions, ethics, government, politics, and economy on the development of opportunities for international business. Students will apply the policies that create economic development and international trade to specific countries and companies. Students will create future scenarios for countries and companies in the global business environment. Prerequisite: Junior standing. Fall. Spring.

Course Title: Introduction to the Culture of Japan
Full Course Description: Taught in English. Introduction to the contemporary culture of Japan and the historical development of cultural norms, customs, popular and fine arts of the Japanese people. Topics addressed include geography of Japan and the Far East, contemporary values, religious, social and political structure. Examples of Japanese literature in translation, music, film, video and other media will supplement traditional textbooks as source material for assignments and class discussion. Spring.
A beginning course stressing comprehension, speaking, reading and writing skills. Extensive listening outside of class is an integral part of the course. Fall.

A continuation of the development of fundamental skills stressed in Japanese I, further development of vocabulary and grammatical structures needed for basic competence. Extensive listening outside of class is an integral part of the course. Prerequisite: JAPN 1311. Spring.

The semester course begins with a short review of basic grammatical patterns taught at the introductory level. There is continued development of vocabulary, listening comprehension, reading and writing skills. Oral language activities stress conversation skills needed for ordinary interactions with Japanese speakers. Prerequisite: JAPN 1312. Fall.
Continuation of the development of intermediate-level skills in listening comprehension, speaking, reading and writing stressed in Japanese 2311. Prerequisite: JAPN 2311. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies the Experiential Learning for Social Justice Mission Marker and a Writing Rich Mission Marker.
Full Course Description: This course is designed to provide extensive practice in editing stories for focus, pace, style and the conventions of written English. It will provide a review of grammar and usage conventions of standard written English, practice with common proofreading and copy-editing symbols used in newsrooms, and practice in editing according to AP style. 2321 Journalism I: Introduction to Writing and Reporting News 3 hours The class introduces students to how news is defined, reported and shared in our minute-by-minute digital media world. Students are introduced to the history of journalism in the U.S. and taught to become thoughtful and consistent news consumers. The course also teaches students how to generate story ideas, write in journalistic style and produce news stories across platforms and with social media. Students are encouraged to publish their work in the student newspaper/website. Students are also introduced to professionals working in the field. Satisfies the Experiential Learning for Social Justice Mission Marker and a Writing Rich Mission Marker. Prerequisite: WRIT 1301.

Prerequisites: JOUR 2321 and JOUR 3322, or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: JOUR 2321 and JOUR 3322, or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Students learn the critical-thinking and creative skills needed to produce responsible digital journalism in the global community. The focus is on writing for broadcast and online journalism, journalistic camera techniques and video editing. Students are encouraged to produce news packages for publication. Prerequisites: JOUR 2321 and JOUR 3322, or permission of instructor.

Students learn the craft of opinion writing and the importance of strong reporting for the persuasive writer. They work individually and in groups to produce editorials, columns and blog posts on behalf of causes and organizations they believe in. Students will learn to apply journalistic techniques to their work for news organizations and/or on behalf of non-profits or advocacy groups. The goal is to learn to write opinion with rigor and depth. Recommended prerequisites: JOUR 2321 and JOUR 3322.

Students are taught to write about the visual, dramatic and musical arts across platforms and venues. This writing and discussion-based course immerses students in the arts and introduces them to blogging, criticism, short and long-form feature writing and celebrity journalism. Students also learn to use social media as a tool for covering a range of events in the vibrant Austin arts scene. Recommended prerequisite: JOUR 2321

Prerequisites: WRIT 2325 or a minimum grade of C in JOUR 2321 (cannot take concurrently).
Other Requirements / Notes: The goal is not only to understand when and how to approach news and other types of stories from an interactive design standpoint, but to explore how and why certain multimedia techniques may allow for a deeper understanding of the world around us. Satisfies the Experiential Learning for Social Justice and an upper-division Writing Rich Mission Marker. It is also the required journalism course for all WRIT majors.
Full Course Description: This course will build on students’ proficiencies with established and emerging multimedia storytelling tools and techniques. The goal is not only to understand when and how to approach news and other types of stories from an interactive design standpoint, but to explore how and why certain multimedia techniques may allow for a deeper understanding of the world around us. This course builds on the skills taught in WRIT 2311. Satisfies the Experiential Learning for Social Justice and an upper-division Writing Rich Mission Marker. It is also the required journalism course for all WRIT majors. Prerequisite: WRIT 2325 or a minimum grade of C in JOUR 2321 (cannot take concurrently).

Prerequisites: JOUR 2321, JOUR 3322 and JOUR 2328, or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: JOUR 2321, JOUR 3322 and JOUR 2328, or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Students produce four news packages building on basics from the Broadcast Journalism class. Students also learn how to produce a television newscast and are encouraged to produce news packages for publication. Prerequisites: JOUR 2321, JOUR 3322 and JOUR 2328, or permission of instructor.

Prerequisites: JOUR 2321 and JOUR 3314 or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: JOUR 2321 and JOUR 3314 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course takes the digital skills learned in JOUR 3314 and puts them to work producing news. Students hone their journalistic online writing skills and produce multimedia content for news sites, magazines and blogs. Designed for the student who wants to learn more about producing great stories for today’s multimedia newsrooms or who hopes to establish a freelance career. Prerequisites: JOUR 2321 and JOUR 3314 or permission of instructor.

Prerequisites: JOUR 2321 and JOUR 3322, or permission of instructor. Taught every two years. 4305 Journalism III: Advanced News Writing and Reporting 3 hours This course is geared to advanced journalism students who want to deepen their skills by investigating an area of specialization. The focus of this course is the requirement and rigors of beat reporting, with special emphasis on sophisticated reporting techniques, advanced news writing and multimedia storytelling. Students will become proficient beat reporters in an area of their choice, such as education, government, politics, sports OR business. They will learn the language of these areas of coverage, along with source development and other skills critical to beat reporting. Students are encouraged to apply for positions on the student newspaper.; JOUR 3322. 4331 Special Topics in Journalism and Digital Media 3 hours This course will explore issues of particular interest within print journalism. These topics will be focused in nature and will assume prior background in print journalism. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.; JOUR 2321.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: JOUR 2321 and JOUR 3322, or permission of instructor. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This class relies a great deal on professional sports journalists to share their expertise with students. Students will be assigned to cover men’s and women’s sports on campus and to provide local television stations with updates and video on sports events. With an emphasis on visual communication, the course explores various theories, historical development and current media practices and their effect on the lives of individuals and society. The course includes studies of objectivity, persuasion, censorship and legal problems. Prerequisites: JOUR 2321 and JOUR 3322, or permission of instructor. Taught every two years. 4305 Journalism III: Advanced News Writing and Reporting 3 hours This course is geared to advanced journalism students who want to deepen their skills by investigating an area of specialization. The focus of this course is the requirement and rigors of beat reporting, with special emphasis on sophisticated reporting techniques, advanced news writing and multimedia storytelling. Students will become proficient beat reporters in an area of their choice, such as education, government, politics, sports OR business. They will learn the language of these areas of coverage, along with source development and other skills critical to beat reporting. Students are encouraged to apply for positions on the student newspaper. Prerequisite: JOUR 3322. 4331 Special Topics in Journalism and Digital Media 3 hours This course will explore issues of particular interest within print journalism. These topics will be focused in nature and will assume prior background in print journalism. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: JOUR 2321.

Course Title: Internship in Journalism and Digital Media
Other Requirements / Notes: Forty-five working hours required per semester- hour of credit. Students may also secure internships in newsrooms off-campus, with the approval of the Journalism and Digital Media coordinator. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Students work at Hilltop Views, the campus newspaper/website, producing stories, photos and video for the website and for the weekly print edition. Interns are involved in every facet of production, from developing story ideas to making assignments and editing writers’ work. Graphic designers and photographers gain valuable experience working for a news organization. Forty-five working hours required per semester- hour of credit. Students may also secure internships in newsrooms off-campus, with the approval of the Journalism and Digital Media coordinator. Students may take a one-, two-, or three-hour internship. Students may take more than one internship for course credit as long as the location and type of work experience vary. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

Full Course Description: Exercise Buddies in the Active Hilltoppers Program will learn about the benefits and risks of exercise, motivational strategies, and effects of exercise on mental health. They will be matched with a participant(s) in the program and will support the participant(s) in beginning and/ or maintaining a regular exercise program. KINE 1158 counts for one credit of the required four total credits for activity/instructional topics course required for the Kinesiology major (without an emphasis area). KINE 1258 counts for two credits of the required four total credits for activity/instructional topics courses. KINE 1358 counts for three credits of the required four total credits for activity/instructional topics courses. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor. Fall. Spring. 1159 Active Hilltoppers Exercise Program – Participants 1 hour The Participants in the Active Hilltoppers Exercise Program will be matched with an exercise buddy who will support them in beginning and/or maintaining a regular exercise program. Students will exercise a minimum of twice a week for 45 minutes with the exercise buddy, and have the opportunity to set their own days/times to exercise and self-select exercise activities (with the support of the exercise buddy) intentionally to reach individual goals. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor. Fall. Spring.

Fall. 1320 Safety and First Aid American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons training for the 3 hours prevention and treatment of accidental injuries and medical self-help for disaster situations. Certification awarded upon completion of course requirements. Fall, odd years. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: Prepares the above-average swimmer with specialized knowledge and skills in all aspects of water safety. Emphasizes personal safety as well as safety techniques utilized to assist others in case of emergency: First Aid, CPR/AED for Lifeguards, Land/Water Rescues and Backboarding. Culminates in American Red Cross Lifeguard certification. Fall. 2324 Physical Activity, Recreation and Sports for Special Populations 3 hours This course is intended to provide the student practitioner with the necessary information to design and implement appropriate activities for individuals with special needs and limitations. Through public service, students will become familiar with various special populations and methods by which to serve their needs in recreation, sports and physical fitness. Fall. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Prerequisites: Comfortable in and around deep water.
Other Requirements / Notes: Course objectives will also include the following: (1) necessary knowledge and skills to conduct a personal training business, (2) designing cardiovascular and resistance training fitness programs to meet the needs of specific clients, (3) conducting oneself in a professional manner in a fitness environment, (4) instructing proper form in conducting important foundational exercises, (5) understanding basic biomechanical deviations and how to stretch and strengthen the body to correct these deviations, and (6) nutritional principles related to achieving specific body composition goals.
Full Course Description: Survey of skills, knowledge, and instructional methods and materials for teaching all levels of swimming and emergency water safety according to American Red Cross standards. Culminates in ARC Water Safety Instructor certification. Prerequisite: Comfortable in and around deep water. Spring. 2328 Practical Training for the Fitness Professional3 hours This course is intended first and foremost to provide the student with the necessary knowledge and skills to sit successfully for the NASM (National Academy of Sports Medicine) CPT (Certified Personal Trainer) examination. Course objectives will also include the following: (1) necessary knowledge and skills to conduct a personal training business, (2) designing cardiovascular and resistance training fitness programs to meet the needs of specific clients, (3) conducting oneself in a professional manner in a fitness environment, (4) instructing proper form in conducting important foundational exercises, (5) understanding basic biomechanical deviations and how to stretch and strengthen the body to correct these deviations, and (6) nutritional principles related to achieving specific body composition goals.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course is designed to instruct the student practitioner in the rules, mechanics and ethical behaviors necessary to successfully officiate interscholastic team sports and to provide practical experiences in officiating sports at the high school and/or intramural level. 2335 Prevention and Treatment of Athletic Injuries3 hours Methods and techniques for the prevention and treatment of injuries related to participation in physical activity and athletics. Emphasis is placed on injury management procedures, knowledge of different athletic injuries and conditions, basic first aid, taping, and athletic- padding procedures. Fall. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: This course in Sports Nutrition is designed to provide students with a basic understanding of nutrition as it relates to general physical activity and sport. Specific focus will be given to understanding how nutritional choices can affect exercise performance, as well as how one can effectively train and structure their diet to improve body composition and performance.
Full Course Description: This course in Sports Nutrition is designed to provide students with a basic understanding of nutrition as it relates to general physical activity and sport. Specific focus will be given to understanding how nutritional choices can affect exercise performance, as well as how one can effectively train and structure their diet to improve body composition and performance. Recommended prerequisite: BIOL 2401. Fall.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Course Title: Concepts of Physical Fitness and Wellness
Full Course Description: This course is designed to promote a healthy lifestyle derived from proper physical activity and informed lifestyle choices. Major topics of emphasis include an overview of the principles of wellness, stress management, nutrition and weight management, and concepts and principles of physical fitness. With the assistance of related laboratory experiences, students will be provided the opportunity to evaluate their current state of wellness in each of the topic areas and plan appropriate lifestyle change strategies. Fall. Spring. 2151– Practica in Athletic Training

Course Title: Concepts of Physical Fitness and Wellness
Other Requirements / Notes: Each student pursuing a Texas License is required to complete and document a minimum of 300 clock-hours of service under the supervision of a staff athletic trainer in the St. These internship hours are part of the required 1,800 clock-hours needed to apply for the State of Texas Licensure Exam and to be eligible for graduation. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

The 1,800 clock-hours must be accrued in no less than five long academic semesters. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

Also, each student is required to complete and document observation hours under the supervision of a staff athletic trainer in the St. Edward’s University Athletic Training Room. Prerequisite: KINE 2335. Spring.

Full Course Description: This course will provide an overview of current issues faced by marketing managers within the sports industry and those outside the industry who market their product through sports. Students will be introduced to the unique qualities of the sports product and also examine the promotion mix, pricing and distribution issues as they relate to the sports industry. Prerequisite: Junior standing. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Course Title: Health and Physical Education for Children
Prerequisites: Junior standing.; Junior standing. 3333 Management of Kinesiology and Sports Programs 3 hours This course is a study of the role and responsibilities of management in the conduct of physical education, recreation and athletic programs. Topics include an overview of management, administration and leadership; human resource management; financial management; marketing and media management; and facility and event planning and management. Course emphasis is focused on the practical application of information using a five-part semester-long case study.; KINE 1311, WRIT 2302, and junior standing.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Junior standing. Prerequisites: KINE 1311, WRIT 2302, and junior standing.
Full Course Description: A course designed for future teachers, providing them with the basic concepts, principles and skills necessary to plan and implement effective and engaging health instruction and physical-education activities for children. Topics covered will include physical fitness, motor skills, rhythms and games, sports, nutrition, stress management, safety, healthy behaviors, and wellness concepts. This course will address the Texas Essential Knowledge and Skills (TEKS) for health and physical education as well as the current Texas educator standards (TExES) for the EC–Grade 6 Generalist, the EC–Grade 6 Bilingual Generalist and the EC–Grade 12 Physical Education certifications. Prerequisite: Junior standing. Fall. 3332 Social and Psychological Issues of Kinesiology and Sports 3 hours This course introduces the student to critical issues and ethical decisions related to the leadership and management of athletic and physical education programs. Topics to be investigated include, but are not limited to, equality, fairness, gender, violence, professionalism, race, sexual orientation and the media. Prerequisite: Junior standing. 3333 Management of Kinesiology and Sports Programs 3 hours This course is a study of the role and responsibilities of management in the conduct of physical education, recreation and athletic programs. Topics include an overview of management, administration and leadership; human resource management; financial management; marketing and media management; and facility and event planning and management. Course emphasis is focused on the practical application of information using a five-part semester-long case study. Prerequisites: KINE 1311, WRIT 2302, and junior standing. Fall. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Junior standing.
Full Course Description: The study of the functional anatomy and mechanical principles fundamental to human motion. Specific application will be made to motor skills pertinent to sports and recreational activities. As an outcome of the course, athletes, coaches and teachers of physical education should be able to analyze human motion with the intent to prevent injuries, rehabilitate the body and enhance motor performance. Prerequisite: Junior standing. Fall. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Course Title: Recreation and Sport for the Adolescent
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Junior standing.
Full Course Description: Encompasses the equipment sequence and progression of games, rhythms, lifetime and team sports, curriculum, and essential elements for middle and high school students. Also included will be lead-up games to sports skills and coaching for the atypical student. Prerequisite: Junior standing. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: The principles of motor-skill acquisition across the life span will be examined to develop an under-standing of the basic concepts and terminology associated with motor learning and human motor development.
Full Course Description: Motor learning is the study of the learning of movement skills and the factors that mediate learning, such as practice or knowledge of results. Motor development is the study of the changes in movement behavior over the life span and the processes or factors underlying these changes. The principles of motor-skill acquisition across the life span will be examined to develop an under-standing of the basic concepts and terminology associated with motor learning and human motor development. Students experience how movements are learned and how the capacity for movement changes over the life span. Fall, even years.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Recommended Prerequisites: BIOL 2401. 3346 Measurement and Evaluation in Kinesiology and Sports 3 hours Includes physical fitness and skills tests, evaluation techniques, and statistical procedures useful to the practitioner in the understanding of test results.
Other Requirements / Notes: This course is designed to provide students with a comprehensive understanding of training as it relates to general physical activity and sport. Specific focus will be given to understanding how variations in programming can have profound impact on exercise performance and overall functionality. Additionally, this course will develop an understanding of exercise testing procedures and administration. 3346 Measurement and Evaluation in Kinesiology and Sports 3 hours Includes physical fitness and skills tests, evaluation techniques, and statistical procedures useful to the practitioner in the understanding of test results. Prerequisite: Junior standing.
Full Course Description: This course is designed to provide students with a comprehensive understanding of training as it relates to general physical activity and sport. Specific focus will be given to understanding how variations in programming can have profound impact on exercise performance and overall functionality. Additionally, this course will develop an understanding of exercise testing procedures and administration. Furthermore, this course will address how to efficaciously program physical fitness throughout all stages of life to continually strive for healthy and functional longevity. Recommended prerequisite: BIOL 2401. 3346 Measurement and Evaluation in Kinesiology and Sports 3 hours Includes physical fitness and skills tests, evaluation techniques, and statistical procedures useful to the practitioner in the understanding of test results. Prerequisite: Junior standing. Fall. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course is for students enrolled in the Pre-Athletic Training degree track. In this lab, they will apply what they learned in the Evaluation lecture to hands-on situations. They will further their practical experience by fulfilling supervised clinical experience in the St. Edward’s University athletic training room, working with an assigned sport. Prerequisite: KINE 2151. Corequisite: KINE 3347. Fall.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Prerequisites: KINE 2335. BIOL 2401 is strongly recommended.; PSYC 2301.
Other Requirements / Notes: This course is designed to provide the student with an advanced understanding of critical thinking and problem-solving skills involved in the evaluation, treatment, and rehabilitation of athletic injuries.
Full Course Description: This course is designed to provide the student with an advanced understanding of critical thinking and problem-solving skills involved in the evaluation, treatment, and rehabilitation of athletic injuries. Topics will include the clinical signs and symptoms of common athletic injuries, advanced evaluation skills and management of injuries. Prerequisite: KINE 2335. BIOL 2401 is strongly recommended. Fall. 3148 Rehabilitation and Therapeutic Modalities Lab 1 hour This course is for students enrolled in the Pre-Athletic Training degree track. In this lab, they will apply what they learned in the Rehabilitation/Modalities lecture to hands-on situations. They will further their practical experience by fulfilling supervised clinical experience in the St. Edward’s University athletic training room, working with an assigned sport. Prerequisite: PSYC 2301. Corequisite: KINE 3348. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Prerequisites: KINE 2335 and KINE 3347. BIOL 2401 is strongly recommended.
Full Course Description: This course will address the principles and techniques used to plan, implement, document and evaluate the efficacy of therapeutic exercise and modalities for the rehabilitation and reconditioning of orthopedic and athletic injuries. Topics include injury/trauma to tissue, tissue healing, muscle contractions, basics on developing muscle tissue, and proper documentation. Prerequisites: KINE 2335 and KINE 3347. BIOL 2401 is strongly recommended. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Junior standing.
Full Course Description: This course will explore the acute biological responses of the human body to physical activity and the chronic adaptations that can occur with repeated bouts of exercise over time. Topics will include nutrition, energy transfer, physiological support systems, exercise training and body composition. Emphasis is on the application of the principles deemed most important for peak performance. Prerequisite: Junior standing. Recommended prerequisite: BIOL 2401. Fall. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Junior standing. Students will be required to select, schedule, and coordinate external site visits for their class; critique sports and recreational facilities; and complete a comprehensive planning/design project.
Full Course Description: This course is designed to identify the qualities, skills, competencies, responsibilities and obligations of coaches; to identify the personal and professional roles that coaches may play and the conflicts inherent in these roles; to identify the biological considerations of athletes important for coaches to consider; to review the appropriate strategies and skills necessary to meet, select, teach, motivate and discipline the athlete; to identify the administrative and human-management skills necessary for success as a coach; and to address the advantages and disadvantages of coaching as a profession. Prerequisite: Junior standing. Spring. 4342 Facility Design and Facility/Event Management 3 hours This course provides the student with a broad, applied introduction to an array of sports and recreational facility types, exploring the design, function, and operation. Students will review the design, planning, and operational aspects of nonprofit and for-profit municipal, state and federally operated indoor and outdoor facilities. Students will be required to select, schedule, and coordinate external site visits for their class; critique sports and recreational facilities; and complete a comprehensive planning/design project. 4345 Legal and Ethical Issues in Sports and Kinesiology 3 hours This course is designed as a survey of the legal implications inherent in coaching and teaching physical education. Topics covered include an overview of the legal system; the anatomy of a civil suit; tort law and negligence theory; administrative issues; issues concerning the First, Fourth and Fourteenth Amendments to the Constitution; intentional torts; product liability; con-tracts; and risk management strategies. When appropriate, the ethical dilemmas associated with coaching and teaching will be discussed. Students will be asked to apply legal theory to case situations. Prerequisite: Junior standing. Fall. Spring.

Prerequisites: Level IV in one of the above-listed languages.
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: For students of any of the European languages offered (French, German, Spanish). In this course, various techniques will be presented to help students learn, from the art of translation, more about the language they are studying. The language being studied will vary; see course listing each semester. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: Level IV in one of the above-listed languages. Fall.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Sophomore standing.
Full Course Description: The work of this course assumes that students are already somewhat familiar with the basic linguistic systems of English: its phonology, morphology, syntax and semantics and the relation be-tween these systems. The principal focus of the course is practical application of this theory to the teaching of one language to speakers of another, primarily English to Spanish speakers. This involves a constant contrast of the systems of the two languages. Where appropriate, references will be made to speakers of other languages. Prerequisite: Sophomore standing.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: This course will study the numerous forms of social enterprise that are appearing on the global business landscape today. A social enterprise is an organization that applies commercial strategies to maximize improvements in human and environmental well-being, rather than maximizing profits for external shareholders. This course uses journal articles and organization reports to study the forms social enterprise is taking in the U.S., such as the Benefit Corporation and social purpose corporation, and in other countries such as China, and the U.K. It will analyze how these organizations apply business principles to achieve their goals, access socially conscious investments, employ social media to communicate their messages, and use global certifications and third- party assessors, such as GRI and KPMG to evaluate their performance. It will include analysis of local organizations that are social enterprises.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Course Title: The Art and Science of Negotiation
Full Course Description: The purpose of this course is to teach students about the theory and practice of negotiation and build their skills as effective negotiators. Negotiation is a crucial skill in leadership and life. Business leaders need both analytic skills and interpersonal skills to be effective negotiators. This course is intended to complement the technical and diagnostic skills learned in other courses in the The Bill Munday School of Business Bachelor of Business Administration program. Students build competence and confidence as negotiators through a combination of pedagogical activities including reading, discussion, projects, and experiential exercises. Each class will focus on a different facet of the negotiation process.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: The course provides an understanding of the key success factors for managing a project in any industry.
Full Course Description: The course objective is to help prospective project managers to understand how projects contribute to the strategic goals of an organization and to master project management tools/techniques and interpersonal skills necessary to orchestrate projects to completion. Students learn the fundamentals of managing all aspects of a project, including scope definition, work planning, project control, resource management, change management, and risk management. The course provides an understanding of the key success factors for managing a project in any industry. The course is interactive and includes hands-on exercises in which the students apply – with the aid of PM software – the methods and concepts studied.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: Provides a basic knowledge of current laws, personnel theories and recommended personnel practices. Students learn basic operative functions of personnel administration, including job analysis, training, recruitment, selection, employment testing, compensation management and performance appraisal. Students study relevant labor law and fair-employment practices, including the Civil Rights Act of 1964, the Americans with Disabilities Act and affirmative action programs. Students develop a working knowledge of the most basic aspects of labor-management relations, including collective bargaining and grievance procedures. Fall. Spring. 3334 Organizational Behavior/Leading Teams and Organizations 3 hours This course focuses on an organization’s most critical resource– its people. It assists students in becoming effective leaders of teams and organizations by helping them think critically about organizational problems and understand the frameworks that decision makers use to solve these problems. The course will cover topics such as motivation, teamwork, power and politics, organizational structure, and organizational culture.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: This course examines the issues of managing global business with an understanding of the differences and similarities in global cultures, and how cultures inform business operations and employee management. Cross-listed with IBUS 3338. Prerequisite: Junior standing.
Full Course Description: This course examines the issues of managing global business with an understanding of the differences and similarities in global cultures, and how cultures inform business operations and employee management. It provides an examination of communication strategies for effective communication, team management, and resolution of ethical conflicts in an integrated and interdependent world economy. Cross-listed with IBUS 3338. Prerequisite: Junior standing.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Prerequisites: BUSI 2305 with a C or higher.
Full Course Description: Study of the design, scheduling and control of systems that make the most economical use of human and capital inputs to create products and services. Topics include forecasting, capacity planning, facility location and layout, production/service processes, employee and task scheduling, inventory management, quality assurance, and project management. Prerequisite: BUSI 2305 with a C or higher.

The course will be based around a company that includes corporate social responsibility in its mission and operates in the global environment. Prerequisites: BUSI 1301 and junior standing. Fall.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: Studies the interaction of the marketing mix and other activities designed to plan, price, promote and distribute products and services to present and potential customers. The course incorporates current developments in marketing, including the changing technological environment and increased globalization of marketing, to acquaint students with present-day marketing challenges.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Prerequisites: MKTG 2301. Fall, even years. 3333 Principles of Retailing and E-commerce Marketing 3 hours The objective of this course is to provide students with the tools necessary to understand and evaluate retailing from the perspectives of the consumer, the organization and society. Specific attention will be given to retailing’s economic impact on the community and the economic wealth of the nation. Retailing’s focus on the consumer involves an investigation of ethics, social responsibility and research techniques.; MKTG 2301. Spring, even years.
Full Course Description: This course is intended to develop practical skills and strategic thinking related to selling and the management of sales functions. Topics include prospecting, handling objections, closing sales, forecasting, budgeting, organizing and motivating the sales force. The role of customer relationship management as an organizing approach is emphasized and relevant software applications to manage the CRM process are incorporated into the course. Prerequisite: MKTG 2301. Fall, even years. 3333 Principles of Retailing and E-commerce Marketing 3 hours The objective of this course is to provide students with the tools necessary to understand and evaluate retailing from the perspectives of the consumer, the organization and society. Specific attention will be given to retailing’s economic impact on the community and the economic wealth of the nation. Retailing’s focus on the consumer involves an investigation of ethics, social responsibility and research techniques. Prerequisite: MKTG 2301. Spring, even years.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: Social Media Marketing focuses on the application of social networking tools and methods in the marketing of the enterprise and related analytical tools available to measure the efficacy and cost-effectiveness of social media programs. The main emphasis will be on the use of social networks for personal and corporate branding and marketing purposes. This course will also focus on tools for managing social media marketing efforts. Prerequisite: MKTG 2301. Fall. Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Other Requirements / Notes: Understanding the behavior, motivations and actions of both business and consumer buyers markets and marketing.
Full Course Description: Understanding the behavior, motivations and actions of both business and consumer buyers markets and marketing. The main objective of the course is to understand the psychological, socio-cultural, and economic behavior of individuals, groups, and organizations and the processes they use to select, consume, and dispose of products, services or ideas. Relevant analytical tools will be covered. Prerequisite: MKTG 2301. Fall.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Prerequisites: BUSI 2305, MKTG 2301, and junior standing. Fall,
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: BUSI 2305, MKTG 2301, and junior standing.
Full Course Description: The purpose of marketing research is to help a firm make better marketing decisions. This class involves the exploration and hands-on application of a variety of marketing-research techniques with the goal of collecting data in an efficient and effective manner. Emphasis on process-related issues with a focus on matching the information sought with an appropriate research method. Students will design, plan, execute and evaluate a basic marketing-research study using statistical and analytical tools. Prerequisites: BUSI 2305, MKTG 2301, and junior standing. Fall, Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: An inquiry into the marketing structure, organization, policies and procedures in international marketing. The impact of foreign competition in domestic markets is considered, along with challenges in penetrating foreign markets. Effects of U.S. and foreign antitrust, taxation and regional trade agreements are explored. Readings, discussion, cases and individual and group assignments are used. Cross- listed with IBUS 4341. Prerequisite: MKTG 2301. Fall. Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Full Course Description: Introduces a paradigm of dynamically and directly interacting with customers through digital means by providing a framework and tools for managing and analyzing an organization’s virtual presence for marketing purposes. Applies concepts of digital marketing in practice through applicable projects and assignments. Students are given the opportunity to experience and analyze the effects of creating, promoting, and adapting an online identity for a marketing, entrepreneurial or digital media organization. Prerequisite: MKTG 2301. Fall. Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Prerequisites: MKTG 2301, BUSI 2305 and junior or senior standing.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: MKTG 2301, BUSI 2305 and junior or senior standing.
Full Course Description: Marketing analytics is a broad and multidimensional field that employs many techniques to find meaningful patterns within data and facilitate its transformation into actionable information. Marketing metrics are the quantitative tools necessary to manage the marketing functions. The purpose of both concepts is to support market critical business decisions allowing companies to adapt to and address new market conditions and business challenges. This course will explore the concepts and tools to manage and utilize a market information system in the context of both online and offline analyses within companies. Tools to identify marketing opportunities and profitable segments will be covered, as well as how to calculate relevant metrics. Web analytics tools will be covered as applicable. Prerequisites: MKTG 2301, BUSI 2305 and junior or senior standing. Spring.

School: The Bill Munday School of Business
Prerequisites: MKTG 2301, MKTG 3343 and junior or senior standing. Fall,
Other Requirements / Notes: The course is required for all Marketing majors. Prerequisites: MKTG 2301, MKTG 3343 and junior or senior standing.
Full Course Description: This is a course covering the application of essential principles of marketing as they relate to identifying, attracting and retaining customers and clients. Using a real-life project students learn how to create and execute effective marketing plans, emphasizing all marketing channels communications, for either profit or nonprofit organizations. The course contains significant components of critical and creative thinking as students solve problems related to ongoing marketing issues. Tools such as a marketing simulation will also be used to integrate marketing metrics in to marketing decision-making. The course is required for all Marketing majors. Prerequisites: MKTG 2301, MKTG 3343 and junior or senior standing. Fall, Spring.

A support course designed to give math students help needed to be successful in Mathematics courses other than those supported by MATH 0104, MATH 0114, and MATH 0124. Each section of the course will be paired with another credit-bearing Mathematics course, typically at the 2000-level or below. Topics will vary depending on the course for which support is provided.

A support course designed to give math students help needed to be successful in statistics. Topics include operations of real numbers, algebraic expressions, equation solving, use of exponents, percents, organizing data, frequency distributions, central tendency, measures of variability and hypothesis testing. Corequisite: CRIJ 3334, PSYC 2317, POLS 3328 or SOCI 2329.

Course Title: Support For Success in College Algebra
Full Course Description: A support course designed to give math students help needed to be successful in College Algebra. Topics include operations of real numbers, algebraic expressions, inequalities, equation solving, use of exponents, percents, geometry, polynomials, factoring, rational expressions and functions. Corequisite: MATH 1314.

Course Title: Support For Success in Math For Business
Full Course Description: A support course designed to give math students help needed to be successful in Math for Business. Topics include operations of real numbers, algebraic expressions, equation solving, use of exponents, percents, polynomials, factoring, rational expressions, business applications and basic probability. Corequisite: MATH 1324.

A course in general quantitative reasoning, intended for the non- Science, non-Mathematics major. Topics include methods of problem solving, real number operations and applications, mathematical modeling, basic finance, geometry and symmetry, voting and apportionment, networks and graph theory, and elementary probability and statistics. Credit for MATH 1312 may not be earned by persons who have received a C in MATH 1314 or above. Fall. Spring. Summer.

A traditional college algebra course intended for students who plan to take further courses in Mathematics or prepare for graduate school admission. Topics include real number and polynomial operations, linear and quadratic equations and inequalities, functions and graphing, systems of equations and inequalities, exponential and logarithmic functions, and the use of technology to graph and solve equations. Credit for MATH 1314 may not be earned by persons who have received a C in MATH 2312 or above. Corequisite: MATH 0114 or satisfactory placement. Fall. Spring. Summer.

This is a course in finite mathematics for business students over topics including sets, basic algebraic properties, linear equations and inequalities, functions and graphs, the exponential and logarithmic functions, the mathematics of finance, systems of linear equations and matrices, linear inequalities and linear programming, the simplex method, and an introduction to probability. Corequisite: MATH 0124 or satisfactory placement.

Prerequisites: MATH 1324 or MATH 2312 or satisfactory placement.
Full Course Description: This is a course in standard one-variable calculus and its applications in business. Calculus topics include the derivative, methods of finding derivatives, applications of derivatives, the integral, methods of integration, applications of integration, and the calculus of exponential and logarithmic functions. Prerequisite: MATH 1324 or MATH 2312 or satisfactory placement. Offered on sufficient demand.

Prerequisites: MATH 1314 with a grade of C or better.
Other Requirements / Notes: Focuses on the ideas central to the theory of functions and stresses conceptual understanding, along with multiple ways of representing mathematical ideas, including symbolic, graphical, numerical and verbal representations of functions.
Full Course Description: Focuses on the ideas central to the theory of functions and stresses conceptual understanding, along with multiple ways of representing mathematical ideas, including symbolic, graphical, numerical and verbal representations of functions. Topics include functions in general; linear, exponential, logarithmic, polynomial and trigonometric functions; transformation, composition, combination and inverses of functions; and the use of technology to graph and solve equations. Credit for MATH 2312 may not be earned by persons who have previously received a C in MATH 2413 or above. Prerequisite: MATH 1314 with a grade of C or better. Fall. Spring. Summer.

Prerequisites: MATH 2312 with a grade of C or better.
Full Course Description: A first course in calculus, covering limits and continuity, derivatives, linear approximations, applications including graphs and extrema, definition and properties of the integral and the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, and antidifferentiation. This course presents a threefold approach to calculus: numerical, graphical, and analytical techniques are all emphasized. The fourth credit hour is devoted to active-learning and inquiry-based activities to reinforce course material. Prerequisite: MATH 2312 with a grade of C or better. Fall. Spring.

Prerequisites: MATH 2413 with a grade of C or better.
Full Course Description: A continuation of MATH 2413. Topics include applications of the integral, additional techniques of anti-differentiation, improper integrals, introduction to differential equations, Taylor polynomials and Taylor series, and the Mean Value Theorem. Prerequisites: MATH 2413 with a grade of C or better. Fall. Spring.

Prerequisites: MATH 2312 or equivalent. Fall on sufficient demand.
Other Requirements / Notes: An introduction to topics in discrete mathematics that are commonly used in computer science and mathematics: sets; number systems; functions, mappings and algebraic structures; understanding written mathematics and the nature of proof; formal logic; combinatorics and discrete probability.
Full Course Description: An introduction to topics in discrete mathematics that are commonly used in computer science and mathematics: sets; number systems; functions, mappings and algebraic structures; understanding written mathematics and the nature of proof; formal logic; combinatorics and discrete probability. Prerequisites: MATH 2312 or equivalent. Fall on sufficient demand. Spring.

Prerequisites: Any MATH course with a grade of C or better, or placement scores equivalent to precalculus placement. Fall and
Full Course Description: A first course in applied statistics emphasizing statistical thinking using real and computer-generated data. Topics include data description, hypothesis testing using z-test, t-test, chi-squared, linear regression and ANOVA. Prerequisites: Any MATH course with a grade of C or better, or placement scores equivalent to precalculus placement. Fall and Spring.

Prerequisites: MATH 2414 with a grade of C or better.
Full Course Description: Calculus of multivariable functions. Topics include graphs in two and three dimensions, contour diagrams, partial derivatives, vectors, gradients and directional derivatives, optimization, multiple integration, parametric curves and surfaces, vector-valued functions, arc length, and calculus of vector fields. Prerequisite: MATH 2414 with a grade of C or better. Fall. Spring on sufficient demand.

Prerequisites: COSC 1305 or COSC 1423, and one of MATH 3326 or PSYC 2317 or BUSI 2305.
Full Course Description: An introduction to techniques for dealing with large data sets. Topics include sourcing and programatically cleaning large data sets, descriptive and inferential statistics, using graphs to visualize and interpret data, making business and scientific decisions based on data analysis and working with databases and cloud storage. Software includes Tableau for data visualization, R for data cleaning, preparation, statistical analysis and visualization, and SQL for interacting with databases. Students will work with data from multiple disciplines. Prerequisites: COSC 1305 or COSC 1423, and one of MATH 3326 or PSYC 2317 or BUSI 2305. Fall.

Systems of equations, determinants, algebra and geometry of finite- dimensional linear vector spaces, linear transformations, the algebra of matrices, and the theory of eigenvalues and eigenvectors. Prerequisite: MATH 2413. Fall on sufficient demand. Spring.

Prerequisites: MATH 2414 with a grade of C or better.
Full Course Description: An introduction to ordinary differential equations. Topics include first- order differential equations, first-order systems, linear systems and parts of nonlinear systems, forcing and resonance, and numerical methods, as time permits. Modeling will be incorporated throughout the course as will a dynamical-systems point of view. Students will be expected to make use of computers as part of the course. Critical thinking will be stressed throughout. Prerequisite: MATH 2414 with a grade of C or better. Fall. Spring on sufficient demand.

Prerequisites: MATH 2413 and MATH 2315 with grades of C or better or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: MATH 2413 and MATH 2315 with grades of C or better or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: A rigorous, guided-inquiry to proof techniques and logical exposition. The content and additional learning objectives for the course will be set in a particular mathematical context (such as point-set topology or elementary number theory) specified each year in the course title. Prerequisites: MATH 2413 and MATH 2315 with grades of C or better or permission of instructor. Spring.

Prerequisites: MATH 2414 and MATH 3311 with a grade of C or better or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: MATH 2414 and MATH 3311 with a grade of C or better or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: A rigorous treatment of the real-number system; of real sequences; and of limits, continuity and derivatives of functions of one real variable. Prerequisites: MATH 2414 and MATH 3311 with a grade of C or better or permission of instructor. Fall.

An introductory course on statistical inference focusing on analytical reasoning with both theoretical concepts and practical data. Key topics include sampling techniques, methods of estimation, biasvariance tradeoff, sampling distributions, Fisher information, and confidence intervals, along with foundational elements of hypothesis testing. Prerequisites: MATH 2413. Fall. Spring on sufficient demand.

This course will cover topics such as probability spaces, discrete and continuous random variables, transformations, expectations, moment- generating functions, conditional distributions, the law of large numbers, and central limit theorems. Pre- or corequisite: MATH 2321. Spring, even numbered years.

In this course we aim to broaden our study of statistical inference approaches by focusing on both the scientific and artistic aspects of applying statistical models to data analysis. Topics include estimation, model diagnostics, transformations, multicollinearity, influence, model building, and variable selection. Advanced topics include nonlinear regression and generalized linear models including logistic and Poisson regression. Prerequisites: MATH 3326. Spring, odd-numbered years.

Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with BINF 3338, COSC 3338 and PHYS 3338.
Full Course Description: A survey of standard techniques used to solve numerical and other science-based problems. Topics include roots of equations, systems of linear equations, curve fitting, numerical integration and differentiation, error analysis, number representation, and data visualization. Problems will be solved using programs written in appropriate languages and operating systems. Cross-listed with BINF 3338, COSC 3338 and PHYS 3338. Prerequisites: MATH 2413 and COSC 1305 or COSC 1423. Offered on sufficient demand.

An introduction to machine learning tools and techniques. Topics include classification and regression models, evaluation metrics and loss functions, over- and under-fitting, model interpretability, working with databases and cloud storage and deploying models to the web. Software includes Python for data cleaning, preparation, statistical analysis and visualization, Tableau for data visualization, and SQL for interacting with databases. Prerequisite: MATH 2327. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit with different topics.
Full Course Description: Topics are selected based on needs and interests of students and can vary from year to year. May be repeated for credit with different topics. A specific course description will be published at the time of offering. Specific topic entered on transcript. Recent topics have included mathematical biology and modern geometries. Prerequisites: Vary by topic. Offered on sufficient demand.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit with different topics.
Full Course Description: Courses will vary from year to year. Topics are selected based on the special interests and needs of upper-division Statistics students and on the areas of faculty expertise. May be repeated for credit with different topics. A specific course description will be published at the time of offering. Specific topic entered on transcript. Offered on sufficient demand.

Prerequisites: MATH 3316 or permission of instructor. Spring, odd years.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: MATH 3316 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: The complex plane, Cauchy-Riemann equations, complex integration, Cauchy integral theorem, conformal mapping, applications. Prerequisite: MATH 3316 or permission of instructor. Spring, odd years.

Prerequisites: MATH 3316 or permission of instructor. Spring, even years.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: MATH 3316 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Includes a rigorous treatment of the Riemann integral, sequences of real functions, uniform convergence, infinite series, series of functions, introduction to metric spaces and other topics as time allows. Prerequisite: MATH 3316 or permission of instructor. Spring, even years.

Students will learn how to develop and deploy machine learning models at scale. Techniques students will learn include fitting and deploying models in the cloud, creating APIs to interact with models, and creating graphical front-ends to interact with models. Students will complete a project related to their chosen minor. Prerequisite: COSC 3337, MATH 3339. Spring

Prerequisites: MATH 3311 and MATH 3305 or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: MATH 3311 and MATH 3305 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Elementary properties of groups and rings, symmetric groups, properties of integers, polynomial rings, fields. Prerequisites: MATH 3311 and MATH 3305 or permission of instructor. Fall.

Prerequisites: MATH 3311 or permission of instructor. Spring, odd years.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: MATH 3311 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: An introduction to topology, including sets, functions, properties of topological spaces and metric spaces. Prerequisite: MATH 3311 or permission of instructor. Spring, odd years.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit with different topics.
Full Course Description: Courses will vary from year to year. Topics are selected based on the special interests and needs of upper-division Mathematics students and on the areas of faculty expertise. May be repeated for credit with different topics. Recent topics have included Number Theory and Algebraic Geometry. A specific course description will be published at the time of offering. Specific topic entered on transcript. Prerequisites: Vary by topic. Offered on sufficient demand.

No more than one instance of MATH 4050, MATH 4057, or MATH 4150 will count toward the Bachelor of Arts or Bachelor of Science with a major in Mathematics. Prerequisites: Permission of instructor.
