Prerequisites: MATH 3311, junior or senior standing, and permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: This course may substitute for one of the four required semesters of MATH 4157 for students who participate in summer research, a group research project, external Research Experience for Undergraduates (REU), or other experience that is comparable to one semester of undergraduate research. No more than one instance of MATH 4050, MATH 4057, or MATH 4150 will count toward the Bachelor of Arts or Bachelor of Science with a major in Mathematics. Prerequisites: MATH 3311, junior or senior standing, and permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course may substitute for one of the four required semesters of MATH 4157 for students who participate in summer research, a group research project, external Research Experience for Undergraduates (REU), or other experience that is comparable to one semester of undergraduate research. Credit in MATH 4057 assumes a student has disseminated, or will disseminate, their undergraduate research results during the final semester of MATH 4157, and includes a reflection assignment in addition to the culminating experience reflection which is completed in MATH 4157. No more than one instance of MATH 4050, MATH 4057, or MATH 4150 will count toward the Bachelor of Arts or Bachelor of Science with a major in Mathematics. Prerequisites: MATH 3311, junior or senior standing, and permission of instructor. Fall. Spring. Summer.

Prerequisites: MATH 3311, junior or senior standing, and permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit. Prerequisites: MATH 3311, junior or senior standing, and permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: A multi-semester course for students conducting research. Content varies with semester. Students will learn research methodology and how to search relevant literature and databases within mathematics, read and analyze research articles, identify a feasible research problem, craft a proposal and summary of the question, and present this proposal in written and oral form. Students will conduct an independent investigation of their research question under the supervision of a faculty member, documenting 60 hours of work per term, and will present their findings in written and oral form each semester. Students will present a public seminar and formal paper in the final semester based on results obtained during the investigation of their research question. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisites: MATH 3311, junior or senior standing, and permission of instructor. Fall. Spring.

Corequisites: Concurrent enrollment in another Military Science course.
Full Course Description: Leadership responsibilities for planning, coordination, execution and evaluation of training and other activities, self-confidence and team-building leadership skills that can be applied throughout life. Corequisite: Concurrent enrollment in another Military Science course.

Designed to increase self-confidence through team study and activities in basic drill, physical fitness, rappelling, leadership reaction course, first aid, making presentations and basic marksman-ship. Fundamental concepts of leadership in a profession. Corequisite: MILS 1000.

Principles of effective leading. Designed to reinforce self-confidence through participation in physically and mentally challenging exercises with upper-division ROTC students. Communication skills that improve individual performance and group interaction. Relationship of organizational ethical values to the effectiveness of a leader. Corequisite: MILS 1000.

Covers development of American Profession of Arms from a dual military tradition evaluating military leadership at the tactical, operational, and strategic levels of war. Explores ways in which the Industrial Revolution transformed the United States and other societies organized armed violence.

Ethics-based leadership skills designed to develop individual abilities and contribute to effective team building. Focus on oral presentations, writing concisely, planning of events, coordination of group efforts, advanced first aid, land navigation and basic military tactics. Fundamentals of ROTC’s Leadership Assessment Program. Corequisite: MILS 1000.

Introduction to individual and team aspects of military tactics in small- unit operations. Includes use of radio communications, making safety assessments, movement techniques, planning for team safety/security and methods of pre-examination checks. Practical exercises with upper- division ROTC students. Techniques for training others as an aspect of continued leadership development. Corequisite: MILS 1000.

Series of practical opportunities to lead small groups, receive personal assessments and encouragement, and lead again in situations of increasing complexity. Use of small-unit defense tactics and opportunities to plan and conduct training for lower-division students. Corequisite: MILS 1000.

Continued study of methods covered in MILS 3320. Students analyze tasks; prepare written or oral guidance for team members to accomplish tasks; delegate tasks and supervise; plan for and adapt to the unexpected in organizations under stress; examine and apply lessons from leadership case studies; examine the importance of ethical decision making in enhancing team performance. Corequisite: MILS 1000.
Military leadership and professional ethics; post and installation support system; introduction to the military justice system. Corequisite: MILS 1000.
Fundamentals of the military justice system; training and logistical management systems; military social functions; role of the second lieutenant. Corequisite: MILS 1000.

Advanced study of planning, executing, and assessing complex operations. Composed primarily of independent study, cadets are given opportunities to plan and execute events, assess risk, make ethical decisions, and lead fellow ROTC cadets. Corequisite: MILS 1000.

Advanced study of planning, executing, and assessing complex operations. Composed primarily of independent study, cadets are given opportunities to plan and execute events, assess risk, make ethical decisions, and lead fellow ROTC cadets. Corequisite: MILS 1000.

Other Requirements / Notes: Course may be repeated as topics change.
Full Course Description: Taught in English. Introduction to the culture of a specific country or region. The course will include the geography of the area and its neighbors; contemporary values; religious, social and political structures; the historical development of cultural norms, customs, and popular and fine arts of the people. Course may be repeated as topics change. The class schedule and transcript will show the specific language and course title (e.g., CHIN 1310, Introduction to Chinese Culture). Offered on sufficient demand.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated as topics change.
Full Course Description: A beginning course in the language stressing comprehension, speaking, reading and writing skills. May be repeated as topics change. The class schedule and transcript will show the specific language and course title (e.g., CHIN 1311, Chinese I). Offered on sufficient demand.

Prerequisites: MDLN 1311 in the same language or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated as topics change. Prerequisite: MDLN 1311 in the same language or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Continuation of development of fundamental language skills, further development of vocabulary and grammatical structures needed for basic competence. May be repeated as topics change. The class schedule and transcript will show the specific language and course title (e.g., CHIN 1312, Chinese II). Prerequisite: MDLN 1311 in the same language or permission of instructor. Offered on sufficient demand.

Prerequisites: MDLN 1312 in the same language or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated as topics change. Prerequisite: MDLN 1312 in the same language or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Continued development of vocabulary, listening comprehension, reading and writing skills. Reading and writing assignments reinforce students’ command of more-complex grammatical structures and provide topics for oral discussion. May be repeated as topics change. The class schedule and transcript will show the specific language and course title (e.g., CHIN 2311, Chinese III). Prerequisite: MDLN 1312 in the same language or permission of instructor. Offered on sufficient demand.

Prerequisites: MDLN 2311 in the same language or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated as topics change. Prerequisite: MDLN 2311 in the same language or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Continuation of development of intermediate-level skills in listening comprehension, speaking, reading and writing. Cultural and literary readings are added to increase exposure to the language and to provide topics for discussion and short oral presentations. May be repeated as topics change. The class schedule and transcript will show the specific language and course title (e.g., CHIN 2312, Chinese IV). Prerequisite: MDLN 2311 in the same language or permission of instructor. Offered on sufficient demand.

Prerequisites: MDLN 2312 in the same language or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated as topics change. Prerequisite: MDLN 2312 in the same language or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This may be advanced language study or advanced study of the traditions, values, literature, institutions or arts of the area. May be taught in English or in the appropriate modern language. May be repeated as topics change. The class schedule and transcript will show the specific language and course title (e.g., RUSS 3331, Modern Russian Literature). Prerequisite: MDLN 2312 in the same language or permission of instructor. Offered on sufficient demand.

This course is an entry-level course for students with no formal training in music reading or ear training. The course covers note reading, musical terminology, basic harmonic concepts and basic sight-singing. Fall.
Introduction to the instrument and musical notation. Designed for students with no previous study, but accommodations can be made for experienced players. Class instruction. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Applied Voice I is a series of ten private vocal lessons designed to develop vocal techniques such as pitch, breathing/support, posture, diction, placement, etc., increase musicianship and begin to explore performance practices. The emphasis on specific aspects of vocal development will vary according to the needs of the student. Students begin to develop technical knowledge and facility as well as greater musicianship while demonstrating a comfort level with basic performance practice. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor. Fall. Spring.

This course will focus on the history of the American musical, its roots and its evolution. Students will learn about the prominent composers, lyricists, performers, directors, choreographers and producers of Broadway. Attention will also be paid to specific shows and the characters and songs contained in each for knowledge for potential casting and audition material. Fall, odd years.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: The second tier in the Applied Voice sequence, this course is a series of ten private vocal lessons designed to develop vocal techniques such as breathing/support, posture, diction, placement, etc., increase musicianship and explore advanced performance practices and professional preparation. The emphasis on each topic will vary according to the needs of the student. This class fosters professional preparation through learning to prepare and present audition cuts of songs and preparing an audition book. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor. Fall. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This course will examine a specific advanced topic in music. Possible topics include music history, composition or instrumental music. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This course will examine a specific advanced topic in music. Possible topics include music history, composition or instrumental music. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This course will examine a specific advanced topic in music. Possible topics include music history, composition or instrumental music. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
A general introduction to seapower and the naval service, including the mission, organization, regulations, warfare components, and personal programs. All midshipmen take this course.
A consideration of the influence of seapower, naval history, and maritime affairs on current events and national policy. All midshipmen take this course.
An introduction to naval engineering and propulsion systems including theoretical background. Only Navy option midshipmen take this course.
An introduction to types and purposes of naval weapon systems currently employed by the naval service. Only Navy option midshipmen take this course.

Focuses on the evolution of the U.S. Marine Corps into a specialized amphibious force, with particular attention devoted to the structure and capabilities of the present day U.S. Marine Corps as a forward deployed and rapid deployment force and the development of Expeditionary Maneuver Warfare concepts.
An introduction to piloting, a survey of navigational aids, and a study of the Rules of the Nautical Road. Only Navy option midshipmen and Officer Candidates take this course.

Other Requirements / Notes: All members of the program are required to take this class.
Full Course Description: Principles of leadership and ethics reinforced through seminar discussion and case studies. Discussion of the duties and responsibilities of a naval officer. All members of the program are required to take this class.

Other Requirements / Notes: All members of the program are required to take this class.
Full Course Description: Study of leadership and management theory in organizations, with emphasis on examining the leadership process in the context of the dynamic interaction of the leader, the followers and the situation. All members of the program are required to take this class.

Introduction to the concept of amphibious warfare; explores its doctrinal origins and traces its evolution as an element of naval policy during the twentieth century. Only Marine option midshipmen and MECEPs take this course.

Study of advanced navigation, communications, shipboard evolutions and operations, naval warfare doctrine and joint operations and an introduction to relative motion and the maneuvering board. Only Navy option midshipmen and Officer Candidates take this course.

Other Requirements / Notes: This course builds on the RN students’ prior nursing knowledge to enhance professional development, prepare for a broader scope of practice, and provide deeper understanding of the cultural, political, economic, and social issues that affect clients, and influence care delivery.
Full Course Description: This course builds on the RN students’ prior nursing knowledge to enhance professional development, prepare for a broader scope of practice, and provide deeper understanding of the cultural, political, economic, and social issues that affect clients, and influence care delivery. Emphasis is on the concepts of culturally-appropriate, patient-centered care, evidence-based practice, interprofessional teams, informatics, clinical reasoning and health promotion across the lifespan in a dynamic complex health environment. This immersive experience provides students with networking and mentoring opportunities with peers and faculty.

This course is designed to assist working RN students to perform comprehensive assessment and develop a foundation for clinical reasoning, data collection, and documentation skills. Focus is on normal findings and variations across the lifespan. This immersive experience provides students with opportunities to practice assessment skills and clinical reasoning with peers and faculty. Three credit hours for theory; one credit for clinical equates to 30 hours of clinical a semester.

This course provides the theory, practice, and patterns of use in complementary and alternative medicine practices and products. Philosophical differences between Western Medicine and Alternative Medicine are explored as well as various alternative healthcare systems and practices. A model for healthcare providers to provide patient- centered care within the context of complementary and alternative medicine practices and products use environment will be discussed with emphasis on National Institute of Health National Center for Complementary Integrative Health (NIH-NCCIH).

Course Title: Aging Issues and Care for Older Adults
Full Course Description: This course explores issues related to aging populations in a global perspective. Topics include care options, palliative care, end of life issues, mistreatment, and elder abuse. Students assimilate knowledge and skills for providing optimum care to diverse older adults and families in a multicultural environment.

This course builds upon prior learning experiences and focuses on pathophysiologic and pharmacologic concepts related to nursing. The emphasis of the course is the principles and concepts of disease processes and drug therapeutics. The course addresses common pathophysiological problems experienced throughout the lifespan. The pharmacodynamics, therapeutic uses, side effects, and nursing implications of various drug groups are studied and related to patient-specific observations, assessments, interventions and evaluations that promote and restore health.

This course examines the relationship between theory, research and practice. Fundamental principles of the research process including the steps of the conduct of research, types of research, and research dissemination as well as ethical and cultural considerations. Emphasis is on critical appraisal of research and application of scientific evidence for best nursing practice.

This is the first of two courses that build upon prior learning experiences and focuses on pathophysiologic and pharmacologic concepts related to nursing. The emphasis of the course is the principles and concepts of disease processes and drug therapeutics. The course addresses common pathophysiological problems experienced throughout the lifespan. The pharmacodynamics, therapeutic uses, side effects, and nursing implications of various drug groups are studied and related to patient- specific observations, assessments, interventions and evaluations that promote and restore health.

Other Requirements / Notes: Demonstrated competency in course concepts, selected assessments and performance of a head-to-toe physical assessment is required to successfully complete this course.
Full Course Description: This is the second of two courses and continues to build upon prior learning experiences and focuses on pathophysiologic and pharmacologic concepts related to nursing. The emphasis of the course is the principles and concepts of disease processes and drug therapeutics. The course addresses common pathophysiological problems experienced throughout the lifespan. The pharmacodynamics, therapeutic uses, side effects, and nursing implications of various drug groups are studied and related to patient-specific observations, assessments, interventions and evaluations that promote and restore health. 3332 Health, Wellness, and Physical Assessment Across the Life Span 3 hours The focus of this course is on the development of clinical judgment, critical reasoning, competency in assessment skills, advocacy and utilization of evidenced based practice of individuals at various stages across the lifespan continuum. Concepts will be explored through the lens of nursing theory, social justice, and DEI. The clinical lab experiences include activities in the health assessment lab, virtual learning platforms, and through simulation experiences. Demonstrated competency in course concepts, selected assessments and performance of a head-to-toe physical assessment is required to successfully complete this course.

Other Requirements / Notes: Demonstrated competency in course concepts and selected clinical skills is required for successful completion of this course.
Full Course Description: The focus of this course will be on clinical judgment, critical reasoning, and competency in clinical skills related to behavioral and mental health in patients with acute and chronic behavioral and mental illness as well as prevention, health maintenance and healing/recovery. Concepts will be explored through the lens of nursing theory, social justice, and DEI. Clinical experiences will include simulation, clinical skills lab, behavioral and mental health settings including acute health care settings and community-based settings. Demonstrated competency in course concepts and selected clinical skills is required for successful completion of this course.

Other Requirements / Notes: Demonstrated competency in course concepts and selected clinical skills is required for successful completion of this course.
Full Course Description: The focus of this course is on the development of clinical judgment, critical reasoning, competency in basic clinical skills, advocacy and utilization of evidenced based practice in the care of patients and families. Concepts will be explored through the lens of nursing theory, social justice, and DEI. Clinical experiences include simulation lab, skills lab, nursing homes, acute care settings, and/or other community health care agencies. Demonstrated competency in course concepts and selected clinical skills is required for successful completion of this course.

Other Requirements / Notes: Demonstrated competency in course concepts and selected clinical skills is required for successful completion of this course.
Full Course Description: The focus of this course is on continued development of clinical judgment, critical reasoning, competency in clinical skills, advocacy and utilization of evidenced based practice in the care of patients and families with chronic complex health alterations. Concepts will be explored through the lens of nursing theory, social justice, and DEI. Clinical experiences will include simulation, clinical skills lab, acute care facilities, and other health care agencies. Demonstrated competency in course concepts and selected clinical skills is required for successful completion of this course.

This course examines the foundations of healthcare policy, the financial structure of healthcare systems, and the regulatory environments that have impact on nursing practice and patient care. Emphasis is on selected issues affecting healthcare policies, economics of healthcare and health promotion of diverse groups. Students focus on the influence of national and state health policies influencing contemporary nursing practice.

Course Title: Legal and Ethical Issues in Nursing
Full Course Description: This course examines legal and ethical issues in nursing practice. Emphasis is on ethical decision-making processes and appreciation of ethical dilemmas and negligent practices. Students will have opportunities for legal and ethical dilemma case analysis.

This course introduces students to the basic public health nursing roles, family and community assessment, descriptive epidemiology, environmental health, and transcultural nursing. Students plan, implement, and evaluate various community projects for at risk populations in cooperation with community stakeholders and faculty mentors. Their relationship with the community moves from development of familiarity with the people and place to a deepening level of engagement and commitment. Their projects focus on health promotion and illness prevention by providing screening, health education, resource development, referrals, and many more. Through these activities, population health concepts are threaded across the curriculum. Three hour class and one hour clinical (30 hours Independent Community Clinical). Three credit hours for theory; One credit for clinical equates to 30 clinical hours a semester.

This course examines leadership and management concepts used to address complex microsystem issues within selected healthcare organizations. Emphasis is on the application of advanced communication skills in collaboration with interprofessional teams. Focus is on the interrelationship of selected roles within the context of specific theoretical frameworks and models of care.

Other Requirements / Notes: Demonstrated competency in course concepts and selected clinical skills is required for successful completion of this course.
Full Course Description: The focus of this course will be on advanced clinical judgment, critical reasoning, and competency in advanced clinical skills, advocacy and utilization of evidenced based practice in the care of patients and families with higher acuity and more complex health problems and situations. Concepts will be explored through the lens of nursing theory, social justice, and DEI. Clinical experiences will include simulation, acute care facilities, and other health care agencies. Demonstrated competency in course concepts and selected clinical skills is required for successful completion of this course.

Other Requirements / Notes: Demonstrated competency in course concepts and selected clinical skills is required for successful completion of this course. Demonstrated competency in selected clinical skills is required for successful completion of this course.
Full Course Description: The focus of this course will be on clinical judgment, critical reasoning, and competency in clinical skills related to women’s health, pregnancy, and care of the infant and families through adolescence. Concepts will be explored through the lens of nursing theory, social justice, and DEI. Clinical experiences will include simulation, clinical skills lab, acute care facilities and other community settings. Demonstrated competency in course concepts and selected clinical skills is required for successful completion of this course. 4143 Nursing Leadership and Coordination of Care 1 hour The focus of this course will be on clinical judgment, critical reasoning, concepts of leadership and management, and competency in clinical skills related to the complexity of patient care requiring multiple providers across settings. Concepts will be explored through the lens of nursing theory, social justice, and DEI. Clinical experiences will include simulation, clinical skills lab, acute care facilities, and other health care agencies. Demonstrated competency in selected clinical skills is required for successful completion of this course.

Other Requirements / Notes: Demonstrated competency in course concepts and selected clinical skills is required for successful completion of this course.
Full Course Description: The focus of this course will be on clinical judgment, critical reasoning, and competency in clinical skills related to community, rural and public health to improve the overall health of individuals, families, and communities. Concepts will be explored through the lens of nursing theory, social justice, and DEI. Clinical experiences will include simulation, clinical skills lab, community, rural or public health settings, community health centers, local health departments and community service organizations. Demonstrated competency in course concepts and selected clinical skills is required for successful completion of this course.

Other Requirements / Notes: Demonstrated competency in KSAs of the professional nurse and a 90% predictability for passing the NCLEX-RN exam is required for successful completion of this course.
Full Course Description: The focus on this Culminating Experience is to provide an integrative experience applying all dimensions of the professional nurse in the care of diverse patients, families, and communities across a variety of healthcare settings. Major competencies and concepts explored as part of the Nursing program are addressed and reviewed in preparation for licensure and transition into professional practice utilizing a lens of nursing theory, social justice, and DEI. Lab experiences will include simulation, NCLEX-RN examination preparation and testing. Demonstrated competency in KSAs of the professional nurse and a 90% predictability for passing the NCLEX-RN exam is required for successful completion of this course.

Other Requirements / Notes: Demonstrated competency in acquired KSAs required for successful completion of this course.
Full Course Description: The focus of this Culminating Experience is to provide students with opportunities to translate, integrate, and apply available evidence to the care of patients. Emphasis is on synthesis of KSAs acquired through the Nursing program utilizing evidence-based, culturally appropriate nursing care to patients, families, and communities of diverse backgrounds. Focus is on transition from student role to entry- level professional nursing practice. Clinical experiences will include simulation and a precepted practicum in acute care facilities and other health care agencies. Demonstrated competency in acquired KSAs required for successful completion of this course.

Other Requirements / Notes: This course satisfies the General Education Culminating Experience requirement.
Full Course Description: This course is designed as a precepted integrative clinical providing the opportunity to synthesize foundational concepts and master the competencies and skills of the advanced beginner in professional nursing. The focus is on integration of one’s professional nursing identity within the complex organizational environment in which health care is delivered. This course satisfies the General Education Culminating Experience requirement. 2 hours Theory and 3 hours Clinical (90 hours Independent Practicum). Two credit hours for Theory; Three credit hours for Clinical hours equates 90 clinical hours a semester.

Other Requirements / Notes: This introductory-level course pursues an understanding of understanding.
Full Course Description: This introductory-level course pursues an understanding of understanding. More specifically, it focuses on what becoming educated involves, on what learning in a variety of disciplines re-quires. Put another way, the primary purpose of the course is to encourage attention to our own minds at work. But minding our minds is not easy. For the most part, we have been educated out of our minds. That is, prior schooling routinely neglected attention to questioning, guessing, evaluation; instead, it emphasized answers, new vocabulary, prepackaged information. The result, not surprisingly, is that what is closest to us (our own thinking) remains most remote (unfamiliar). The course challenges this neglect of our own minds. Fall.

Other Requirements / Notes: While logic has fundamental applications in law, mathematics and computer science, we derive its greatest benefit in increased sensitivity to, understanding of and ability to use language.
Full Course Description: The use of reasoning to construct an argument is one of the most powerful tools available to seekers of wisdom. Starting from certain premises, one builds to a conclusion. Each step in reasoning comprises an inference. Logic is the study of reasoning, of argument and inference. It is basic to all the sciences and humanities as well as to philosophy and mathematics. While logic has fundamental applications in law, mathematics and computer science, we derive its greatest benefit in increased sensitivity to, understanding of and ability to use language. Logic can help our communication skills and help us develop convincing defenses of our viewpoints. It can help us better understand what we read and hear and reduce our gullibility. This introductory course assumes no prior study of logic. Fall, odd years.

This course covers a period of almost a thousand years, beginning with the Greeks circa 600 B.C. and continuing through the transition from the classical to the medieval period. Students are introduced to the ways major philosophical and religious figures in the ancient world variously understood God, human beings and the universe. It is thus the study of the intellectual roots of Western civilization. Emphasis is on primary sources with concentration on the Pre-Socratics, Socrates, Plato, Aristotle and the transition to a distinctively Christian worldview as represented by St. Thomas Aquinas. Fall, odd years.
Surveys the modern period of Western philosophy with special attention to Descartes, Hume, Kant, Hegel, Marx, Kierkegaard and Nietzsche. Spring, even years.

This course will focus on contemporary philosophy either by surveying a single intellectual movement (e.g., phenomenology, analytical philosophy, poststructuralism) or by selecting several major philosophers representative of different movements (e.g., Heidegger, Davidson, Derrida). Fall, even years. 2329+ Ethical Analysis 3 hours Philosophical ethics can be described as the attempt to think clearly and deeply about fundamental moral questions that arise for us as humans. Ethics is concerned with evaluating appropriate action, proper character, the characteristics of the good life and what is involved in acting rightly. The course explores readings in foundational ethical theory, including the systematic analysis of moral beliefs, as well as the application of ethical theory to particular issues in applied ethics, such as punishment and suicide, physician-assisted suicide, and euthanasia. Fall. Spring. 3301+ Legal Ethics 3 hours The readings and course activities will help students to understand (1) the relationship of law and morality, (2) notions of justice in different legal philosophies, (3) the potential corruption of law by ideology, and (4) contemporary ethical quandaries of practitioners. Fall. 3302+ Ethics and Public Policy 3 hours The course is divided into three sections: (1) the foundations of ethics and public policy; (2) the methodical analysis of policy proposals; and (3) the application of theory and method to con-temporary controversies. The primary learning objectives are (a) recognizing in ourselves the operations of intelligent planning and moral deciding that are preconditions to effective policymaking and (b) making deliberate and controlled use of these operations in clarifying and criticizing policy recommendations. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: Different philosophical topics may be offered. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. 3311+ Medical Ethics 3 hours In this course students will explore moral concerns and ethical decision making in medicine and health care. Topics include, but are not limited to, health care reform, informed consent, competency, surrogate decision making, quality-of-life decision making, decisions about allowing death, futility and allocation of scarce health care resources. The class will also analyze concerns regarding equity, justice and individual rights to health care. 3312+ Environmental Ethics 3 hours In this course students will explore moral concerns and ethical decision making with regard to the environment. Topics include, but are not limited to, individual, corporate and state freedoms and responsibilities, permissible uses of animals, and comparison of strategies for protecting natural resources, as well as case studies regarding equity, justice, and individual and community rights to environmental resources. 3313+ Business Ethics 3 hours In this course students will explore moral concerns and ethical decision making in business circumstances and the corporate environment. Topics include, but are not limited to, individual and corporate freedoms and responsibilities, the virtues and vices of corporate behavior, and individual and corporate rights, as well as case studies regarding equity and justice.

Other Requirements / Notes: From a variety of perspectives, the class studies how different communities have experienced and understood human spirituality and how they have symbolized, evaluated and acted upon that understanding.
Full Course Description: This course explores the complex relations between religion and culture. From a variety of perspectives, the class studies how different communities have experienced and understood human spirituality and how they have symbolized, evaluated and acted upon that understanding. Spring, odd years.

Other Requirements / Notes: The course is open to all undergraduates, but upper-division standing and some familiarity with major figures in intellectual history are recommended.
Full Course Description: In keeping with the great books tradition, sections of this course offer opportunities for reading and discussing classic texts of Western civilization. Reading lists vary, so this course may be repeat-ed for credit. The course is open to all undergraduates, but upper-division standing and some familiarity with major figures in intellectual history are recommended.

This course investigates the complex process of human knowing, its basic structure and its limitations. Emphasis is placed on the students’ discovery of their own cognitional abilities as a basis for evaluating conflicting theories about human knowing. Fall, even years.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: PHIL 2303 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Building on the basic skills of PHIL 2303, this course offers more- advanced consideration of sequential logic, first-order predicate logic and modal logic (logic of possibility and necessity). Topics will include complex argument recognition, construction, structure and evaluation, as well as meta-theoretical topics, such as the soundness and completeness of logical systems. Prerequisite: PHIL 2303 or permission of instructor.

This course will introduce students to basic themes in the philosophy of science. Topics to be covered include, but are not limited to, (1) Aristotle’s philosophy of science; (2) the medieval refinement and critique of Aristotle’s philosophy of science; (3) the competitive interplay of the Scholastic, mechanistic and Newtonian philosophies of science during the scientific revolution; (4) the philosophical debates during the Darwinian revolution among competing accounts of everything from empirical data and axiology to theology; (5) the nature of scientific change (with particular emphasis on Thomas Kuhn’s work); (6) scientific explanation (with attention given to covering law models, pragmatism and agent causation); (7) scientific confirmation and the problem of induction (with special attention paid to Hume’s famous critique of induction and subsequent responses to his challenge); and (8) the realism/anti-realism debate (emphasizing inter alia van Fraassen’s constructive empiricism and Churchland’s realist critique).

Other Requirements / Notes: Although not required, it is recommended that students enrolling in Directed Readings have a prior back-ground in the history of philosophy.
Full Course Description: Special elective: Research for advanced students in primary works of philosophy. Although not required, it is recommended that students enrolling in Directed Readings have a prior back-ground in the history of philosophy. Fall.

Course modeled on the graduate seminar: Primary sources researched and the results defended. Specialized interests of advanced Philosophy students are provided with a forum for exchange and debate. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: If this course is taken as part of the Professional Ethics minor, an experiential learning project is required. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course allows students to apply their knowledge of philosophy to a work situation. If this course is taken as part of the Professional Ethics minor, an experiential learning project is required. The project should involve active participation in organizational activities and written reports. The Philosophy faculty will be responsible for approving the design and for evaluating the projects. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

Other Requirements / Notes: Significant time in lab required on weekly basis.
Full Course Description: This is an introductory course in analog photography and visual literacy with an emphasis on discovery and creativity. Students learn about film, light and basic camera operation and are introduced to black-and- white processes. No prior experience is necessary. Significant time in lab required on weekly basis. Students should plan to spend approximately $300 on materials over the course of semester. Fall. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: VISU 1311 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Intermediate Digital Imaging is a project-based digital shooting, post- production, and printing course that invites students to experiment with formats possibly new to them such as large multiple-image pieces, choreographed panoramic scenes, and sequential images in basic book forms. Students will build on post-production skills gained in Visual Studies I to include color management and ICC profiling of camera, monitor, and inkjet printer/paper sets. Students will work on a number of smaller assigned projects as well as a larger self-designed semester project. Primary software programs for this course are Lightroom, Photoshop, and InDesign. Primary production is exhibition-quality inkjet prints in various forms with the semester project serving as a demonstration of both aesthetic power and technical excellence. Prerequisite: VISU 1311 or permission of instructor. Spring.

This course explores the uses of photography in the humanities, emphasizing documentary photography and its social and historical significance. This course will also examine the relationship between words and images using classic examples of books utilizing photos and text; the ethical relationship between photographers and subjects is also discussed. Students will produce a portfolio centered on a class project.

Tracing the history of photography over some 200 years, this course lays a foundation both for students of photography but also for those of the history and practice of the broader visual arts. The course contextualizes the technology, practice, social functioning, and critical evaluation of photography while concurrently providing a journey through its rich visual archive. Lectures, diverse resources, writing, and field trips contribute to the student’s engagement with photography’s history and—first and foremost—with the object at its center. Recommended prerequisite: ARTS 2304. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: VISU 1311 or permission of instructor. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: An introduction to theory and technical aspects of video art and production with emphasis on the aesthetic and communicative potential of the medium. This course emphasizes the development of narrative content, as well as compositional, camera, and digital-video-editing skills. Prerequisite: VISU 1311 or permission of instructor. Spring. 2399 Special Topics in Photography and Media Arts 3 hours This course allows students to pursue a special topic within photography or related media. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

Prerequisites: VISU 1311 or PHOT 1318 or permission of instructor based on portfolio review.
Other Requirements / Notes: Students will view relevant contemporary and historical works of significance and will complete assignments and projects to enhance their understanding of the course topic. May be repeated for credit as topics change. Prerequisite: VISU 1311 or PHOT 1318 or permission of instructor based on portfolio review.
Full Course Description: This course will focus on varied technical or stylistic topics selected by the instructor in the areas of photography and related media. Topics may include advanced black-and-white printing, alternative printing processes, portfolio production, editorial photography, artists’ books, presentation methods, multimedia projects, and other stylistic and subject-based topics. Students will view relevant contemporary and historical works of significance and will complete assignments and projects to enhance their understanding of the course topic. May be repeated for credit as topics change. Prerequisite: VISU 1311 or PHOT 1318 or permission of instructor based on portfolio review. Fall. Spring.

Prerequisites: PHOT 1318 and VISU 1311 or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: PHOT 1318 and VISU 1311 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Large Camera focuses on use, characteristics, and creative potential of the view camera and large format material as tools for artistic expression. Initially building on darkroom film skills, this course connects traditional film to contemporary as well as historical processes. Students receive instruction in photographing in the field, production of high quality scans from large format negatives, and inkjet printing. Additionally, this course will introduce students to alternative processes – e.g., cyanotype, Vandyke brown, palladium – and production of digital negatives from large camera originals. Along with technical skills, emphasis is given to the image as a tool for communication. Prerequisites: PHOT 1318 and VISU 1311 or permission of instructor. Fall.

Prerequisites: PHOT 1318 and VISU 1311 or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: PHOT 1318 and VISU 1311 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: With an emphasis on the descriptive and narrative qualities light can impart to scene and subject and the use of images as powerful tools for communication, this course introduces students to professional use of studio and location lighting and production techniques. Emphasis will be on mastering the shaping of available and added light to include extensive use of electronic strobe and light modifiers. Based on creative assignments and individual interests, students will have opportunity and gain skills to engage with subject matter including portraiture, still-life, and contemporary fashion. In addition to gaining proficiency in the forming of light, students will learn how to create camera-specific ICC profiles, work with tethered capture, and develop appropriate post- production workflows. Prerequisites: PHOT 1318 and VISU 1311 or permission of instructor. Spring.

Course Title: Internship in Photography and Media Arts
Other Requirements / Notes: A required course for advanced students who have a working knowledge of the various aspects of the photographic process. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: A required course for advanced students who have a working knowledge of the various aspects of the photographic process. Students approved for an internship work either with professional photographers, publishers, advertisers, graphic designers and archivists or with other relevant professionals, chosen by the student in consultation with the instructor. Through the internship, students gain career-related experience in a real-world setting and prepare for the school-to-work transition. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor. Fall. Spring. Summer.

Course Title: Topics in Photography and Media Arts
Full Course Description: In this course students will engage in independent creative research on advanced topics; undertake advanced projects and/or complete portfolio work relevant to their career goals. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: While few equations are used, some basic algebra will be required. Satisfies the General Education Natural Sciences requirement.
Full Course Description: This course is intended for the non-Science major. It provides an overview of physics with a particular emphasis on topics that relate to policy decisions. Topics include energy, nuclear process-es, space flight, climate change, relativity, and technology derived from the principles of quantum mechanics. While few equations are used, some basic algebra will be required. Satisfies the General Education Natural Sciences requirement.

Other Requirements / Notes: Elementary principles of fluid mechanics, traveling and standing waves, vibrating bodies, and acoustical phenomena are considered.
Full Course Description: An introduction to kinematics and Newtonian mechanics that emphasizes vector methods and applies concepts from calculus. Elementary principles of fluid mechanics, traveling and standing waves, vibrating bodies, and acoustical phenomena are considered. Prerequisite: MATH 2312 or higher. Recommended prerequisite: MATH 2413. Corequisite: PHYS 2125. Fall.

A survey of the basic concepts of electricity and magnetism followed by elementary concepts of reflection, refraction, lenses, interference, diffraction, polarization and propagation of light. A brief introduction to radioactivity and resultant radiation. Prerequisite: PHYS 2320. Corequisite: PHYS 2126. Spring.

Experiments in mechanics and waves. Includes experience with computer-assisted data recording, analysis and graphing; basic considerations of experimental design; introduction to report writing. Corequisite: PHYS 2320 or PHYS 2325. Fall.

Topics include fundamentals of classical mechanics. Primarily for science or pre-engineering students. Calculus is used in derivations and problem solving. Pre- or corequisite: MATH 2413. Corequisite: PHYS 2125. Fall.

Topics include fundamentals of classical mechanics. Primarily for science or pre-engineering students. Calculus is used in derivations and problem solving. Pre- or corequisite: MATH 2413. Corequisite: PHYS 2125. Fall.

Experiments involving electricity, magnetism and optics. Continued emphasis on computer-assisted data recording, analysis and graphing; instruction on experimental design; additional practice in report writing. Corequisite: PHYS 2321 or PHYS 2326. Spring.

4 hours Topics include fundamentals of electricity, magnetism and light. Primarily for science or pre-engineering students. Calculus is used in derivations and problem solving. Prerequisite: PHYS 2325. Pre- or corequisite: MATH 2414. Corequisite: PHYS 2126. Spring.

4 hours Topics include fundamentals of electricity, magnetism and light. Primarily for science or pre-engineering students. Calculus is used in derivations and problem solving. Prerequisite: PHYS 2325. Pre- or corequisite: MATH 2414. Corequisite: PHYS 2126. Spring.

Corequisites: CHEM 3336 or PHYS 3336. Cross- listed with CHEM 3136.
Full Course Description: An introduction to experimental and computational techniques used in the study of thermodynamics and quantum mechanics. Students will explore the behavior of real and ideal gases, heat release during combustion, rotational and vibrational signatures of small polyatomic molecules and more. Corequisite: CHEM 3336 or PHYS 3336. Cross- listed with CHEM 3136. Spring. .

Prerequisites: CHEM 2320 or CHEM 2334, MATH 2414 and PHYS 2326.
Corequisites: CHEM 3136 or PHYS 3136. Cross-listed with CHEM 3336.
Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with CHEM 3336.
Full Course Description: An introduction to the fundamental concepts in thermodynamics and quantum mechanics. The first half of the course covers gaseous behavior and the laws of thermodynamics. The second half of the course covers the origin of quantum mechanics and steps through foundational systems including a particle in a box, the harmonic oscillator, and the Hydrogen atom. Prerequisites: CHEM 2320 or CHEM 2334, MATH 2414 and PHYS 2326. Corequisite: CHEM 3136 or PHYS 3136. Cross-listed with CHEM 3336. Spring.

Prerequisites: CHEM 3336, MATH 2321 and PHYS 2326. Cross-listed with CHEM 3337.
Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with CHEM 3337.
Full Course Description: A survey of physical chemistry topics with an emphasis on real-world challenges and interdisciplinary problem solving. Core topics rotate but may include kinetics and dynamics, chemical equilibrium, solution and solid-phase chemistry, and advanced spectroscopies. Prerequisites: CHEM 3336, MATH 2321 and PHYS 2326. Cross-listed with CHEM 3337. Fall.

Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with BINF 3338, COSC 3338, and MATH 3338.
Full Course Description: A survey of standard techniques used to solve numerical and other science-based problems. Topics include roots of equations, systems of linear equations, curve fitting, numerical integration and differentiation, error analysis, number representation, and data visualization. Problems will be solved using programs written in appropriate languages and operating systems. Cross-listed with BINF 3338, COSC 3338, and MATH 3338. Prerequisites: MATH 2413 and COSC 1305 or COSC 1423. Offered on sufficient demand.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: Topics are selected based on needs and interests of students and can vary from year to year. The 4-credit-hour option must also include a substantial lab component. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. A specific course description will be published at the time of offering. Specific topic entered on transcript. Prerequisites: Vary by topic. Offered on sufficient demand.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: A multi-semester course for students conducting research. Content varies with semester. Students will learn research methodology and how to search relevant literature and databases within physics, read and analyze research articles, identify a feasible research problem, craft a proposal and summary of the question, and present this proposal in written and oral form. Students will conduct an independent investigation of their research question under the supervision of a faculty member, documenting 40 hours of work per term, and will present their findings in written and oral form each semester. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: The course involves a series of lectures designed to broaden understanding of the nature and processes of American government.
Full Course Description: An overview of the dynamics and structure of American national government and the American political system. The course involves a series of lectures designed to broaden understanding of the nature and processes of American government.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: A consideration of the structure and dynamics of state and local governments in the United States. Particular attention is given to Texas state government, but it is compared with the political cultures and styles of the other 49 states.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: Since its invention in the 1890s, cinema has been closely linked to American life through its ability to reflect on and shape the course of events. Movies, as a form of entertainment, encapsulate the beliefs, values, struggles, and aspirations of both their audiences and creators. The democratic nature of American society makes the exploration of political cinema especially interesting. Movies and politics are not the same, but they are closely related. This course examines how movies and politics intersect and interact. We will focus on how they inform each other, what each tells us about the other, and how cinematic and political trends are intertwined with the society and the era from which they come. We will discuss what movies say about politics in America, how they say it, and what their contribution and legacy to the political culture has become. 2320 Political Management: Campaigns and Crises3 hours This course will focus on the role and responsibilities of contemporary campaign professionals, particularly at the state and local levels. Students will be exposed to the latest strategies and tactics of political campaigns, as taught by some of their leading practitioners. The course will also examine the management of crisis situations in electoral, legislative and public policy campaigns and help develop some of the techniques useful to predict, identify and manage such situations.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: The Civics Lab provides students the opportunity to engage in practical and applied civics activities that work toward solving public policy dilemmas in Austin, throughout Texas, and on the national level.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: The Civics Lab provides students the opportunity to engage in practical and applied civics activities that work toward solving public policy dilemmas in Austin, throughout Texas, and on the national level.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: Topics are selected to coincide with the political environment, student interests, and on areas of faculty expertise. This fulfills course requirements in the Applied Politics and Political and Civic Engagement concentrations of the Political Science major. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: International Relations is the study of interactions of countries, international organizations, multinational corporations, terrorist groups and other entities across international borders. This course will introduce students to the major theories of international relations, which in turn attempt to explain such phenomenon as the causes of international conflict and its resolution, the international political economy, international law and organizations, and the processes of globalization. International Relations will help prepare students for such upper-division courses as American Foreign Relations, International Political Economy, International Security and Conflict Resolution, International Organizations and Strategic Intelligence and Covert Action. Fall. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: Comparative Politics is the study of the similarities and differences of political systems within countries around the world. This course introduces students to the concepts and methods necessary to systematically compare and contrast different political systems and governments. Students will learn about various forms of democratic and non-democratic governments, the differences be-tween parliamentary and presidential systems, and the processes of political change leading to democracy or to authoritarianism. Through a brief overview of a group of diverse countries, students will examine the role of ideology, political institutions, political economy, political culture, participation and representation, and public policy in different regions and different types of systems. Comparative Politics is not only a stand-alone introduction to the field but will also help prepare students for upper-division courses on the politics of Asia, Europe, Latin America, the Middle East and Africa. Fall. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: The course emphasizes critical reading, analytical thinking, and discussion to deepen understanding of complex political concepts.
Full Course Description: This course provides a foundational exploration of political theory, examining the enduring questions and ideas that have shaped governance, society, and justice throughout history. Students will engage with primary texts from influential thinkers, gaining insights into the philosophical underpinnings of political systems and ideologies. The course emphasizes critical reading, analytical thinking, and discussion to deepen understanding of complex political concepts.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences

Full Course Description: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

Full Course Description: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

Full Course Description: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: From founding member of the Roman Empire to current member of the European Union, over the course of the past two millennia Spain has often been near the center of global events. This course will follow Spain’s political development, focusing on domestic Spanish politics, which have been a laboratory of political phenomenon: military coups and governments; struggles be-tween liberal democracy, reactionary monarchism, fascism, Communism, and anarchism; regional nationalism; and, most recently, its remarkable transition to democracy that served as a model for countries of South America and Eastern Europe. It has also been at the center of processes of globalization from Roman antiquity to the Arab Empire of the Middle Ages to the era of discovery and finally to the modern European Union.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course will examine the major economic, political, and social developments of Brazil in the 20th and 21st centuries. It will concentrate on the broad themes of state-led industrialization and economic management; the alternation of authoritarian politics and democratic government; and the multiple challenges brought on by high levels of social inequality. Students will be able to explain the ways in which politics, economics and society have interacted to shape contemporary Brazil and understand the opportunities and challenges that globalization creates for Brazil and other developing countries.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Course Title: Politics and Governments of the Middle East
Full Course Description: In this course, we will discuss various Middle Eastern countries and their experience with democracy. Among the questions addressed in the course will be: Are there any preconditions for the emergence of democracy? What roles do wealth and inequality play in democratization? Are there any cultural causes for the emergence of democracy? Are Islam and democracy compatible?

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Course Title: Politics and Governments of Latin America
Full Course Description: This course will survey the politics and governmental systems of a representative selection of countries from South and Central America and the Caribbean. While the course will cover the various types of governmental institutions prevalent in Latin America, the primary focus will be on political development and change, in particular emphasizing the role of the military, the Catholic Church, economic interests, indigenous groups and foreign actors.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course will survey the politics and governmental systems of a representative selection of countries from Asia and the Pacific region. While the course will cover the various types of governmental institutions prevalent in Asia, a special emphasis will be placed on the complex relationship between economics and politics domestically within each of the countries and the role Asia plays internationally in the process of globalization. Given the ever-growing influence of the Asian economies, this course will be of interest to anyone wanting to learn more about the region specifically, and about international political economy and international politics generally.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course offers an introductory survey of the politics of global economic relations. It will examine the role international and domestic politics have on the issues of international trade, the international monetary system, foreign investment and labor movements and on relations between the “haves and have-nots.” Besides a focus on such international actors as the IMF, the World Bank and the WTO, this course will examine the role of multinational corporations and intergovernmental organizations like the EU and NAFTA and finally questions surrounding the issue of economic globalization. Prerequisite: POLS 2332.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: The general objective of this course is to develop and enhance the ability of students to plan, execute, report and understand empirical political research. Some of the topics covered in this applied statistics course: frequency distribution, measures of central tendency, measures of variability, regression and correlation. Corequisite: MATH 0104 or satisfactory placement.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course will survey the politics and governmental systems of a representative selection of countries from Europe as well as the institutions and policies of the European Union. While the course will cover the various types of governmental institutions prevalent in Europe, the primary focus will be on political development and change, in particular emphasizing the role of political culture, society, economic interests and international politics. Hence, The Politics and Governments of Europe and the European Union ought to engage not only students of Europe but those who contemplate a career in foreign affairs or international politics. Fall.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: Public officials and citizens need to be able to understand and address critical issues in order for our democracy to remain strong. This course introduces students to the policy making process and applies this process to contemporary policy areas. Students will think critically about controversial issues, and understand how the discourse surrounding those issues influences government action.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: A critical study of American foreign relations. The course emphasizes those relations that the United States has had with other states from the earliest days to the present post–Cold War era. At the end of the course the student will be capable of separating the rhetoric from the reality of the American political tradition.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course introduces students to U.S. constitutional law and Supreme Court decision making in cases involving federalism and the separation of powers. We will learn how to interpret and analyze some of the nation’s most fundamental rules as understood by the justices on the Court, especially rules pertaining to who holds authority in the American political system. Rulings will be viewed in the context of what was going on in American politics at the time and will help students assess the role and impact of the Supreme Court in the evolution of American democracy. 3338 International Security and Conflict Resolution 3 hours Peace and conflict issues are core topics in global studies. While international conflict and war seem to be a constant in the human experience, in fact much of our everyday global interaction is marked by peaceful exchanges and cooperation. This course will provide a theoretical overview of the debates associated with conflict and peace studies, focusing in particular on the causes of war and the nature of diplomacy. It will examine the evolution of, and trends in, warfare, as well as the consequences of war and proposed solutions to help prevent or limit war.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course will deal broadly with relations between the United States and the countries of Latin America. Students will explore different theories used to explain and analyze U.S. Latin American relations and then critically evaluate American actions abroad. The course will address such issues as the role and impact of U.S. intervention in the region, the promotion of democracy, the drug trade, and economic integration.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course examines approaches to race and ethnicity within political science scholarship. Race and ethnicity not only affect political behaviors and attitudes but also shape a country’s political institutions. The primary context of this course will be in the United States, but other comparative case studies will also be explored.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course offers an overview of nonprofit organizations and interest group tactics in the public policy process. The course is concerned with the activities and resources nonprofits and interest groups utilize to influence public policy decisions. The course will focus on the process through advocacy activities, which include direct lobbying and indirect grassroots efforts and public relations (to influence public opinion).

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with ENSP 3399.
Full Course Description: This course is designed to introduce you to America’s attempts to regulate the environment. The primary goal of the course is to provide participants with the tools to find the law, interpret the law and use it. After an initial introduction to the American legal system itself, the majority of the coursework will involve the reading and discussion of environmental legislation, regulations and cases at the U.S. federal level; focus will be made at the end of the course on state environmental law. Particular emphasis will be placed on the legislative model as a tool to address environmental problems. Cross-listed with ENSP 3399.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course examines factors which influence the development and expression of public opinion, voter turnout, partisanship and electoral choices in the context of recent elections. Students will examine the determinants of vote choice and their outcomes on elections and policy. Special emphasis will be placed on the role of the media, as well as the importance of informal and formal electoral institutions.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course educates students about the fundamental aspects of the United States Congress. This knowledge will provide a foundation enabling students to understand and evaluate Congress and the laws it passes. The course will focus on both the historical development of Congress and current trends and events. Topics range from elections to the party structure to the committee system.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course examines the history and evolution of the modern party system. It focuses on the organization and activities of the parties, including the various ways that they interact with citizens. It explores the ideological positions and socioeconomic composition of the parties, as well as their influence among public officials.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Semester Offered: offered simultaneously with the biennial sessions of the Texas legislature, which will provide a laboratory for those enrolled.
Full Course Description: An examination of the state and national legislative bodies as they fulfill their representative and legislative functions in the American political system. The course is offered simultaneously with the biennial sessions of the Texas legislature, which will provide a laboratory for those enrolled. Spring, odd years.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course will examine media coverage of public officials, political campaigns, legislative activities, interest groups and several issues of public policy. Students will learn how to design news-paper, radio and television ads for candidates or interest groups. Discussion of strategies for getting favorable news coverage for an issue or candidate.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: An assessment of the development and powers of the American presidency as the central leadership institution of the United States government and political system. Analysis of the presidential character, impeachment politics, presidential decision making and the merchandising of presidents will be stressed.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: In this class we examine the ways in which the Constitution protects individual rights while accommodating the often-competing claims of groups and communities. One of the controversial jurisprudential issues surrounding the work of the modern court has to do with the role of the judiciary in performing as an occasional counter-majoritarian institution in defense of individual rights. For example, if it is important to defend a right like privacy against the intrusive reach of the state, must all rights be so defended with equal vigilance? Is there a principled way to distinguish among rights, say between speech and the right to bear arms, such that the court would be justified in treating them differently as far as a constitutional defense is concerned? By the end of the course students should have an informed judgment on such questions, which is to say, on the role of the Supreme Court in contemporary American politics.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: A reading-lecture-discussion course examining the various schools of thought across the American political spectrum. Although there is some consideration of Hamiltonian and Jeffersonian approaches to political thinking, most of the time will be spent examining current views, a time of much rethinking and reformulating of political ideas and attitudes.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Junior standing.
Full Course Description: Internships are designed to make supervised, practical experience of the political world available to students. The Austin area offers many placements in the agencies and branches of city, county and state government. The internship is not restricted to majors, but majors are expected to do an internship. Prerequisite: Junior standing. Fall. Spring. Summer.

The Austin area offers many placements in the agencies and branches of city, county and state government. The internship is not restricted to majors, but majors are expected to do an internship. Prerequisite: Junior standing. Fall. Spring. Summer.
