Full Course Description: Internships are designed to make supervised, practical experience of the political world available to students. The Austin area offers many placements in the agencies and branches of city, county and state government. The internship is not restricted to majors, but majors are expected to do an internship. Prerequisite: Junior standing. Fall. Spring. Summer.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: Strategic intelligence is perhaps among the most glamorized and consequently least understood instruments of foreign policy. The concept of strategic intelligence embraces a wide variety of activities and processes, including the covert and overt collection of information, counter-intelligence and counter-espionage efforts, and covert operations. This course covers the role intelligence has played in American foreign relations over the past century as well as the evolution of the profession of intelligence and the associated intelligence community.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies the Culminating Experience for Political Science majors. Prerequisite: Senior standing.
Full Course Description: While content varies from semester-to-semester, course requirements include a substantial research project on a topic approved by the instructor demonstrating the integration and application of knowledge of theories and debates within the discipline of political science. Satisfies the Culminating Experience for Political Science majors. Prerequisite: Senior standing.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course provides an in-depth analysis into the politics of Austin, with the goal of developing public strategies for addressing and solving urban and suburban problems in the central Texas region. The course pays particular attention to the region’s population and economic growth, the diversity in Austin, and the challenges of a growing wealth disparity in addressing issues of equality in Austin and central Texas, along with exploring the barriers preventing some from equitable access to the policy process.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: Topics are selected to coincide with the political environment, student interests, and on areas of faculty expertise. This fulfills course requirements in the Applied Politics and Political and Civic Engagement concentrations of the Political Science major. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Open to first-year students.
Full Course Description: This course introduces the scientific study of the mind and behavior. Topics include the history of psychology, sensation and perception, learning, motivation and emotion, the biological bases of behavior, intelligence, human development, personality, mental disorders, and basic concepts of statistics and research design. Open to first-year students.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This introductory course surveys the field of forensic psychology. The role of forensic psychologists in court systems, law enforcement agencies, correctional programs, criminal investigations, and victim services will be studied. Other topics will include the historical development of forensic psychology and current controversies in the field.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course is an introduction to the psychology of human sexuality. It is broad in scope and covers a variety of topics, such as female and male physiology, sexual orientation, sexual expression and variation, sexual difficulties, birth control, reproduction, and many others. A primary goal of this course is for students to develop a greater awareness and acceptance of their own sexuality and the sexuality of others, as well as to become more comfortable with and knowledgeable about the topic of sexual behavior.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Theories regarding adolescent development and the application of scientific studies to understanding adolescent behavior are emphasized.
Full Course Description: The physical, intellectual, social, emotional and moral development of adolescents is considered in this course. Theories regarding adolescent development and the application of scientific studies to understanding adolescent behavior are emphasized. The principles and methods of guiding adolescents in their educational, vocational, social and personal problems are also discussed. Prerequisite: PSYC 2301 (except for teacher certification candidates).

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course presents an overview of the multiple factors affecting a child’s total development. The interaction of physical, cognitive, emotional and psychosocial factors across the span of child-hood are discussed. Prerequisite: PSYC 2301 (except for teacher certification candidates).

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Theories regarding growth, development, and change over time, and the application of scientific studies to understanding human behavior, are emphasized.
Full Course Description: The physical, intellectual, social, emotional, and personality development of humans are considered in this course. Theories regarding growth, development, and change over time, and the application of scientific studies to understanding human behavior, are emphasized. Prerequisite: PSYC 2301.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course provides students with the tools to understand individual differences in thought, emotion and behavior by examining major theories of personality and the empirical evidence that supports those theories. Students will be equipped to understand the assumptions various personality theories make, to apply different theories to individuals and groups of people, and to critically evaluate the strengths and weaknesses of personality theories. Prerequisite: PSYC 2301.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Prerequisites: Proficiency in basic arithmetical operations. A grade of C or higher is required for Psychology and Behavioral Neuroscience majors.
Other Requirements / Notes: Topics include understanding and creating frequency distributions and graphs, scales of measurement, measures of central tendency, measures of variability, Analysis of Variance, t-tests, chi square, correlation, and the selection of appropriate inferential techniques. A grade of C or higher is required for Psychology and Behavioral Neuroscience majors.
Full Course Description: This course examines basic statistical methods for analyzing the results of studies conducted in psychological science. Topics include understanding and creating frequency distributions and graphs, scales of measurement, measures of central tendency, measures of variability, Analysis of Variance, t-tests, chi square, correlation, and the selection of appropriate inferential techniques. Prerequisite: Proficiency in basic arithmetical operations. A grade of C or higher is required for Psychology and Behavioral Neuroscience majors. Corequisite: MATH 0104 or satisfactory placement.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with SOCW 2320.
Full Course Description: This course builds practical skills for effective interpersonal communication, supportive counseling, efficient interviewing, active listening and impactful mentoring. The course will cover the problem- solving process, issues of diversity, the importance of a nonjudgmental attitude, and related ethical principles. The course is intended to provide skills that will enhance students’ ability to later work with people in community service or in internships. Cross-listed with SOCW 2320.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course focuses on the physiological foundations of behavior through the exploration of topics such as electrical and chemical transmission of nerves, neuroanatomy, nervous system development, and the basic anatomy and physiology underlying sensation and perception in the human nervous system.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Students should gain a better understanding of how drugs operate in the brain and in the body to produce psychological and physiological effects. Cross-listed with SOCW 2326.
Full Course Description: Students will discuss issues of drug use and abuse, and the effects of drugs on behavior. Students will also evaluate both human and animal research relevant to chemical dependency. Students should gain a better understanding of how drugs operate in the brain and in the body to produce psychological and physiological effects. The roles that drugs play in societies and some of the clinical models used to treat those who use drugs are also discussed. Contemporary issues — such as the link between chemical abuse and crime, our society’s war on drugs, legalization proposals, allocation of priorities and resources, and changing definitions of addiction — will be explored. Cross-listed with SOCW 2326.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This course addresses special topics in psychological science. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This course addresses special topics in psychological science. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This course addresses special topics in psychological science. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: PSYC 2301 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course looks at the application of psychological theories and principles to the workplace. Employee selection, management of people in particular jobs, workgroup interactions, employee satisfaction and employee motivation are addressed. Research into consumer behavior, the relationship of human beings to machines, and the influence of the work environment on worker productivity are also considered. Prerequisite: PSYC 2301 or permission of instructor.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course examines research findings related to human behavior in a social context. Topics include person perception, social cognition, nonverbal communication, attitude change, attraction and close relationships, conformity, obedience, interpersonal attraction, and aggression, among others. Prerequisite: PSYC 2301.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: This course provides the knowledge base for understanding one’s own family in a broader societal context.
Full Course Description: This course examines families and intimate relationships in society. Topics include dating, marriage and partnerships, parenting, multigenerational relationships, care work, relationship violence, and divorce and blended families. Current research findings related to each topic will be covered. Cultural diversity in family and relationship dynamics will be emphasized. This course provides the knowledge base for understanding one’s own family in a broader societal context. Cross- listed with SOCI 3327 and SOCW 3327. Prerequisite: SOCW 1301 or SOCI 1301 or PSYC 2301. Fall. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Course Title: Addiction Counseling Ethics and Practice I
Other Requirements / Notes: The course is part of a two-part sequence that addresses cultural competency, clinical ethics for helping professionals, and the transdisciplinary foundations that guide evidence-based addiction treatment, including (1) Understanding Addiction, (2) Treatment Knowledge, (3) Application to Practice, and (4) Professional Readiness.
Full Course Description: This course prepares students for pursuing a career in addiction counseling and partially fulfills requirements for licensure as a Licensed Chemical Dependency Counselor in Texas. The course is part of a two-part sequence that addresses cultural competency, clinical ethics for helping professionals, and the transdisciplinary foundations that guide evidence-based addiction treatment, including (1) Understanding Addiction, (2) Treatment Knowledge, (3) Application to Practice, and (4) Professional Readiness. It can be taken before or after Addiction Counseling Ethics and Practice II. Prerequisites: PSYC/SOCW 2326 and PSYC/SOCW 3356. Fall.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Course Title: Addiction Counseling Ethics and Practice II
Other Requirements / Notes: The course is part of a two-part sequence that addresses cultural competency, clinical ethics for helping professionals, and the transdisciplinary foundations that guide evidence-based addiction treatment, including (1) Understanding Addiction, (2) Treatment Knowledge, (3) Application to Practice, and (4) Professional Readiness.
Full Course Description: This course prepares students for pursuing a career in addiction counseling and partially fulfills requirements for licensure as a Licensed Chemical Dependency Counselor in Texas. The course is part of a two-part sequence that addresses cultural competency, clinical ethics for helping professionals, and the transdisciplinary foundations that guide evidence-based addiction treatment, including (1) Understanding Addiction, (2) Treatment Knowledge, (3) Application to Practice, and (4) Professional Readiness. It can be taken before or after Addiction Counseling Ethics and Practice I. Prerequisites: PSYC/SOCW 2326 and PSYC/SOCW 3356. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Prerequisites: PSYC 2301 and a grade of C or better in PSYC 2317.
Full Course Description: This course provides training in the scientific methods of psychology. Topics include the application of statistical techniques to data, designing and conducting different kinds of research studies, report writing, and laboratory experience. Prerequisites: PSYC 2301 and a grade of C or better in PSYC 2317.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Prerequisites: PSYC 2301. 3356 Counseling and Treatment for Substance Use Disorders 3 hours This course will explore historical and contemporary approaches to counseling and treatment of substance use disorders. It will examine scientific evidence related to the effectiveness of different approaches, and will explore key competencies for addiction counseling and the “12 Core Functions” of chemical dependency counseling (including the knowledge, skills, and attitudes of professional practice). Instruction will include demonstrations, case studies, role plays, and experiential activities designed to promote integration and application of principles related to diversity and inclusion, clinical ethics, and client-centered evidence-based practice. Cross-listed with SOCW 3356.; PSYC 2326 (same as SOCW 2326). 4325 Trauma Informed Care with Children, Teens, and Families 3 hours This course will introduce students to the core concepts (general theory and foundational knowledge), informing evidence-based assessment and intervention for traumatized children and adolescents who are in the child welfare system. Trauma is broadly defined, and includes children and adolescents exposed to traumatic events, including abuse and neglect and witnessing interpersonal crime (e.g. domestic violence), community violence and other traumatic events, who have come into contact with the child welfare system. The course will highlight the role of development, culture and empirical evidence in trauma-specific assessment, referral and interventions with children, adolescents and their families within a child welfare context. It will ad-dress the level of functioning of primary care giving environments and assess the capacity of the community and the child welfare system to facilitate restorative processes.
Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with SOCW 3356.
Full Course Description: The theories and techniques used in counseling and working with groups and individuals are presented. Demonstrations, discussion and participation are utilized. Prerequisite: PSYC 2301. 3356 Counseling and Treatment for Substance Use Disorders 3 hours This course will explore historical and contemporary approaches to counseling and treatment of substance use disorders. It will examine scientific evidence related to the effectiveness of different approaches, and will explore key competencies for addiction counseling and the “12 Core Functions” of chemical dependency counseling (including the knowledge, skills, and attitudes of professional practice). Instruction will include demonstrations, case studies, role plays, and experiential activities designed to promote integration and application of principles related to diversity and inclusion, clinical ethics, and client-centered evidence-based practice. Cross-listed with SOCW 3356. Prerequisite: PSYC 2326 (same as SOCW 2326). 4325 Trauma Informed Care with Children, Teens, and Families 3 hours This course will introduce students to the core concepts (general theory and foundational knowledge), informing evidence-based assessment and intervention for traumatized children and adolescents who are in the child welfare system. Trauma is broadly defined, and includes children and adolescents exposed to traumatic events, including abuse and neglect and witnessing interpersonal crime (e.g. domestic violence), community violence and other traumatic events, who have come into contact with the child welfare system. The course will highlight the role of development, culture and empirical evidence in trauma-specific assessment, referral and interventions with children, adolescents and their families within a child welfare context. It will ad-dress the level of functioning of primary care giving environments and assess the capacity of the community and the child welfare system to facilitate restorative processes.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: The behavioral, psychological and physiological bases of disorders are considered. Topics include affective disorders, schizophrenia, anxiety disorders, personality disorders and dissociative disorders, among others. Research and controversies surrounding diagnosis, treatment and prevention are discussed. Prerequisite: PSYC 2301.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: The study and post-analysis of known serial killers is the focus of this course. It will also include crime scene information; video discussions with psychologists, scientists and law enforcement officials; interviews with perpetrators and their families; and case summary reviews.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: The social, psychological and sexual motives associated with violent sex offenses are discussed, and the characteristics and behaviors of these offenders are analyzed. This course also covers such topics as victimology, pre/post-offense behavior, and the attributes of offenders and their crime scenes. This course can be taken alone or with an optional one-hour self-defense course (KINE 1119).

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Prerequisites: Junior or senior standing and PSYC 2301. Course fees may apply.
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisites: Junior or senior standing and PSYC 2301.
Full Course Description: This course provides lecture, supervised study or research determined by student interests and needs. Pedagogy may include writing an original paper, research project or theoretical analysis of a subspecialty area. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisites: Junior or senior standing and PSYC 2301. Course fees may apply.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: PSYC 4359 and permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Students will serve as principal investigator for an original experiment in one of the major areas of psychology, working closely with the instructor on a topic of mutual interest. Prerequisites: PSYC 4359 and permission of instructor.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: The practicum must provide 150 hours of experience that exposes students to treatment activities with at least 10 hours of experiences in each of the KSA dimensions (Understanding Addiction, Treatment Knowledge, Application to Practice, and Professional Readiness). Enrollment in this course requires approval of the Evidence- Based Addiction Counseling program director.
Full Course Description: Students enroll in this course during the semester they will be completing their practicum in evidence-based addiction counseling. The practicum experience must be approved by the Evidence-Based Addiction Counseling program director to ensure it meets Texas standards for Licensed Chemical Dependency Counselor Interns. The practicum must provide 150 hours of experience that exposes students to treatment activities with at least 10 hours of experiences in each of the KSA dimensions (Understanding Addiction, Treatment Knowledge, Application to Practice, and Professional Readiness). The practicum must include the student observing treatment delivery and the intern providing services under direct observation of a qualified credentialed counselor. Enrollment in this course requires approval of the Evidence- Based Addiction Counseling program director. Prerequisites: PSYC/ SOCW 2326 and PSYC/SOCW 3356. Pre- or corequisites: PSYC 3233 and PSYC 3235. Fall.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: The practicum must provide 150 hours of experience that exposes students to treatment activities with at least 10 hours of experiences in each of the KSA dimensions (Understanding Addiction, Treatment Knowledge, Application to Practice, and Professional Readiness). Enrollment in this course requires approval of the Evidence- Based Addiction Counseling program director.
Full Course Description: Students enroll in this course during the semester they will be completing their practicum in evidence-based addiction counseling. The practicum experience must be approved by the Evidence-Based Addiction Counseling program director to ensure it meets Texas standards for Licensed Chemical Dependency Counselor. Interns. The practicum must provide 150 hours of experience that exposes students to treatment activities with at least 10 hours of experiences in each of the KSA dimensions (Understanding Addiction, Treatment Knowledge, Application to Practice, and Professional Readiness). The practicum must include the student observing treatment delivery and the intern providing services under direct observation of a qualified credentialed counselor. Enrollment in this course requires approval of the Evidence- Based Addiction Counseling program director. This course can be taken simultaneously with PSYC 4355 for students who will complete their entire 300-hour practicum in the same semester. The practicum site must be the same for this course and PSYC 4355. Prerequisites: PSYC/ SOCW 2326 and PSYC/SOCW 3356. Pre- or corequisites: PSYC 3233, PSYC 3235, and PSYC 4355. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Course Title: Research and Field Experience in Psychology
Prerequisites: Psychology or Behavioral Neuroscience major, PSYC 2301, PSYC 3438, and a grade of C or higher in PSYC 2317.
Other Requirements / Notes: Students are required to apply for acceptance in the course one semester in advance, and must plan to spend 150 hours in the field. Satisfies the Culminating Experience for Psychology and Behavioral Neuroscience majors.
Full Course Description: This is an educationally directed course in experiential learning under the supervision of Psychology faculty and professionals in the field that offers students opportunities to acquire skills and to test in a field setting theories and principles learned in the classroom. Internship students will volunteer at a psychology-related site in the community and address theoretical issues in an applied setting. Students working directly with a faculty member on a research project will conduct an in-depth study in one of the major areas in psychology, and pursue the integration of that knowledge with other areas in psychology. Students are required to apply for acceptance in the course one semester in advance, and must plan to spend 150 hours in the field. Satisfies the Culminating Experience for Psychology and Behavioral Neuroscience majors. Prerequisites: Psychology or Behavioral Neuroscience major, PSYC 2301, PSYC 3438, and a grade of C or higher in PSYC 2317.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Prerequisites: PSYC 2301, a grade of C or Higher in PSYC 2317, and senior standing.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: PSYC 2301, a grade of C or Higher in PSYC 2317, and senior standing.
Full Course Description: The development of psychology, from its historical roots to current theories and practices, is explored. Major schools of thought, such as structuralism, functionalism, behaviorism, humanism, and the cognitive revolution, are discussed. Disciplines that have contributed to the historic development of psychology, such as philosophy, physiology, or computer science, are also examined. Prerequisites: PSYC 2301, a grade of C or Higher in PSYC 2317, and senior standing.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Students demonstrate understanding by applying ideas and theories learned in class during internships. Prerequisites: Sophomore standing and EDUC 1330.
Full Course Description: In this application course, students master a variety of strategies for teaching oral and silent reading, comprehension, word recognition, phonics, spelling and writing. The course focuses on ways to teach emerging and early literacy that will promote success for every child. Students demonstrate understanding by applying ideas and theories learned in class during internships. After each teaching experience, students will share, discuss and problem-solve ways to utilize the strategies more effectively. Prerequisites: Sophomore standing and EDUC 1330. Corequisite: SPED 2324. Fall. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with EDUC 3336. Prerequisite: Sophomore standing.
Full Course Description: Literature appropriate to children: its sources, prominent authors and illustrators, critical evaluation and presentation. Attention is given to the fundamental principles underlying the choice of children’s stories and the techniques of selecting and telling stories. Students preparing to become bilingual teachers will become acquainted with Spanish children’s literature. Does not meet requirements for English Literature major. Cross-listed with EDUC 3336. Prerequisite: Sophomore standing. Fall.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Prerequisites: EDUC 1330, EDUC 2331, READ 2341 and admission into the Teacher Education program.
Full Course Description: Students will learn how to administer, assess and interpret results from both formal and informal literacy tests. Instructional decisions will be made in the areas of emergent literacy, phonological and phonemic awareness, oral language, vocabulary, word recognition, phonics, text readability, comprehension, fluency, spelling, writing, study strategies, interests and attitudes. Applied instructional decisions will be assessed to determine response to instruction. Students will participate in an internship at a local school where they will assess, teach and evaluate results. Prerequisites: EDUC 1330, EDUC 2331, READ 2341 and admission into the Teacher Education program. Corequisite: EDUC 3333 or EDUC 3337. Fall. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: As part of the secondary teacher preparation program, this course will focus on the changing landscape of the urban secondary school setting and the demands placed on teachers within secondary schools. Areas of focus will include adolescent literacy, multilingual students, writing and 21st century literacy skills. Students will consider the multiple literacies that adolescents, especially those identified as long-term English- language learners, bring into the secondary classroom and the tools and techniques necessary to leverage those literacies in that classroom. The course will also support pre-service teachers as they prepare for certification exams. Prerequisites: EDUC 1330, EDUC 2331 and SPED 2324.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Prerequisites: EDUC 1330, EDUC 2331, EDUC 2332, junior standing, and admission into the Teacher Education program.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: EDUC 1330, EDUC 2331, EDUC 2332, junior standing, and admission into the Teacher Education program.
Full Course Description: The primary focus of this teacher-preparation course is on strategies designed to develop critical-reading, writing and study skills that maximize elementary and secondary students’ content area learning (e.g., literature, mathematics, science and social studies). Content area literacy includes the development of comprehension, study skills, metacognitive awareness, purposeful reading, critical thinking, vocabulary, word recognition, writing, questioning and discussion skills. Also addressed are authentic assessment, cooperative learning, instructional technology, motivation, children’s and adolescent literature, and special needs learners. This course has an internship component and must be taken prior to student teaching. Prerequisites: EDUC 1330, EDUC 2331, EDUC 2332, junior standing, and admission into the Teacher Education program. Fall. Spring.
This course examines key aspects of at least four diverse world religions, including the historical context from which they emerged and their contemporary expressions.
This course is concerned with helping students clarify their own stories, questions, and values and to consider both a sense of calling and the journey ahead.

This course is designed to offer a systematic approach to Christian thought in which each issue discussed lays the foundation for the succeeding questions. In this way, students will see Christian doctrine not as discrete, unrelated ideas, but as an integrated whole. The attempt will not be so much to explore Catholic, Methodist, Baptist, etc., “points of view,” but to explore theological issues held in common by the Catholic, Orthodox and Protestant traditions.

This course will provide a survey and study of the major beliefs, traditions, and practices of the Catholic Church. Making use of historical documents and contemporary theology, this course will explore Catholic theological reflections on topics such as community, revelation, Christ, morality, and spirituality. This course considers Catholicism in dialogue with other traditions. Crosslisted with CATH 1316.

This course will investigate the foundations of Christian morality contained within Scripture, natural law, church tradition and various theological systems. Among the many themes and topics covered will be Christ as the model of Christian behavior, the formation of a Christian conscience, and the application of Christian principles to such issues as medical ethics, sexual morality, social justice, and discussions of war and peace.

This course introduces the student to the varieties of Jewish literature as reflective of a religious and ethnic culture that has enriched ancient and modern Israel as well as America and Europe. The various units focus on literary expressions of Judaism in a range of forms, including those of myth, history, short story, essay, poem, and novel.
This topics course which concerns world religions could take up Abrahamic Traditions, Asia Traditions, Indigenous Traditions, or Religions of Africa.

This course studies ancient Israel in terms of its history, religion and literature as they are articulated in the books of the Hebrew Bible. Students study the formation of individual books and of the Hebrew Bible as a collection.

This course takes a multifaceted approach to the study of the gospels, the epistles and the Book of Revelation. The course will emphasize the methodology of historical criticism for the purpose of identifying the authors of each New Testament work, as well as their respective theologies and witness to Jesus Christ. Crosslisted with CATH 2322.

This course examines the issues of justice and peace within the context of theology and ethics. Grounded in the teachings of the Israelite prophets, the New Testament teachings of Jesus, and recent works in contextual theology, this course will examine current social issues in light of Catholic Social Teaching. Crosslisted with CATH 2323.

This topics course can take up a particular area within the Hebrew scriptures (for example, “Covenant” or “Prophets”) or New Testament (“Pauline Epistles” or “Revelation and Apocalyptic Literature”) and can be repeated depending on topic.

This course provides a survey of women in the Hebrew Bible and New Testament. We consider women in their Near Eastern and Greco-Roman contexts by means of textual, literary, historical and anthropological approaches. 2342 Topics in Social Justice and Critical Methodologies 3 hours This topics course can take up a variety of areas that generally address issues of justice, ethics/morality, or critical methodology. Topics could include courses on Faith and Ecology or on Community, Pluralism and Religion.

Other Requirements / Notes: This course examines how the ancient physical remains of the Mediterranean Basin have contributed to our understanding of the biblical text as well as of the religions of ancient Israel and the Greco- Roman world. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This course examines how the ancient physical remains of the Mediterranean Basin have contributed to our understanding of the biblical text as well as of the religions of ancient Israel and the Greco- Roman world. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
This upper level topics course could concern an in-depth examination of a Global Tradition such as Buddhism, Hinduism, Judaism, Islam or Religion and Border Studies.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Sophomore standing.
Full Course Description: This course will explore the history of Christian spirituality, from the ancient to contemporary church. Major spiritual movements such as the Desert Fathers and Mothers, the rise of monasticism, various forms of mysticism and popular devotions will be examined. Prerequisite: Sophomore standing.

Prerequisites: Sophomore standing. 3338 Ritual and Imagination: Sacramental Theology 3 hours This course examines the relationship between ritual and imagination with particular attention to Catholic sacramental life. Using a variety of methods and tools from the study of religion including history, ritual studies, sacramental and liturgical theology, and anthropology of religion, students will consider the role of the sacramental life in the Church in historical and contemporary contexts.; Sophomore standing. Crosslisted with CATH 3338.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Sophomore standing.
Full Course Description: This course will explore the historical and theological factors in the critical study of Christian tradition. Prerequisite: Sophomore standing. 3338 Ritual and Imagination: Sacramental Theology 3 hours This course examines the relationship between ritual and imagination with particular attention to Catholic sacramental life. Using a variety of methods and tools from the study of religion including history, ritual studies, sacramental and liturgical theology, and anthropology of religion, students will consider the role of the sacramental life in the Church in historical and contemporary contexts. Prerequisite: Sophomore standing. Crosslisted with CATH 3338.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Sophomore standing.
Full Course Description: This class will explore issues of particular interest within theology. Possible topics include: Priests, Prophets and Kings; Traditions of the End Times and Messiah; Mysticism; Feminist Theology. Prerequisite: Sophomore standing.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: Sophomore standing.
Full Course Description: This course will examine select issues within religious studies. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: Sophomore standing.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Sophomore standing.
Full Course Description: Perennial issues in biblical studies are examined through the lens of ancient and contemporary approaches to Scripture. Prerequisite: Sophomore standing.

Prerequisites: Sophomore standing and one course in Religious and Theological Studies, preferably RELS 1315 or RELS 1316.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: Sophomore standing and one course in Religious and Theological Studies, preferably RELS 1315 or RELS 1316.
Full Course Description: This course will explore philosophical and theological shifts that have taken place since the Enlightenment. Prerequisites: Sophomore standing and one course in Religious and Theological Studies, preferably RELS 1315 or RELS 1316.

Prerequisites: Junior standing and one course in Religious and Theological Studies, preferably RELS 1305. 4350 Experiential Learning and Internship in Religious and Theological Studies 3 hours This course is; Sophomore standing and one course in Religious and Theological Studies, preferably RELS 1305. 4351 Mentored Research in Religious and Theological Studies 3 hours This course, which emphasizes experiential learning, provides students with opportunities to develop and hone research skills. Students learn to articulate research questions, identify peer-reviewed sources, create bibliographies, and communicate research effectively in writing. Finally, students explore dissemination of research in juried contexts. While students develop individual research projects, they examine the components of research in the context of a class environment.; Sophomore standing.
Semester Offered: offered as an internship course, emphasizing experiential learning.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: Junior standing and one course in Religious and Theological Studies, preferably RELS 1305. Prerequisites: Sophomore standing and one course in Religious and Theological Studies, preferably RELS 1305. Prerequisite: Sophomore standing.
Full Course Description: This course, which emphasizes experiential learning, provides students with the opportunity for advanced research. In formal meetings with their instructor and cohort, students build on research skills acquired in RELS 4351. Employing previous skills in discerning peer reviewed sources, students produce annotated bibliographies and learn judicious use of sources in written work. Students refine engagement with disciplinary style sheets and use of notes in papers. Students work on revising written work and peer review. The course includes production of a research paper and can serve as preparation for advanced study. As this course serves as the Culminating Experience, students also reflect on their research project as the culmination of their St. Edward’s experience and/or its connection with the University’s mission. Prerequisites: Junior standing and one course in Religious and Theological Studies, preferably RELS 1305. 4350 Experiential Learning and Internship in Religious and Theological Studies 3 hours This course is offered as an internship course, emphasizing experiential learning. While including a formal internship with campus partners in the community and Austin, students will have regular meetings with their instructor and RELS 4350 cohort. Class discussions take up vocation, professional opportunities in religious and theological studies, and career readiness. This course contributes to discernment of aspirations and interests. Prerequisites: Sophomore standing and one course in Religious and Theological Studies, preferably RELS 1305. 4351 Mentored Research in Religious and Theological Studies 3 hours This course, which emphasizes experiential learning, provides students with opportunities to develop and hone research skills. Students learn to articulate research questions, identify peer-reviewed sources, create bibliographies, and communicate research effectively in writing. Finally, students explore dissemination of research in juried contexts. While students develop individual research projects, they examine the components of research in the context of a class environment. Prerequisite: Sophomore standing.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: Sophomore standing.
Full Course Description: This course will examine advanced topics in religious studies. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: Sophomore standing.

Course Title: First Year Seminar in Science and

Full Course Description: The First Year Seminar in Science and Mathematics is a one credit hour course for majors in the School of Natural Sciences. The course introduces faculty, programming, and academic and career advising specific to majors in the School of Natural Sciences to help new students adjust to the university. The course emphasizes the development of skills necessary for success in college (e.g., academic research and critical thinking skills, time management and study skills, wellness, and personal responsibility) and the importance of research experience and internships to prepare for careers in science and mathematics. Topics in science and mathematics are based on the interests and needs of students and on areas of faculty expertise. Enrollment is restricted to incoming first-year students whose majors are in the School of Natural Sciences. Credit may not be earned for both SCIE 1100 and FYSM 1100. Fall.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course is a one-hour introduction to topics of special interest to science majors. Topics may include the study of a particular book, seminars on current research activities in various scientific disciplines, and/or training in new and advanced scientific methodologies and techniques. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course is a one-hour introduction to topics of special interest to science majors. Topics may include the study of a particular book, seminars on current research activities in various scientific disciplines, and/or training in new and advanced scientific methodologies and techniques. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

Other Requirements / Notes: Required for students participating in the STEM Leadership LLC. May be repeated as a no- credit option (SCIE 2001). Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This seminar course is intended to prepare students majoring in the sciences and mathematics to assume leadership positions in their future careers by providing them with opportunities to enhance basic skills taught in General Education courses and add new skills sets to their resumes in areas not formally included in their degree plans. Course units will prepare participants to serve as mentors, tutors, teaching assistants, and supplemental instruction leaders through the presentation of teaching techniques particularly applicable to the pedagogies being utilized in current STEM courses. Other unit topics may include data presentation, scientific communication, scientific ethics, entrepreneurship, development of research skills, STEM related social issues, research proposal design, online literature search, and career research and planning. Students enrolling in the course will prepare applications for off-campus research or internship positions, research proposals to be submitted for funding, applications to professional or graduate schools, and/or applications for scholarships and fellowships at both the undergraduate and graduate levels. Required for students participating in the STEM Leadership LLC. May be repeated as a no- credit option (SCIE 2001). Prerequisite: Permission of instructor. Fall. Spring. 2324 Science Exploration: Myths, Mysteries and the Truth 3 hours This course is designed specifically for non-science majors to satisfy the Natural Science General Education program. This hands-on course encourages students to explain the process of science and its role in society; apply scientific principles to explain the natural world, to engage in science by applying its principles through experiential learning, and to communicate science in an evidence-based way verbally and in writing. Students will explore global climate change, evolution, alternative energy sources, and human health and nutrition. Fall. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with ARTS 3399 and PHCO 3322. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Students will be introduced to research methodology in an interdisciplinary environment. Given guidance from instructors in both the Arts and Humanities and Natural Sciences, students will select a research question pertaining to the Wild Basin Preserve and will work in interdisciplinary groups to conduct an independent investigation. During this course, students will learn how to search relevant literature/ databases within the requisite field of study and will conduct “on-site and in-field” work at the Wild Basin Preserve in gathering their data. The course will culminate in a presentation of the work as part of the course and students will be encouraged and aided in presentation of their findings at campus, regional, or national meetings in written and/or oral formats. Cross-listed with ARTS 3399 and PHCO 3322. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor. Spring.

Prerequisites: Junior standing and prior completion of six hours of Physics, Chemistry or Biology, COSC 1423, MATH 2321 or PHIL 2303.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: Junior standing and prior completion of six hours of Physics, Chemistry or Biology, COSC 1423, MATH 2321 or PHIL 2303.
Full Course Description: An introduction to the philosophy of mathematics and science by way of a historical analysis of philosophic-scientific debates. It will include study of the major developments in the Western world, especially the effects of the scientific thought on the worldview of different cultures, and their legal and social institutions. Examples will be drawn from mathematics, physics, computer science, chemistry and biology. Questions such as wave-particle duality, the relationship of mathematics to all of the sciences, and the role of computational instrumentation in the development of science will also be considered. The course may be used to satisfy the General Education requirement for Science in Perspective (for Computer Science, History and Mathematics majors). Prerequisites: Junior standing and prior completion of six hours of Physics, Chemistry or Biology, COSC 1423, MATH 2321 or PHIL 2303. Fall. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: An overview of the profession of social work and an introduction to generalist practice, ecological systems theory and the problem-solving process. Examines the roles and techniques of social work practice in a variety of social service settings. Requires three hours per week of community volunteer work, arranged to fit the student’s interests and schedule. Recommended for any student wishing to explore social work as a career possibility.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Throughout the semester, students will explore a variety of theoretical frameworks for understanding migration and will survey humanitarian responses, resettlement services, domestic and international policies, and grassroots advocacy work with migrating individuals and families, including undocumented immigrants, asylees, internally displaced persons, refugees, and survivors of human trafficking.
Full Course Description: This course will investigate the movement of marginalized populations within and across borders. Throughout the semester, students will explore a variety of theoretical frameworks for understanding migration and will survey humanitarian responses, resettlement services, domestic and international policies, and grassroots advocacy work with migrating individuals and families, including undocumented immigrants, asylees, internally displaced persons, refugees, and survivors of human trafficking. The course will draw from both global and local expertise, incorporate community-based experiential learning opportunities, and offer direct integration of classroom content with contemporary issues related to both policy and practice at the local, state, national, and international levels. The course will include a focus on multidisciplinary work with immigrant and refugee communities and is relevant to disciplines beyond social work (such as psychology, political science, global studies, and criminal justice).

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course explores the systems, policies, and programs that make up the social safety net in the United States. Students explore the role of social welfare policy in the daily lives of individuals, families, groups and communities and practice skills in social welfare policy analysis and advocacy. The course explores multiple social welfare policy areas, which may include Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, Temporary Assistance to Needy Families, Medicaid and Medicare, as well as social welfare policies and programs related to childcare, health and mental health care, immigration, housing, and disability rights. Crosslisted with SOCI 2318.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Semester Offered: offered by the Social Work program for students who are not majoring in Social Work.
Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with PSYC 2320.
Full Course Description: This course builds basic practical skills for effective interpersonal communication, supportive counseling, interviewing, active listening and mentoring. It is offered by the Social Work program for students who are not majoring in Social Work. The course will cover the problem-solving process, issues of diversity, the importance of a nonjudgmental attitude and related ethical principles. The course is intended to build skills that will enhance students’ ability to work later with people in community service or in internships. Cross-listed with PSYC 2320.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Course Title: Breaking the School to Prison Pipeline
Full Course Description: This course will address the way that the school systems and larger society in the United States are currently structured to track some students to college and others to prison, as well as how people can intervene to change its trajectory. This course is designed to prepare the next generation of school leaders, activists, practitioners, public safety professionals, policy makers and community members to understand the complex challenges of systematically-oppressed youth and their families who are impacted by the school-to-prison pipeline. Students will study the history and components of this system and learn theories that explain its structure as well as how to change it. Students will engage in experiential learning, practice, and research to address complex challenges and work toward achieving sustainable social, racial, economic, and educational justice.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: The main goal of this course is for students to gain a better understanding of how drugs work in the brain and in the body to produce their psychological and physiological effects. Cross-listed with PSYC 2326.
Full Course Description: Students will discuss issues of drug use and abuse and the effects of drugs on behavior. Students will also evaluate both human and animal research. The main goal of this course is for students to gain a better understanding of how drugs work in the brain and in the body to produce their psychological and physiological effects. The roles that drugs play in societies and some of the clinical models used to treat those who use drugs will also be discussed. Contemporary issues such as the link between chemical abuse and crime, our society’s war on drugs, legalization proposals, allocation of priorities and resources, and changing definitions of addiction will be explored. Cross-listed with PSYC 2326. Fall.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with CRIJ 2341. This course is open to all students although it is part of a series designed for students pursuing licensure in the state of Texas as a Licensed Chemical Dependency Counselor (LCDC).
Full Course Description: This course will take a more in-depth look at the impact of alcohol and/or drugs on special populations, including women, children and adolescents, gays and lesbians, the aged, and the incarcerated. Prevention models, interventions and treatment issues will be explored. The class will additionally include an experiential component in which students will derive practical experience(s) in the application of a direct intervention with one of these groups. Cross-listed with CRIJ 2341. This course is open to all students although it is part of a series designed for students pursuing licensure in the state of Texas as a Licensed Chemical Dependency Counselor (LCDC).

Full Course Description: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

Full Course Description: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: This course provides the knowledge base for understanding one’s own family in a broader societal context.
Full Course Description: This course examines families and intimate relationships in society. Topics include dating, marriage and partnerships, parenting, multigenerational relationships, care work, relationship violence, and divorce and blended families. Current research findings related to each topic will be covered. Cultural diversity in family and relationship dynamics will be emphasized. This course provides the knowledge base for understanding one’s own family in a broader societal context. Cross- listed with SOCI 3327 and PSYC 3328. Prerequisite: SOCW 1301 or SOCI 1301 or PSYC 2301. Fall. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with SOCI 3330.
Full Course Description: Fundamentals of research design, data collection and analysis are presented. Topics include: scientific knowledge, logic, sampling, methods (surveys, experiments, field research, unobtrusive techniques), ethical practices, and program evaluation. The development of skills for using and conducting research is emphasized. Cross-listed with SOCI 3330. Prerequisite: SOCI 2329. Fall. Spring. 3331 Human Behavior and the Social Environment 3 hours This course examines the normal processes of biological, psychological and sociocultural development through the life cycle with attention to the influences of diversity — including, but not limited to, race/ ethnicity, social class, gender and sexual orientation. Students enhance self-awareness and assessment skills by applying course content to analyses of their own development. Common problems associated with particular stages of the life cycle are explored. This course is a foundation course in human behavior and the social environment and is essential for direct social work practice with individuals and families. Pre- or corequisite: SOCW 1301 or SOCI 1301 or PSYC 2301.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: The course places disability in a political, social, cultural and economic context to increase understanding of the societal barriers for individuals with disabilities throughout their life cycle. Twenty hours of related community service learning are required. 3347 Generalist Practice with Individuals and Families 3 hours This is a three-hour course that is only open to students who are Social Work majors.
Full Course Description: This course provides a broad base of knowledge concerning the needs of individuals with disabilities and their families. The course places disability in a political, social, cultural and economic context to increase understanding of the societal barriers for individuals with disabilities throughout their life cycle. The course includes a historical perspective, social justice issues and consideration of ethical issues confronting professionals who serve individuals with disabilities and their families. Twenty hours of related community service learning are required. The course is in-tended as an elective for a variety of social and behavioral science majors. Fall. 3347 Generalist Practice with Individuals and Families 3 hours This is a three-hour course that is only open to students who are Social Work majors. The primary focus of this class is to provide a theoretical foundation of social work practice with diverse client systems, while developing basic skills in the actual application of social work theory to practice. Using a generalist practice framework, students will examine the major theoretical foundations of social work practice that include ecological systems theory, the strengths perspective, and the empowerment process while learning other more specific theories and practice models. Students will explore relevant issues related to the practice of social work with diverse client systems that include individuals and families. Special practice issues such as dealing with diversity, working with oppressed groups and development of culturally specific interventions will also be a focus of this class. Incorporated are various learning assignments that will augment student learning while promoting the development of basic social work knowledge, skills, methods, and abilities and eventually help serve as the cornerstone for student master of the nine core competencies (CSWE, 2008) during future Field Practicum and Seminar I and II. Prerequisite: SOCW 1301. Pre- or corequisites: SOCW 3327 and SOCW 3331.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Prerequisites: SOCW 1301.; PSYC 2326 (same as SOCW 2326). 4324 Trauma Informed Care with Children, Teens, and Families 3 hours This course introduces students to the core concepts (general theory and foundational knowledge), informing evidence-based assessment and intervention for traumatized children and adolescents who are in the child welfare system. Trauma is broadly defined, and includes children and adolescents exposed to traumatic events including, abuse and neglect and witnessing interpersonal crime (e.g. domestic violence), community violence and other traumatic events who have come into contact with the child welfare system. The course highlights the role of development, culture and empirical evidence in trauma-specific assessment, referral and interventions with children, adolescents and their families within a child welfare context. It addresses the level of functioning of primary care giving environments and assess the capacity of the community and the child welfare system to facilitate restorative processes.
Pre- or Corequisites: SOCW 3327 and SOCW 3331. 3356 Counseling and Treatment for Substance Use Disorders 3 hours This course will explore historical and contemporary approaches to counseling and treatment of substance use disorders. It will examine scientific evidence related to the effectiveness of different approaches, and will explore key competencies for addiction counseling and the “12 Core Functions” of chemical dependency counseling (including the knowledge, skills, and attitudes of professional practice). Instruction will include demonstrations, case studies, role plays, and experiential activities designed to promote integration and application of principles related to diversity and inclusion, clinical ethics, and client-centered evidence-based practice. Cross-listed with PSYC 3356.
Other Requirements / Notes: The course will acknowledge and address power dynamics in groups and students will apply their understanding to a variety of group types and populations. Cross-listed with PSYC 3356.
Full Course Description: This course provides foundational knowledge, values, and skills related to group processes in social work practice. Students will develop insight into the structure and function of support and task groups, and learn ways to encourage healthy group development. The values and ethics of the social work profession will be emphasized throughout the course, and students will learn to recognize and demonstrate the roles and responsibilities of a group facilitator. The course will acknowledge and address power dynamics in groups and students will apply their understanding to a variety of group types and populations. Students will practice group skills through active learning. Prerequisite: SOCW 1301. Pre- or corequisites: SOCW 3327 and SOCW 3331. 3356 Counseling and Treatment for Substance Use Disorders 3 hours This course will explore historical and contemporary approaches to counseling and treatment of substance use disorders. It will examine scientific evidence related to the effectiveness of different approaches, and will explore key competencies for addiction counseling and the “12 Core Functions” of chemical dependency counseling (including the knowledge, skills, and attitudes of professional practice). Instruction will include demonstrations, case studies, role plays, and experiential activities designed to promote integration and application of principles related to diversity and inclusion, clinical ethics, and client-centered evidence-based practice. Cross-listed with PSYC 3356. Prerequisite: PSYC 2326 (same as SOCW 2326). 4324 Trauma Informed Care with Children, Teens, and Families 3 hours This course introduces students to the core concepts (general theory and foundational knowledge), informing evidence-based assessment and intervention for traumatized children and adolescents who are in the child welfare system. Trauma is broadly defined, and includes children and adolescents exposed to traumatic events including, abuse and neglect and witnessing interpersonal crime (e.g. domestic violence), community violence and other traumatic events who have come into contact with the child welfare system. The course highlights the role of development, culture and empirical evidence in trauma-specific assessment, referral and interventions with children, adolescents and their families within a child welfare context. It addresses the level of functioning of primary care giving environments and assess the capacity of the community and the child welfare system to facilitate restorative processes.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Pre- or Corequisites: SOCW 3331. Corequisite for Social Work majors: SOCW 4650 or SOCW 4651.
Full Course Description: This course analyzes central historical and current issues related to race, class, gender, and other social identities in the United States. Privilege and oppression are explored and mechanisms that perpetuate inequality are identified. Students are expected to analyze a variety of viewpoints and clarify their own beliefs and values regarding issues of social justice. Students practice taking action on social inequities. Prerequisite: SOCI 1301 or SOCW 1301. Fall. Spring. 4344 Practice and Theory with Organizations and Communities 3 hours Using a generalist perspective, this course introduces students to the social science knowledge base and theoretical frameworks as they relate to social work practice with organizational and community systems. This course explores the relationship between micro-level challenges and the need for macro-level system intervention. Students examine strategies used to assess service delivery networks and to initiate change efforts, including social service program development and evaluation. Course content also includes concepts and principles of organizational administration and of community organization, and ethical considerations in macro-level social work practice. Prerequisite: SOCW 1301. Pre- or corequisite: SOCW 3331. Corequisite for Social Work majors: SOCW 4650 or SOCW 4651.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Prerequisites: SOCW 1301, SOCW 2318, SOCW 3327, SOCW 3331, SOCW 3347 and SOCW 3348.
Other Requirements / Notes: A GPA of 2.25 in Social Work core courses and at least a grade of C in SOCW 3347 and SOCW 3348 are required for admission to the practicum.
Full Course Description: Practicum Seminar I includes and accompanies a two-semester practicum placement of 200 clock hours per semester (400 hours total) in an agency setting. It is designed to help students take the knowledge, values, and skills attained in social work education and supporting courses and integrate and apply them into actual practice in practicum settings. At their practicum, students will receive weekly one-hour, one-on-one direct supervision from an experienced social worker that has been pre-approved as a SEU Practicum Instructor. The seminar provides an opportunity for students to formally share and consult on cases and informally discuss relevant issues that arise from interactions in practice. Students will integrate and apply what they have learned and continue learning from each other through peer supervision as they pose and examine various problems and challenges faced at their practicum placements and as they write and submit journals regarding practice activities. Assignments that integrate learning from practicum experiences will be used to deepen reflection and self-awareness. A GPA of 2.25 in Social Work core courses and at least a grade of C in SOCW 3347 and SOCW 3348 are required for admission to the practicum. Prerequisites: SOCW 1301, SOCW 2318, SOCW 3327, SOCW 3331, SOCW 3347 and SOCW 3348. Fall. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: A cumulative GPA of 2.25 in Social Work core courses and a minimum grade of C in SOCW 4650 are required for admission to the practicum.
Full Course Description: Practicum Seminar II is the second course that includes and accompanies a two-semester practicum placement of 200 clock hours per semester (400 hours total) in an agency setting. It is designed to help students continue to integrate and synergize knowledge, values and skills attained in social work education and supporting courses and integrate and apply it into actual practice in practicum settings. The seminar provides an opportunity for students to formally staff, and informally discuss relevant issues that arise from interactions in practice. Students will integrate and apply what they have learned and continue learning from each other through peer supervision as they pose and examine various problems and challenges faced at their practicum placements and as they write and submit journals regarding practice activities. Assignments that integrate learning from practicum experiences will be used to deepen reflection and self-awareness. Each student will prepare a professionally written portfolio, which contains examples of their ongoing undergraduate work and personal growth regarding social work knowledge, values and skills. The Culminating Experience for Social Work will be fulfilled by SOCW 4651. A cumulative GPA of 2.25 in Social Work core courses and a minimum grade of C in SOCW 4650 are required for admission to the practicum. Pre- or corequisite: SOCW 4650. Fall. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: This course is an introduction to understanding the sociological imagination. Students are expected to confirm their understanding of these basic sociological concepts about the social world.
Full Course Description: This course is an introduction to understanding the sociological imagination. It explores the discipline through an examination of sociological questions and concepts and ways of analyzing social life. The focus is on how human societies organize themselves; how culture, socialization, norms, values, power relations, social institutions and group interaction affect the individual; and how, in turn, societies are transformed by human action. Of particular concern are the problems facing contemporary societies. Students are expected to confirm their understanding of these basic sociological concepts about the social world. This course is a prerequisite for many of our other Sociology courses. Fall. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course explores the systems, policies, and programs that make up the social safety net in the United States. Students explore the role of social welfare policy in the daily lives of individuals, families, groups and communities and practice skills in social welfare policy analysis and advocacy. The course explores multiple social welfare policy areas, which may include Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, Temporary Assistance to Needy Families, Medicaid and Medicare, as well as social welfare policies and programs related to childcare, health and mental health care, immigration, housing, and disability rights. Crosslisted with SOCW 2318.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course includes descriptive and inferential statistical methods, emphasizing a social science application of measurement. Topics covered include: measures of central tendency, measures of variability, graphical devices, sampling, scales, t-distribution, correlation, chi square, multiple regression and causality. Students are expected to be able to calculate and interpret basic statistical tests. This course is a prerequisite for SOCI 3330. Corequisite: MATH 0104 or satisfactory placement. Fall. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course is a survey of sociological theories from the beginning of sociology to the present. Students are expected to apply this theoretical knowledge to contemporary social problems, in preparation for their upper division courses in Sociology. Prerequisite: SOCI 1301. Fall.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course examines how people interact with the natural environments in which they are embedded. Topics covered include the economic, political, cultural and demographic forces that drive human modification of the natural world, the resulting social and environmental problems, public controversies and the role of inequality and social justice in relation to the environment. The course also examines movements and policies related to environmental issues, and the prospects for the emergence of more environmentally “sustainable” societies. Students are expected to conduct an independent analysis of an environmental issue.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: This course provides the knowledge base for understanding one’s own family in a broader societal context.
Full Course Description: This course examines families and intimate relationships in society. Topics include dating, marriage and partnerships, parenting, multigenerational relationships, care work, relationship violence, and divorce and blended families. Current research findings related to each topic will be covered. Cultural diversity in family and relationship dynamics will be emphasized. This course provides the knowledge base for understanding one’s own family in a broader societal context. Cross- listed with SOCW 3327 and PSYC 3328. Prerequisite: SOCW 1301 or SOCI 1301 or PSYC 2301. Fall. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with SOCW 3330.
Full Course Description: Fundamentals of research design, data collection and analysis are presented. Topics include: scientific knowledge, logic, sampling, methods (surveys, experiments, field research, unobtrusive techniques), ethical practices, and program evaluation. The development of skills for using and conducting research is emphasized. Cross-listed with SOCW 3330. Prerequisite: SOCI 2329. Fall. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course examines religious beliefs, behaviors and structures as they relate to contemporary America. In addition to studying established religious forms, attention is given to public controversies connected with religion and to new religious movements and trends. Students are expected to conduct an independent analysis of a religious issue.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Cross-listed with SOCW 4343.
Full Course Description: This course analyzes the forms and consequences of inequalities related to race, class, gender, and other types of social statuses in society. Both structural and interpersonal causes of inequality are discussed. Related social research findings and data are examined. Students are expected to analyze a variety of viewpoints and to clarify their own beliefs and values regarding issues of social justice. Cross-listed with SOCW 4343. Prerequisite: SOCI 1301. Fall. Spring.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Full Course Description: This course is about how sociologists look at cities, how the urban landscape affects people and how people modify it. Topics include the origin and development of cities, the social-psychological consequences of life in an urban environment, urban social organization, social relationships within metropolitan areas, urban planning and contemporary urban redevelopment. Students are expected to conduct an independent analysis of an urban issue.
