Other Requirements / Notes: Open to both majors and non-majors.
Full Course Description: This course serves as an introduction into the language and practice of acting through improvised/devised theater. This course probes the fundamentals of acting (as well as the depths of the human spirit) explored through a variety of Improvisational exercises that ultimately result in a public presentation of a Devised/New piece of theater. Open to both majors and non-majors. Spring.

Students will focus on the various forms of dance, which they will encounter in performing musical theater. A major concentration on jazz, but also tap and ballet will be focal points of this course. Preparation for musical theater dance audition will be a key component of this course as well. Prerequisite: Ballet or modern dance.

Course Title: Theater Skills I: Stagecraft and Lighting
Other Requirements / Notes: A laboratory is required for this class.
Full Course Description: Introduction to the materials, tools and methodology used in the construction and painting of scenery and to basic drafting techniques. Introduction to the theories and skills of stage lighting. A laboratory is required for this class. Fall. Spring.

Course Title: Theater Skills II: Costume and Makeup
Other Requirements / Notes: A laboratory is required for this class.
Full Course Description: Introduction to the materials, tools and methodology used in costume construction and to the theories and techniques of stage makeup. A laboratory is required for this class. Fall. Spring.

An introduction to the process of making meaning through performance, including a historical review of methods of teaching acting and the development of a conceptual vocabulary and a movement vocabulary for use in rehearsal and performance. Introduction to character design and script breakdown. Fall.

A continuation of the process begun in Performance IA, including the application of conceptual and movement vocabulary to text analysis, character design and scene study. Prerequisite: THAR 1351. Spring.

Drafting for the Theater introduces the student to the basic hand- drafting skills needed for the theater, including orthographic drawings, ground plans, sections and front elevations. In the second half of the class we translate those skills into CAD utilizing the program Vectorworks. In addition, an introduction to Spotlight for light plots will be included in the second half of the class.

Course Title: Drawing and Rendering for the Theater
Full Course Description: This course focuses on drawing and painting techniques used for theatrical costume and set design. Figure drawing, mechanical perspective, light and shadow studies, and painting with watercolor, gouache and markers give students the tools to express their design ideas with greater artistry and precision.

Prerequisites: THAR 1330 and THAR 1331 for Theater Arts majors; no prerequisites for non-majors.
Full Course Description: This introductory course will examine the shared conceptual principles employed to formulate three-dimensional designs for scenery, lighting, costumes, makeup and sound. The course introduces the basic vocabulary of design and the principles and elements of design as they apply to theater. Prerequisites: THAR 1330 and THAR 1331 for Theater Arts majors; no prerequisites for non-majors. Fall. Spring.

Through a chronological study of clothing and architecture in tandem, students trace the development of design and changing aesthetics from pre-historic times through the 20th century. By collecting examples to represent each historical period, students build a reference notebook, which may inspire and inform future research and production work. Fall, even years.

Prerequisites: THAR 1351 or THAR 1313 or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: THAR 1351 or THAR 1313 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: A great beginner’s class to learn the basics of voice acting. Students will receive training in commercial voiceover technique and a chance to record multiple scripts with direction and playback. Your voice will be analyzed so you can discern your vocal type. Class covers vocal warm- ups and auditioning techniques. Prerequisite: THAR 1351 or THAR 1313 or permission of instructor.

Prerequisites: THAR 1331 or demonstrated sewing experience and permission of instructor.
Recommended Prerequisites: Theater Practicum or Work Study in the Costume Shop.
Other Requirements / Notes: Even year topics cover basic principles required for flat patterning and millinery for the theater. Prerequisite: THAR 1331 or demonstrated sewing experience and permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course is a two-segment class designed to introduce students to the basic materials, tools and methodology used in costume construction and crafting. The content of the class rotates between even and odd years, allowing students to retake the class for additional credit. Odd year topics cover basic principles utilized in pattern creation through draping, with crafting segments on painting/dyeing for the theater. Even year topics cover basic principles required for flat patterning and millinery for the theater. The studio nature of the course provides opportunity for hands-on training through various exercises, projects, presentations, and practical application. Prerequisite: THAR 1331 or demonstrated sewing experience and permission of instructor. Recommended prerequisite: Theater Practicum or Work Study in the Costume Shop.

Prerequisites: THAR 1313 or THAR 1351 or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: THAR 1313 or THAR 1351 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course provides students the opportunity to research, share knowledge and learn with populations outside of the campus community while examining a specific issue on social justice. Topics addressed may include: incarceration, homelessness, addiction, gender equity, food insecurity, etc. Prerequisite: THAR 1313 or THAR 1351 or permission of instructor.

Non-majors are encouraged to take Theater Practicum I to get “behind the scenes” experiences. This course involves the student in one or more aspects of theater production at Mary Moody Northen Theatre: scenery, lighting, costumes, makeup, props, sound, publicity and front-of-house. Fall. 2329 Body-Mind Awareness, Breath and Movement 3 hours This course teaches basic techniques in a specific body-mind movement modality, such as yoga, Feldenkrais, Pilates and/or Alexander Technique. The course addresses alignment, form, breath awareness methods, and meditation techniques that lead to a feeling of inner peace and a natural state of relaxation. Regular practice in body-mind techniques can help improve flexibility, strength, body awareness and concentration. The course also incorporates a powerful system to investigate and dissolve stressful thought patterns in order to release creativity and effectiveness.

For majors only. This course involves the student in one or more aspects of theater production at Mary Moody Northen Theatre: scenery, lighting, costumes, makeup, props, sound, publicity and front-of-house. Prerequisite: THAR 2128. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This course is designed for an exploration of one of the many facets of theater and drama. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

Play Analysis is a course that gives students a basic familiarity with the principles of play reading, which will provide a foundation for their future work in all areas of the theater: acting, directing, design or management. The script or scenario is the fundamental document on which all theater is based. This course is also aimed at all inquiring minds that recognize the vast riches available in the body of dramatic literature through the ages. The fundamental assumption is that play analysis — reading plays — is a skill acquired through repeated use and experience.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit.
Full Course Description: Both majors and non-majors can take Basic Theater Practicum to get “behind the scenes” experiences. This course involves the student in one or more aspects of theater production at Mary Moody Northen Theatre: scenery, lighting, costumes, makeup, props, sound, publicity and front- of-house. May be repeated for credit.
A survey of the development of drama from Greek origins through the Elizabethan period. Reading of plays from each period and critical discussion. This course is recommended for non-majors. Fall.

A continuation of the survey begun in Theater History I, focusing on the Restoration period through the present. Reading of plays from each period and critical discussion. This course is recommended for non- majors. Theater History I and II need not be taken in sequence. Spring.

Vocal training for actors. Techniques based in somatic movement approaches teach the actor how to create a practice of breathing coordination with phonation, resonance and articulation. Application of techniques for various purposes, including stage, camera and voiceovers. Prerequisites: THAR 1351 and THAR 1352. Spring.
Continuation of voice and movement techniques learned in Voice I, with emphasis on heightened vocal demands and Shakespeare. Prerequisites: THAR 3330 and THAR 3307. Fall.

Physical exploration of theatrical movement styles as they relate to performance. Focus is on physical awareness, engagement, breath and approaches to character development. Prerequisite: THAR 1352. Spring.

Prerequisites: THAR 1351, THAR 1352, MUSI 1101, MUSI 3336 (completed or concurrent enrollment), and two semesters of Applied Voice classes MUSI 2143 or MUSI 4143. Applied Voice classes must be taken in residency. Fall, even years.
Full Course Description: This course will address stylistic performance practices and inherent challenges in musical theater. Students will develop a systematic approach for preparing songs for auditions and performance, incorporating musical analysis and scoring the text. Musical Theatre Performance will prepare students for auditions and for work in musical theater productions. Prerequisites: THAR 1351, THAR 1352, MUSI 1101, MUSI 3336 (completed or concurrent enrollment), and two semesters of Applied Voice classes MUSI 2143 or MUSI 4143. Applied Voice classes must be taken in residency. Fall, even years.

Other Requirements / Notes: Development and application of the necessary performance techniques required for film and television acting.
Full Course Description: Development and application of the necessary performance techniques required for film and television acting. Students receive practical experience in front of the camera and are introduced to the unique vocabulary of the medium. Prerequisite: THAR 3330. Fall.

Theories and skills of stage lighting approached from a technology and an aesthetic viewpoint leading to the practical planning of light plot and hook-ups for theatrical productions. Includes work in lighting fixtures, color and basic design. Prerequisites: THAR 1330 and THAR 2310.

An examination of the principles involved in creating a scenic design for the theater and other applications. The class will include the study of the elements and principles of design, research, design procedures and model building. Prerequisites: THAR 1330 and THAR 2310.

Students learn how to read a text for clues to costume, the basics of historical and inspirational research, and the creative and practical process of the costume designer. By designing selected costumes on paper for several contrasting plays and musicals, students learn to interpret research, translate ideas to the page, and practice storytelling through clothing and character. Prerequisites: THAR 1331 and THAR 2310.

This course will examine the design of makeup for theater, film and television. Emphasis will be placed on characterization based on information found in the script, the director’s concept, the style of production, and research reflecting the period and environment of the play. The course will include advanced makeup techniques and practical application. Prerequisites: THAR 1330, THAR 1331 and THAR 2310.

Other Requirements / Notes: Through weekly workshops, students will deepen their understanding, awareness and connection to the lyrics and music and invest their work with appropriate detail using musical and text score analysis, as well as information gained from background research. Prerequisites: THAR 3220 and permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course will address stylistic performance practices in musical theater, working to elevate singing a song to performing a song. Through weekly workshops, students will deepen their understanding, awareness and connection to the lyrics and music and invest their work with appropriate detail using musical and text score analysis, as well as information gained from background research. The class will culminate in a public performance of the materials covered in the workshops. Musical Theater Showcase will prepare students for auditions and for work in musical theater productions. Prerequisites: THAR 3220 and permission of instructor.

Prerequisites: THAR 1330, THAR 1331 and junior standing. Fall, odd years.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: THAR 1330, THAR 1331 and junior standing.
Full Course Description: This class will introduce the student to more advanced technology concepts then can be covered in the Skills classes and the Practicum requirements, such as advanced electrical concepts, programming of a variety of moving light fixtures, sound engineering practices and advance rigging concepts. A laboratory component will be part of this class. Developing and implementing a project for a specific show need is possible. Prerequisites: THAR 1330, THAR 1331 and junior standing. Fall, odd years.

Prerequisites: Acceptance into the Bachelor of Fine Arts degree program and permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: Acceptance into the Bachelor of Fine Arts degree program and permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Techniques necessary for the development of a character and the creation of a performance. Focus on interaction with other characters through extensive scene work in a variety of modern styles. Prerequisites: Acceptance into the Bachelor of Fine Arts degree program and permission of instructor. Fall.

This course provides an introduction to the concepts of management as an element of theater production and organizational operations. The course focuses on the requirements of the day-to-day operations of a theater center with a special emphasis on the agency’s responsibility to its stakeholders, including the community, the board, audience members, production staff, guest artists, company members and volunteers. Topics include organizational philosophies and analysis, establishing audience attitudes and expectations, box office and accounting, front of house management and publicity. Fall.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: Designed for an exploration of one of the many facets of theater and drama. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: Designed for an exploration of one of the many facets of theater and drama. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: Designed for an exploration of one of the many facets of theater and drama. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

Prerequisites: THAR 2361, THAR 2362, THAR 3307 or THAR 3308, and qualifying audition.
Full Course Description: Advanced training in acting styles, selected from the Greek, Roman, Medieval, Shakespearean, Restoration, Commedia and various 20th- century styles. Prerequisites: THAR 2361, THAR 2362, THAR 3307 or THAR 3308, and qualifying audition. Fall.

This course will be divided into preproduction, rehearsal period, tech/ running and maintenance of production segments. The objective is to thoroughly introduce the student to the different venues of theater from small storefront theaters to large regional theaters. Although the emphasis will be on management practices for theater in all venues, there will be an element of the practical day-to-day “nuts and bolts” in the stage management process, divided into preproduction and postproduction processes.

This course involves the student more intensely in one or more aspects of theater production at Mary Moody Northen Theatre: scenery, lighting, costumes, makeup, props, sound, publicity and front-of-house. Prerequisites: THAR 2128 and THAR 2130. Fall.

Other Requirements / Notes: Open to both majors and non-majors who have successfully completed two hours of THAR 2160. May be repeated for credit.
Full Course Description: Open to both majors and non-majors who have successfully completed two hours of THAR 2160. This course involves the student more intensely in one or more aspects of theater production at Mary Moody Northen Theatre: scenery, lighting, costumes, makeup, props, sound, publicity, and front-of-house. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisites: Two credits of THAR 2160.

Prerequisites: At least two of the following: THAR 3323, THAR 3324, THAR 3325, THAR 3326, THAR 3336.
Full Course Description: This course focuses on the development of a presentational and professional portfolio. Initial assessment of student work deals with portfolio quality as a means of communication appropriate to the student’s aspirations as a designer. Simulated job interviews and outside critique are components. Ideally taken second semester of the junior year to allow for ongoing development. Prerequisites: At least two of the following: THAR 3323, THAR 3324, THAR 3325, THAR 3326, THAR 3336. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: This course introduces the student to various methods of learning, including understanding of the International Phonetic Alphabet, kinesthetic understanding, and rhythmic integration.
Full Course Description: Techniques of acquiring and incorporating dialects and accents for stage and film. This course introduces the student to various methods of learning, including understanding of the International Phonetic Alphabet, kinesthetic understanding, and rhythmic integration. Prerequisites: THAR 3330, THAR 3307, and THAR 3308. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: Continuation of the development and application of the necessary performance techniques required for film and television acting.
Full Course Description: Continuation of the development and application of the necessary performance techniques required for film and television acting. Students receive experience in front of the camera with more demanding material and will complete the course with some material for their professional reel. Prerequisites: Performance IA through Performance IV. Spring.

Prerequisites: THAR 2310, THAR 2347, THAR 2361, THAR 2362, nine additional hours of Theater Arts courses, junior standing or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: THAR 2310, THAR 2347, THAR 2361, THAR 2362, nine additional hours of Theater Arts courses, junior standing or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Study of the principles and techniques of play direction, with particular attention focused on analysis of scripts, development of plans of staging and work with actors. Prerequisites: THAR 2310, THAR 2347, THAR 2361, THAR 2362, nine additional hours of Theater Arts courses, junior standing or permission of instructor. Fall.
Intensive individual work at an advanced level, culminating in an acting recital. Prerequisites: All Acting emphasis requirements. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Deals with the interpretation of a script in relation to the scenic requirements of a production. Scenic projects include the development of floor plans, elevations, painter’s elevations, and a working model or rendering. Students work with properties and scenic painting techniques. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Deals with the interpretation of a script in relation to the lighting requirements of a production. Lighting projects include the development of the light plot,section, instrument schedule and work related to the actual production. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Deals with the interpretation of a script in relation to the costuming requirements of a production. Costume projects include researching, designing, budgeting, swatching and constructing an actual production. Costume work also includes some rendering and a continuation of costume history. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Students will complete special projects connected to specified aspects of production work. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Students will complete special projects connected to specified aspects of production work. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Students will complete special projects connected to specified aspects of production work. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: THAR 3326 and permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: The student will interpret a script in relation to the makeup design requirements of a production in theater, film or television. Makeup projects include researching, designing, creating budgets, purchasing products and creating facial plots for an actual production in our main theater. The course also includes a continuation of the history of makeup. Prerequisites: THAR 3326 and permission of instructor.

Prerequisites: THAR 3334, PSYC 2301 or PSYC 2316, PHIL 2303, and MGMT 2320.
Full Course Description: This course develops and expands upon the topics presented in Theater Management I. Includes a significant focus on theater management case studies and the application of concepts. Prerequisites: THAR 3334, PSYC 2301 or PSYC 2316, PHIL 2303, and MGMT 2320. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This course is designed to allow for exploration of one of the many facets in theater management. Topics may include, but are not limited to, Eventology, Production Management, House Management, Box Office Management, Nonprofit Theater Management, directed reading and the like. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated once for credit. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Designed to meet the professional needs of an advanced student through assignment to various theater or arts organizations. May be repeated once for credit. For positions requiring 40–60 hours of work during a semester. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated once for credit. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Designed to meet the professional needs of an advanced student through assignment to various theater or arts organizations. May be repeated once for credit. For positions requiring 80–100 hours of work during a semester, including assistantships for THAR classes. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated once for credit. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Designed to meet the professional needs of an advanced student through assignment to various theater or arts organizations. May be repeated once for credit. For positions requiring 120–150 hours of work during a semester. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

This course involves the student in one or more aspects of theater production at Mary Moody Northen Theatre. It provides students with more responsibility for such things as scenery, lighting, costumes, makeup, props, sound, publicity and box-office activities. Prerequisites: THAR 2128, THAR 2130 and THAR 3153. Spring.

Prerequisites: ALL required previous performance, voice and movement courses in degree sequence.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: ALL required previous performance, voice and movement courses in degree sequence.
Full Course Description: A professional and practical application of techniques learned in Performance IV with emphasis placed on preparing for a professional showcase with agents and casting directors. Students will properly prep work and line up showcase guests. Prerequisites: ALL required previous performance, voice and movement courses in degree sequence. Spring.

Prerequisites: THAR 2306 and THAR 3323 or THAR 3324 or THAR 3325.
Semester Offered: Offered in even-numbered school years, this class will let the student explore and grow in their use of several computer programs to realize visual representations of their designs.
Full Course Description: Offered in even-numbered school years, this class will let the student explore and grow in their use of several computer programs to realize visual representations of their designs. Programs used in the class include Vectorworks, Renderworks, Photoshop, InDesign and Sketch up. Prerequisites: THAR 2306 and THAR 3323 or THAR 3324 or THAR 3325.

Other Requirements / Notes: An understanding of the roles of the actors’ unions, agents and casting directors in the professional world as well as the reality of living in a major city like New York or Los Angeles will be discussed.
Full Course Description: This course is designed to prepare the student for the realities and rigors of the business side of the arts with specific emphasis on theater, television and film. An understanding of the roles of the actors’ unions, agents and casting directors in the professional world as well as the reality of living in a major city like New York or Los Angeles will be discussed. The audition process for stage and screen, from how to get the audition to booking the role, will be explored using both performance and projects, as well as the audition/interview process for graduate school. Prerequisite: THAR 4341. Fall.

Other Requirements / Notes: Professional stage management procedures will be stressed with a full understanding of Actor’s Equity Association’s different contracts, rules and procedures, safety issues, stage management forms and formats as well as other industry unions and organizations.
Full Course Description: This course will cover in-depth procedures from first production meeting to closing/strike of a professional theatrical production. Professional stage management procedures will be stressed with a full understanding of Actor’s Equity Association’s different contracts, rules and procedures, safety issues, stage management forms and formats as well as other industry unions and organizations.

Expanding on what the student learned in Lighting Design I, this class will focus on creating a more complete lighting design project completing two full thought-out paper designs with shop orders, which will each include a lab component. The class will also collaborate with select Performance Classes. Prerequisites: THAR 2304, THAR 2306 and THAR 3323.

Expanding on what the student learned in Scene Design I, this class will focus on more difficult scenic design challenges and take the student further toward a more complete design packet, including colored models, full set of designer draftings and painter elevations. Class projects will result in portfolio pieces. Prerequisites: THAR 2304, THAR 2306 and THAR 3324.

Other Requirements / Notes: This course asks students who have completed Costume Design I to take their design ideas closer to realization through fabric choices, more detailed research, and the overall design perspective required for complete plays.
Full Course Description: This course asks students who have completed Costume Design I to take their design ideas closer to realization through fabric choices, more detailed research, and the overall design perspective required for complete plays. Class projects should result in portfolio pieces virtually ready to go into production. Prerequisites: THAR 2320, THAR 2306 and THAR 3325.

Prerequisites: The student’s respective Design II class, THAR 3325 or THAR 3323 or THAR 3324, or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: The student’s respective Design II class, THAR 3325 or THAR 3323 or THAR 3324, or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Students who have completed courses in at least two areas of design study will have the opportunity to create cohesive production designs on paper and in model form, incorporating all the areas of set, costume, lighting and makeup. Instructors and invited guests in each field of design and direction will participate in guidance and critique of the student work. Prerequisites: The student’s respective Design II class, THAR 3325 or THAR 3323 or THAR 3324, or permission of instructor.

Other Requirements / Notes: Through analysis and practice, students will gain an understanding of how various modes of information delivery and methods of interaction affect user experiences.
Full Course Description: This course builds awareness of designed experiences across the natural, built, and digital worlds. Through analysis and practice, students will gain an understanding of how various modes of information delivery and methods of interaction affect user experiences. Coursework consists of a fast succession of hands-on experiments and application of fundamental user experience principles.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This course is intended to provide students in UXDE with an opportunity to focus on a user experience related topic not covered in the major. Specific topics vary depending upon the faculty area of expertise. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

This course is a survey of the methods, processes, and tools used in UX Design. Students will explore how to approach, identify, and address problems. Special focus will be given to studying users, aka people, and the systems that shape their experiences.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for elective credit when topics vary, but does not count toward progression in the UXDE major or minor.
Full Course Description: This course is intended to provide students interested in UXDE with an opportunity to discover a user experience related topic not covered in other offered courses. Specific topics vary depending upon the faculty area of expertise. May be repeated for elective credit when topics vary, but does not count toward progression in the UXDE major or minor.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for elective credit when topics vary, but does not count toward progression in the UXDE major or minor.
Full Course Description: This course is intended to provide students interested in UXDE with an opportunity to discover a user experience related topic not covered in other offered courses. Specific topics vary depending upon the faculty area of expertise. May be repeated for elective credit when topics vary, but does not count toward progression in the UXDE major or minor.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for elective credit when topics vary, but does not count toward progression in the UXDE major or minor.
Full Course Description: This course is intended to provide students interested in UXDE with an opportunity to discover a user experience related topic not covered in other offered courses. Specific topics vary depending upon the faculty area of expertise. May be repeated for elective credit when topics vary, but does not count toward progression in the UXDE major or minor.

This course explores the characteristics, conventions, and components of UX projects made for digital spaces. Students will integrate skills and concepts from prior courses to create digital UX solutions to assigned problems. Pre- or corequisite: UXDE 2311. Spring.

This course explores the various aspects and interdependencies of UX projects in hybrid modalities. Students will integrate skills and concepts from prior courses to create hybrid UX solutions to assigned problems. Pre- or corequisite: UXDE 2311. Fall.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Junior standing.
Full Course Description: This course will give students the opportunity to work with a community partner. Students will apply UX processes to address a social issue in the partner’s specific community. Collaboration will be a key aspect of the course. Prerequisite: Junior standing. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This course is intended to provide students in UXDE with an opportunity to focus on a user experience related topic not covered in the major. Specific topics vary depending upon the faculty area of expertise. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: UXDE 3320, UXDE 3322, and senior standing.
Full Course Description: This course serves as the Culminating Experience for UXDE majors. Students apply their accumulated skills and knowledge to design a self- initiated UX project. Students will write both a narrative of the project and a reflection of their experiences at St. Edward’s. Prerequisites: UXDE 3320, UXDE 3322, and senior standing. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This course is intended to provide students in UXDE with an opportunity to focus on a user experience related topic not covered in the major. Specific topics vary depending upon the faculty area of expertise. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

This course is an introduction to the video game medium from the perspectives of technology, business, and culture. The course teaches global developments in video games and contextualizes issues in terms of their impacts on the local and regional industry. Fall.

This course explores the fundamentals of play: its needs in human behavior and expressions in games. Students will study non-digital games and methods of paper prototyping, with an emphasis on play/ test/iterate methodology. Spring.

This course covers the basics of logic, computer literacy, and software creation. Students initially learn programming concepts using spreadsheets, and then move to Python to create simple games, automate tasks, and solve real-world problems. Fall. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This course is intended to provide students with an opportunity to focus on a specialized topic in game studies. Specific topics vary depending on the faculty area of expertise. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

This course explores imagined worlds in fairy tales, science fiction, horror, and fantasy, through such media as novels, comics, film and video games. World building describes a creator’s development of rules and paradigms for a fictional universe to underpin stories and reflect the concerns of genre and audience. Students will learn how writers use world building to develop characters, plot, and lore. Prerequisite: WRIT 1301.

This course builds on concepts introduced in Beginning Coding for Non-Coders by exploring client/server models, databases, and simple app design and development. Prerequisite: VGAM 1310 or COSC 1305. Spring.

This course gives students a solid background in the theory and techniques of two-dimensional animation, including the production of animated assets and user interface elements for video games. Prerequisite: ARTS 1316. Spring.

This course will introduce students to digital audio design and production for animation and video games, with emphasis on sound effects, digital audio recording, audio editing, post production, dialog recording, and the integration of sound assets into video game and film projects.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This course is intended to provide students with an opportunity to focus on a specialized topic in game studies. Specific topics vary depending on the faculty area of expertise. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

Prerequisites: VGAM 1303 and either VGAM 1310 or COSC 1305.
Full Course Description: This studio course covers the basics of digital gameplay and gives students the opportunity to begin their game design portfolio by conceiving, creating, critiquing, and correcting a number of two- dimensional video game projects. Prerequisites: VGAM 1303 and either VGAM 1310 or COSC 1305. Fall.

Other Requirements / Notes: This course will give students a solid understanding of the theory and techniques of digital three-dimensional art creation and animation, with emphasis on the production of both static and animated digital assets.
Full Course Description: This course will give students a solid understanding of the theory and techniques of digital three-dimensional art creation and animation, with emphasis on the production of both static and animated digital assets. Prerequisite: VGAM 2323. Fall.

Other Requirements / Notes: It develops skills necessary for writers and designers of interactive works: a solid understanding of traditional narrative theory, character development, plot, dialogue, backstory and world creation.
Full Course Description: This seminar gives students experience with the particular challenges of interactive narrative. It develops skills necessary for writers and designers of interactive works: a solid understanding of traditional narrative theory, character development, plot, dialogue, backstory and world creation. The course will explore storytelling in video games, interactive fiction, and collaborative story construction. No particular technical skills are necessary to succeed in this course. Prerequisite: WRIT 2303.

This studio course will instruct the basics of game design and gives students the opportunity to expand their game design portfolio with the creation three-dimensional video games using sound game design techniques and methods. Prerequisite: VGAM 3320. Spring.

This course focuses on methods of designing, planning, and producing digital applications. Students work in teams to create their own simple games or apps. Topics covered will include designing to a specification, efforts estimates, task tracking, types of testing, and release considerations. Prerequisite: VGAM 1310 or COSC 1305. Fall.

Prerequisites: Junior standing or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: Junior standing or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course is intended to provide students with an opportunity to focus on a specialized topic in game studies. Specific topics vary depending on the faculty area of expertise. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: Junior standing or permission of instructor.

This course, the first of two culminating courses in the major, will be a hands-on experience of the overall process of video game development. Students will develop and pitch game concepts, then form teams to develop selected concepts through the pre-production phase to a “pre- Alpha” state. Prerequisite: VGAM 3330. Fall.

This course, the final of two culminating courses in the major, will continue a hands-on experience in game development and testing. Students will work in teams to develop their game projects through the Alpha, Beta, and release phases. Prerequisite: VGAM 4345. Spring.

Prerequisites: Junior standing or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: Junior standing or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course is intended to provide students with an opportunity to focus on a specialized topic in game studies. Specific topics vary depending on the faculty area of expertise. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. Prerequisite: Junior standing or permission of instructor.

In this course, students interested in ANIM, ARTS, GDES, PHOT and VGAM will explore a variety of media and methods for creative visual expression. With an emphasis on general visual literacy, students will develop needed technical facility for project realization, including competencies in basic digital camera operation, image organization, basic image post-production and basic scanning techniques. Fall. Spring.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This course is intended to provide students majoring in ANIM, ARTS, GDES, PHOT and VGAM with an opportunity to focus on an interdisciplinary topic in visual studies not covered in the traditional curriculum. Specific topics vary depending upon the faculty area of expertise. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This course is intended to provide students majoring in ANIM, ARTS, GDES, PHOT and VGAM with an opportunity to focus on an interdisciplinary topic in visual studies not covered in the traditional curriculum. Specific topics vary depending upon the faculty area of expertise. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

This course will explore various two-dimensional sequential art forms, with a focus on comics and graphic novels. Students will practice both textual and visual analysis of readings, and use the unique language of comics to create original visual narratives.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: Junior standing.
Full Course Description: In this course, students explore career options available within creative disciplines, develop a portfolio of their work, and build basic career management skills. A portfolio review and introduction of strategies for success as a creative professional allow students to begin planning for graduation and beyond. Intended for ARTS, GDES, and PHOT majors. Prerequisite: Junior standing. Fall.

Other Requirements / Notes: This course is open to all students majoring in ARTS, GDES, PHOT, VGAM, or with permission of instructor. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. This course may substitute for ANIM 3399, ARTS 3399, GDES 3399, PHOT 3322, or VGAM 3399 with approval of the student’s academic advisor.
Full Course Description: This course is intended to provide students majoring in ANIM, ARTS, GDES, PHOT and VGAM with an opportunity to focus on an interdisciplinary topic in visual studies not covered in the traditional curriculum. Specific topics vary depending upon the faculty area of expertise. This course is open to all students majoring in ARTS, GDES, PHOT, VGAM, or with permission of instructor. May be repeated for credit when topics vary. This course may substitute for ANIM 3399, ARTS 3399, GDES 3399, PHOT 3322, or VGAM 3399 with approval of the student’s academic advisor.

Other Requirements / Notes: This required senior-level course is part one of the two-part Culminating Experience for students majoring in ANIM, ARTS, GDES, and PHOT. Prerequisite: Senior standing.
Full Course Description: This required senior-level course is part one of the two-part Culminating Experience for students majoring in ANIM, ARTS, GDES, and PHOT. Students apply their knowledge from previous coursework combined with their personal interests and related research to develop and execute a self-initiated project related to their discipline or area of study. Work in this course continues in the Senior Studio II course. Prerequisite: Senior standing. Fall.

Other Requirements / Notes: This required senior-level course is part two of the two-part Culminating Experience for students majoring in ANIM, ARTS, GDES, and PHOT.
Full Course Description: This required senior-level course is part two of the two-part Culminating Experience for students majoring in ANIM, ARTS, GDES, and PHOT. In this course, students complete and publish, exhibit, or otherwise publicly present their creative research projects begun in Senior Studio I. Students also complete professional portfolios appropriate to their career interests and goals. Through class discussions, students refine their personal post-graduation goals, reflect on their experiences here at St. Edward’s, and complete a written thesis paper. Prerequisites: VISU 3335 and VISU 4340. Spring.

This course entails students working in a research setting utilizing the Wild Basin Preserve as a living laboratory. Students will apply for enrollment and will be selected from each of the schools on the St. Edward’s University campus. Applications put forward by students will consist of the proposed project along with the faculty member who will be mentoring that particular project for the semester. A course coordinator will have administrative oversight of the course, and will host weekly meetings with all students throughout the semester. Students for whom projects are chosen will receive a total package of $600 to help purchase supplies and pay for miscellaneous needs during the semester. All projects will require a final paper and presentation of the work at the SOURCE conference in the Spring semester. Fall.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Course Title: Introduction to Women’s and Gender Studies
Other Requirements / Notes: We will focus on understanding the concept of gender and gender identities, along with concepts such as selfhood, subjectivity, socialization, marginality, privilege, difference, commonality and community. Satisfies Diverse American Perspectives and Social Identities requirements.
Full Course Description: The goal of this course is to acquaint students with issues and concepts central to women’s and gender studies as academic disciplines. We will focus on understanding the concept of gender and gender identities, along with concepts such as selfhood, subjectivity, socialization, marginality, privilege, difference, commonality and community. Students will also learn about other social identities, such as class, race, ethnicity and sexuality as categories of cultural analysis and as factors that intersect and shape people’s particular experiences. We will approach and examine these topics through a range of theoretical, historical, (auto)biographical and literary readings as well as film, print media and guest lectures. Satisfies Diverse American Perspectives and Social Identities requirements.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Course Title: Community Service in Women’s and Gender

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: WGST 1301 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Provides a learning experience in a community service organization or agency. Provides an opportunity to learn how social agencies grapple with gender, sexuality and/or women’s issues and to test interest and aptitude for careers in such fields. May be repeated for credit. This course fulfills the Experiential Learning for Social Justice Mission Marker and the Social Identities Mission Marker. 2339 Special Topics in Women’s and Gender Studies 3 hours Topics are selected based on the interests and needs of students and on areas of faculty expertise. Prerequisite: WGST 1301 or permission of instructor.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: WGST 1301 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: Provides a learning experience in a community service organization or agency. Provides an opportunity to learn how social agencies grapple with gender, sexuality and/or women’s issues and to test interest and aptitude for careers in such fields. May be repeated for credit. This course fulfills the Experiential Learning for Social Justice Mission Marker and the Social Identities Mission Marker. 2339 Special Topics in Women’s and Gender Studies 3 hours Topics are selected based on the interests and needs of students and on areas of faculty expertise. Prerequisite: WGST 1301 or permission of instructor.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies the Social Identities requirement.
Full Course Description: This course explores feminist theory as it has been constructed by activists, artists, academics, and political figures and helps students identify how to apply this body of knowledge in their field. Students will conduct research to determine how WGST applies to their discipline or major. Satisfies the Social Identities requirement.

School: School of Behavioral and Social Sciences
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: WGST 1301 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course focuses on the rich diversity of women’s voices in literature, emphasizing works by authors from various racial, socioeconomic, sexual, and cultural identities, with an emphasis on intersectional identity. Students will read, discuss, and write about literature across genres, including novels, short stories, essays, and poetry. In addition to exploring the contributions of women writers to feminist thought and the American literary canon, the course also situates their work within relevant historical and social contexts. 3339 Special Topics in Women’s and Gender Studies 3 hours Topics are selected based on the interests and needs of students and on areas of faculty expertise. Prerequisite: WGST 1301 or permission of instructor.

This course prepares students for college-level literacy practices in three distinct ways: (1) critical engagement with research-based (nonfiction) texts, (2) adaptation of (previous) literacy practices, and (3) rigorous revision. Students will develop a vocabulary for talking about reading and writing to understand their writing practices. Students will demonstrate an awareness of writing as a social and rhetorical activity by completing formal and informal assignments that require them to make strategic choices as a writer in a variety of situations. Class work includes discussion, analysis of rhetorical situations, experimentation with heuristics for composing and revising as well as participating in writing as a social activity through reading, collaboration, peer review, and other forms of feedback. Grades assigned are A, B, C, N and F. 1306 Writing and Rhetoric I (International Students) 3 hours Instruction and practice in the techniques of composing in English with emphasis on referential writing and self-expression primarily through journal writing. Students will write both in-class and out-of-class essays. Strategies of the composing process are also presented. Grades assigned are A, B, C, N and F.

The Writing Practicum provides writing instruction to help students focus on individual problems with written English. Taken concurrently with WRIT 1301 or 2302, Writing Practicum offers additional support as students learn to apply research-based practices and argument-focused writing strategies. The practicum will also help students gain competent control of standard English, as well as the mechanical conventions of writing. Grades assigned are A, B, C, N and F. Corequisite: WRIT 1301 or WRIT 2302.

Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies the Creativity and Making requirement and a Writing Rich Mission Marker.
Full Course Description: This course provides an introduction to creative writing in any two or three of the following genres selected by the instructor: fiction, poetry, playwriting, and creative nonfiction. It also provides an overview of and practice using the terminology and techniques involved in writing in these genres as well as exposure to and appreciation for published works in the selected forms. Students will be expected to write, workshop, and revise pieces in both genres. Satisfies the Creativity and Making requirement and a Writing Rich Mission Marker.

Prerequisites: WRIT 1301 and sophomore standing. Grades assigned are A, B, C, N and F.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: WRIT 1301 and sophomore standing.
Full Course Description: This course prepares students for college-level research-based writing practices in three distinct ways: (1) critical analysis of scholarly texts for research-based purposes, (2) adaptation of (previous) research and research-based writing practices, and (3) rigorous revision. This course builds on students’ vocabulary and awareness of writing as a social and rhetorical activity (begun in WRIT 1301) by asking them to develop their abilities to ask questions about how writing works as a way of knowing in discourse communities. Students will use the writing and research produced in various discourse communities as artifacts (or objects) of study in the writing course. Students will develop and refine their research-based writing practices, including reading (scholarly) sources, collecting and analyzing data, and presenting findings. Class work includes rhetorical analyses of writing and data, development of research practices and procedures suited for a research question and appropriate for a revision-centered workflow, and presentation of findings in multiple genres for various audiences. Prerequisites: WRIT 1301 and sophomore standing. Grades assigned are A, B, C, N and F.

Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies a Writing Rich requirement.
Full Course Description: This seminar course provides an introduction to writing and critiquing the dramatic form, an overview of and practice using craft terminology and techniques, and exposure to and appreciation for a variety of published work. Students will be expected to complete write, revise, participate in small- and large-group discussions, and produce write and revise several short scripts for table readings during the course of the semester. Satisfies a Writing Rich requirement.

Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies the Creativity and Making requirement for General Education.
Full Course Description: This special topic seminar course will give writers the opportunity to develop craft skills and creative writing projects. Satisfies the Creativity and Making requirement for General Education.

Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies Creativity and Making as well as a Writing Rich requirement.
Full Course Description: This seminar course provides an introduction to writing and critiquing creative nonfiction (including memoir and essay), an overview of and practice using craft terminology and techniques, and exposure to and appreciation for a variety of published work. Students will be expected to write, workshop, and revise their own creative nonfiction during the course of the semester. Satisfies Creativity and Making as well as a Writing Rich requirement.

This course will introduce students to the study of media artifacts, analysis, and production through the frame of digital storytelling, exposition, and/or argument. In exploring the ubiquity of (digital) text, students will gain an ability to understand, criticize, choose, and apply different media to match different messages. This course takes a project-based approach to learning about media and producing media objects such as digital essays, podcasts, social media, video, websites, and mobile apps. Although the course will ask students to work with a variety of technologies and tools, the primary aim of in-class activities will be to experiment with available means of production to tell stories that are personally and culturally relevant. Ultimately this course will demonstrate to (potential) English majors how digital media is changing (and being changed by) the forms of writing present in rhetorical studies, professional writing, creative writing, and journalism.

Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies the Creativity and Making requirement and fulfills the Social Identities Mission Marker.
Full Course Description: This seminar course provides an introduction to writing and critiquing poetry, an overview of and practice using craft terminology and techniques, and exposure to and appreciation for a variety of published work. Students will be expected to write, workshop, and revise their own poems during the course of the semester. Satisfies the Creativity and Making requirement and fulfills the Social Identities Mission Marker.

Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies a Writing Rich Mission Marker.
Full Course Description: In this course, students examine what writing looks like in different workplaces, such as in Editing and Publishing, Technical Writing, and Content Management and Creation. Such study reveals the underlying principles that guide professional writing. Students put these principles into practice through a major project where they employ professional research methods and principles. Professional portfolio is launched in this course. Satisfies a Writing Rich Mission Marker.

Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies Social Identities, Writing Rich, and Creativity and Making requirements.
Full Course Description: This seminar course provides an introduction to writing and critiquing the short story form, an overview of and practice using craft terminology and techniques, and exposure to and appreciation for a variety of published work. Students will be expected to write, workshop, and revise their own short fiction during the course of the semester. Satisfies Social Identities, Writing Rich, and Creativity and Making requirements.

Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies the Creativity and Making and a Writing Rich requirement.
Full Course Description: This seminar course introduces students to environmental texts. Students investigate how narratives of nature are crafted and recrafted by humans. Students analyze craft choices and come to understand key questions that drive environmental writing. By producing their own work, students gain an appreciation for what makes writing memorable, resonant, and meaningful. Students will be expected to write, workshop, and revise their own drafts during the course of the semester. Satisfies the Creativity and Making and a Writing Rich requirement.

Pre- or Corequisites: WRIT 2302. Recommended prerequisites for WRIT majors: WRIT 2311 and either WRIT 2300 or WRIT 2313.
Full Course Description: In this course, students will apply grammatical conventions and terminology, identifying the structure of sentences to enhance proficiency in writing and editing for a variety of audiences, with an emphasis on clarity, cohesion, coherence, rhythm, and figurative language. Pre- or corequisite: WRIT 2302. Recommended prerequisites for WRIT majors: WRIT 2311 and either WRIT 2300 or WRIT 2313.

This course focuses on the processes, aims and modes of referential writing, including appropriate documentation of sources. Emphasis is on writing essays based upon source material and on the critical thinking skills of analysis, summary, synthesis and evaluation. Students will be expected to demonstrate intermediate competence in the strategies of the writing processes of invention, composition and revision. Coursework includes analysis of occasion, audience and purpose, as well as peer critiques and evaluation.

Pre- or Corequisites: WRIT 2302. Recommended prerequisites for WRIT majors: WRIT 2311 and either WRIT 2300 or WRIT 2313.
Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies a Writing Rich Mission Marker.
Full Course Description: Students will analyze communication in different media for audience, purpose, genre, values, and other goals using varied methods of rhetorical criticism and discourse analysis. Students will learn to pose and answer a clear research question, to argue substantively, coherently, and concisely in crafted expository prose, to give and receive useful feedback on writing, and to revise their work effectively. Satisfies a Writing Rich Mission Marker. Pre- or corequisite: WRIT 2302. Recommended prerequisites for WRIT majors: WRIT 2311 and either WRIT 2300 or WRIT 2313.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: A course that offers students a specific focus or topic in the area of writing and editing. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: WRIT 2314 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course is an intermediate seminar where writers will analyze published work as well as develop advanced craft and workshop skills. Students will be expected to write, workshop, and revise their own short fiction during the course of the semester. Prerequisite: WRIT 2314 or permission of instructor.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: WRIT 2303 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course is an intermediate seminar where writers will develop craft skills and scripts for stage, film, television, and video games. Students will read and discuss exemplary scripts, learn format, and complete genre-appropriate exercises to develop and visualize stories and characters. Prerequisite: WRIT 2303 or permission of instructor.

Prerequisites: WRIT 2302 and at least one of the following WRIT workshops 2300, 2303, 2305, 2312, or 2314. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit when topics vary.
Full Course Description: This special topic seminar course will give writers the opportunity to develop intermediate craft skills and creative writing projects. Prerequisites: WRIT 2302 and at least one of the following WRIT workshops 2300, 2303, 2305, 2312, or 2314. May be repeated for credit when topics vary.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: WRIT 2312 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course is an intermediate seminar where writers will analyze full- length published work as well as continue to develop advanced craft and workshop skills. Students will be expected to write, workshop, and revise their own poems during the course of the semester. Prerequisite: WRIT 2312 or permission of instructor.

Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies an upper-division Writing Rich Mission Marker.
Full Course Description: This course focuses on applied rhetoric and is designed to give undergraduate students practical experience in writing clear, effective plain-language legal documents. The course offers a range of realistic legal writing problems but does not presume any specialized legal knowledge. The writing assignments will help students to improve their writing and editing skills and to refine their abilities to analyze and develop arguments within a legal context. Satisfies an upper-division Writing Rich Mission Marker. Prerequisite: WRIT 2302.

In this course, students conduct research and convey complex, technical information to different audiences. Topics include audience and organizational needs, visual rhetoric and information design, ethics, technical style, usability testing, and team writing. Prerequisite: WRIT 2302.

Prerequisites: WRIT 2320 or JOUR 2310 (and for WRIT majors, at least one course from concentration area).
Full Course Description: Students will receive extensive practice in revising for purpose, audience, form, arrangement, euphony, tone, and rhythm. The course explores a variety of editing techniques for achieving particular goals, including clarity, emphasis, and integrating source material effectively and ethically. Assigned readings will demonstrate a range of approaches to style and craft. Prerequisite: WRIT 2320 or JOUR 2310 (and for WRIT majors, at least one course from concentration area).

Other Requirements / Notes: Students will produce multimedia messages, but no prior technology training is required. Satisfies the Exploring Artistic Works and Social Identities requirements.
Full Course Description: This course introduces principles of copywriting for print and digital advertising as well as strategies for writing customized press kits designed to reach journalists and bloggers. Mock campaigns will be created for nonprofit and for-profit organizations. Students will produce multimedia messages, but no prior technology training is required. Satisfies the Exploring Artistic Works and Social Identities requirements. Prerequisite: WRIT 2302.

Prerequisites: WRIT 2302. Satisfies a Writing Rich requirement.
Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies a Writing Rich requirement.
Full Course Description: This course blends rhetorical principles and writing practices, focusing on effective, ethical communication in workplace writing situations. Students will compose projects in a variety of relevant contexts, media, and genres and for multiple purposes and audiences. Students will have opportunities to learn how to be effective collaborators as well as strong individual business writers. Prerequisite: WRIT 2302. Satisfies a Writing Rich requirement.

Prerequisites: WRIT 2302 and WRIT 2311, or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: WRIT 2302 and WRIT 2311, or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course is designed to help students produce sophisticated documents for print production. As they use desktop publishing software, students will strengthen their skills in producing texts for specific occasions, audience and purposes. Students will also learn to critique the content and design of print documents in various genres. Most assignments are group projects designed to develop skills necessary for effective workplace collaboration. Prerequisites: WRIT 2302 and WRIT 2311, or permission of instructor.

In this course, students will explore and strategize digital content’s place in social media through the study of information architecture, user experience, and case studies of successful social media campaigns. This advanced course builds on the theory and practices contained in introductory professional writing courses by aligning content to organizational goals and audiences and assessing content’s effective reach through tools such as analytics and usability testing. Prerequisite: WRIT 2302.

This course examines important theories, ancient and contemporary, of rhetoric and writing. Students will analyze and apply those theories to recurring problems at the intersections of rhetoric, writing, literacy, language, media, and power via a variety of research methods and assignments. Prerequisites: WRIT 2320 or JOUR 2310, and WRIT 2325.

Building on foundational coursework in grammar and revision, Advanced Editing provides extensive practice with a full spectrum of print and digital editorial practices. Tracing the potential career path of a professional editor, instruction begins with proofreading and copyediting in a variety of styles before progressing to line editing, macro editing, and selecting works for publication. Applications range from technical and academic texts to mainstream and literary works. Prerequisites: WRIT 2302, WRIT 2320, and WRIT 3330.

This course examines the methods and methodologies of the research community that takes writing as its subject—defining method as a technique of gathering evidence and methodology as the underlying theory and analysis of how research proceeds. We review research methodologies and foundational research theories and protocols to understand how the community of researchers formed since the 1960s has relied upon diverse ways to make knowledge about writing and writing processes. We examine qualitative approaches that are common or that are gaining prominence in this community, with emphasis on ethnography and case study as well as external influences, such as feminism and collaborative scholarship. The curriculum is sequential, helping students to identify a study topic, formulate inquiry questions, organize a literature review, and select appropriate research designs and methodologies. Prerequisites: WRIT 2300 and WRIT 2313.

Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisite: WRIT 2302 or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: A course introducing students to the processes, principles and techniques of writing and editing for specialized writing situations. Prerequisite: WRIT 2302 or permission of instructor.

This practical course builds on concepts introduced in WRIT 2320 and WRIT 3330, and gives students the opportunity to apply stylistic strategies to their own and others’ writing. Students will learn to fine- tune style using diction, sound qualities, and sentence architecture to customize their work for particular audiences and publications. Students will work primarily with essay writing and will learn to submit their work to both online and print publications. Prerequisite: WRIT 3330.

Prerequisites: WRIT 2302 and junior or senior standing, or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies an upper-division Writing Rich requirement. Prerequisites: WRIT 2302 and junior or senior standing, or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: This course is designed to prepare students to write feature articles for publication in magazines and other periodicals. Students learn to analyze markets, write a query letter, conduct interviews and write effective articles for targeted audiences. Satisfies an upper-division Writing Rich requirement. Prerequisites: WRIT 2302 and junior or senior standing, or permission of instructor.

Prerequisites: Junior or senior standing and at least one of the following: WRIT 2312, WRIT 2314, WRIT 2303, WRIT 2304, WRIT 2305, or permission of instructor.
Other Requirements / Notes: Prerequisites: Junior or senior standing and at least one of the following: WRIT 2312, WRIT 2314, WRIT 2303, WRIT 2304, WRIT 2305, or permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: A workshop seminar for experienced writers to develop further their creative skills and the kinds of writing they prefer. Writers over the course of the semester will be expected to develop a small selection of writing projects, participate in small-group discussions and individual meetings with the instructor, revise their work extensively, and produce a substantial manuscript at the end of the semester. Prerequisites: Junior or senior standing and at least one of the following: WRIT 2312, WRIT 2314, WRIT 2303, WRIT 2304, WRIT 2305, or permission of instructor.

Course Title: Career Preparation in Writing and Rhetoric
Full Course Description: This course is designed to help students transition from college to a profession or graduate school. Students will have the opportunity to reflect on the work they have done in the major as they produce a portfolio of writing to present to a faculty evaluation committee and to future employers or graduate schools. They will also have the opportunity to work on a graduate school application, to update their résumés and to write sample job application cover letters. In addition, students will learn about a variety of career opportunities available to writers. Prerequisite: WRIT 2302 or equivalent.

Course Title: Career Preparation in Writing and Rhetoric
Full Course Description: This course is designed to help students transition from college to a profession or graduate school. Students will have the opportunity to reflect on the work they have done in the major as they produce a portfolio of writing to present to a faculty evaluation committee and to future employers or graduate schools. They will also have the opportunity to work on a graduate school application, to update their résumés and to write sample job application cover letters. In addition, students will learn about a variety of career opportunities available to writers. Prerequisite: WRIT 2302 or equivalent.

Prerequisites: WRIT 2302. Non-majors welcome with junior or senior standing.
Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies an upper-division Writing Rich Mission Marker and an Experiential Learning for Social Justice Mission Marker. Non-majors welcome with junior or senior standing.
Full Course Description: Writing grant proposals is a high-stakes game. The rhetorical and political challenges associated with proposal writing are unique among workplace documents. This course is designed to give students real- world experience in authoring grant proposals. It is a hands-on course in which students will learn to navigate the entire grant-funding process. The course will work through the identification of grant opportunities, crafting of proposal documents, and submission through digital, print and oral presentation channels. Special emphasis will be placed on negotiating the rhetorical dynamics of the grant process: expressing client needs clearly and addressing the funding agency’s requirements. Satisfies an upper-division Writing Rich Mission Marker and an Experiential Learning for Social Justice Mission Marker. Prerequisite: WRIT 2302. Non-majors welcome with junior or senior standing.

Course Title: Senior Project in Writing and Rhetoric
Prerequisites: WRIT 3336, JOUR 3314, WRIT 3330, and at least three courses from concentration.
Other Requirements / Notes: Satisfies an upper-division Writing Rich Mission Marker and serves as the Culminating Experience course for WRIT majors.
Full Course Description: The culminating course for WRIT majors, this writing-intensive class is designed to help students reflect on their disciplinary identity. Showcasing the students’ rhetorical mastery, the course requires the design, execution, and production of a multi-phase project as well as reflection on connections to university mission and liberal arts education and work completed in the major. Satisfies an upper-division Writing Rich Mission Marker and serves as the Culminating Experience course for WRIT majors. Prerequisites: WRIT 3336, JOUR 3314, WRIT 3330, and at least three courses from concentration.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: The internship provides Writing and Rhetoric students with real- world professional experience that involves the practical use of theories and skills that have been introduced in the major. Students can serve internships on student publications, in mainstream or new media, as technical writers or editors, or as student assistants in introductory courses, particularly Freshman Studies. Students will report regularly to their internship advisor and will receive ongoing advice and evaluation. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: The internship provides Writing and Rhetoric students with real- world professional experience that involves the practical use of theories and skills that have been introduced in the major. Students can serve internships on student publications, in mainstream or new media, as technical writers or editors, or as student assistants in introductory courses, particularly Freshman Studies. Students will report regularly to their internship advisor and will receive ongoing advice and evaluation. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

Other Requirements / Notes: May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
Full Course Description: The internship provides Writing and Rhetoric students with real- world professional experience that involves the practical use of theories and skills that have been introduced in the major. Students can serve internships on student publications, in mainstream or new media, as technical writers or editors, or as student assistants in introductory courses, particularly Freshman Studies. Students will report regularly to their internship advisor and will receive ongoing advice and evaluation. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
